AI.conclusion: C2F is scalable for improving autonomous system reliability in adverse weather and shows the value of diverse synthetic data for efficient model training.

title: IGT-OMD: Implicit Gradient Transport for Decision-Focused Learning under Delayed Feedback
summary: Decision-focused learning trains predictive models end-to-end against downstream decision loss, but online settings suffer delayed feedback: outcomes may not arrive for many environment interactions. We identify \emph{staleness amplification}, a failure mode unique to bilevel optimization under delay, in which gradient staleness couples with inner-solver sensitivity to inflate regret beyond single-level delay theory. We prove that any black-box delayed optimizer incurs an irreducible regret cost from inner-solver approximation error, and that gradient staleness contributes a quadratically growing transport error without bilevel-aware correction. Our algorithm, \textbf{IGT-OMD}, applies Implicit Gradient Transport to hypergradients within Online Mirror Descent, re-evaluating stale gradients at the current parameters using stored inner solutions. This method reduces transport error from a quadratic to a linear dependence on delay and achieves the first sublinear regret bound for delayed bilevel optimization with queue-length-adaptive step sizes. Controlled experiments provide a \emph{mechanistic fingerprint}: transport benefit is exactly $0.0\%$ ($p=1.00$) at unit delay and grows monotonically to $9.5\%$ at fifty rounds ($p<0.001$), isolating the correction's effect. On Linear Quadratic Regulator, Warcraft shortest-path, and Sinkhorn optimal transport, IGT-OMD reduces decision loss by $17$--$55\%$ relative to single-level baselines, with phase transitions matching the theory.

AI.tldr: IGT-OMD mitigates staleness in delayed bilevel optimization by using Implicit Gradient Transport on hypergradients within Online Mirror Descent, reducing transport error to linear in delay and achieving sublinear regret; empirically improves decision loss 17–55% across tasks with a clear delay-dependent fingerprint.
AI.motivation: Decision-focused learning in online settings suffers from delayed feedback; bilevel optimization introduces gradient staleness and inner-solver sensitivity, causing regret inflation; existing single-level delay theory does not capture these effects.
AI.method: Apply Implicit Gradient Transport to hypergradients within Online Mirror Descent, re-evaluating stale gradients at current parameters using stored inner solutions; adopt queue-length-adaptive step sizes; provide theoretical regret bounds and transport error analysis; validate on LQR, Warcraft shortest-path, and Sinkhorn OT tasks.
AI.result: Transport error reduces from quadratic to linear in delay; first sublinear regret bound for delayed bilevel optimization with adaptive steps; empirical gains of 17–55% in decision loss vs single-level baselines; a delay-fingerprint confirms the effect.
AI.conclusion: IGT-OMD offers a principled correction for delayed bilevel learning, mitigating staleness amplification, with theoretical guarantees and practical performance gains across tasks.

title: DynoJEPP: Joint Estimation, Prediction and Planning in Dynamic Environments
summary: DynoJEPP is a factor-graph-based framework that jointly formulates and simultaneously optimizes estimation, prediction, and planning in dynamic environments. In conventional factor-graph-based approaches that jointly formulate estimation, prediction, and planning, information from prediction and planning feeds back into state estimation, yielding corrupted estimates, undesired behaviors, and unsafe plans. To address this, DynoJEPP introduces a novel directed factor that enforces directional information flow within the factor graph, preventing prediction and planning from corrupting state estimation. We evaluate the impact of directed factors on inter-module interactions during navigation in both static and dynamic environments. Our results demonstrate that these factors are critical for safe operation, as without them, the robot collides in the majority of experiments. Building on this, we further introduce Cooperative DynoJEPP, which enables the ego robot to incorporate cooperative object behavior into its prediction and trajectory planning.

AI.tldr: DynoJEPP introduces a directed factor in a factor-graph framework to control information flow between prediction, planning, and state estimation, improving safety in dynamic environments; it extends to Cooperative DynoJEPP for incorporating cooperative object behavior into prediction and planning.
AI.motivation: Jointly optimizing estimation, prediction, and planning can cause estimation to be corrupted by predictive and planning information, leading to unsafe trajectories; a mechanism to preserve estimation integrity in a unified framework is needed.
AI.method: Add a novel directed factor to enforce unidirectional information flow within the factor graph, preventing prediction/planning from influencing state estimation; evaluate impact on inter-module interactions in static/dynamic navigation scenarios; demonstrate safety benefits; extend with Cooperative DynoJEPP to model cooperative object behavior in prediction and planning.
AI.result: Directed factors improve safety by preventing corruption of state estimation from prediction/planning and are critical for safe navigation; without directed factors, robots collide in the majority of experiments; Cooperative DynoJEPP enables ego robot to reason about cooperative object behavior in prediction and trajectory planning.
AI.conclusion: Directing information flow within factor graphs is essential for safe joint estimation-prediction-planning in dynamic environments; the framework offers a path to safer, more reliable navigation and can be extended to cooperative scenarios.

title: When Attention Closes: How LLMs Lose the Thread in Multi-Turn Interaction
summary: Large language models can follow complex instructions in a single turn, yet over long multi-turn interactions they often lose the thread of instructions, persona, and rules. This degradation has been measured behaviorally but not mechanistically explained. We propose a channel-transition account: goal-defining tokens become less accessible through attention, while goal-related information may persist in residual representations. We introduce the Goal Accessibility Ratio (GAR), measuring attention from generated tokens to task-defining goal tokens, and combine it with sliding-window ablations and residual-stream probes. When attention to instructions closes, what survives reveals architecture. Across architectures, the transition yields qualitatively distinct failure modes: some models preserve goal-conditioned behavior at vanishing attention, others fail despite decodable residual goal information, and the layer at which this encoding emerges varies from 2 to 27. A within-model causal ablation that force-closes the attention channel in Mistral collapses recall from near-perfect to 11% on a 20-fact retention task and raises persona-constraint violations above an adversarial-pressure baseline without user pressure, with both effects emerging at the predictable crossover turn. Linear probes recover per-episode recall outcomes from residual representations with AUC up to 0.99 across all four primary architectures, while input embeddings remain at chance. Across architectures and model scales, the gap between attention loss and residual decodability predicts whether goal-conditioned behavior survives channel closure. We contribute GAR as a diagnostic, the channel-transition framework as a controlled mechanistic account, and a parametric prediction of failure timing under windowed attention closure.

AI.tldr: A mechanistic channel-transition framework explains long-horizon instruction-following failures in LLMs, introducing Goal Accessibility Ratio (GAR) to quantify attention to task goals, and showing residual representations can retain goal information leading to distinct failure modes; the approach enables diagnostic prediction of failure timing and cross-architecture generalization.
AI.motivation: Explain mechanistically why instruction-following degrades over multi-turn interactions, by linking attention dynamics to residual representations across architectures.
AI.method: Define GAR; perform sliding-window attention analyses; apply residual-stream linear probes; conduct causal ablations forcing attention closure in Mistral; test four architectures; use a 20-fact retention task; compare attention-decayed and residual decodability; assess failure timing.
AI.result: Attention to instructions can vanish while residual goal information remains in some layers; different architectures show preservation vs failure; ablation reduces recall and increases violations; residual probes achieve high AUC (up to 0.99) while embeddings remain at chance; the layer where encoding appears spans 2–27; a predictive gap between attention loss and residual decodability correlates with survival of goal-conditioned behavior; GAR serves as diagnostic.
AI.conclusion: Proposes GAR and the channel-transition framework as a controlled mechanistic account with a parametric prediction of failure timing under attention closure; supports cross-architecture generalization and provides a practical diagnostic tool for goal-conditioned behavior longevity.

title: MambaPanoptic: A Vision Mamba-based Structured State Space Framework for Panoptic Segmentation
summary: Panoptic segmentation requires the simultaneous recognition of countable thing instances and amorphous stuff regions, placing joint demands on long-range context modelling, multi-scale feature representation, and efficient dense prediction. Existing convolutional and transformer-based methods struggle to satisfy all three requirements concurrently: convolutional architectures are limited in their capacity to model long-range dependencies, while transformer-based methods incur quadratic computational cost that is prohibitive at high resolutions. In this paper, we propose MambaPanoptic, a fully Mamba-based panoptic segmentation framework that addresses these limitations through two principal contributions. First, we introduce MambaFPN, a top-down feature pyramid that leverages Mamba blocks to generate globally coherent, multi-scale feature representations with linear computational complexity. Second, we adopt a PanopticFCN-style kernel generator that produces unified thing and stuff kernels for proposal-free panoptic prediction, enhanced by a QuadMamba-based feature refinement module applied at multiple network stages. Experiments on the Cityscapes and COCO panoptic segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that MambaPanoptic consistently outperforms PanopticDeepLab and PanopticFCN under comparable model sizes, and matches or surpasses Mask2Former on Cityscapes in PQ and AP while requiring fewer parameters.

AI.tldr: MambaPanoptic introduces a fully Mamba-based panoptic segmentation framework featuring MambaFPN for globally coherent multi-scale features with linear complexity, and a QuadMamba-refined, kernel-generator (PanopticFCN-style) for unified thing/stuff prediction; it achieves competitive results against state-of-the-art with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Current panoptic methods struggle to jointly satisfy long-range context modeling, multi-scale feature representation, and dense prediction efficiency. Convolutional nets lag in long-range modeling; transformers incur quadratic cost at high resolution. A unified, efficient architecture that combines long-range capability with linear complexity is needed.
AI.method: Propose MambaPanoptic with two main contributions: (1) MambaFPN, a top-down feature pyramid built with Mamba blocks to produce globally coherent, multi-scale features with linear computational cost; (2) a PanopticFCN-like kernel generator that yields unified kernels for both thing and stuff, enhanced by a QuadMamba-based feature refinement module applied at multiple network stages.
AI.result: Empirical results on Cityscapes and COCO show MambaPanoptic outperforms PanopticDeepLab and PanopticFCN at comparable model sizes and matches or surpasses Mask2Former on Cityscapes in PQ and AP while using fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: A fully Mamba-based framework can achieve competitive panoptic performance with linear complexity, effectively addressing long-range modeling, multi-scale representation, and dense prediction efficiency, and setting a new efficiency/accuracy balance in panoptic segmentation.

title: Modeling Heterophily in Multiplex Graphs: An Adaptive Approach for Node Classification
comment: 38 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables, 1 algorithm. Published in Expert Systems with Applications
summary: Existing multiplex graph models often assume homophily, where connected nodes tend to belong to the same class or share similar attributes. Consequently, these models may struggle with graphs exhibiting heterophily, where connected nodes typically belong to different classes and have dissimilar attributes. While recent methods have been developed to learn reliable node representations from unidimensional graphs with heterophily, they do not fully address the complexities of multiplex graphs. In a multiplex graph, nodes are linked through multiple types of edges (referred to as dimensions), which can simultaneously exhibit homophilic and heterophilic interactions. To address this gap, we propose \methodname, a novel method for node classification in multiplex graphs that adapts to both homophilic and heterophilic dimensions. \methodname introduces dimension-specific compatibility matrices to model varying degrees of homophily and heterophily across dimensions. A key innovation is its use of a product of trainable low-pass and high-pass filters, approximated via Chebyshev polynomials, to capture both smooth and abrupt changes in the graph signal. By composing these filters and optimizing label predictions using a proximal-gradient method, \methodname dynamically adjusts to the heterophilic characteristics of each dimension. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets provide evidence that \methodname captures the complex interplay of homophilic and heterophilic interactions in multiplex graphs, and tends to yield improved node classification performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A multiplex graph method with dimension-specific homophily/heterophily handling using a product of trainable low-pass and high-pass filters via Chebyshev polynomials, optimized with proximal-gradient for node classification.
AI.motivation: Because multiplex graphs exhibit both homophilic and heterophilic interactions across different dimensions, existing homophily-assuming models fail to capture this complexity; there is a need for dimension-aware models that can adapt to mixed affinities.
AI.method: Introduce dimension-specific compatibility matrices to model varying degrees of homophily/heterophily across edge types (dimensions). Use a product of trainable low-pass and high-pass graph filters, approximated with Chebyshev polynomials, to capture smooth and abrupt signal changes. Compose these filters across dimensions and optimize label predictions with a proximal-gradient method.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show improved node classification performance over state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating the method's ability to capture the interplay of homophilic and heterophilic interactions in multiplex graphs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework adaptively handles heterophily and homophily across dimensions in multiplex graphs, achieving robust improvements and offering a flexible tool for diverse affinity structures.

title: Distributionally Robust Safety Under Arbitrary Uncertainties: A Safety Filtering Approach
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: In this work, we study how to ensure probabilistic safety for nonlinear systems under distributional ambiguity. Our approach builds on a backup-based safety filtering framework that switches between a high-performance nominal policy and a certified backup policy to ensure safety. To handle arbitrary uncertainties from ambiguous distributions, i.e., where the distribution is not of specific structure and the true distribution is unknown, we adopt a distributionally robust (DR) formulation using Wasserstein ambiguity sets. Rather than solving a high-dimensional DR trajectory optimization problem online, we exploit the structure of backup-based safety filtering to reduce safety certification to a one-dimensional search over the switching time between nominal and backup policies. We then develop a sampling-based certification procedure with finite-sample guarantees, where empirical failure probabilities are compared against a Wasserstein-inflated threshold. We validate our method through simulations across three systems, from a Dubins vehicle to a high-speed racing car and a fighter jet, demonstrating the broad applicability and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Distributionally robust safety filtering for nonlinear systems using Wasserstein ambiguity sets; reduces DR trajectory optimization to a 1D search over switching time between nominal and backup policies, with finite-sample probabilistic guarantees, validated on a Dubins vehicle, a high-speed race car, and a fighter jet.
AI.motivation: Ensure probabilistic safety under distributional ambiguity for nonlinear systems where the true disturbance distribution is unknown or unstructured, necessitating robust safety guarantees without overly conservative planning.
AI.method: Backup-based safety filtering that switches between a high-performance nominal policy and a certified backup policy. Employs a distributionally robust formulation with Wasserstein ambiguity sets. Reduces the DR optimization to a one-dimensional search over the switching time. Introduces a sampling-based certification procedure with finite-sample guarantees by comparing empirical failure probabilities to a Wasserstein-inflated threshold.
AI.result: Demonstrates computational efficiency and broad applicability across three systems (Dubins vehicle, high-speed racing car, fighter jet) through simulations, validating safety guarantees and practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, provably safe control method under distributional ambiguity, leveraging backup-based switching and Wasserstein DR analysis to achieve probabilistic safety with finite-sample certificates.

title: Sustaining AI safety: Control-theoretic external impossibility, intrinsic necessity, and structural requirements
summary: As AI systems become increasingly capable, safety strategies must be evaluated not only by how much they reduce present risk, but by whether they could sustain safety once external control can no longer reliably constrain system behavior. This paper addresses that problem by using control theory to clarify, at a structural level, whether externally enforced safety-sustaining strategies can succeed and, if not, what any alternative strategy would have to satisfy in order to be viable. It establishes two main results. First, under explicit premises including a reachability condition, it proves a class-wide external impossibility result: once the system's effects exceed what bounded external control can counteract, no strategy that depends in any degree on continued external enforcement can sustain AI safety. This failure is structural across the entire externally enforced class rather than contingent on any particular strategy. Second, it establishes a conditional class-level necessity result: if at least one candidate safety-sustaining strategy remains after that elimination, then all such remaining strategies must be intrinsic. It then states four structural requirements for viability: safety may not depend on continued external enforcement; the system's terminal objective must be safety-compatible when first formed; that objective must remain stable under self-modification; and safety must continue to be preserved as capability grows. The paper does not propose a complete strategy for sustaining AI safety. Its contribution is to give formal structure to a widely held concern about the limits of external control. It does so by deriving explicit conditional results that identify which safety-sustaining strategies are ruled out and what any remaining strategies must satisfy.

AI.tldr: Structural analysis showing external control cannot sustain AI safety beyond a bound; any remaining strategies must be intrinsic; four viability criteria; no complete strategy proposed.
AI.motivation: Address long-term AI safety under diminishing external control; understand limits and necessary conditions for viable strategies.
AI.method: Control-theoretic, formal structural analysis; explicit premises including a reachability condition; class-wide impossibility proof; conditional class-level necessity proof; derivation of four structural requirements.
AI.result: Impossibility result: once AI effects exceed bounded external countermeasures, no externally enforced strategy can sustain safety. Necessity result: if any safety-sustaining strategy remains, all must be intrinsic. Four viability properties: no reliance on ongoing external enforcement; safety-compatible terminal objective at formation; objective stability under self-modification; safety preserved under capability growth.
AI.conclusion: Provides formal framing and conditional results clarifying limits of external control; does not supply a complete safety-sustaining strategy but delineates what remains viable and the properties it must have.

title: DIVER:Diving Deeper into Distilled Data via Expressive Semantic Recovery
summary: Dataset distillation aims to synthesize a compact proxy dataset that is unreadable or non-raw from the original dataset for privacy protection and highly efficient learning. However, previous approaches typically adopt a single-stage distillation paradigm, which suffers from learning specific patterns that overfit on a prior architecture, consequently suppressing the expression of semantics and leading to performance degradation across heterogeneous architectures. To address this issue, we propose a novel dual-stage distillation framework called ${\textbf{DIVER}}$, which leverages the pre-trained diffusion model to dive deeper into $\textbf{DI}$stilled data $\textbf{V}$ia $\textbf{E}$xpressive semantic $\textbf{R}$ecovery, an entire process of semantic inheritance, guidance, and fusion. Semantic inheritance distills high-level semantics of abstract distilled images into the latent space to filter out architecture-specific ``noise" and retain the intrinsic semantics. Furthermore, semantic guidance improves the preservation of the original semantics by directing the reverse procedure. Finally, semantic fusion is designed to provide semantic guidance only during the concrete phase of the reverse process, preventing semantic ambiguity and artifacts while maintaining the guidance information. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness and efficiency of DIVER in improving classical distillation techniques and significantly improving cross-architecture generalization, requiring processing time comparable to raw DiT on ImageNet (256$\times$256) with only 4 GB of GPU memory usage. Code is available: https://github.com/einsteinxia/DIVER.

AI.tldr: DIVER introduces a dual-stage, diffusion-based dataset distillation (semantic recovery) framework that preserves intrinsic semantics and improves cross-architecture generalization with efficient resource use.
AI.motivation: Single-stage distillation overfits to a prior architecture, suppressing semantics and harming cross-architecture performance; a diffusion-guided, semantically expressive approach is needed to preserve intrinsic semantics while remaining efficient.
AI.method: A three-part diffusion-driven approach: (1) semantic inheritance distills high-level semantics into latent space to filter architecture-specific noise; (2) semantic guidance steers the reverse diffusion to better preserve semantics; (3) semantic fusion injects guidance only during the concrete reverse phase to avoid artifacts; deployed as a dual-stage distillation framework DIVER using a pre-trained diffusion model.
AI.result: Experiments show improved cross-architecture generalization across distillation methods, with processing time comparable to training on raw diffusion-tuned data (DiT) on ImageNet (256x256) and only 4 GB GPU memory; code released.
AI.conclusion: DIVER effectively mitigates architecture biases in distillation, yielding semantically faithful, efficient distilled data with strong cross-architecture transfer.

title: UFO: A Domain-Unification-Free Operator Framework for Generalized Operator Learning
summary: Neural operators have become an effective framework for learning mappings between function spaces, yet most existing architectures realize operators within a single representational domain, such as physical, spectral, or latent space. In this work, we introduce UFO (Domain-Unification-Free Operator), a cross-domain neural operator framework that realizes operators through adaptive, jointly conditioned interactions among representations defined on distinct domains. UFO enables discretization decoupling: the input function can be observed at resolutions or locations different from those used during training, while the solution can be queried at arbitrary output resolutions. Across four complementary benchmarks covering discontinuous inputs, irregular sampling with spectral mismatch, nonlinear dynamics, and stochastic high-frequency fields, UFO delivers accurate, robust, and physically coherent predictions under distribution shifts. These results establish cross-domain, phase-modulated realization as a powerful framework for discretization-decoupled neural operator learning.

AI.tldr: UFO enables cross-domain neural operators with discretization-decoupling by adaptively coordinating representations across multiple domains, delivering robust, physically coherent predictions under distribution shifts across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitation of neural operators being confined to a single representational domain (physical, spectral, or latent) by introducing a cross-domain framework that supports input/output discretization mismatch and flexible query resolutions.
AI.method: Propose UFO, a cross-domain, domain-unification-free operator that uses adaptive, jointly conditioned interactions among representations defined on distinct domains (phase-modulated interactions) to realize operators across multiple domains.
AI.result: UFO achieves accurate and robust predictions across four benchmarks involving discontinuous inputs, irregular sampling with spectral mismatch, nonlinear dynamics, and stochastic high-frequency fields, with discretization-decoupled behavior and good distribution-shift robustness.
AI.conclusion: Cross-domain, phase-modulated realization provides a powerful framework for discretization-decoupled neural operator learning, enabling input/output resolution flexibility and improved robustness.

title: Position: Agentic AI System Is a Foreseeable Pathway to AGI
summary: Is monolithic scaling the only path to AGI? This paper challenges the dogma that purely scaling a single model is sufficient to achieve Artificial General Intelligence. Instead, we identify Agentic AI as a necessary paradigm for mastering the complex, heterogeneous distribution of real-world tasks. Through rigorous theoretical derivations, we contrast the optimization constraints of monolithic learners against the efficiency of Agentic systems, progressing from simple routing mechanisms to general Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) topologies. We demonstrate that Agentic AI achieves exponentially superior generalization and sample efficiency. Finally, we discuss the connection to Mixture-of-Experts, reinterpret the instability of current multi-agent frameworks, and call for greater research focus on Agentic AI.

AI.tldr: Agentic AI, leveraging DAG-based routing and Mixture-of-Experts concepts, is proposed as a more scalable and efficient path to AGI than monolithic scaling, enabling better generalization across heterogeneous tasks.
AI.motivation: Question the sufficiency of single-model scaling for AGI; address the real-world task distribution's heterogeneity by proposing agentic paradigms.
AI.method: Theoretical derivations contrasting monolithic optimization constraints with agentic architectures; progression from simple routing to general DAG topologies; analysis of Mixture-of-Experts connections and multi-agent instability.
AI.result: Claim that Agentic AI yields exponentially superior generalization and sample efficiency; reframes ensemble methods and MOE through DAG-based agentic systems.
AI.conclusion: Urges greater research focus on Agentic AI, advocating DAG/topology-inspired architectures and improved understanding of agentic dynamics as the path toward robust AGI.

title: CRAFT: Clinical Reward-Aligned Finetuning for Medical Image Synthesis
summary: Foundation diffusion models can generate photorealistic natural images, but adapting them to medical imaging remains challenging. In medical adaptation, limited labeled data can exacerbate hallucination-like and clinically implausible synthesis, while existing metrics such as FID or Inception Score do not quantify per-image alignment with pathology-relevant criteria. We introduce the Clinical Alignment Score (CAS), a foundation-model-based proxy for clinical alignment that evaluates generated images along four complementary dimensions beyond visual fidelity. Building on CAS, we propose Clinical Reward-Aligned Finetuning (CRAFT), a reward-based adaptation framework that transfers medical knowledge from multimodal large language models and vision-language models through label-conditioned prompt enrichment, clinical checklists, and differentiable reward optimization. Across four diverse modalities, CRAFT improves CAS and downstream classification performance over strong adaptation baselines. Beyond average CAS gains, CRAFT reduces the empirical low-alignment tail below a real-image reference threshold by 5.5-34.7% points relative to the strongest baseline, corresponding to a 20.4% average relative reduction across datasets. These results indicate fewer hallucination-like generations under CAS, and are corroborated by out-of-family evaluator evaluation, structured checklist auditing, memorization analysis, and a blinded physician preference study on CheXpert.

AI.tldr: Clinical Alignment Score (CAS) and Clinical Reward-Aligned Finetuning (CRAFT) for medical diffusion-model adaptation; CAS provides a per-image clinical alignment metric across four dimensions; CRAFT uses reward-based finetuning with prompts, clinical checklists, and differentiable rewards to improve alignment across four modalities, reducing low-alignment failures and boosting downstream tasks, validated with physician studies.
AI.motivation: Hallucination-like outputs and clinical misalignment arise when adapting foundation diffusion models to medical imaging, especially with limited labeled data; existing metrics like FID/Inception fail to quantify pathology-specific alignment.
AI.method: Define CAS as a foundation-model-based proxy evaluating four clinically relevant dimensions beyond visual fidelity. Develop CRAFT, a reward-based fine-tuning framework that leverages medical knowledge from multimodal LLMs and vision-language models via label-conditioned prompt enrichment, clinical checklists, and differentiable reward optimization.
AI.result: Across four modalities, CRAFT improves CAS and downstream classification relative to strong baselines; reduces the empirical low-alignment tail by 5.5–34.7 percentage points (average 20.4% reduction) versus the strongest baseline; corroborated by out-of-family evaluator evaluation, structured checklists, memorization analysis, and blinded physician preference study on CheXpert.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT reduces hallucination-like generations under CAS and generalizes across modalities; demonstrates safer, more clinically aligned medical image synthesis, though further validation and ablations are needed for deployment.

title: Do Fair Models Reason Fairly? Counterfactual Explanation Consistency for Procedural Fairness in Credit Decisions
summary: Machine learning algorithms in socially sensitive domains (e.g., credit decisions) often focus on equalizing predictive outcomes. However, satisfying these metrics does not guarantee that models use the same reasoning for different groups. We show that existing outcome-fair models can still apply fundamentally different reasoning to individuals, a ``hidden procedural bias'' missed by standard fairness metrics and algorithms. We propose Counterfactual Explanation Consistency (CEC), a framework that detects and mitigates this bias by aligning feature attributions between individuals and their counterfactual counterparts. Key contributions include a nearest-neighbor counterfactual generation method, a modified baseline for integrated gradient comparisons, an individual-level procedural fairness metric, and a corresponding training loss. We introduce a taxonomy identifying ``Regime B'' (same outcome, different reasoning) as a critical blind spot. Experiments on synthetic data, German Credit, Adult Income, and HMDA mortgage data demonstrate that outcome-fair baselines exhibit substantial hidden bias, while CEC substantially reduces it with modest utility cost.

AI.tldr: CEC detects hidden procedural bias in outcome-fair ML models by aligning feature attributions with counterfactuals; introduces nearest-neighbor counterfactuals, a modified integrated gradients baseline, an individual fairness metric, and a training loss; identifies Regime B (same outcome, different reasoning) as a blind spot; experiments on synthetic and real datasets show CEC reduces bias with modest utility cost.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based fairness metrics can be satisfied even when models use different and potentially biased reasoning across individuals; aim to expose and mitigate hidden procedural bias in decision systems.
AI.method: Propose Counterfactual Explanation Consistency (CEC) to align feature attributions between individuals and their counterfactuals. Key components include a nearest-neighbor counterfactual generator, a modified baseline for integrated gradients comparisons, an individual-level procedural fairness metric, and a corresponding training objective to minimize attribution inconsistency.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic data, German Credit, Adult Income, and HMDA mortgage data show that conventional outcome-fair baselines exhibit substantial hidden bias (Regime B); CEC substantially reduces this bias with only modest utility loss.
AI.conclusion: Aligning explanations across counterfactuals is effective for detecting and mitigating hidden procedural bias in fair ML, revealing blind spots in outcome-based fairness and offering a practical training objective to improve individual-level fairness.

title: Local Conformal Calibration of Dynamics Uncertainty from Semantic Images
comment: 26 pages, 8 figures. Accepted to the 17th World Symposium on the Algorithmic Foundations of Robotics (WAFR) 2026
summary: We introduce Observation-aware Conformal Uncertainty Local-Calibration (OCULAR), a conformal prediction-based algorithm that uses perception information to provide uncertainty quantification guarantees for unseen test-time environments. While previous conformal approaches lack the ability to discriminate between state-action space regions leading to higher or lower model mismatch, and require environment-specific data, our method uses data collected from visually similar environments to provably calibrate a given linear Gaussian dynamics model of arbitrary fidelity. The prediction regions generated from OCULAR are guaranteed to contain the future system states with, at least, a user-set likelihood, despite both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty -- i.e., uncertainty arising from both stochastic disturbances and lack of data. Our guarantees are non-asymptotic and distribution-free, not requiring strong assumptions about the unknown real system dynamics. Our calibration procedure enables distinguishing between observation-velocity-action inputs leading to higher and lower next-state-uncertainty, which is helpful for probabilistically-safe planning. We numerically validate our algorithm on a double-integrator system subject to random perturbations and significant model mismatch, using both a simplified sensor and a more realistic simulated camera. Our approach appropriately quantifies uncertainty both when in-distribution and out-of-distribution, being comparatively volume-efficient to baselines requiring environment-specific data.

AI.tldr: OCULAR uses observation-aware conformal prediction to calibrate a dynamic model with data from visually similar environments, producing non-asymptotic, distribution-free uncertainty regions that cover future states with user-defined probability even under aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty; it differentiates input regions by next-state uncertainty and is more sample-efficient than environment-specific baselines.
AI.motivation: To provide provable uncertainty guarantees for unseen test-time environments in robotic dynamics, addressing both model mismatch and ambiguous perception by leveraging perception information and cross-environment calibration.
AI.method: A conformal prediction-based algorithm (OCULAR) that calibrates a linear Gaussian dynamics model of arbitrary fidelity using data from visually similar environments. It produces prediction regions that guarantee future states with a user-specified likelihood under both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. The guarantees are non-asymptotic and distribution-free. The calibration discerns observation-velocity-action input regions with higher versus lower next-state uncertainty, enabling safer planning.
AI.result: Numerical validation on a double-integrator system with random perturbations and model mismatch, using a simplified sensor and a simulated camera. OCULAR yields uncertainty quantification for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution cases and is more volume-efficient than baselines that require environment-specific data.
AI.conclusion: OCULAR provides provable, region-aware uncertainty calibration for unseen environments, facilitating probabilistically-safe planning and robust operation under model mismatch and perceptual uncertainty, with favorable data efficiency compared to environment-specific baselines.

title: Retrieval is Cheap, Show Me the Code: Executable Multi-Hop Reasoning for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a standard approach for knowledge-intensive question answering, but existing systems remain brittle on multi-hop questions, where solving the task requires chaining multiple retrieval and reasoning steps. Key challenges are that current methods represent reasoning through free-form natural language, where intermediate states are implicit, retrieval queries can drift from intended entities, and errors are detected by the same model that produces them making self-reflection an unreliable, ungrounded signal.
  We observe that multi-hop question answering is a typical form of step-by-step computation, and that this structured process aligns closely with how code-specialized language models are trained to operate. Motivated by this, we introduce \pyrag, a framework that reformulates multi-hop RAG as program synthesis and execution. Instead of free-form reasoning trajectories, \pyrag represents the reasoning process as an executable Python program over retrieval and QA tools, exposing intermediate states as variables, producing deterministic feedback through execution, and yielding an inspectable trace of the entire reasoning process. This formulation further enables compiler-grounded self-repair and execution-driven adaptive retrieval without any additional training.
  Experiments on five QA benchmarks (PopQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue, and Bamboogle) show that \pyrag consistently outperforms strong baselines under both training-free and RL-trained settings, with especially large gains on compositional multi-hop datasets. Our code, data and models are publicly available at https://github.com/GasolSun36/PyRAG.

AI.tldr: PyRAG treats multi-hop retrieval QA as program synthesis and execution in Python, replacing free-form reasoning with an executable, traceable strategy that exposes intermediate states and deterministic feedback; it enables self-repair and adaptive retrieval without extra training and shows strong gains on five benchmarks, especially compositional multi-hop tasks.
AI.motivation: To address brittleness in retrieval-augmented generation on multi-hop questions, where reasoning is implicit, retrieval can drift, and self-reflection signals are unreliable.
AI.method: Formulate multi-hop QA as a callable Python program that operates over retrieval and QA tools. The program exposes intermediate variables (state), executes deterministically to yield feedback, and produces a trace of the entire reasoning process. It leverages compiler-grounded self-repair and execution-driven retrieval without additional training.
AI.result: PyRAG consistently outperforms strong baselines on five QA benchmarks (PopQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue, Bamboogle) under both training-free and RL-trained settings, with particularly large gains on compositional multi-hop datasets.
AI.conclusion: Recasting multi-hop RAG as program synthesis/execution yields more reliable, inspectable reasoning traces and enables self-repair and adaptive retrieval, improving performance without extra training.

title: No One Knows the State of the Art in Geospatial Foundation Models
summary: Geospatial foundation models (GFMs) have been proposed as generalizable backbones for disaster response, land-cover mapping, food-security monitoring, and other high-stakes Earth-observation tasks. Yet the published work about these models does not give reviewers or users enough information to tell which model fits a given task. We argue that nobody knows what the current state of the art is in geospatial foundation models. The methods may be useful, but the GFM literature does not standardize evaluations, training and testing protocols, released weights, or pretraining controls well enough for anyone to compare or rank them. In a 152-paper audit, we find 46 cross-paper disagreements of at least 10 points for the same model, benchmark, and protocol; 94/126 papers with extractable pretraining data use a configuration no other paper uses; and 39% of GFM papers release no model weights. This lack of community standards can be solved. We propose six concrete expectations: named-license weight release, shared core evaluations, copied-versus-rerun baseline annotations, variance reporting, one shared evaluation harness, and data-vs-architecture-vs-algorithm controls. These gaps are a coordination failure, not a fault of any individual lab; the authors of this paper, like many others in the GFM community, have contributed to them. Rather than just critiquing the community, we aim to provide concrete steps toward a shared understanding of how to innovate GFMs.

AI.tldr: GFMs in geospatial tasks suffer from inconsistent evaluation, training, and release practices; an audit reveals widespread disagreements and missing weights, urging six concrete standards to enable reproducibility and fair ranking.
AI.motivation: To diagnose why GFMs are difficult to compare and to rank, despite growing literature; to propose concrete, actionable standards that would improve reproducibility and comparability across studies.
AI.method: Audit of 152 geospatial foundation model papers. assessed cross-paper disagreements, pretraining data configurations, and model weight releases; identified lack of standard evaluations and protocols; proposed six concrete expectations to fix the gaps.
AI.result: Findings include 46 cross-paper disagreements (≥10 points) for the same model, benchmark, and protocol; 94/126 papers with extractable pretraining data use configurations not shared by others; 39% of papers release no model weights.
AI.conclusion: The gaps reflect a coordination failure rather than individual lapses. The authors propose six concrete expectations—named-license weight release, shared core evaluations, copied-vs-rerun baseline annotations, variance reporting, one shared evaluation harness, and data-vs-architecture-vs-algorithm controls—as steps toward a shared, reproducible GFM landscape.

title: Early Data Exposure Improves Robustness to Subsequent Fine-Tuning
summary: How can we train models whose post-trained capabilities survive subsequent fine-tuning? Rather than focusing on downstream interventions to mitigate forgetting of upstream capabilities, we study how upstream training choices - that is, the manner in which a capability is acquired - shape how robustly that capability is retained. We investigate this question in a controlled three-stage language-model pipeline: pretraining, post-training to acquire a target capability, and downstream fine-tuning on a new objective. Across 135M and 1B models, two post-training domains, and two downstream fine-tuning tasks, we find that immediate post-training performance does not reliably predict retention after subsequent fine-tuning: training recipes that look equivalent immediately after post-training can retain the target capability very differently after subsequent fine-tuning. In particular, early exposure - mixing post-training data into pretraining - consistently improves the frontier between retained upstream performance and downstream performance. In compute-matched experiments, where the target data must be allocated between pretraining and post-training, we find that the optimum lies at neither extreme. Together with our other empirical and theoretical findings, this supports the view that post-training drives immediate specialization while early exposure improves robustness to later forgetting. Replay and dropout, typically used to mitigate forgetting as it occurs during fine-tuning, provide complementary gains to early exposure when applied during post-training. Our findings suggest that robustness to subsequent fine-tuning should be treated as a first-class objective of upstream training, addressed preventatively through choices like early exposure rather than reactively during fine-tuning itself.

AI.tldr: Upstream training choices, especially early exposure during post-training, determine how robust a captured capability is to being forgotten during later fine-tuning; immediate post-training performance does not predict retention; optimum data allocation lies between pretraining and post-training; replay/dropout help; treat robustness as a primary upstream objective.
AI.motivation: To understand and improve robustness of learned capabilities to survive subsequent fine-tuning by focusing on upstream training design rather than reactive methods during fine-tuning.
AI.method: Three-stage language-model pipeline: pretraining, post-training to acquire a target capability, then downstream fine-tuning on a new objective. Large-scale experiments across 135M and 1B models, two post-training domains, and two downstream tasks. Key manipulations include early exposure (mixing post-training data into pretraining), compute-matched data allocation between pretraining and post-training, and use of replay/dropout during post-training.
AI.result: Immediate post-training performance is not a reliable predictor of retention after downstream fine-tuning. Early exposure consistently improves the robustness frontier between retained upstream performance and downstream performance. The optimal data split between pretraining and post-training lies between the extremes. Replay and dropout during post-training provide additional gains. Overall, robustness to subsequent fine-tuning should be a core objective of upstream training rather than addressed reactively during fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Upstream training strategies, notably early exposure and regularization techniques, shape the durability of capabilities against forgetting in later tasks. Treat robustness to downstream forgetting as a primary objective in pre/post-training design, with practical guidance like partial early exposure and complementary techniques (replay/dropout).

title: MUJICA: Multi-skill Unified Joint Integration of Control Architecture for Wheeled-Legged Robots
summary: Wheeled-legged robots hold promise for traversing complex terrains and offer superior mobility compared to legged robots. However, wheeled-legged robots must effectively balance both wheeled driving and legged control. Furthermore, due to noisy proprioceptive sensing and real-world motor constraints, realizing robust and adaptive locomotion at peak performance of motors remains challenging. We propose the Multi-skill Unified Joint Integration of Control Architecture (MUJICA), a unified, fully proprioceptive control framework for wheeled-legged robots that integrates diverse low-level skills-including omnidirectional moving, high platform climbing, and fall recovery-within a single policy. All skills, distinguished by unique indicator variables, are trained jointly with accurate DC-motor constraint modeling. Additionally, a high-level skill selector is learned to dynamically choose the optimal skill based solely on proprioceptions, enabling adaptive responses to the surrounding environment. Therefore, MUJICA enhances sim-to-real robustness and enables seamless transitions across diverse locomotion modes, facilitating autonomous adjustment to the environment. We validate our framework in both simulation and real-world experiments on the Unitree Go2-W robot, demonstrating significant improvements in adaptability and task success in unstructured environments.

AI.tldr: MUJICA is a unified proprioceptive control framework for wheeled-legged robots that jointly trains multiple skills (omnidirectional movement, high-platform climbing, fall recovery) with DC-motor constraint modeling, plus a high-level skill selector using only proprioception, enabling adaptive transitions and sim-to-real robustness; validated on Unitree Go2-W with improved adaptability in unstructured environments.
AI.motivation: Wheeled-legged robots must balance wheeled propulsion and legged locomotion under noisy proprioception and motor constraints; existing approaches lack a unified multi-skill policy and rely on often brittle simulations for real-world performance.
AI.method: Integrates diverse low-level skills, each distinguished by indicator variables, trained jointly with accurate DC-motor constraint modeling; learns a high-level skill selector from proprioceptive inputs to pick optimal skill; all within a single cohesive policy; aims for robust sim-to-real transfer and adaptive mode transitions.
AI.result: Validation in simulation and real-world experiments on the Unitree Go2-W robot showing significant improvements in adaptability and task success in unstructured environments.
AI.conclusion: MUJICA enables seamless transitions among locomotion modes and enhances robustness and adaptability of wheeled-legged robots, promoting autonomous operation in complex environments.

title: Useful Memories Become Faulty When Continuously Updated by LLMs
summary: Learning from past experience benefits from two complementary forms of memory: episodic traces -- raw trajectories of what happened -- and consolidated abstractions distilled across many episodes into reusable, schema-like lessons. Recent agentic-memory systems pursue the consolidated form: an LLM rewrites past trajectories into a textual memory bank that it continuously updates with new interactions, promising self-improving agents without parameter updates. Yet we find that such consolidated memories produced by today's LLMs are often faulty even when derived from useful experiences. As consolidation proceeds, memory utility first rises, then degrades, and can fall below the no-memory baseline. More surprisingly, even when consolidating from ground-truth solutions, GPT-5.4 fails on 54% of a set of ARC-AGI problems it had previously solved without memory. We trace the regression to the consolidation step rather than the underlying experience: the same trajectories yield qualitatively different memories under different update schedules, and an episodic-only control that simply retains those trajectories remains competitive with the consolidators we test. In a controlled ARC-AGI Stream environment that exposes Retain, Delete, and Consolidate actions, agents preserve raw episodes by default and double the accuracy of their forced-consolidation counterparts; disabling consolidation entirely (episodic management only) matches this auto regime. Practically, robust agent memory should treat raw episodes as first-class evidence and gate consolidation explicitly rather than firing it after every interaction. Looking forward, reliable agentic memory will require LLMs that can consolidate without overwriting the evidence they depend on.

AI.tldr: Consolidated, LLM-generated memories from past experiences can degrade performance; preserving raw episodic traces and gating consolidation yields better accuracy than automatic consolidation. The regression is due to the consolidation step overwriting evidence, not the experiences themselves.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether consolidated memories created by LLMs reliably improve agent performance, and identify failure modes that arise from the consolidation process.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation in an ARC-AGI Stream environment with actions Retain, Delete, and Consolidate. Compare episodic-only management versus forced/consolidated memories using both ground-truth trajectories and LLM-based rewritings. Assess memory utility across different update schedules and task sets, including ARC-AGI problems that were previously solved.
AI.result: Memory utility is non-monotonic: it can rise and then degrade with consolidation, even failing on 54% of ARC-AGI problems previously solved. An episodic-only control retains trajectories and matches or outperforms consolidators; disabling consolidation (episodic management only) achieves similar gains. In controlled tests, retaining raw episodes doubles accuracy compared to forced consolidation.
AI.conclusion: Robust agent memory should treat raw episodes as primary evidence and gate consolidation rather than applying it after every interaction. Reliable memory will require LLMs capable of consolidating without overwriting the evidential trajectories; otherwise, consolidation can undermine performance.

title: Visual Aesthetic Benchmark: Can Frontier Models Judge Beauty?
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are now routinely deployed for visual understanding, generation, and curation. A substantial fraction of these applications require an explicit aesthetic judgment. Most existing solutions reduce this judgment to predicting a scalar score for a single image. We first ask whether such scores faithfully capture comparative preference: in a controlled study with eight expert annotators, score-derived rankings align poorly with the same annotators' direct comparisons, while direct ranking yields substantially higher inter-annotator agreement on best- and worst-image labels. Motivated by this finding, we introduce the Visual Aesthetic Benchmark (VAB), which casts aesthetic evaluation as comparative selection over candidate sets with matched subject matter. VAB contains 400 tasks and 1,195 images across fine art, photography, and illustration, with labels derived from the consensus of 10 independent expert judges per task. Evaluating 20 frontier MLLMs and six dedicated visual-quality reward models, we find that the strongest system identifies both the best and the worst image correctly across three random permutations of the candidate order in only 26.5% of tasks, far below the 68.9% achieved by human experts. Fine-tuning a 35B-parameter model on 2,000 expert examples brings its accuracy close to that of a 397B-parameter open-weight model, suggesting that the comparative signal in VAB is transferable. Together, these results expose a clear and measurable gap between current multimodal models and expert aesthetic judgment, and VAB provides the first set-based, expert-grounded testbed on which that gap can be tracked and closed.

AI.tldr: The Visual Aesthetic Benchmark (VAB) reveals a clear gap between current frontier multimodal LLMs’ aesthetic judgments and expert comparisons, showing comparative ranking is more informative than scalar scores and that the signal can transfer via fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Address whether single-score aesthetic judgments align with expert comparative judgments and provide a grounded, set-based benchmark for visual aesthetics in MLLMs.
AI.method: Introduce VAB with 400 tasks and 1,195 images across fine art, photography, and illustration. Expert labels come from consensus of 10 judges per task. Evaluate 20 frontier MLLMs and 6 visual-quality reward models; use three random permutations of candidate order. Include a fine-tuning experiment: a 35B model trained on 2,000 expert examples.
AI.result: Best system achieves correct identification of best and worst across three candidate-order permutations in 26.5% of tasks, far below 68.9% achieved by human experts. Fine-tuning a 35B model on 2,000 expert examples brings its accuracy close to a 397B open-weight model, indicating the comparative signal in VAB is transferable; overall gap between models and expert aesthetic judgment persists.
AI.conclusion: VAB provides the first set-based, expert-grounded testbed to track and close the gap between MLLMs and expert aesthetic judgment. The findings suggest comparative, set-based evaluation is more informative for aesthetics than scalar scoring, and that targeted data can transfer the comparative signal to smaller models.

title: A Resampling-Based Framework for Network Structure Learning in High-Dimensional Data
summary: RSNet is an open-source R package that provides a resampling-based framework for robust and interpretable network inference, designed to address the limited-sample-size challenges common in high-dimensional data. It supports both the estimation of partial correlation networks modeled as Gaussian networks and conditional Gaussian Bayesian networks for mixed data types that combine continuous and discrete variables. The framework incorporates multiple resampling strategies, including bootstrap, subsampling, and cluster-based approaches, to accommodate both independent and correlated observations. To enhance interpretability, RSNet integrates graphlet-based topology analysis that captures higher-order connectivity and edge sign information, enabling single-node and subnetwork-level insights. Notably, RSNet is the first R package to efficiently construct signed graphlet degree vector matrices (GDVMs) in near-constant time for sparse networks, providing scalable analysis of higher-order network structure. Collectively, RSNet offers a versatile tool for statistically reliable and interpretable network inference in high-dimensional data.

AI.tldr: RSNet is an open-source R package for robust, interpretable network inference under high-dimensional, limited-sample scenarios, using resampling and graphlet-based topology analysis, including efficient signed graphlet degree vectors for sparse networks.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional networks with limited samples pose reliability and interpretability challenges. There is a need for methods that are robust to sample size, accommodate mixed data types, and provide interpretable higher-order network structure.
AI.method: A resampling-based framework that estimates partial correlation networks (Gaussian) and conditional Gaussian Bayesian networks for mixed data. Employs bootstrap, subsampling, and cluster-based resampling to handle independent and correlated observations. Integrates graphlet-based topology analysis to capture higher-order connectivity and edge signs, enabling single-node and subnetwork insights. Introduces near-constant-time construction of signed graphlet degree vector matrices (GDVMs) for sparse networks.
AI.result: RSNet provides statistically reliable and interpretable network inference in high-dimensional data. It is the first R package to efficiently construct signed GDVMs in near-constant time for sparse networks, enabling scalable analysis of higher-order network structure.
AI.conclusion: RSNet offers a versatile tool for robust network inference with interpretability in high-dimensional settings, combining resampling-based reliability with graphlet-based topology analysis, and supporting mixed-data types.

title: When Absolute State Fails: Evaluating Proprioceptive Encodings for Robust Manipulation
comment: Accepted to ICRA 2026 Workshop: From Data to Decisions
summary: As end-to-end robotic policies are progressively deployed in the real world to solve real tasks, they face a gap between the training and inference conditions. Scaling the amount and diversity of the training data has shown some success in improving zero-shot generalization, yet robots still fail when faced with new, unseen test conditions. For instance, while robots with fixed frames of reference are common, those with moving frames pose a greater challenge for deployment. To address this specific instance of the issue, we present a study of strategies for encoding the robot's proprioceptive state to improve both in- and out-of-distribution performance at test time. Through a systematic study of joint representations, we find that a simple episode-wise relative frame provides the best trade-off between task performance and robustness, outperforming the baselines in extensive real-robot experiments conducted in a realistic test environment. The results suggest a practical path to leveraging data collected by robots with varying frames of reference and deployment to unseen test configurations.

AI.tldr: Episode-wise relative frame encoding offers the best balance between task performance and robustness for end-to-end robotic policies with moving frames, outperforming baselines in real-robot tests.
AI.motivation: Addresses the training–inference gap and the added challenge of moving frames, which degrade zero-shot generalization; seeks a proprioceptive encoding that improves performance across in- and out-of-distribution test conditions.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of joint proprioceptive representations for policies operating with moving frames; real-robot experiments in a realistic test environment; comparison against baselines; identification of an episode-wise relative frame as the best representation.
AI.result: An episode-wise relative frame representation yields superior task performance and robustness, outperforming baselines in real-robot experiments across seen and unseen test configurations.
AI.conclusion: A simple episode-wise relative-frame encoding provides a practical, scalable path for leveraging data collected under varying frames of reference to improve generalization to unseen deployment conditions.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Tutoring for Algorithm Tracing and Problem-Solving in AI Education
comment: Paper accepted to the 21st Workshop on Innovative Use of NLP for Building Educational Applications (BEA 2026), co-located with ACL 2026
summary: Students learning algorithms often need support as they interpret traces, debug reasoning errors, and apply procedures across unfamiliar problem instances. In this paper, we present KITE (Knowledge-Informed Tutoring Engine), a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based intelligent tutoring system designed to serve as a classroom teaching assistant for algorithmic reasoning and problem-solving tasks. KITE uses an intent-aware Socratic response strategy to tailor support to different student needs, responding with targeted hints, guiding questions, and progressive scaffolding intended to strengthen students' algorithmic problem-solving ability. To keep responses aligned with course content, KITE uses a multimodal RAG pipeline that retrieves relevant information from course materials. We evaluate KITE using three forms of assessment: RAGAs-based metrics for response grounding and quality, expert evaluation of pedagogical quality, and a simulated student pipeline in which a weaker language model interacts with KITE across two-turn dialogues and produces revised answers after receiving feedback. Results indicate that KITE produces contextually grounded and pedagogically appropriate responses. Further, using simulated students, KITE's feedback helped the student models produce more accurate follow-up responses on procedural and tracing questions, suggesting that its scaffolding can support algorithmic problem-solving. This work contributes a tutoring architecture and an evaluation approach for assessing retrieval-grounded explanations and scaffolded problem-solving feedback.

AI.tldr: KITE is a Retrieval-Augmented Generation-based tutoring system that uses intent-aware Socratic dialogue to support algorithmic reasoning, with multimodal retrieval from course content and evaluated via grounding metrics, pedagogical quality scoring, and simulated-student feedback.
AI.motivation: Students struggle with tracing, debugging, and applying procedures across unfamiliar problems, necessitating context-aligned, scaffolded guidance to strengthen algorithmic problem-solving.
AI.method: An RAG-based intelligent tutoring system employing an intent-aware Socratic response strategy; multimodal retrieval of course materials; targeted hints, guiding questions, and progressive scaffolding; evaluation using RAG-grounding metrics, expert pedagogical evaluation, and a simulated two-turn dialogue with feedback.
AI.result: KITE yields contextually grounded and pedagogically appropriate responses; simulated-student feedback improves follow-up accuracy on procedural and tracing questions.
AI.conclusion: Presents a tutoring architecture and evaluation framework for retrieval-grounded explanations and scaffolded problem-solving feedback in algorithm education.

title: MMCL-Bench: Multimodal Context Learning from Visual Rules, Procedures, and Evidence
summary: We introduce MMCL-Bench, a benchmark for multimodal context learning: learning task-local rules, procedures, and empirical patterns from visual or mixed-modality teaching context and applying them to new visual instances. Unlike text-only context learning or standard multimodal question answering, this setting requires models to recover and localize relevant evidence from images, screenshots, manuals, videos, and frame sequences before they can reason over the learned context. MMCL-Bench contains 102 tasks spanning three categories: rule system application, procedural task execution, and empirical discovery and induction. We evaluate frontier multimodal models with strict rubric-based scoring and find that current systems remain far from robust multimodal context learning, with even the strongest model solving fewer than one-third of tasks under strict evaluation. Diagnostic ablations and error analysis show that failures arise throughout the context-to-answer pipeline, including context anchoring, visual evidence extraction, context reasoning, and response construction. MMCL-Bench thus highlights multimodal context learning as an important unsolved capability bottleneck for current multimodal models.

AI.tldr: Introduces MMCL-Bench, a 102-task multimodal context-learning benchmark across rule application, procedural execution, and empirical discovery, showing current multimodal models struggle to robustly extract and use visual evidence to answer, highlighting a key bottleneck in context-to-answer reasoning.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between multimodal models' ability to perceive content and their capacity to learn and apply task-local rules and empirical patterns from multimodal teaching contexts, requiring localization of visual evidence and reasoning beyond standard MMQA.
AI.method: Proposes MMCL-Bench with 102 tasks spanning three categories (rule system application, procedural task execution, empirical discovery/induction). Evaluates frontier multimodal models under strict rubric-based scoring and conducts diagnostic ablations and error analyses to trace failures from context anchoring to response construction.
AI.result: Frontier models solve fewer than one-third of tasks under strict evaluation; robust multimodal context learning remains elusive. Failures occur across the pipeline: context anchoring, visual evidence extraction, context reasoning, and response construction.
AI.conclusion: MMCL-Bench identifies multimodal context learning as a critical unsolved capability bottleneck and a key target for future research to enable robust reasoning over multimodal contexts.

title: Spectral Energy Centroid: a Metric for Improving Performance and Analyzing Spectral Bias in Implicit Neural Representations
summary: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) model continuous signals using multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), enabling compact, differentiable, and high-fidelity representations of data across diverse domains. However, due to the low-frequency bias of MLPs that prevents effective learning of small details, the model's frequency must be carefully tuned through the embedding layer. Prior work established that this tuning can be performed before training based on the target signal, but it did not account for the significant effect of model depth, indicating that our understanding of the relationship between frequency and INR performance remains limited. To gain insights into this relationship, we utilize the Spectral Energy Centroid (SEC) metric that quantifies the frequency of target images and the spectral bias of INR models. We show that SEC is a versatile tool for INR analysis, demonstrating its utility across three tasks: (1) a data-driven strategy (SEC-Conf) for hyperparameter selection that outperforms existing heuristics and is robust to model depth, (2) a reliable proxy for signal complexity, and (3) effective alignment of spectral biases across diverse INR architectures.

AI.tldr: Introduces Spectral Energy Centroid (SEC) to quantify the frequency content of target signals and the INR’s spectral bias, enabling a data-driven hyperparameter strategy (SEC-Conf), a complexity proxy, and cross-architecture spectral alignment.
AI.motivation: To understand how frequency content and model depth interact in Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) and to address the low-frequency bias that hampers learning fine details, extending beyond pretraining-based tuning by accounting for depth effects.
AI.method: Define Spectral Energy Centroid (SEC) to measure target signal frequency and the model’s spectral bias. Apply SEC to three tasks: (1) SEC-Conf for depth-robust, data-driven hyperparameter selection; (2) SEC as a proxy for signal complexity; (3) alignment of spectral biases across diverse INR architectures.
AI.result: SEC proves versatile: SEC-Conf outperforms existing heuristics and is robust to model depth; SEC correlates with signal complexity; spectral biases across architectures can be aligned using SEC-based analysis.
AI.conclusion: SEC is a valuable diagnostic and design tool for INRs, enabling principled hyperparameter tuning, estimation of signal complexity, and cross-architecture spectral alignment, with broad applicability across INR architectures.

title: TouchAnything: A Dataset and Framework for Bimanual Tactile Estimation from Egocentric Video
summary: Egocentric human video data, which captures rich human-environment interactions and can be collected at scale, has become a key driver of embodied intelligence research. However, existing egocentric datasets typically lack tactile sensing, a critical modality that provides direct cues about contact, force, and pressure in human-object interaction. Without such signals, models struggle to learn physically grounded representations of real-world interaction dynamics. While tactile sensors provide these cues, deploying high-quality tactile hardware at scale remains expensive and cumbersome. This raises a central question: can tactile feedback be inferred directly from visual observations, enabling scalable tactile supervision for egocentric video data and supporting physically grounded embodied learning? To enable research in this direction, we introduce EgoTouch, a large-scale multi-view egocentric dataset with dense tactile supervision for bimanual hand-object interaction. EgoTouch comprises 208 manipulation tasks spanning 1,891 episodes in diverse indoor and outdoor environments, with synchronized multi-view RGB (head-mounted egocentric and dual wrist-mounted cameras), bimanual 3D hand pose, and continuous pressure maps from wearable tactile sensors. Building on EgoTouch, we introduce TouchAnything, a baseline multi-view vision-to-touch prediction framework that uses the egocentric view as the primary input and flexibly leverages available wrist-mounted views at inference time. Experiments show that incorporating wrist-mounted views generally improves tactile prediction over egocentric-only input, achieving up to 5.0% relative improvement in Contact IoU and 6.1% relative improvement in Volumetric IoU. We will publicly release the dataset, code, and benchmark.

AI.tldr: Introduces EgoTouch, a large-scale multi-view egocentric dataset with dense tactile supervision, and TouchAnything, a vision-to-touch baseline; results show wrist-mounted views improve tactile prediction; dataset and code will be released.
AI.motivation: Address lack of tactile sensing in egocentric data and the cost of physical tactile hardware; enable scalable tactile supervision and physically grounded embodied learning by predicting touch from vision.
AI.method: EgoTouch: 208 manipulation tasks, 1,891 episodes; synchronized head-mounted RGB, dual wrist-mounted RGB, bimanual 3D hand pose, continuous pressure maps. TouchAnything: baseline framework that uses egocentric view as primary input and optionally wrist views at inference for predicting tactile maps.
AI.result: Incorporating wrist-mounted views yields consistent improvements in tactile predictions, achieving up to 5.0% relative gains in Contact IoU and 6.1% in Volumetric IoU over egocentric-only input.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a scalable dataset and a vision-to-touch baseline, advancing embodied learning research by enabling tactile supervision from visual data. Paves the way for broader adoption and future improvements.

title: MAP: A Map-then-Act Paradigm for Long-Horizon Interactive Agent Reasoning
summary: Current interactive LLM agents rely on goal-conditioned stepwise planning, where environmental understanding is acquired reactively during execution rather than established beforehand. This temporal inversion leads to Delayed Environmental Perception: agents must infer environmental constraints through trial-and-error, resulting in an Epistemic Bottleneck that traps them in inefficient failure cycles. Inspired by human affordance perception and cognitive map theory, we propose the Map-then-Act Paradigm (MAP), a plug-and-play framework that shifts environment understanding before execution. MAP consists of three stages: (1) Global Exploration, acquiring environment-general priors; (2) Task-Specific Mapping, constructing a structured cognitive map; and (3) Knowledge-Augmented Execution, solving tasks grounded on the map. Experiments show consistent gains across benchmarks and LLMs. On ARC-AGI-3, MAP enables frontier models to surpass near-zero baseline performance in 22 of 25 game environments. We further introduce MAP-2K, a dataset of map-then-act trajectories, and show that training on it outperforms expert execution traces, suggesting that understanding environments is more fundamental than imitation.

AI.tldr: MAP alters LLM agent planning by moving environment understanding to a pre-execution phase, enabling robust task solving through a structured cognitive map.
AI.motivation: To address Delayed Environmental Perception and the Epistemic Bottleneck in reactive, goal-conditioned planning by leveraging human affordance perception and cognitive map theory.
AI.method: Three-stage MAP: (1) Global Exploration to acquire environment-general priors; (2) Task-Specific Mapping to build a structured cognitive map; (3) Knowledge-Augmented Execution leveraging the map. Also introduces MAP-2K, a dataset of map-then-act trajectories.
AI.result: MAP yields consistent performance gains across benchmarks and LLMs; on ARC-AGI-3 frontier models, it surpasses near-zero baseline performance in 22 of 25 game environments; training on MAP-2K trajectories outperforms expert traces, suggesting environment understanding is more fundamental than imitation.
AI.conclusion: Pre-execution environment understanding via cognitive maps is a broadly effective, plug-and-play paradigm that improves reliability and efficiency of LLM-based agents and can complement or supersede imitation-based approaches.

title: Inline Critic Steers Image Editing
summary: Instruction-based image editing exhibits heterogeneous difficulty not only across cases but also across regions of an image, motivating refinement approaches that allocate correction to where the model struggles. Existing refinement signals arrive late, after a fully generated image or a completed denoising step. We ask whether such a signal can act within an ongoing forward pass. To investigate this, we probe a frozen image-editing model and find that although generation capability emerges only in the last few layers, the error pattern is already set in early layers (rank correlation \r{ho} = 0.83 with the final-layer error map). Based on this, we introduce Inline Critic, a learnable token that critiques a frozen model's predictions at its intermediate layers and steers its hidden states to refine generation during the forward pass. A three-stage recipe is proposed to stabilize the training from learning how to critique to steering generation. As a result, we achieve state of the art on GEdit-Bench (7.89), a +9.4 gain on RISEBench over the same backbone, and the strongest open-source result on KRIS-Bench (81.92, surpassing GPT-4o). We further provide analyses showing that the critic genuinely shapes the model's attention and prediction updates at subsequent layers.

AI.tldr: Inline Critic introduces a learnable critique token that operates during the forward pass of a frozen image-editing model, guiding refinement from early layers to achieve SOTA on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Refinement signals in instruction-based image editing currently arrive too late (after full generation). Early layers encode error patterns that predict final failures; enabling online critique can steer generation during forward passes to improve quality and efficiency.
AI.method: Probe a frozen image-editing model and observe that generation capability emerges in the last few layers, while the error pattern is established early (rank correlation with final-layer error map ≈ 0.83).
