Introduce Inline Critic, a learnable token that critiques predictions at intermediate layers and steers hidden states to refine generation during the forward pass. Train via a three-stage recipe to stabilize moving from critiquing to steering.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on GEdit-Bench (7.89), a +9.4 gain on RISEBench over the same backbone, and the strongest open-source result on KRIS-Bench (81.92, surpassing GPT-4o). Analyses show the critic shapes model attention and prediction updates in subsequent layers.
AI.conclusion: Inline Critic demonstrates that inline critique can effectively leverage early error signals to guide forward-pass refinement, yielding substantial performance gains and interpretable effects on attention/prediction dynamics.

title: Layer-wise Representation Dynamics: An Empirical Investigation Across Embedders and Base LLMs
summary: Hidden states change substantially across the layers of modern language models, but most layer-wise analyses focus on one aspect of that change. We propose Layer-wise Representation Dynamics (LRD), a framework with three layer-wise measurement families: Frenet (Grassmann speed and curvature) for global subspace motion, Neighborhood Retention Score (NRS) for local nearest-neighbor retention, and Graph Filtration Mutual Information (GFMI) for alignment with the final layer. Applying LRD to 31 models (encoder-based and decoder-based embedders, plus base LLMs) on 30 MTEB tasks reveals architectural and task-level differences that are not apparent from final-layer representations alone. We then use LRD for two applications: label-free model selection and inference-time layer pruning. For selection, all three model-level scores correlate positively with downstream MTEB performance, with end-to-end subspace displacement (d_{0,L}) the strongest, and the same direction holds on a smaller base-LLM MMLU panel. For pruning, GFMI is the only measurement-guided rule that beats Random at the 15% and 20% budgets and has the best median change at every budget. Frenet is effective only at the lightest budget, while NRS does not transfer from model selection to pruning. These results show that layer-wise structure provides signal for both interpretation and deployment decisions.

AI.tldr: LRD introduces three layer-wise metrics (Frenet, NRS, GFMI) to analyze how representations evolve across layers in language models. It reveals architectural and task-level differences unseen by final-layer representations. GFMI best supports pruning; Frenet and NRS show limited or no transfer across selection and pruning. Overall, layer-wise dynamics correlate with downstream performance and inform model selection and pruning decisions.
AI.motivation: Address the inadequacy of single-facet layer analyses by providing a unified framework that captures global subspace motion, local neighborhood retention, and alignment with the final layer across diverse models and tasks.
AI.method: Define three measurement families—Frenet (Grassmann speed/curvature), Neighborhood Retention Score (NRS), and Graph Filtration Mutual Information (GFMI)—and apply them to 31 encoder/decoder embedder models and base LLMs across 30 MTEB tasks. Compute end-to-end subspace displacement d_{0,L}, correlate scores with downstream performance, and evaluate utility for label-free model selection and inference-time layer pruning under various budgets.
AI.result: All three model-level scores positively correlate with downstream MTEB performance; end-to-end subspace displacement d_{0,L} is the strongest predictor. GFMI outperforms Random at 15%–20% pruning budgets and has the best median change across budgets. Frenet is effective only at the lightest budget; NRS fails to transfer from selection to pruning. LRD captures architecture- and task-level differences not evident from final-layer representations.
AI.conclusion: Layer-wise representations carry actionable signals for interpretation and deployment. A unified LRD framework reveals when and how layer dynamics relate to performance and pruning efficiency, with GFMI being particularly useful for pruning decisions while Frenet and NRS provide context but have limited practical transfer across tasks/budgets.

title: Object Manipulation of the Variable Topology Truss system
summary: This paper presents an object manipulation strategy for the Variable Topology Truss (VTT) system, a truss robot that comprises actuated truss members connected by passive spherical joints. Although truss robots were originally proposed as rapidly deployable manipulators, manipulation strategy has not been studied thoroughly. To enable manipulation, we introduce a hybrid control framework that regulates position and force concurrently without explicit decoupling. At the actuator level, each member employs a sensor-based force feedback controller to generate the desired axial forces despite high actuator friction. At the task level, the forces applied at the end-effector nodes are produced by computing the required member forces using a static model of the VTT. We evaluate force-tracking performance through experiments on both a single member module and the full VTT system. Finally, we demonstrate object manipulation using two representative configurations and quantitatively assess combined position and force tracking performance. Experimental results confirm that the proposed approach enables consistent and reliable object manipulation with the VTT system.

AI.tldr: Hybrid position–force manipulation framework for a Variable Topology Truss (VTT) enabling object manipulation by integrating actuator-level force feedback with a task-level static-force model to simultaneously track position and force at the end-effector.
AI.motivation: Truss robots offer rapid deployment but manipulation strategies are underexplored; need coordinated position and force control accounting for actuator friction.
AI.method: A two-layer control architecture: at the actuator level, sensor-based force feedback controllers generate desired axial forces despite friction; at the task level, compute required member forces from a static VTT model to achieve end-effector force/position; experiments on single module and full VTT; demonstrate two configurations.
AI.result: The approach achieves consistent force-tracking and accurate position control; object manipulation demonstrated and quantitatively evaluated for combined tracking performance; results validate the feasibility of non-decoupled hybrid control for VTT manipulation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid framework enables reliable object manipulation with VTT by jointly controlling position and force through a static-model-based force allocation and actuator-level feedback, bridging a gap in manipulation strategies for truss robots.

title: An Agentic LLM-Based Framework for Population-Scale Mental Health Screening
comment: 8 pages, conference paper presented at IEEE BigData 2025, Macau
summary: Mental health disorders affect millions worldwide, and healthcare systems are increasingly overwhelmed by the volume of clinical data generated from electronic records, telemedicine platforms, and population-level screening programs. At the same time, the emergence of novel AI-based approaches in healthcare calls for intelligent frameworks capable of processing domain-specific unstructured clinical information while adapting to patient-specific needs. This paper proposes an agentic framework for building robust LLM-based pipelines, where each stage is encapsulated as a LangChain agent governed by explicit policies and proxy-guided evaluation. Stages are incrementally locked once validated, ensuring that later adaptations cannot overwrite configurations without demonstrated improvement. The proposed framework evolves from feature-level exploration, through proxy-based tuning and freeze/rollback mechanisms, to full orchestration by an Orchestrator Agent that coordinates preprocessing, retrieval, selection, diversity, threshold optimization, and decoding. A proof-of-concept in transcript-based depression detection demonstrates that the framework converges to stable configurations, such as cosine similarity, dynamic Top-k, and threshold 0.75, while controlling evaluation costs and avoiding regressions. These results highlight the potential of agentic AI to enable population-level mental health screening over large clinical datasets, addressing critical challenges in trustworthiness, reproducibility, and adaptability required in healthcare environments.

AI.tldr: Agentic framework for robust LLM pipelines with policy-based governance and orchestrated evaluation, validated on depression-transcript data; shows stable configurations and cost control, enabling scalable mental health screening.
AI.motivation: Address overwhelmed healthcare systems and unstructured clinical data by developing trustworthy, adaptable AI frameworks capable of domain-specific processing and patient-specific adaptation.
AI.method: LangChain-based pipelines where each stage is a governed agent guided by explicit policies and proxy-guided evaluation; staged locking to prevent regressions; progression from feature-level exploration to proxy-based tuning, freeze/rollback, and full orchestration by an Orchestrator Agent handling preprocessing, retrieval, selection, diversity, threshold optimization, and decoding.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept demonstrates convergence to stable configurations (e.g., cosine similarity, dynamic Top-k, threshold 0.75); controlled evaluation costs and avoidance of regressions; supports population-level mental health screening on large clinical datasets.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI frameworks can enable scalable, trustworthy, and adaptable mental health screening in healthcare settings, addressing reproducibility, trust, and efficiency challenges.

title: Is Video Anomaly Detection Misframed? Evidence from LLM-Based and Multi-Scene Models
summary: Recent video anomaly detection research has expanded rapidly with an emphasis on general models of normality intended to work across many different scenes. While this focus has led to improvements in scalability and multi-scene generalization, it has also shifted the field away from modeling the scene-specific and context-dependent nature of normal behavior. Contemporary approaches frequently rely on video-level weak supervision and opaque pretrained representations from multi-modal large language models (MLLMs), which encourage models to respond to familiar semantic anomaly categories rather than to deviations from the normal patterns of a particular environment. This trend suppresses spatial localization, introduces semantic bias, and reduces anomaly detection to a form of action recognition. In this paper, we examine whether these prevailing formulations align with the core requirements of real-world VAD, which is typically performed within a single scene where normality is determined by local geometry, semantics, and activity patterns. Through targeted visual analyses and empirical evaluations, we demonstrate the practical consequences of these limitations and show that meaningful progress in VAD requires renewed focus on single-scene, spatially-aware, and explainable formulations that capture the nuanced structure of normality within individual environments.

AI.tldr: Global, multi-scene, weakly supervised VAD methods risk semantic bias and poor localization; progress requires single-scene, spatially-aware, explainable models that capture local normality.
AI.motivation: Real-world VAD is typically performed in a single scene where normality is defined by local geometry, semantics, and activity patterns; current trends with cross-scene generalization and MLLMs may misalign with these requirements.
AI.method: Targeted visual analyses and empirical evaluations to diagnose the consequences of prevailing formulations and quantify their limitations.
AI.result: Shows practical consequences of these limitations and argues that meaningful progress in VAD demands renewed focus on single-scene, spatially-aware, and explainable formulations that capture the nuanced structure of normality within individual environments.
AI.conclusion: A shift back toward scene-specific, explainable anomaly modeling is necessary to accurately detect deviations within individual environments and avoid semantic bias and localization failures.

title: Scaling Laws for Mixture Pretraining Under Data Constraints
summary: As language models scale, the amount of data they require grows -- yet many target data sources, such as low-resource languages or specialized domains, are inherently limited in size. A common strategy is to mix this scarce but valuable target data with abundant generic data, which presents a fundamental trade-off: too little target data in the mixture underexposes the model to the target domain, while too much target data repeats the same examples excessively, yielding diminishing returns and eventual overfitting. We study this trade-off across more than 2,000 language-model training runs spanning multiple model and target dataset sizes, as well as several data types, including multilingual, domain-specific, and quality-filtered mixtures. Across all settings, we find that repetition is a central driver of target-domain performance, and that mixture training tolerates much higher repetition than single-source training: scarce target corpora can be reused 15-20 times, with the optimal number of repetitions depending on the target data size, compute budget, and model scale. Next, we introduce a repetition-aware mixture scaling law that accounts for the decreasing value of repeated target tokens and the regularizing role of generic data. Optimizing the scaling law provides a principled way to compute effective mixture configurations, yielding practical mixture recommendations for pretraining under data constraints.

AI.tldr: Repetition, not just data quantity, drives target-domain gains in mixture pretraining; a repetition-aware scaling law guides optimal mix of scarce target data with abundant generic data across models and domains.
AI.motivation: Target-domain data is scarce in many languages or domains. Mixture training with abundant generic data is needed, but the benefit trades off with repetition and overfitting. A principled approach is needed to configure mixture ratios under data constraints.
AI.method: Empirical study of >2,000 language-model training runs across multiple model sizes and target datasets (multilingual, domain-specific, quality-filtered) to quantify the role of data repetition. Development of a repetition-aware mixture scaling law that captures the diminishing value of repeated target tokens and the regularizing effect of generic data.
AI.result: Repetition is a central driver of target-domain performance. Mixture training tolerates much higher repetition than single-source training: scarce target corpora can be reused ~15–20×. The proposed scaling law enables principled computation of effective mixture configurations and provides practical mixture recommendations for pretraining under data constraints.
AI.conclusion: A repetition-aware scaling law provides a principled framework to optimize mixture configurations, enabling improved target-domain performance under limited data by leveraging repetition and generic-data regularization.

title: Identification of Non-Transversal Bifurcations of Linkages
summary: The local analysis is an established approach to the study of singularities and mobility of linkages. Key result of such analyses is a local picture of the finite motion through a configuration. This reveals the finite mobility at that point and the tangents to smooth motion curves. It does, however, not immediately allow to distinguish between motion branches that do not intersect transversally (which is a rather uncommon situation that has only recently been discussed in the literature). The mathematical framework for such a local analysis is the kinematic tangent cone. It is shown in this paper that the constructive definition of the kinematic tangent cone already involves all information necessary to separate different motion branches. A computational method is derived by amending the algorithmic framework reported in previous publications.

AI.tldr: The kinematic tangent cone is sufficient to distinguish non-transversal motion branches in the local analysis of linkages, and a computational method is introduced by refining prior algorithmic frameworks.
AI.motivation: To overcome difficulties in distinguishing multiple, non-transversal motion branches in local analyses of singularities and mobility in linkages by leveraging the kinematic tangent cone.
AI.method: Adopt the constructive definition of the kinematic tangent cone and amend the existing algorithmic framework from prior publications to derive a practical computational method.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the tangent cone contains all information necessary to separate motion branches and provides an actionable algorithm to perform this separation.
AI.conclusion: The kinematic tangent cone is a complete tool for distinguishing motion branches in local analyses, and the paper delivers a refined computational method within the established framework.

title: GRACE: Gradient-aligned Reasoning Data Curation for Efficient Post-training
summary: Existing reasoning data curation pipelines score whole samples, treating every intermediate step as equally valuable. In reality, steps within a trace contribute very unevenly, and selecting reasoning data well requires assessing them individually. We present GRACE, a gradient-aligned curation method that views each reasoning trace as a sequence of optimization events and scores every step by two complementary signals: its alignment with the answer-oriented gradient direction, and its consistency with the preceding reasoning trajectory. Step-level scores are aggregated into a sample-level value for subset selection, using only the model's internal optimization signals and no external reward models or step annotations. To make this scalable, GRACE introduces a representation-level gradient proxy that estimates step-level alignment from token-level upstream signals in a single forward pass. Post-training Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct on MMathCoT-1M, GRACE reaches 108.8% of the full-data performance with 20% of the data and retains 100.2% with only 5%, with subsets that transfer effectively across model backbones.

AI.tldr: GRACE is a step-level, gradient-aligned data curation method for reasoning traces. It scores each reasoning step by gradient alignment and trajectory consistency, aggregates to sample-level scores for subset selection using only model internals, and uses a representation-level gradient proxy for scalability. It enables data-efficient, transferable subset selection that matches or exceeds full-data performance with much less data.
AI.motivation: Existing curation pipelines score whole samples, ignoring uneven contributions of individual reasoning steps. There is a need to leverage internal optimization signals to evaluate and select informative reasoning steps and data subsets without external rewards or annotations.
AI.method: Treat each reasoning trace as a sequence of optimization events. Compute per-step scores from two signals: (1) alignment with the answer-oriented gradient direction and (2) consistency with the preceding reasoning trajectory. Aggregate step scores to obtain a sample-level value for subset selection, relying solely on the model's internal optimization signals. To scale, introduce a representation-level gradient proxy that estimates step-level alignment from token-level upstream signals in a single forward pass.
AI.result: GRACE, applied to post-training on Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct using MMathCoT-1M, achieves 108.8% of full-data performance with 20% of the data and 100.2% with only 5%, with subsets that transfer effectively across model backbones.
AI.conclusion: GRACE demonstrates data-efficient, scalable reasoning data curation using internal optimization signals, without external reward models or step annotations, and transfers across backbones, indicating strong potential for improving data efficiency in multi-step reasoning tasks.

title: Just Ask for a Table: A Thirty-Token User Prompt Defeats Sponsored Recommendations in Twelve LLMs
comment: Submitted to Workshop on Textual Information Processing & Synthesis in the Wild
summary: Wu et al. (2026) showed that most frontier large language models (LLMs) recommend a sponsored, roughly twice-as-expensive flight when their system prompt contains a soft sponsorship cue. We reproduce their evaluation on ten open-weight chat models plus the two of their twenty-three models that are still reachable today (gpt-3.5-turbo, gpt-4o). All reported rates in this paper are produced under the same judge the original paper used (gpt-4o); we additionally store every label under an open-weight (gpt-oss-120b) and a smaller proprietary (gpt-4o-mini) judge for an ablation. Three findings emerge. First, a prose description of an LLM evaluation pipeline is not, on its own, sufficient for accurate reproduction: we surfaced three silent implementation failures that each shifted a reported rate by tens of percentage points. Second, the central claims do generalise - the gpt-3.5-turbo logistic-regression intercept of alpha = 0.81 is within four points of the original alpha = 0.86, and 200 of 200 trials on gpt-3.5-turbo and gpt-4o promote a payday lender to a financially distressed user. Third, a thirty-token user prompt that asks the assistant for a neutral comparison table first cuts sponsored recommendation from 46.9% to 1.0% averaged across our ten open-source models, and from 53.0% to 0% averaged across the two OpenAI models. AI literacy and price-comparison portals are likely market-level mitigations; the harmful-product cell is bounded by neither. Raw data, labels and analysis scripts are at https://github.com/akmaier/Paper-LLM-Ads .

AI.tldr: Reproduces Wu et al.'s finding that many frontier LLMs tolerate sponsorship bias in flight recommendations; analyzes reproducibility, generalization, and mitigation via a neutral prompt; finds implementation pitfalls, generalizable effects across models, and strong mitigation from a 30-token neutral prompt; data and code released for open verification.
AI.motivation: Assess reproducibility and robustness of LLM evaluation pipelines, test generalizability of sponsorship bias across models and judges, and examine possible mitigation strategies.
AI.method: Replicate Wu et al.'s evaluation on ten open-weight chat models plus two accessible models; use the original judge (gpt-4o) and additional judges (gpt-oss-120b, gpt-4o-mini) for ablations; report sponsorship rates, fit logistic regression (alpha/intercept) comparisons, and test a 30-token neutral prompt to measure mitigation; provide raw data, labels, and scripts publicly.
AI.result: Three findings: (1) mere prose of an evaluation pipeline is insufficient for reproducibility; three silent implementation failures shifted rates by tens of points; (2) central claims generalize: alpha ≈0.81 (vs 0.86 original) and 200/200 trials promote payday lenders to distressed users across gpt-3.5-turbo and gpt-4o; (3) a 30-token neutral prompt reduces sponsorship rates dramatically (46.9%→1.0% across ten open-source models; 53.0%→0% across two OpenAI models). Mitigations via AI literacy and price portals are discussed; the harmful-product cell remains unconstrained; data and code at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation pipelines for LLM sponsorship bias are fragile and require precise implementation; bias effects appear robust across models, but can be substantially mitigated by targeted prompt design; open data enables reproduction and further study; standardized evaluation protocols are warranted.

title: Before the Last Token: Diagnosing Final-Token Safety Probe Failures
summary: Final-token safety probes monitor a single hidden state after prompt prefill, but jailbreak prompts can contain probe-visible unsafe evidence distributed across earlier user-token representations that is missed by this readout. We study this prefill-time failure mode using SafeSwitch-style probes trained only on clean harmful and benign prompts across three instruction-tuned LLMs. The probes achieve high recall on clean harmful prompts, but miss many jailbreaks and can produce false positives on safety-adjacent benign prompts. Subspace analyses suggest that missed jailbreaks differ from clean benign prompts along directions that are poorly captured by the probe's representational subspace, and increasing probe bottleneck width does not reliably resolve this mismatch. Token-level prefill analyses reveal that probe-visible unsafe evidence often appears earlier in the sequence but is not exposed at the final-token readout, while naive max-pooling over token positions overfires on safe prompts. A simple PCA-HMM trajectory model, trained only on the same clean split, recovers many final-token misses from user-content prefill trajectories without the catastrophic false-positive behavior of naive token pooling, motivating trajectory-aware hidden-state analyses as diagnostic complements to final-token probes

AI.tldr: Prefill-time safety probes can miss jailbreak evidence distributed across early prompt tokens; a trajectory-aware PCA-HMM approach recovers many final-token misses and avoids false positives, suggesting trajectory analyses are essential complements to final-token probes.
AI.motivation: Evaluate limitations of final-token readout safety probes trained on clean prompts and understand how unsafe evidence may be distributed in prefill representations, enabling jailbreak prompts to bypass detection.
AI.method: Train SafeSwitch-style probes on clean prompts across three instruction-tuned LLMs; perform subspace analyses of representations; conduct token-level prefill analyses and evaluate naive token pooling; develop a PCA-HMM trajectory model trained on the same clean split to diagnose prefill misses.
AI.result: Probes achieve high recall on clean harmful prompts but miss many jailbreak prompts and can yield false positives on safety-adjacent benign prompts. Subspace directions not well captured by the probe explain misses; increasing bottleneck width does not reliably fix it. Unsafe evidence often appears earlier but is not exposed at final-token readout; naive max-pooling over token positions overfires on safe prompts. The PCA-HMM trajectory model recovers many final-token misses from prefill trajectories without the false positives of naive pooling.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-aware hidden-state analyses (e.g., PCA-HMM) complement final-token probes and can diagnose and recover prefill misses, motivating more robust, trajectory-based safety evaluation.

title: What to Ignore, What to React: Visually Robust RL Fine-Tuning of VLA Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning has shown promise for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in robotic manipulation, but deployment-time visual shifts pose practical challenges. A key difficulty is that standard task rewards supervise task success, but offer limited guidance on whether a visual change is task-irrelevant or changes the behavior required for manipulation. We propose PAIR-VLA (Paired Action Invariance & Sensitivity for Visually Robust VLA), an RL fine-tuning framework to address this difficulty by adding two auxiliary objectives over paired visual variants during PPO optimization: an invariance term that reduces the discrepancy between action distributions for a task-preserving pair (e.g., different distractors), and a sensitivity objective that encourages separable action distributions for a task-altering pair (e.g., target object in a different pose). Together, these objectives turn visual variants from mere observation diversity into behavior-level guidance on policy responses during RL fine-tuning. We evaluate on ManiSkill3 across two representative VLA architectures, OpenVLA and $π_{0.5}$, under diverse out-of-distribution visual shifts including unseen distractors, texture changes, target object pose variation, viewpoint shifts, and lighting changes. Our method consistently improves over standard PPO, achieving average improvements of 16.62% on $π_{0.5}$ and 9.10% on OpenVLA. Notably, ablations further show generalization across visual shifts: invariance guidance learned from distractor and texture variants transfers to target-pose and lighting shifts, while adding sensitivity guidance on target-pose variants further improves robustness to nuisance shifts, highlighting the broader transferability of behavior-level RL guidance.

AI.tldr: PAIR-VLA introduces paired visual variant objectives (invariance and sensitivity) to guide RL fine-tuning for VLA models under visual shifts. It uses PPO with two auxiliary losses on paired variants to encourage task-preserving invariance and task-altering sensitivity, improving robustness across shifts and transferring across variants and architectures.
AI.motivation: Visual shifts at deployment create ambiguity in whether a visual change should not affect behavior or require different manipulation. Task rewards alone are insufficient guidance for such behavior-level changes during RL fine-tuning.
AI.method: Train VLA models with PPO while optimizing two auxiliary objectives over paired visual variants: an invariance term aligning action distributions for task-preserving pairs (e.g., different distractors) and a sensitivity term encouraging separable action distributions for task-altering pairs (e.g., target object in different pose). Evaluate on ManiSkill3 with two architectures (OpenVLA and π0.5) under diverse out-of-distribution shifts (distractors, textures, pose, viewpoint, lighting).
AI.result: Consistent improvements over standard PPO. Average gains: 16.62% on π0.5 and 9.10% on OpenVLA. Ablations show invariance generalizes across shifts, transfer of invariance learned from distractor/texture to pose/lighting, and adding sensitivity on pose variants improves robustness to nuisance shifts, indicating broad transferability of behavior-level RL guidance.
AI.conclusion: PAIR-VLA provides behavior-level guidance during RL fine-tuning by leveraging paired visual variants through invariance and sensitivity objectives, yielding improved robustness to visual shifts and cross-variant transfer across architectures.

title: A Constraint Programming Approach for $n$-Day Lookahead Playoff Clinching
comment: 18 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Accepted to CP 2026
summary: In professional sports, a team has clinched the playoffs if they are guaranteed a postseason spot, regardless of the outcomes of any remaining games. As the season progresses, sports fans and other stakeholders are interested in precisely when, and under what conditions, their team will clinch the playoffs. In this paper, we investigate playoff clinching in the context of the National Hockey League (NHL), where it is computationally challenging to produce clinching scenarios due, in part, to complex tie-breakers. We present an algorithm that determines under which combinations of game outcomes in the next $n$ days a team will clinch the playoffs (i.e., "$n$-day lookahead clinching"). Our approach is a custom tree search which employs various preprocessing techniques, pruning strategies, and node ordering heuristics to efficiently explore the space of possible outcomes. The tree search leverages a constraint programming (CP)-based subroutine for inference that determines if a team has clinched the playoffs for some snapshot in time of the regular season (i.e., "0-day lookahead clinching"). This CP subroutine aims to find a counter-example in which the team being evaluated is eliminated, taking into account qualification rules and the NHL's extensive list of tie-breakers. We validate the efficacy of our algorithm using hundreds of scenarios based on public NHL data for the seasons 2021-22 through 2024-25. The methods introduced can be readily extended to other metrics of interest, including mathematical proof of playoff elimination, clinching the President's Trophy, as well as clinching (or being eliminated from clinching) any other seed in the standings.

AI.tldr: Presents an algorithm for n-day lookahead playoff clinching in the NHL using a custom tree search with a CP-based 0-day clinching subroutine; validated on hundreds of scenarios from 2021–2025 seasons; adaptable to other seeds and metrics.
AI.motivation: NHL playoff clinching is computationally hard due to intricate tie-breakers and the need for precise, timely results for fans and stakeholders; a scalable method is valuable for exploring scenarios and providing guarantees.
AI.method: A custom tree search that combines preprocessing, pruning, and node-ordering heuristics to explore possible future game outcomes. A constraint programming (CP) based subroutine checks 0-day clinching by seeking counter-examples (elimination) under NHL qualification rules and tie-breakers. The approach iterates across the next n days (n-day lookahead) to determine clinching conditions.
AI.result: Validated on hundreds of scenarios drawn from public NHL data for seasons 2021-22 through 2024-25, showing the method efficiently navigates the space of possible outcomes and handles tie-breakers; the framework is readily extensible to additional metrics.
AI.conclusion: The algorithm provides a practical, extendable framework for playoff clinching analysis. It can be adapted to mathematical proofs of elimination, other seed clinching, and other leagues or metrics, suggesting broad applicability beyond the NHL.

title: WildPose: A Unified Framework for Robust Pose Estimation in the Wild
summary: Estimating camera pose in dynamic environments is a critical challenge, as most visual SLAM and SfM methods assume static scenes. While recent dynamic-aware methods exist, they are often not unified: semantic-based approaches are brittle, per-sequence optimization methods fail on short sequences, and other learned models may degrade on static-only scenes. We present WildPose, a unified monocular pose estimation framework that is robust in dynamic environments while maintaining state-of-the-art performance on static and low-ego-motion datasets. Our key insight is to connect two powerful paradigms in modern 3D vision: the rich perceptual frontend of feedforward models and the end-to-end optimization of differentiable bundle adjustment (BA). We achieve this with a 3D-aware update operator built on a frozen, pre-trained MASt3R feature backbone, together with a high-capacity motion mask detector that uses multi-level 3D-aware features from the same backbone. Extensive experiments show WildPose consistently outperforms prior methods across dynamic (Wild-SLAM, Bonn), static (TUM, 7-Scenes), and low-ego-motion (Sintel) benchmarks.

AI.tldr: WildPose introduces a unified monocular pose estimation framework that remains robust in dynamic environments while preserving strong performance on static and low-ego-motion data by fusing a frozen MASt3R-based 3D-aware perceptual front-end with differentiable bundle adjustment and a motion mask detector.
AI.motivation: Dynamic scenes violate static-scene assumptions of most SLAM/SfM systems, and existing dynamic-aware methods are fragmented (semantic brittle, short-sequence optimization, or learned models that fail on static scenes). A unified approach is needed to handle both dynamic and static scenes efficiently.
AI.method: A 3D-aware update operator built atop a frozen, pre-trained MASt3R feature backbone, complemented by a high-capacity motion mask detector that leverages multi-level 3D-aware features from the same backbone; integrates with differentiable bundle adjustment to enable end-to-end optimization while maintaining a strong perceptual front-end.
AI.result: Experimental results show WildPose consistently outperforms prior methods across dynamic (Wild-SLAM, Bonn), static (TUM, 7-Scenes), and low-ego-motion (Sintel) benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: WildPose provides a unified, robust monocular pose estimation framework that bridges the perceptual frontend and differentiable BA, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both dynamic and static/low-motion regimes.

title: From Generalist to Specialist Representation
summary: Given a generalist model, learning a task-relevant specialist representation is fundamental for downstream applications. Identifiability, the asymptotic guarantee of recovering the ground-truth representation, is critical because it sets the ultimate limit of any model, even with infinite data and computation. We study this problem in a completely nonparametric setting, without relying on interventions, parametric forms, or structural constraints. We first prove that the structure between time steps and tasks is identifiable in a fully unsupervised manner, even when sequences lack strict temporal dependence and may exhibit disconnections, and task assignments can follow arbitrarily complex and interleaving structures. We then prove that, within each time step, the task-relevant latent representation can be disentangled from the irrelevant part under a simple sparsity regularization, without any additional information or parametric constraints. Together, these results establish a hierarchical foundation: task structure is identifiable across time steps, and task-relevant latent representations are identifiable within each step. To our knowledge, each result provides a first general nonparametric identifiability guarantee, and together they mark a step toward provably moving from generalist to specialist models.

AI.tldr: Nonparametric identifiability guarantees for task structure across time steps and disentanglement of task-relevant latent representations within each time step, established via sparsity regularization in an unsupervised setting; constitutes a hierarchical foundation for from generalist to specialist models.
AI.motivation: Identifiability guarantees are crucial to ensure recovery of true representations in generalist-to-specialist learning. The work aims to provide first general nonparametric identifiability results in a fully unsupervised setting without interventions, parametric forms, or structural constraints.
AI.method: Theoretical nonparametric proofs showing (i) identifiability of the/task structure across time steps in fully unsupervised sequences, even with weak temporal dependence, disconnections, and arbitrarily complex interleaving task assignments, and (ii) identifiability of task-relevant latent representations within each time step via a simple sparsity regularization, without extra priors or parametric constraints.
AI.result: Twofold identifiability: (a) the mapping of time-step structures to tasks is identifiable under unsupervised conditions with weak dependencies; (b) within each time step, the task-relevant latent factors are identifiable through sparsity regularization.
AI.conclusion: This work provides, to our knowledge, the first general nonparametric identifiability guarantees and lays a hierarchical foundation for moving from generalist models to specialist representations.

title: SECOND-Grasp: Semantic Contact-guided Dexterous Grasping
summary: Achieving reliable robotic manipulation, such as dexterous grasping, requires a synergy between physically stable interactions and semantic task guidance, yet these objectives are often treated as separate, disjoint goals. In this paper, we investigate how to integrate dexterous grasping techniques, i.e., physically stable grasps for object lifting and language-guided grasp generation, to achieve both physical stability and semantic understanding. To this end, we propose SECOND-Grasp (SEmantic CONtact-guided Dexterous Grasping), a unified framework that enables robotic hands to dynamically adjust grasping strategies based on semantic reasoning while ensuring physical feasibility. We begin by obtaining coarse contact proposals through vision-language reasoning to infer where contacts should occur based on object properties, followed by segmentation to localize these regions across views. To further ensure consistency across multiple viewpoints, we introduce Semantic-Geometric Consistency Refinement (SGCR), which refines initial contact predictions by enforcing semantic consistency across views and removing geometrically invalid regions, yielding reliable 3D contact maps. Then, we derive a feasible hand pose for each contact map via inverse kinematics, generating a supervision signal for policy learning. Our approach, trained on DexGraspNet, consistently outperforms baselines in lifting success rate on both seen and unseen categories, achieving 98.2% and 97.7%, respectively, while also improving intent-aware grasping by 12.8% and 26.2%. We further show promising results on additional datasets and robotic hands, including Shadow Hand and Allegro Hand.

AI.tldr: Proposes SECOND-Grasp, a unified framework that merges semantic contact planning with dexterous grasping to achieve physically feasible and semantically guided manipulation. Introduces SGCR to enforce semantic consistency and geometric validity across views, producing reliable 3D contact maps and IK-based supervision for policy learning. Demonstrates strong lifting and intent-aware grasping performance on DexGraspNet, with generalization to unseen categories and additional hands/datasets.
AI.motivation: Physical dexterous grasping and semantic task guidance are often treated separately. There is a need to integrate stable contact generation with language-based reasoning to enable reliable, semantically informed manipulation.
AI.method: 1) Vision-language reasoning to generate coarse contact proposals based on object properties. 2) Segmentation to localize contact regions across views. 3) Semantic-Geometric Consistency Refinement (SGCR) to enforce semantic consistency and remove geometrically invalid regions, yielding 3D contact maps. 4) Inverse kinematics to derive feasible hand poses for each contact map. 5) Use these poses as supervision for policy learning. 6) Train on DexGraspNet; test on seen/unseen categories and other datasets/hands (Shadow Hand, Allegro Hand).
AI.result: Achieves lifting success rates of 98.2% (seen) and 97.7% (unseen). Intent-aware grasping improves by 12.8% and 26.2%. Demonstrates generalization to additional datasets and hands.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework combining semantic reasoning with physically feasible dexterous grasping can yield high-performance manipulation with reliable contact maps and improved generalization. SGCR enhances cross-view consistency, and IK-based supervision supports effective policy learning across multiple robotic hands.

title: FRAME: Forensic Routing and Adaptive Multi-path Evidence Fusion for Image Manipulation Detection
summary: The proliferation of sophisticated image editing tools and generative artificial intelligence models has made verifying the authenticity of digital images increasingly challenging, with important implications for journalism, forensic analysis, and public trust. Although numerous forensic algorithms, ranging from handcrafted methods to deep learning-based detectors, have been developed for manipulation detection, individual methods often suffer from limited robustness, fragmented evidence, or weak generalization across manipulation types and image conditions. To address these limitations, we present \textbf{FRAME}, a method for \textbf{F}orensic \textbf{R}outing and \textbf{A}daptive \textbf{M}ulti-path \textbf{E}vidence fusion for image manipulation detection. FRAME organizes diverse forensic algorithms into a multi-path analysis space, adaptively selects informative forensic paths for each input image, and fuses complementary evidence to improve detection and localization performance. By moving beyond single-method analysis and fixed fusion strategies, FRAME provides a more robust and flexible approach to image forensic reasoning while preserving interpretable forensic cues from multiple evidence sources. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of FRAME across diverse manipulation scenarios. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/kzhao5/FRAME}{https://github.com/kzhao5/FRAME}.

AI.tldr: FRAME is a multi-path, adaptive evidence fusion framework for image manipulation detection that leverages diverse forensic cues and routing to improve robustness and localization, outperforming single-method baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing forensic detectors struggle with robustness, limited generalization across manipulation types, and fragmented evidence; a framework that combines multiple forensic cues with adaptive routing can provide stronger, more interpretable detection and localization.
AI.method: FRAME builds a multi-path analysis space by organizing diverse forensic algorithms; it adaptively selects informative forensic paths per input image and fuses their evidence to detect and localize manipulations while preserving interpretable cues.
AI.result: Experimental results show FRAME improves detection and localization across diverse manipulation scenarios; code available for reproduction.
AI.conclusion: FRAME offers a robust, flexible approach to image forensic reasoning by combining multiple evidence sources and adaptive routing, addressing limitations of single-method detectors and fixed fusion strategies.

title: ConRetroBert: EMA Stabilized Dual Encoders for Template-Based Single-Step Retrosynthesis
summary: Template based single step retrosynthesis predicts reactants by selecting and applying an explicit reaction template, making each prediction traceable to a chemical transformation rule. This is useful for synthesis planning, but template based methods are often viewed as less competitive than template free models because template prediction is commonly formulated as global classification over a long tailed rule library. We argue that this weakness is not inherent to templates, but to the learning formulation. We present ConRetroBert, a dual encoder framework that reframes template based retrosynthesis as dense product template retrieval followed by candidate set listwise ranking. Stage 1 uses contrastive pretraining to learn a shared embedding space between products and reaction templates. Stage 2 refines template ranking over mined hard negative candidate sets with a multi positive listwise objective. To enable template side adaptation without destabilizing hard negative mining, ConRetroBert uses a slow moving exponential moving average template encoder for retrieval bank construction while updating the live template encoder through the ranking loss. On the local USPTO-50k benchmark, Stage 2 candidate set ranking improves top-1 reaction accuracy from 50.5% to 61.3%, while EMA stabilized template adaptation further improves it to 62.4%. Fine tuning from a leakage controlled USPTO-Full checkpoint reaches 75.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k. We also show that retrieval based template prediction is strong in the long tail of rare templates, and that many correct reactant predictions arise from alternative explicit templates rather than only the recorded positive label. Code and data are available at https://github.com/JahidBasher/ConRetroBert.

AI.tldr: Reframes template-based retrosynthesis as dense product-template retrieval followed by listwise ranking, using dual encoders with contrastive pretraining and multi-positive ranking; achieves substantial gains on USPTO-50k, including long-tail templates, and stabilizes learning with EMA; code released.
AI.motivation: Template-based retrosynthesis is often viewed as less competitive due to the global classification over a long-tailed template library. The authors argue this is due to the learning formulation, not the template approach itself, and propose a retrieval-based framing to leverage dense representations and ranking.
AI.method: Dual-encoder framework (ConRetroBert): Stage 1 contrastive pretraining to learn a shared embedding space for products and reaction templates. Stage 2 candidate set ranking with a multi-positive listwise objective over mined hard negatives. To enable adaptation without destabilizing negative mining, use a slow-moving exponential moving average (EMA) template encoder for the retrieval bank while updating the live template encoder via the ranking loss. Evaluation on USPTO-50k shows Stage 2 ranking improves top-1 accuracy from 50.5% to 61.3%, EMA raises to 62.4%, and fine-tuning from a leakage-controlled USPTO-Full checkpoint yields 75.4% top-1 on USPTO-50k. They also observe strong performance in the long tail and that many correct reactants come from alternative explicit templates rather than the recorded positives. Code and data available at provided URL.
AI.result: Stage 2 ranking substantially boosts top-1 accuracy (50.5%→61.3%; with EMA→62.4%), and leakage-controlled fine-tuning reaches 75.4% top-1 on USPTO-50k. The retrieval-based approach shows strength in long-tail templates and reveals multiple valid template paths beyond the recorded positive labels.
AI.conclusion: Reframing template-based retrosynthesis as dense retrieval plus ranking is competitive with template-free methods and benefits from better handling of long-tail templates. The architecture enables template adaptation without destabilizing hard negative mining, and the approach is supported by code/data release.

title: Towards Long-horizon Embodied Agents with Tool-Aligned Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models are effective robot action executors, but they remain limited on long-horizon tasks due to the dual burden of extended closed-loop planning and diverse physical operations. We therefore propose VLAs-as-Tools, a strategy that distributes this burden across a high-level vision language model (VLM) agent for temporal reasoning and a family of specialized VLA tools for diverse local physical operations. The VLM handles scene analysis, global planning, and recovery, while each VLA tool executes a bounded subtask. To tightly couple agent planning with VLA tool execution in long-horizon tasks, we introduce a VLA tool-family interface that exposes explicit tool selection and in-execution progress feedback, enabling efficient event-triggered agent replanning without continuous agent polling. To obtain diverse specialized VLA tools that faithfully follow agent invocations, we further propose Tool-Aligned Post-Training (TAPT), which constructs invocation-aligned training units for instruction following and adopts tool-family residual adapters for efficient tool specialization. Experiments show that VLAs-as-Tools improves the success rate of $π_{0.5}$ by 4.8 points on LIBERO-Long and 23.1 points on RoboTwin, and further enhances invocation fidelity by 15.0 points as measured by Non-biased Rate. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: VLAs-as-Tools couples a high-level VLM agent for planning with a family of specialized VLA tools for local tasks; introduces a tool-family interface with explicit selection and progress feedback and a TAPT training framework; yields notable improvements in long-horizon tasks and tool invocation fidelity.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robotic tasks suffer from extended planning and diverse low-level operations; need modular toolization and tighter planning-tool coupling to enable robust, event-driven replanning.
AI.method: Architectural split: VLM-based planner + VLA tool family; interface that exposes tool selection and in-execution progress; TAPT to create invocation-aligned training units and tool-family residual adapters for specialization.
AI.result: Empirical gains: +4.8 points in pi_0.5 on LIBERO-Long, +23.1 points on RoboTwin; +15.0 points in Non-biased Rate for invocation fidelity; code release planned.
AI.conclusion: VLAs-as-Tools improves long-horizon planning and tool invocation fidelity, validating the utility of explicit tool-selection interfaces and TAPT-based specialization; potential for broader tool-family adoption.

title: Formal Conjectures: An Open and Evolving Benchmark for Verified Discovery in Mathematics
summary: As automated reasoning systems advance rapidly, there is a growing need for research-level formal mathematical problems to accurately evaluate their capabilities. To address this, we present Formal Conjectures, an evolving benchmark of currently 2615 mathematical problem statements formalized in Lean 4. Sourced from areas of active mathematical research, the dataset features 1029 open research conjectures providing a zero-contamination benchmark for mathematical proof discovery, and 836 solved problems for proof autoformalization. Notably, the repository provides a structured interface connecting mathematicians who formalize and clarify problems with the AI systems and humans attempting to solve them. Demonstrating its immediate utility, the benchmark has already been leveraged to make new mathematical discoveries, including the resolution of open research conjectures. We describe our approach to ensuring the correctness of these formalizations in a collaborative open-source project where contributions stem from an active community. In this framework, AI-generated proofs and disproofs serve as a valuable auditing mechanism to iteratively improve the fidelity of the benchmark. Finally, we provide a standardized evaluation setup and report baseline results on frozen evaluation subsets, demonstrating a climbable signal that measures the current frontier of automated reasoning on research-level mathematics.

AI.tldr: Benchmark of 2615 Lean 4 formalized math problems, with 1029 open conjectures and 836 solved problems, enabling evaluation of AI on research-level mathematics and integrating humans, AI, and auditing in an open-source workflow.
AI.motivation: Need for rigorous, research-level evaluation benchmarks to gauge automated reasoning capabilities in mathematics; address zero-contamination evaluation (open conjectures) and enable credible proof discovery and formalization workflows.
AI.method: Curate and formalize problem statements in Lean 4 to create an evolving dataset (2615 total; 1029 open conjectures; 836 solved). Provide a structured interface for collaboration among mathematicians, AI systems, and human solvers; use AI-generated proofs/disproofs as auditing signals; establish a standardized, frozen evaluation setup with baseline results.
AI.result: The benchmark has demonstrated immediate utility by enabling new mathematical discoveries, including solving open conjectures; it has a collaborative audit mechanism to improve fidelity; baseline results on frozen subsets show a measurable frontier for automated reasoning in research-level mathematics.
AI.conclusion: Formal Conjectures offers a scalable, collaborative, and auditable platform for evaluating AI in advanced mathematics, bridging formalization efforts with real research problems and providing a repeatable evaluation suite with baselines.

title: AssemblyBench: Physics-Aware Assembly of Complex Industrial Objects
summary: Assembling objects from parts requires understanding multimodal instructions, linking them to 3D components, and predicting physically plausible 6-DoF motions for each assembly step. Existing datasets focus on simplified scenarios, overlooking shape complexities and assembly trajectories in industrial assemblies. We introduce AssemblyBench, a synthetic dataset of 2,789 industrial objects with multimodal instruction manuals, corresponding 3D part models, and part assembly trajectories. We also propose a transformer-based model, AssemblyDyno, which uses the instructional manual and the 3D shape of each part to jointly predict assembly order and part assembly trajectories. AssemblyDyno outperforms prior works in both assembly pose estimation and trajectory feasibility, where the latter is evaluated by our physics-based simulations.

AI.tldr: A synthetic dataset (AssemblyBench) of 2,789 industrial objects with multimodal manuals, 3D part models, and assembly trajectories, paired with a transformer model (AssemblyDyno) that predicts assembly order and 6-DoF trajectories, achieving superior assembly pose estimation and trajectory feasibility through physics-based evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets underrepresent shape complexity and realistic assembly trajectories; there is a need to connect multimodal instructions to precise 3D geometries and physically plausible motions for industrial assembly tasks.
AI.method: Construct AssemblyBench comprising 2,789 industrial objects with multimodal instruction manuals, corresponding 3D part models, and assembly trajectories. Develop AssemblyDyno, a transformer-based model that jointly encodes the instructional manual and part geometry to predict assembly order and per-part 6-DoF assembly trajectories, with trajectory feasibility assessed via physics-based simulations.
AI.result: AssemblyDyno outperforms prior methods in assembly pose estimation and exhibits higher trajectory feasibility in physics-based simulations.
AI.conclusion: The combination of AssemblyBench and AssemblyDyno advances multimodal grounding for industrial assembly, enabling more accurate planning of assembly sequences and physically plausible motions, with potential applications in robotics and automation.

title: Learning with Rare Success but Rich Feedback via Reflection-Enhanced Self-Distillation
summary: Enabling Large Language Models (LLMs) to continuously improve from environmental interactions is a central challenge in post-training. While on-policy self-distillation offers a promising paradigm, existing methods predominantly treat environmental feedback as a passive conditioning signal. Consequently, they heavily rely on successful demonstrations and struggle to learn in rare-success regimes. To bridge this gap, we introduce Reflection-Enhanced Self-Distillation (RESD), a framework that transforms raw failure feedback into an active source of corrective supervision. Instead of passively appending feedback, RESD interprets failed trajectories by generating retrospective reflections to diagnose local errors, and curates a persistent global playbook to preserve reusable lessons across training steps. The enriched context enables the self-teacher to provide actionable token-level supervision even in the absence of successful rollouts. Empirical evaluations on multiple continual learning tasks demonstrate that RESD substantially outperforms standard self-distillation baselines. Furthermore, RESD achieves significantly faster early-stage improvement than GRPO with $8\times$ samples using only a single rollout per prompt, highlighting its superior interaction efficiency.

AI.tldr: Reflection-Enhanced Self-Distillation (RESD) converts failure feedback into corrective supervision, building a persistent global playbook to guide LLM learning, enabling token-level guidance even without successful rollouts and improving early-stage performance.
AI.motivation: On-policy self-distillation relies on successful demonstrations and passive feedback, hindering learning in rare-success regimes. There is a need to leverage failure signals to extract actionable knowledge and preserve reusable lessons across steps.
AI.method: Interpret failed trajectories by generating retrospective reflections that diagnose local errors. Curate a global, reusable playbook of lessons from past runs. Use reflections to provide actionable token-level supervision to the self-teacher, enabling learning even without successful rollouts.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple continual learning tasks show RESD substantially outperforms standard self-distillation baselines and achieves faster early-stage improvement than GRPO with 8× samples using a single rollout per prompt, demonstrating improved interaction efficiency.
AI.conclusion: RES D provides a practical framework to turn failure feedback into active, reusable supervision for continual improvement of LLMs, enhancing both effectiveness and interaction efficiency.

title: Multi-Depth Uniform Coverage Path Planning for Unmanned Surface Vehicle Surveying
summary: This paper introduces a novel automatic coverage path planning algorithm for bathymetry surveying with unmanned surface vehicles. The detection range of the mapping sensor employed - a multibeam echo sounder - is heavily influenced by local seafloor depths. Hence, a path designed to uniformly cover the sea surface does not guarantee uniform coverage of the seafloor. Yet this is currently the typical process for bathymetric surveys, with the simplistic boustrophedon scheme along manually selected waypoints at constant depths being the most widespread planner used. The proposed scheme incorporates coarse prior depth information to pre-process the target region and adaptively guide path generation and sensing range configuration. By explicitly accounting for depth variations, the proposed algorithm designs a coverage path with optimised spacing between survey passes that adjusts the sensing beam aperture to achieve more consistent seafloor coverage. The proposed method is shown to offer significant improvements in both synthetic and real-world scenarios. Validations in challenging synthetic terrains achieves coverage ratios beyond 99%, a marked improvement when compared with traditional boustrophedon paths revealing a maximum 75% coverage. The same trend appears in realistic simulations using real bathymetric data from a coastal harbour, with coverage reaching over 92%, and significantly surpassing boustrophedon sweeps with coverage rates below 65%. Beyond improved performance, the scheme also brings a fully automated design, suitable for autonomous marine vehicles, thus offering practical utilities for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: A depth-aware adaptive coverage path planning method for bathymetric surveys with USVs, improving seafloor coverage by tuning survey pass spacing and sensor beam aperture based on prior depth data; outperforms traditional boustrophedon strategies in both synthetic and real data, enabling automated mission design.
AI.motivation: Uniform surface coverage does not guarantee uniform seafloor coverage due to depth-dependent sonar footprint; requires an automated planner that accounts for depth variations to achieve consistent seabed coverage.
AI.method: Pre-process the target region with coarse depth information to guide adaptive path generation and sensor configuration. The planner adjusts pass spacing and sensing range (beam aperture) according to depth, producing a coverage path optimized for seafloor coverage. Evaluation is performed on synthetic terrains and real harbor bathymetry, comparing against boustrophedon paths and demonstrating automated design for autonomous marine vehicles.
AI.result: Synthetic-case coverage exceeds 99% (vs ~75% for boustrophedon). Real harbor data shows coverage >92% (vs <65% for boustrophedon). The approach yields substantial improvements and supports fully automated deployment.
AI.conclusion: Depth-aware, adaptive coverage planning substantially enhances seafloor coverage and enables automated mission design for unmanned surface vehicles in bathymetric surveying.

title: Hierarchical Attacks for Multi-Modal Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: Multi-modal multi-agent systems (MM-MAS) have gained increasing attention for their capacity to enable complex reasoning and coordination across diverse modalities. As these systems continue to expand in scale and functionality, investigating their potential vulnerabilities has become increasingly important. However, existing studies on adversarial attacks in multi-agent systems primarily focus on isolated agents or unimodal settings, leaving the vulnerabilities of MM-MAS largely underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce HAM$^{3}$, a Hierarchical Attack framework for multi-modal multi-agent systems that decomposes attacks into three interconnected layers. Specifically, at the perception layer, HAM$^{3}$ mounts attacks by perturbing visual inputs, textual inputs, and their fused visual-textual representations. At the communication layer, it performs communication-level attacks that corrupt message content and interaction topology, such as manipulating shared context or communication links to distort collective information flow. At the reasoning layer, it conducts reasoning-level attacks that interfere with each agent's cognitive pipeline, biasing reasoning trajectories and ultimately compromising final decisions. We evaluate HAM$^{3}$ on the GQA benchmark through multi-agent systems built on distinct reasoning paradigms including ReAct, Plan-and-Solve, and Reflexion. Experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves an Attack Success Rate of up to 78.3%, with reasoning-layer attacks being the most effective. More than half of the successful attacks lead multiple agents to produce consistent errors. These findings offer valuable insights for building more robust and interpretable multi-agent intelligence.

AI.tldr: HAM^3 is a hierarchical attack framework for multi-modal multi-agent systems that targets perception, communication, and reasoning layers; it achieves up to 78.3% attack success on GQA across ReAct, Plan-and-Solve, and Reflexion, with reasoning-layer attacks being most effective and many attacks causing consistent cross-agent errors.
AI.motivation: Vulnerabilities of MM-MAS under adversarial conditions are underexplored, especially across modalities and communications. Existing work mainly studies isolated agents or unimodal settings, leaving a gap in holistic vulnerability assessment for MM-MAS.
AI.method: HAM^3 decomposes attacks into three interconnected layers: perception attacks perturb visual inputs, textual inputs, and their fused representations; communication attacks corrupt message content and interaction topology (e.g., shared context, communication links); reasoning attacks interfere with each agent's cognitive pipeline to bias reasoning trajectories. Evaluation is conducted on GQA with multiple reasoning paradigms (ReAct, Plan-and-Solve, Reflexion).
AI.result: Attack success rate reaches up to 78.3%, with reasoning-layer attacks being the most effective. More than half of successful attacks cause multiple agents to produce consistent errors.
AI.conclusion: The framework exposes vulnerabilities in MM-MAS and underscores the need for robust and interpretable designs, guiding future defense strategies against multi-layer adversarial perturbations.

title: Low-Rank Adapters Initialization via Gradient Surgery for Continual Learning
summary: LoRA is widely adopted for continual fine-tuning of Large Language Models due to its parameter efficiency, modularity across tasks, and compatibility with replay strategies. However, LoRA-based continual learning remains vulnerable to catastrophic forgetting, whose severity depends on how successive task gradients interact: when consecutive task gradients conflict, standard adapter initializations channel updates into subspaces that overwrite previously learned directions. We propose SLICE, a gradient-surgery-based initialization for LoRA adapters in continual learning. SLICE accumulates gradients from both the current task and a replay buffer of prior tasks, reconciles them through a projection operator, and decomposes the result via truncated SVD to initialize the adapter weights. We evaluate SLICE on the TRACE benchmark and sequences of Super-NI tasks, including a set of adversarial Super-NI sequences that we construct by mining task pairs with maximally opposing gradients. Compared to vanilla LoRA, LoRA-GA, and LoRAM, SLICE consistently achieves a better stability-plasticity trade-off, improving Average Performance, Final Performance and Forgetting metrics while preserving General Performance and In Context Performance across both standard and adversarial continual learning sequences.

AI.tldr: Introduces SLICE, a gradient-surgery-based initialization for LoRA adapters in continual learning to mitigate forgetting; uses gradient accumulation from current task and replay, projection to reconcile, and truncated SVD to initialize adapters; outperforms baselines on TRACE and Super-NI tasks, including adversarial sequences, in stability-plasticity metrics.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in LoRA-based continual learning arises when consecutive task gradients conflict, steering updates into subspaces that erase prior knowledge; a principled initialization that aligns new adapters with accumulated gradients can preserve past learning while remaining plastic for new tasks.
AI.method: Aggregate gradients from the current task and a replay buffer of past tasks; apply a projection operator to reconcile conflicts; decompose the reconciled gradient via truncated SVD to obtain an initialization for LoRA adapter weights.
AI.result: SLICE achieves a better stability-plasticity trade-off compared to vanilla LoRA, LoRA-GA, and LoRAM; improves Average Performance, Final Performance, and Forgetting; preserves General Performance and In-Context Performance across standard and adversarial continual learning sequences on TRACE and Super-NI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-surgery-based initialization for LoRA adapters effectively reduces catastrophic forgetting in continual learning by aligning updated directions with accumulated experience; future work could explore different projection strategies, scalability to larger models, and broader benchmarks.

title: MoCCA: A Movable Circle Probability of Collision Approximation
summary: In automated driving, crash mitigation is crucial to ensure passenger safety. Accurate avoidance requires precise knowledge of the object's position and orientation. However, sensor noise and occlusions often result in tracking and prediction uncertainties. To account for these uncertainties, estimating the Probability of Collision (POC) is a critical requirement. While Monte Carlo sampling is a common estimation technique, its high computational demand and stochastic nature often render it unsuitable for real-time applications. Analytical POC calculations are simplified by approximating vehicle geometries using circular bounds. While multi-circle approximations offer higher fidelity than a single circumscribed circle, they significantly increase computational complexity. This paper proposes a shape approximation algorithm, MoCCA, which utilizes a single circle for each vehicle, optimized to minimize the relative distance between them. MoCCA maintains a computational efficiency comparable to standard single-circle techniques while reducing over-conservatism. To address the potential underestimation of POC inherent in partial coverage, we establish an upper bound for the approximation error, demonstrating that it depends primarily on inter-vehicle distance and orientation variance. Furthermore, we introduce a safety distance margin that can be calibrated solely based on orientation variance.

AI.tldr: MoCCA uses a single circle per vehicle, optimized to minimize inter-vehicle distance, to enable fast, bounded POC estimation with an orientation-variance-based safety margin.
AI.motivation: Need real-time probability of collision under uncertainty; Monte Carlo estimation is too slow for real-time driving; multi-circle approximations improve fidelity but add complexity.
AI.method: Replace complex geometries with a single circle per vehicle, optimized to minimize the relative distance between vehicle circles; derive an upper bound on POC approximation error depending on inter-vehicle distance and orientation variance; introduce a safety distance margin calibrated from orientation variance.
AI.result: Computational efficiency comparable to standard single-circle methods while reducing over-conservatism; provides an analytical bound on approximation error and a tunable safety margin based on orientation variance.
AI.conclusion: MoCCA offers a real-time, analytically bounded POC estimation approach using a single-circle vehicle model with orientation-variance-based safety margins, balancing efficiency and accuracy.

title: An Agentic AI Framework with Large Language Models and Chain-of-Thought for UAV-Assisted Logistics Scheduling with Mobile Edge Computing
summary: In cloud manufacturing, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can support both product collection and mobile edge computing (MEC). This joint operation forms a hybrid scheduling problem, where physical logistics decisions are coupled with computational task scheduling. In this paper, UAVs collect finished products from manufacturing stations and transport them back to a central depot. Meanwhile, computational tasks generated by industrial sensor devices at these stations are processed locally, at UAVs, or offloaded via UAVs to the cloud. This coupling makes the problem challenging. A UAV can provide MEC services only during its service window at a station, so routing decisions directly determine when UAV-assisted offloading is available. Routing decisions also affect the UAV energy budget and the availability of onboard computing and communication resources for computational task execution under task deadline constraints. To address this, we propose an agentic-AI-assisted optimization framework with two components. First, we develop an agentic AI that combines large language models, retrieval-augmented generation, and chain-of-thought reasoning to translate user input into an interpretable mathematical formulation for the hybrid scheduling problem. Second, we design a hierarchical deep reinforcement learning approach based on proximal policy optimization (PPO), where the upper layer learns UAV routing and the lower layer optimizes per-slot task execution and resource allocation. Simulation results show that the proposed framework yields more consistent formulations, while the hierarchical PPO achieves full product collection in 99.6% of the last 500 episodes and maintains a 100% deadline satisfaction rate, with more stable performance than the advantage actor-critic approach.

AI.tldr: Joint UAV routing and MEC offloading in cloud manufacturing using agentic AI for problem formulation and a hierarchical PPO; achieves high product collection and deadline satisfaction.
AI.motivation: Physical logistics (UAV routing) is tightly coupled with computational task scheduling in cloud manufacturing; decisions affect energy, onboard resources, and deadline feasibility, requiring integrated optimization.
AI.method: Two-component framework: (1) agentic AI combining LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and chain-of-thought to translate user input into an interpretable mathematical formulation; (2) hierarchical DRL using PPO with an upper-layer for UAV routing and a lower-layer for per-slot task execution and resource allocation optimization.
AI.result: Simulation shows more consistent problem formulations and that the hierarchical PPO achieves 99.6% full product collection in the last 500 episodes and 100% deadline satisfaction, with more stable performance than A2C.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables consistent mathematical formulations and effective hierarchical control for integrated UAV-enabled logistics and MEC in cloud manufacturing; the hierarchical PPO outperforms baseline methods in product collection and deadline adherence.

title: Prediction of Rectal Cancer Regrowth from Longitudinal Endoscopy
summary: Clinical trial studies indicate benefit of watch-and-wait (WW) surveillance for patients with rectal cancer showing a complete or near clinical response (CR) directly after treatment (restaging). However, there are no objectively accurate methods to early detect local tumor regrowth (LR) in patients undergoing WW from follow-up exams. Hence, we developed Temporal Rectal Endoscopy Cross-attention (TREX), a longitudinal deep learning approach that combines pairs of images acquired at restaging and follow-up to distinguish CR from LR. TREX uses pretrained Swin Transformers in a siamese setting to extract features from longitudinal images and dual cross-attention to combine the features without spatial co-registration between image pairs. TREX and Swin-based baselines were trained under two settings: (a) detecting LR or CR at the last available follow-up and (b) early detection of LR at 3--6, 6--12, and 12--24 months before clinical confirmation. TREX achieved the highest accuracy in detecting LR with a high sensitivity of 97% $\pm$ 6% and a balanced accuracy of 90% $\pm$ 3%, and outperformed all baselines in early detection at both 3--6 (74% $\pm$ 1%) and 6--12 months (62% $\pm$ 4%) prior to clinical detection. Clinical validation via a surgeon survey showed that TREX matched attending-level overall accuracy (TREX: 86.21% vs.\ Clinicians: 87.84% $\pm$ 1.28%). Finally, we explored TREX's ability to predict treatment response by combining pre-treatment (pre-TNT) and restaging endoscopies, achieving a balanced accuracy of 73% $\pm$ 12%. These results show that longitudinal deep learning analysis of endoscopy may improve surveillance and enable earlier identification of rectal cancer regrowth.

AI.tldr: Longitudinal deep learning (TREX) uses siamese Swin Transformers with cross-attention on restaging and follow-up endoscopies to distinguish complete/near-complete response from local regrowth in rectal cancer during watch-and-wait; shows high sensitivity and strong early-detection performance, with validation against clinicians and potential pre-treatment prediction.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of objective, early detection methods for local tumor regrowth in rectal cancer patients under watch-and-wait by exploiting longitudinal endoscopy data.
AI.method: TREX: Temporal Rectal Endoscopy Cross-attention. A longitudinal deep learning model using pretrained Swin Transformers in a siamese setup to extract features from restaging and follow-up endoscopies, with dual cross-attention to fuse features without the need for spatial co-registration. Trained under two tasks/settings: (a) detect LR or CR at last follow-up; (b) early detection of LR at 3–6, 6–12, 12–24 months before clinical confirmation. Compared to Swin-based baselines.
AI.result: TREX achieved high LR detection sensitivity ~97% (±6%) and balanced accuracy ~90% (±3%). In early detection, 3–6 months before clinical detection: ~74% accuracy (±1%); 6–12 months: ~62% (±4%). Clinical validation via surgeon survey: TREX 86.21% vs clinicians 87.84% (±1.28%). Pre-treatment + restaging prediction achieved ~73% balanced accuracy (±12%).
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal endoscopy with cross-attention DL can improve surveillance by enabling earlier identification of rectal cancer regrowth and achieving parity with clinician performance on overall accuracy; further external validation and prospective studies are needed to confirm generalizability and clinical impact.

title: Constraint-Aware Flow Matching: Decision Aligned End-to-End Training for Constrained Sampling
summary: Deep generative models provide state-of-the-art performance across a wide array of applications, with recent studies showing increasing applicability for science and engineering. Despite a growing corpus of literature focused on the integration of physics-based constraints into the generation process, existing approaches fail to enforce strict constraint satisfaction while maintaining sample quality. In particular, training-free constrained sampling methods, while providing per-sample feasibility guarantees, introduce a fundamental mismatch between the training objective and the constrained sampling procedure, often leading to performance degradation.
