Identifying this training-sampling misalignment as a central limitation of current constrained generative modeling approaches, this paper proposes Constraint-Aware Flow Matching, a novel end-to-end framework that explicitly incorporates constraint projections into the training objective. By aligning the model's learned dynamics with the constrained sampling process, the proposed method mitigates distributional shift induced by projection-based corrections, enabling high-quality constrained generation. The proposed approach is evaluated on three challenging real-world benchmarks, illustrating the generality and efficacy of the method.

AI.tldr: Introduces Constraint-Aware Flow Matching, an end-to-end method that integrates constraint projections into training to align learning dynamics with constrained sampling, mitigating distributional shift and improving constrained generation; validated on three real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current constrained generative methods rely on training-free projection that guarantees per-sample feasibility but creates a mismatch between the training objective and the constrained sampling process, leading to degraded sample quality. A training-aware approach is needed to enforce strict constraints without sacrificing generation quality.
AI.method: Proposes Constraint-Aware Flow Matching, an end-to-end framework that explicitly incorporates constraint projections into the training objective, aligning the model's learned dynamics with the constrained sampling process and reducing distributional shift from projection-based corrections.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three real-world benchmarks demonstrates generality and efficacy, showing improved constrained generation quality under physics-informed constraints.
AI.conclusion: Training–sampling misalignment is a central limitation of current constrained generative models; incorporating constraint projections into training via flow matching yields high-quality constrained generation across benchmarks.

title: Dynamics Computation of Soft-Rigid Hybrid-Link System and Its Application to Motion Analysis of an Athlete Wearing Sport Prosthesis
summary: This paper presents a motion analysis framework for an athlete wearing sport-specific flexible prosthesis based on the soft-rigid hybrid-link system. Such a motion analysis is a challenging problem because we need to consider the interaction force between the rigid human skeleton system and a flexible prosthesis. However, most of human musculoskeletal models are based on the computation framework of a rigid-body multi-link system. Recently in soft robotics research field, fast and efficient modeling methods were developed for a flexible rod deformation, which allows us to build a hybrid-link system that integrates rigid-link and soft-bodies in a unified formulation. We apply inverse kinematics of the hybrid-link system to motion reconstruction from a motion captured data, and also present the estimation of the joint torques and ground reaction force by inverse dynamics. Through a human subject experiment, we show that the inverse dynamics achieved approximately 12% error on the ground reaction force estimation. Furthermore, we provide the muscle force estimation considering muscle amputation and interaction force with the prosthesis leg deformation.

AI.tldr: Hybrid rigid-soft prosthesis modeling with inverse kinematics/dynamics for athlete motion; ~12% GRF error; muscle force estimation accounting for amputation.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate motion analysis and torque/ground-reaction force estimation for athletes using sport-specific flexible prostheses by bridging rigid-body musculoskeletal models with soft-rod deformation modeling.
AI.method: Develop a hybrid-link framework combining rigid links and soft-body deformation; apply inverse kinematics for motion reconstruction from motion capture; use inverse dynamics to estimate joint torques and ground-reaction forces; estimate muscle forces considering amputation and prosthesis interaction; validation with human subject showing ~12% GRF error.
AI.result: Inverse dynamics estimated GRF with about 12% error; muscle-force estimation considering prosthesis interaction; demonstration via human subject experiment.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid-link approach enables integrated analysis of interaction forces and muscle activity for amputee athletes with flexible prostheses; promising accuracy; potential extensions include improved soft-robotic modeling and more comprehensive validation.

title: Improving Code Translation with Syntax-Guided and Semantic-aware Preference Optimization
comment: Accepted in the 35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2016)
summary: LLMs have shown immense potential for code translation, yet they often struggle to ensure both syntactic correctness and semantic consistency. While preference-based learning offers a promising alignment strategy, it is hindered by unreliable semantic rewards derived from sparse test cases or restrictive reference translations. We argue that a robust semantic reward for code translation must be derived directly from the source code. In this paper, we propose CTO to improve code translation with syntax-guided and semantic-aware preference optimization. Through contrastive learning, we train a cross-lingual semantic model to directly assess functional equivalence between source and translated code. By formulating code translation as a multi-objective optimization problem, this robust semantic signal is seamlessly unified with compiler-based syntactic feedback within the direct preference optimization framework. Extensive experiments on C++, Java, and Python translations demonstrate that CTO significantly outperforms existing baselines and alternative preference optimization strategies.

AI.tldr: CTO presents a syntax-guided, semantic-aware approach to cross-language code translation that learns a cross-lingual semantic model to assess functional equivalence and combines this semantic signal with compiler-based syntax feedback in a multi-objective direct preference optimization framework; experiments on C++, Java, Python show improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs for code translation often struggle to satisfy both syntax and semantics; semantic rewards from sparse test cases or single reference translations are unreliable. The authors argue for a robust semantic signal derived directly from the source code.
AI.method: Introduce CTO; use contrastive learning to train a cross-lingual semantic model to evaluate functional equivalence between source and translated code; formulate translation as a multi-objective optimization integrating semantic feedback and syntactic feedback via direct preference optimization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across C++, Java, Python translations show CTO significantly outperforms existing baselines and alternative preference optimization strategies.
AI.conclusion: Robust source-derived semantic reward improves code translation; combining semantic and syntactic feedback in a multi-objective direct preference optimization framework is effective; results suggest potential for broader applicability.

title: Adaptive Conformal Prediction for Reliable and Explainable Medical Image Classification
summary:
Deep learning models for medical imaging often exhibit overconfidence, creating safety risks in ambiguous diagnostic scenarios. While Conformal Prediction (CP) provides distribution-free statistical guarantees, standard methods such as Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets (RAPS) optimize for average efficiency and can mask severe failures on difficult inputs.
  We propose an Adaptive Lambda Criterion for RAPS that minimizes the worst-case coverage violation across prediction set size strata. On OrganAMNIST (58,850 abdominal CT images, 11 classes), standard size-optimized RAPS converges to near-deterministic behavior with stratified undercoverage on uncertain samples, while our method achieves 95.72 percent global coverage with average set size 1.09 and at least 90 percent coverage across all strata. Cross-domain validation on PathMNIST (107,180 pathology images, 9 classes) confirms generalizability.
  Quantitative Grad-CAM analysis (rho = -0.30, p < 1e-22) shows that multi-label predictions correspond to focused attention on anatomically ambiguous regions. These results demonstrate that the proposed method improves reliability while maintaining efficiency, making it suitable for safety-critical medical AI applications.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Lambda Criterion for RAPS (ALCR) reduces worst-case coverage violations in conformal prediction for medical imaging, improving reliability while preserving efficiency. On OrganAMNIST, ALCR achieves 95.72% global coverage with mean set size 1.09 and ≥90% stratified coverage, outperforming standard RAPS which trends toward near-deterministic behavior with stratified undercoverage. Cross-domain validation on PathMNIST confirms generalizability. Quantitative Grad-CAM (rho = -0.30, p < 1e-22) indicates multi-label predictions focus on anatomically ambiguous regions.
AI.motivation: Conformal Prediction provides distribution-free guarantees, but Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets optimize for average efficiency and can mask severe failures on difficult inputs. There is a need to minimize worst-case coverage violations across prediction set-size strata to ensure safety in uncertain medical imaging scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce an Adaptive Lambda Criterion for RAPS that minimizes the worst-case coverage violation across prediction-set-size strata. Evaluate on OrganAMNIST (58,850 images, 11 classes) and PathMNIST (107,180 images, 9 classes). Compare against standard size-optimized RAPS. Use cross-domain validation and a Grad-CAM interpretability analysis.
AI.result: On OrganAMNIST, the proposed method achieves 95.72% global coverage with an average set size of 1.09 and at least 90% coverage across all strata, whereas standard RAPS converges to near-deterministic behavior with stratified undercoverage. Cross-domain validation on PathMNIST confirms generalizability. Quantitative Grad-CAM analysis (rho = -0.30, p < 1e-22) shows multi-label predictions align with focused attention on anatomically ambiguous regions.
AI.conclusion: The Adaptive Lambda Criterion for RAPS improves reliability of CP-based predictions in medical imaging without sacrificing efficiency, making it suitable for safety-critical AI applications.

title: Predicting Channel Closures in the Lightning Network with Machine Learning
summary: The Lightning Network (LN) is a second-layer protocol for Bitcoin designed to enable fast and cost-efficient off-chain transactions. Channels in the LN can be closed either by mutual agreement or unilaterally through a forced closure, which locks the involved capital for an extended period and degrades network reliability. In this paper, we study the problem of predicting channel closure types from publicly available gossip data, framing it as a temporal link classification task over the evolving channel graph. We construct a dataset spanning over two years of LN activity and benchmark a range of machine learning approaches, from MLPs to temporal graph neural networks and spectral encodings. Our experiments reveal that the dominant predictive signals are temporal and behavioural, namely how recently each endpoint was active and the per-node history of past closures, while the surrounding network topology provides no additional benefit. We find that a simple MLP operating on edge-level features, node-level event counts, and temporal patterns outperforms all graph-based approaches, and discuss how the inherent privacy of the LN, where critical information such as channel balances and payment flows remains hidden, fundamentally limits the predictability of closures from gossip data alone. We publicly release the dataset and code at https://github.com/AmbossTech/ln-channel-closure-prediction to encourage further research on this practically relevant task.

AI.tldr: Temporal and behavioral signals dominate the prediction of LN channel closure types; simple edge- and node-level features with an MLP outperform graph-based methods, suggesting limited value of gossip topology due to inherent LN privacy.
AI.motivation: Understand whether publicly observable gossip data can predict channel closure types in the Lightning Network and assess the value of network structure vs. temporal behavior in such predictions.
AI.method: Construct a two-year LN activity dataset and evaluate a spectrum of models from MLPs to temporal graph neural networks and spectral encodings on the task of temporal link classification for channel closures.
AI.result: Temporal signals (recent activity) and per-node history of past closures are the strongest predictors; network topology adds little to no predictive power. A simple MLP using edge features, node event counts, and temporal patterns outperforms graph-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Gossip-derived topology is insufficient for reliable closure-type prediction in LN due to privacy constraints; the LN’s hidden balances and payments reduce signal leakage. The work releases the dataset and code for further research.

title: Calibration-Free Gas Source Localization with Mobile Robots: Source Term Estimation Based on Concentration Measurement Ranking
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), 2026
summary: Efficient Gas Source Localization (GSL) in real-world settings is crucial, especially in emergency scenarios. Mobile robots equipped with low-cost, in-situ gas sensors offer a safer alternative to human inspection in hazardous environments. Probabilistic algorithms enhance GSL efficiency with scattered gas measurements by comparing gas concentration measurements gathered by robots to physical dispersion models. However, accurately deriving gas concentrations from data acquired with low-cost sensors is challenging due to the nonlinear sensor response, environmental dependencies (e.g., humidity, temperature, and other gas influences), and robot motion. Mitigating these disturbance factors requires frequent sensor calibration in controlled environments, which is often impractical for real-world deployments. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel feature extraction algorithm that leverages the relative ranking of gas measurements within the dynamically accumulated dataset. By comparing the rank differences between gathered and modeled values, we estimate the probabilistic distribution of source locations across the entire environment. We validate our approach in high-fidelity simulations and physical experiments, demonstrating consistent localization accuracy with uncalibrated gas sensors. Compared to existing methods, our technique eliminates the need for gas sensor calibration, making it well-suited for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: A rank-based feature extraction method for gas source localization using relative measurement rankings to infer source distributions without calibrating gas sensors; validated in simulations and real experiments, showing robust localization with uncalibrated sensors.
AI.motivation: Real-world gas source localization with low-cost sensors is challenged by sensor nonlinearity, environmental factors, and the impracticality of frequent calibration. A calibration-free approach could enable safer, real-world deployment.
AI.method: Introduce a feature extraction algorithm that uses the relative ranking of gas measurements within a dynamically accumulated dataset. It compares rank differences between collected measurements and dispersion-model predictions to infer a probabilistic distribution of source locations across the environment.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent localization accuracy using uncalibrated gas sensors in both high-fidelity simulations and physical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The ranking-based feature approach reduces or eliminates the need for gas sensor calibration, enhancing robustness and practicality of GSL in real-world deployments.

title: It's not the Language Model, it's the Tool: Deterministic Mediation for Scientific Workflows
comment: 18 pages, 4 figures, 2 appendices. Submitted to SETN 2026
summary: Language models can produce convincing scientific analyses, but repeated generations on the same data do not guarantee the same result. A researcher may regenerate an identical query and receive a different fit, a different peak position or a different analysis procedure, without an obvious way to decide which output to trust. We propose typed mediation, a pattern in which the model orchestrates deterministic tools rather than generating analytical code. Each tool encodes one researcher's exact procedure for one instrument, ported through structured interviews. The model selects which tool to call and with what parameters. The tool produces the result. Regeneration does not change it. We evaluate this claim by running the same photoluminescence analysis on four platforms, including three commercial foundation models, four times each with the same prompt. The typed tool produces identical results across all runs. The commercial platforms either vary in numerical output and analytical methodology across runs, or fail to produce valid results on the task. We deploy this pattern on two instruments serving users over approximately six months, with very positive user feedback. Both cases are very challenging: they involve proprietary binary formats and per-seat licensed software, which force the tool to remain on local infrastructure alongside the data and the instrument it operates. We argue that deployment topology is not just a preference, but a structural requirement of scientific tool mediation. The result is a practical pattern for deploying language models in scientific workflows where reproducibility is mandatory, reducing analysis time from weeks to minutes while guaranteeing identical outputs across runs.

AI.tldr: Typed mediation: an LM-driven pattern that uses deterministic, instrument-specific tools to ensure reproducible scientific analyses. Tested on photoluminescence tasks across four platforms; results are identical across runs with the typed tool, while commercial LMs show variability or fail. Deployed on two instruments over six months on local hardware; reduces analysis time and enforces reproducibility as a structural guideline.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility concerns arise when LMs regenerate analyses that differ across runs. The work proposes a deterministic, auditable workflow that maps a researcher’s procedure into concrete tools, mitigating variability and enabling trust in scientific outputs.
AI.method: Introduce the typed mediation pattern: the LM selects and calls deterministic tools that encode one instrument’s exact procedure, ported via structured interviews. Tools produce results; regeneration yields identical outputs. Evaluation via a photoluminescence analysis on four platforms (three commercial LMs) four times each with the same prompt.
AI.result: The typed tool produced identical results across all runs; commercial platforms showed variability in numerical output or methodology or failed to produce valid results. The pattern was deployed on two instruments for about six months with very positive user feedback, despite constraints of proprietary binary formats and per-seat licensed software, requiring local infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: Typed mediation offers a practical, reproducible pattern for deploying LMs in scientific workflows where reproducibility is mandatory. It can drastically reduce analysis time and guarantees identical outputs across runs. Deployment topology (local, instrument-connected) is a structural requirement, not a preference.

title: GuardMarkGS: Unified Ownership Tracing and Edit Deterrence for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is becoming a practical representation for novel view synthesis, but its growing adoption, together with rapid advances in instruction-driven 3DGS editing, also exposes a dual copyright risk: once a 3DGS-based asset is released, it can be used without permission and manipulated through 3D editing. Existing protection methods address only one side of this problem. Watermarking can trace ownership after unauthorized use, but it cannot prevent malicious editing. Adversarial edit-deterrence methods can disrupt editing, but they do not provide evidence of ownership. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first unified protection framework for 3DGS that jointly optimizes ownership tracing and unauthorized editing deterrence. Our framework combines a scene-wide watermarking objective over all Gaussians with an adversarial objective for edit deterrence. The adversarial branch combines latent-anchor separation, denoising-trajectory diversion, and cross-attention diversion to divert the editing trajectory, while an update-saliency-motivated Gaussian selection strategy assigns stronger adversarial updates to mask-selected Gaussians, improving the balance among watermark recovery, edit deterrence, and rendering fidelity. Experiments on scenes from Mip-NeRF 360 and Instruct-NeRF2NeRF demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a favorable balance among bit accuracy, edit deterrence, and rendering quality. These results suggest that practical copyright protection of 3DGS-based assets can be more effectively addressed by integrating ownership tracing and unauthorized editing deterrence into a single optimization framework.

AI.tldr: Jointly protects 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) assets by embedding a scene-wide watermark for ownership and an adversarial edit-deterrence objective to hinder unauthorized edits, optimized in a single framework.
AI.motivation: 3DGS is increasingly used for novel view synthesis, creating dual copyright risks: post-release misuse and malicious editing. Existing methods cover only one side, leaving assets vulnerable to either unauthorized use or edits.
AI.method: A unified optimization framework combining a scene-wide watermarking objective over all Gaussians with an adversarial objective for edit deterrence. The adversarial branch uses latent-anchor separation, denoising-trajectory diversion, and cross-attention diversion. An update-saliency-motivated Gaussian selection strategy concentrates updates on masked Gaussians to balance watermark recoverability, edit deterrence, and rendering fidelity.
AI.result: On scenes from Mip-NeRF 360 and Instruct-NeRF2NeRF, the method achieves a favorable balance among bit accuracy (watermark recoverability), edit deterrence, and rendering quality.
AI.conclusion: Integrating ownership tracing and unauthorized editing deterrence into a single optimization is effective for practical copyright protection of 3DGS-based assets.

title: Multi-Quantile Regression for Extreme Precipitation Downscaling
summary: Deep super-resolution networks for precipitation downscaling achieve strong bulk skill yet systematically under-predict the heavy-tail events that drive flood risk. We demonstrate that the primary obstacle is the loss function, not the data: under intensity-weighted MAE, real and synthetic labels at the same input are simply averaged, meaning data augmentation shifts the predicted mean rather than the conditional distribution. We resolve this with Q-SRDRN, a multi-quantile super-resolution network trained with pinball loss at tau in 0.50, 0.95, 0.99, 0.999. Two CNN-specific design choices make this practical: IncrementBound enforces monotonicity while preserving each quantile channel's gradient identity, and separate per-quantile output heads provide independent filter banks for bulk and tail detection. Under this design, data augmentation via cVAE becomes complementary: the median head absorbs synthetic patterns without contaminating upper quantiles. Empirically, on Florida (convective/tropical-cyclone dominated), the un-augmented Q-SRDRN P999 head detects 1,598 of 2,111 events at 200 mm/day versus 88 for the deterministic baseline--an 18x detection-rate gain (4.2% to 75.7%)--with 63% lower KL divergence and 3.9% lower RMSE. Adding cVAE-generated samples lifts the P50 channel from 14 to 1,038 hits at 200 mm/day. On California (atmospheric-river dominated), the architecture reaches near-perfect detection (P999 SEDI >= 0.996 through 300 mm/day). On Texas, the baseline catches only 2 of 10,720 events at 200 mm/day while the P999 head catches 8,776 (81.9%). While the cVAE does not transfer across regions, multi-quantile regression captures extremes wherever the large-scale signal is strong, while augmentation rescues the median where it is not.

AI.tldr: A multi-quantile SR network (Q-SRDRN) using pinball loss at multiple taus to capture tail distributions in precipitation downscaling; architectural design enforces monotonicity and dedicated per-quantile heads; cVAE augmentation complements tail predictions; results show substantial gains in heavy-tail event detection across Florida, California, Texas; cVAE improves median but does not transfer regionally.
AI.motivation: Address systematic under-prediction of heavy-tail rainfall events in single-quantile SR models due to loss functions that optimize conditional mean; seek to recover full conditional distribution via multi-quantile regression and data augmentation.
AI.method: Propose Q-SRDRN with pinball loss at taus 0.50, 0.95, 0.99, 0.999; IncrementBound monotonicity constraint preserving gradient properties; separate per-quantile output heads; train with cVAE-based augmentation; evaluate on Florida, California, Texas datasets with 200 mm/day threshold, measure P999 hits, KL divergence, RMSE; assess transferability.
AI.result: Substantial improvements in tail event detection: Florida P999 1598/2111 vs 88 baseline (18x); KL reduced by ~63%; RMSE reduced by ~3.9%; cVAE raises P50 from 14 to 1038; California P999 SEDI ≥ 0.996 up to 300 mm/day; Texas P999 detections 8776/10720 (81.9%); cVAE does not transfer across regions; overall, multi-quantile approach captures extremes when large-scale signal strong and augmentation rescues median.
AI.conclusion: Multi-quantile SR with tailored architecture and loss function significantly improves tail-sensitive precipitation downscaling, with complementary augmentation. Regional transfer of augmentation is limited; the framework effectively models conditional distribution and prioritizes extreme events.

title: Galilean State Estimation for Inertial Navigation Systems with Unknown Time Delay
summary: Many Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) use Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) position as the primary measurement to drive filter performance and bound error growth. However, commercial-grade GNSS receivers introduce unknown measurement delays ranging from 50 ms to 300 ms depending on sensor quality and operating mode. Such time delays can significantly degrade INS performance unless they are explicitly compensated for. Existing algorithms commonly estimate this delay offline, run the filter concurrently with GNSS measurements using buffered Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) data, and predict the current state by forward-integrating buffered inertial measurements via IMU preintegration. The state-of-the-art online method is an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) that explicitly models the time delay as a state parameter, which defines the preintegration duration. This paper introduces a novel geometric framework for modeling time-delayed INS, in which Galilean symmetry is leveraged to provide a joint representation of space and time for consistent state estimation. An Equivariant Filter (EqF) is derived for the coupled estimation of navigation states and time delay. Validation is performed on two fixed-wing Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAV) with GNSS time lags of 90 ms and 120 ms. The test flights last two to three minutes. Simulations further investigate delays up to 500 ms and provide a statistical comparison against the state-of-the-art EKF. Results show that the EqF preserves accuracy and consistency, while the EKF lacks consistency and its performance degrades significantly with increasing measurement delays.

AI.tldr: Introduces an Equivariant Filter (EqF) for time-delayed INS that jointly estimates navigation state and GNSS time delay using a Galilean-invariant geometric framework; shows improved consistency and robustness to delays vs EKF, validated on two UAVs and in simulations up to 500 ms.
AI.motivation: GNSS measurement delays (50–300 ms) degrade INS performance. Offline buffering/preintegration is common, while existing online EKF approaches model delay as a state. A geometry-based, invariant approach aims to preserve consistency under delay.
AI.method: Develop a geometric framework leveraging Galilean symmetry to represent space-time for joint estimation of navigation state and time delay. Derive an Equivariant Filter (EqF) for coupled estimation. Validate on two fixed-wing UAVs with GNSS delays of 90 ms and 120 ms; perform simulations up to 500 ms; compare against EKF.
AI.result: EqF preserves accuracy and consistency under time-delay conditions; EKF becomes inconsistent and its performance degrades as delays increase.
AI.conclusion: A Galilean-invariant EqF can robustly and consistently estimate both navigation states and GNSS time delay in time-delayed INS, offering an improvement over the standard EKF in scenarios with sizable measurement delays; validated experimentally and via simulations.

title: Respecting Self-Uncertainty in On-Policy Self-Distillation for Efficient LLM Reasoning
summary: On-policy self-distillation trains a reasoning model on its own rollouts while a teacher, often the same model conditioned on privileged context, provides dense token-level supervision. Existing objectives typically weight the teacher's token-level signal uniformly across a chain-of-thought sequence, despite substantial variation in the entropy of the teacher's predictive distribution. We propose EGRSD (Entropy-Guided Reinforced Self-Distillation), which unifies token-level updates through three signals: a reward-grounded direction, a teacher-student likelihood-ratio magnitude, and the proposed teacher-entropy confidence gate that down-weights high-entropy token positions while maintaining a nonzero lower bound on every token weight. We further introduce CL-EGRSD, a causal-lookahead variant that distinguishes sustained high-entropy spans from transient high-entropy positions whose following context rapidly becomes low entropy. Experiments with Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B in thinking mode show that EGRSD and CL-EGRSD advance the accuracy-length frontier among the compared trainable methods.

AI.tldr: Entropy-guided reinforced self-distillation (EGRSD) uses three token-level signals—reward-grounded direction, teacher-student likelihood ratio, and an entropy gate—to weight supervision; CL-EGRSD adds causal-lookahead to distinguish persistent vs. transient high-entropy spans. Demonstrates improved accuracy-length frontier on Qwen3-4B/3-8B thinking mode versus baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard token-level teacher supervision in on-policy self-distillation weights tokens uniformly, ignoring the varying uncertainty of the teacher's predictions. This can underutilize or misweight supervision, especially for high-entropy tokens.
AI.method: Unify token updates using three signals: (1) reward-grounded direction, (2) teacher-student likelihood-ratio magnitude, and (3) a teacher-entropy confidence gate that down-weights high-entropy positions but imposes a nonzero lower bound. CL-EGRSD adds causal-lookahead to separate sustained high-entropy spans from transient high-entropy positions whose context later becomes low-entropy.
AI.result: Empirical results on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B (thinking mode) show EGRSD and CL-EGRSD improve the accuracy-length frontier compared with the trainable baselines and existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-aware, reinforced self-distillation and its causal-lookahead variant yield more effective teacher supervision in on-policy training, enabling better thinking-mode performance across model scales.

title: Anatomy-Slot: Unsupervised Anatomical Factorization for Homologous Bilateral Reasoning in Retinal Diagnosis
summary: Retinal diagnosis is inherently bilateral: clinicians compare homologous structures across eyes (e.g., optic disc asymmetry), yet most deep models operate on monocular representations. We investigate whether explicit structural correspondence improves diagnosis, and propose Anatomy-Slot to operationalize this hypothesis. Anatomy-Slot introduces an unsupervised anatomical bottleneck by decomposing patch tokens into slots and aligning slots across eyes via bidirectional cross-attention. On ODIR-5K with $n=10$ seeds, the method improves AUC by 4.2% over a matched ViT-L baseline (95% CIs; Wilcoxon signed-rank test, $W=0$, $p=0.002$). Pairing disruption and stress testing under Gaussian noise provide controlled tests of correspondence dependence and robustness under corruption. We further report quantitative optic disc grounding on REFUGE and cross-attention localization analysis.

AI.tldr: An Anatomy-Slot unsupervised bottleneck enforces cross-eye slot alignment to capture bilateral retinal structure, improving diagnosis on ODIR-5K with 10 seeds by 4.2% AUC over ViT-L, with robustness analyses and localization grounding.
AI.motivation: Clinicians compare homologous structures bilaterally; most models are monocular; bilateral correspondence may enhance accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: Decompose patch tokens into learnable slots forming an unsupervised anatomical bottleneck; align slots across eyes via bidirectional cross-attention; evaluate on ODIR-5K with n=10 seeds; conduct disruption and Gaussian-noise stress tests; report optic disc grounding on REFUGE and cross-attention localization.
AI.result: AUC improvement of 4.2% over a matched ViT-L baseline; statistical significance: 95% CIs; Wilcoxon signed-rank test W=0, p=0.002; robustness and localization analyses demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Explicit bilateral anatomical correspondence via Anatomy-Slot yields better diagnostic performance and provides interpretable grounding, with demonstrated robustness to corruption.

title: State-Space NTK Collapse Near Bifurcations
summary: Rich feature learning in tasks that unfold over time often requires the model to pass through bifurcations, constituting qualitative changes in the underlying model dynamics. We develop a local theory of gradient descent near these transitions through the empirical state-space neural tangent kernel (sNTK). Our central finding is that bifurcations both dominate and simplify learning dynamics: near bifurcations, we can reduce sNTK to a rank-one operator corresponding to learning in a classical normal form system, providing an analytically tractable description of the local learning geometry, even for high-dimensional recurrent systems. Concretely, we give a procedure for decomposing sNTK into bifurcation-relevant and residual channels, showing that near commonly codimension-1 bifurcations the relevant channel is a rank-one operator that is highly amplified. This amplification causes the bifurcation channel to dominate the full sNTK. Thus, bifurcations locally warp the learning landscape, funneling gradient descent into a few critical dynamical directions and making the nearby kernel and loss geometry predictable from classical normal forms. We illustrate this in a student-teacher recurrent neural network: the first learned bifurcation coincides with a sharp collapse in sNTK effective rank and the emergence of a dominant parameter direction whose restricted sNTK closely matches the landscape predicted by the scalar pitchfork normal form. Finally, we show that low-rank natural gradient methods resolve the resulting learning instability near bifurcations with very little overhead over SGD.

AI.tldr: Near bifurcations in time-evolving networks, the state-space NTK becomes effectively rank-one, aligning learning with a normal-form direction; this simplifies analysis of dynamics and enables efficient optimization via low-rank natural gradients.
AI.motivation: To understand how qualitative dynamical transitions (bifurcations) shape feature learning in temporally unfolding tasks and to provide an analytical, tractable framework using the empirical state-space neural tangent kernel (sNTK).
AI.method: Develop a local gradient-descent theory around bifurcations using the empirical sNTK; decompose sNTK into bifurcation-relevant and residual channels; show that near codimension-1 bifurcations the relevant channel is rank-one and highly amplified; illustrate with a student–teacher recurrent neural network; assess low-rank natural-gradient methods.
AI.result: Bifurcations dominate and simplify learning: near transitions the sNTK reduces to a rank-one operator corresponding to a learning in a normal-form system; the dominant channel is amplified and governs dynamics; the first learned bifurcation accompanies a collapse in sNTK rank and emergence of a dominant direction consistent with a scalar pitchfork normal form; low-rank natural gradients mitigate instability with little SGD overhead.
AI.conclusion: Bifurcations warp the learning landscape, funneling gradient descent into a few critical dynamical directions; the local geometry is predictable from classical normal forms, enabling tractable analysis and efficient optimization via low-rank methods.

title: HCSG: Human-Centric Semantic-Geometric Reasoning for Vision-Language Navigation
summary: VLN has achieved remarkable progress by scaling data and model capacity. However, the assumption of a static environment breaks down in real-world indoor scenarios, where robots inevitably encounter dynamic pedestrians. Existing human-aware approaches typically treat humans merely as moving obstacles based on implicit visual cues, lacking the explicit reasoning required to interpret human intentions or maintain social norms. To address this, we propose HCSG, the first human-centric framework for VLN. This framework provides a robust foundation for safe, socially intelligent navigation in dynamic human-robot environments that shifts the paradigm from passive collision avoidance to active human behavior understanding. Specifically, HCSG introduces a unified Human Understanding Module that synergizes two key capabilities: (i) geometric forecasting, which predicts human pose and trajectory to anticipate future motion dynamics; and (ii) semantic interpretation, which leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to generate natural language descriptions of human actions and intentions. These semantic-geometric representations are fused into the agent's topological map for instruction-conditioned planning. Furthermore, a social distance loss is introduced to enforce socially compliant interaction distances. Extensive experiments on the HA-VLNCE benchmark demonstrate that HCSG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving a 14% improvement in Success Rate and a 34% reduction in Collision Rate. Our project can be seen at https://haoxuanxu1024.github.io/HCSG/.

AI.tldr: A VLN framework (HCSG) that is human-centric, combining geometric forecasting and semantic interpretation via a Vision-Language Model to understand human actions/intents and enable socially compliant navigation in dynamic indoor environments; achieves notable gains on HA-VLNCE benchmark (14% SR, 34% fewer collisions).
AI.motivation: Real-world VLN environments are dynamic due to pedestrians; existing methods treat humans as simple moving obstacles without explicit reasoning about human intentions or social norms.
AI.method: A unified Human Understanding Module with (i) geometric forecasting (human pose and trajectory prediction) and (ii) semantic interpretation (VLM-based natural-language descriptions of human actions/intents). These semantic-geometric representations are fused into the agent’s topological map for instruction-conditioned planning, plus a social distance loss to enforce safe, socially compliant interactions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on HA-VLNCE show significant improvements over state-of-the-art, notably a 14% increase in Success Rate and a 34% reduction in Collision Rate.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a shift from passive collision avoidance to active understanding of human behavior in dynamic environments; establishes a robust foundation for safe, socially intelligent VLN; demonstrates strong empirical gains, with avenues for further work on semantics, robustness, and generalization.

title: D-VLA: A High-Concurrency Distributed Asynchronous Reinforcement Learning Framework for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: The rapid evolution of Embodied AI has enabled Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to excel in multimodal perception and task execution. However, applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) to these massive models in large-scale distributed environments faces severe systemic bottlenecks, primarily due to the resource conflict between high-fidelity physical simulation and the intensive VRAM/bandwidth demands of deep learning. This conflict often leaves overall throughput constrained by execution-phase inefficiencies. To address these challenges, we propose D-VLA, a high-concurrency, low-latency distributed RL framework for large-scale embodied foundation models. D-VLA introduces "Plane Decoupling," physically isolating high-frequency training data from low-frequency weight control to eliminate interference between simulation and optimization. We further design a four-thread asynchronous "Swimlane" pipeline, enabling full parallel overlap of sampling, inference, gradient computation, and parameter distribution. Additionally, a dual-pool VRAM management model and topology-aware replication resolve memory fragmentation and optimize communication efficiency. Experiments on benchmarks like LIBERO show that D-VLA significantly outperforms mainstream RL frameworks in throughput and sampling efficiency for billion-parameter VLA models. In trillion-parameter scalability tests, our framework maintains exceptional stability and linear speedup, providing a robust system for high-performance general-purpose embodied agents.

AI.tldr: D-VLA is a high-concurrency, low-latency distributed RL framework for large-scale embodied vision-language-action models, introducing plane decoupling, a four-thread Swimlane pipeline, and dual-pool VRAM management to achieve superior throughput and scalable stability on billion- to trillion-parameter models.
AI.motivation: Address the bottlenecks in applying reinforcement learning to large embodied VLA models in distributed settings, specifically interference between high-fidelity simulation and intensive DL computation, to improve throughput, sampling efficiency, and scalability.
AI.method: Plane Decoupling to physically separate high-frequency training data from low-frequency weight updates; a four-thread asynchronous Swimlane pipeline for overlapping sampling, inference, gradient computation, and parameter distribution; a dual-pool VRAM management scheme with topology-aware replication to reduce memory fragmentation and optimize communication.
AI.result: Empirical results show D-VLA significantly outperforms mainstream RL frameworks in throughput and sampling efficiency on billion-parameter VLA models (LIBERO benchmarks). In trillion-parameter scalability tests, it demonstrates stable linear speedups and robustness for high-performance embodied agents.
AI.conclusion: D-VLA provides a practical, scalable system for high-performance embodied agents by decoupling computation and data flow, enabling full overlap of operations, and optimizing memory and communication in large-scale distributed RL.

title: AuraMask: An Extensible Pipeline for Developing Aesthetic Anti-Facial Recognition Image Filters
summary: Anti-facial recognition (AFR) image filters alter images in ways that are subtle to people but blinding to computer vision. Yet, despite widespread interest in these technologies to subvert surveillance, users rarely use them in practice -- because the ``subtle'' alterations are visible enough to conflict with users' self-presentation goals. To address this challenge, we propose AuraMask: a novel approach to creating AFR filters that are both adversarially effective and aesthetically acceptable. Using AuraMask, we produce 40 ``aesthetic'' filters that emulate popular ``one-click'' Instagram image filters. We show that AuraMask filters meet or exceed the adversarial effectiveness of prior methods against open-source facial recognition models. Moreover, in a controlled online user study ($N=630$) we confirm these filters achieve significantly higher user acceptance than prior methods. Lastly, we provide our AFR pipeline to the community for accelerated research in adversarially effective and aesthetically acceptable protections.

AI.tldr: AuraMask creates 40 aesthetically pleasing anti-facial recognition (AFR) image filters that mimic Instagram-style edits, achieving strong adversarial effectiveness against open-source face recognition while significantly improving user acceptance; the authors publicly release their AFR pipeline for research acceleration.
AI.motivation: Users want to subvert surveillance, but subtle AFR alterations are visually discordant with self-presentation goals. The need is for AFR filters that are both effective and aesthetically acceptable.
AI.method: Develop AuraMask to produce 40 'aesthetic' filters resembling popular one-click Instagram filters. Evaluate their adversarial effectiveness against open-source facial recognition models. Conduct a controlled online user study (N=630) to compare user acceptance against prior methods. Release the AFR pipeline to the research community.
AI.result: AuraMask filters meet or exceed the adversarial effectiveness of prior methods against open-source facial recognition models. They achieve significantly higher user acceptance in the user study (N=630).
AI.conclusion: AuraMask improves feasibility of AFR defenses by combining adversarial effectiveness with aesthetic acceptance, and the authors contribute their AFR pipeline to accelerate further research.

title: Inference-Time Machine Unlearning via Gated Activation Redirection
summary: Large Language Models memorize vast amounts of training data, raising concerns regarding privacy, copyright infringement, and safety. Machine unlearning seeks to remove the influence of a targeted forget set while preserving model performance, ideally approximating a model retrained from scratch without the forget set. Existing approaches aim to achieve this by updating model parameters via gradient-based methods. However, these updates are computationally expensive, lead to irreversible weight changes, and degrade when the model is quantized for deployment. A recent alternative to changing model weights is activation engineering, where activations are changed during inference to steer model behavior. Despite circumventing weight editing, naive activation steering introduces its own failure modes, as a single global steering vector applies the same intervention to every input, leading to unintended changes in model behavior. We introduce Inference-Time Unlearning via Gated Activation Redirection (GUARD-IT), a training- and gradient-free method that unlearns via input-dependent activation steering at inference time. The resulting intervention is applied as a norm-preserving rotation in the residual stream, leaving model weights untouched. Experiments on TOFU and MUSE show that GUARD-IT matches or exceeds 12 gradient-based baselines across three model scales, while being the only method to simultaneously preserve utility, suppress memorization, and avoid catastrophic collapse across all settings. GUARD-IT further supports continual unlearning without retraining, and remains effective under quantization, a scenario in which parameter-editing methods degrade.

AI.tldr: A training-free, gradient-free activation-based method (GUARD-IT) enables inference-time unlearning by input-dependent activation steering, preserving weights and remaining effective under quantization and continual unlearning, achieving competitive performance against gradient-based baselines across multiple model scales.
AI.motivation: Memorization in large language models raises privacy, copyright, and safety concerns. Desired unlearning should remove influence of targeted data while preserving utility, without expensive retraining or irreversible weight edits, and remain effective under deployment constraints (quantization, continual unlearning).
AI.method: Inference-Time Unlearning via Gated Activation Redirect (GUARD-IT): apply input-dependent, norm-preserving rotation in the residual stream to steer activations at inference time. No training or gradient updates; weights are untouched. Uses gating to avoid a single global steering vector; provides a per-input activation steering decision.
AI.result: Matches or exceeds 12 gradient-based unlearning baselines across three model scales (TOFU and MUSE). Demonstrates simultaneous preservation of utility, suppression of memorization, and avoidance of catastrophic collapse across settings. Supports continual unlearning without retraining and remains effective under model quantization where parameter-editing methods degrade.
AI.conclusion: GUARD-IT offers a practical, gradient-free solution for inference-time unlearning that preserves weights, tolerates quantization, and supports continual unlearning, with competitive to superior performance relative to gradient-based approaches.

title: What Limits Vision-and-Language Navigation ?
summary: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) is a cornerstone of embodied intelligence. However, current agents often suffer from significant performance degradation when transitioning from simulation to real-world deployment, primarily due to perceptual instability (e.g., lighting variations and motion blur) and under-specified instructions. While existing methods attempt to bridge this gap by scaling up model size and training data, we argue that the bottleneck lies in the lack of robust spatial grounding and cross-domain priors. In this paper, we propose StereoNav, a robust Vision-Language-Action framework designed to enhance real-world navigation consistency. To address the inherent gap between synthetic training and physical execution, we introduce Target-Location Priors as a persistent bridge. These priors provide stable visual guidance that remains invariant across domains, effectively grounding the agent even when instructions are vague. Furthermore, to mitigate visual disturbances like motion blur and illumination shifts, StereoNav leverages stereo vision to construct a unified representation of semantics and geometry, enabling precise action prediction through enhanced depth awareness. Extensive experiments on R2R-CE and RxR-CE demonstrate that StereoNav achieves state-of-the-art egocentric RGB performance, with SR and SPL scores of 81.1% and 68.3%, and 67.5% and 52.0%, respectively, while using significantly fewer parameters and less training data than prior scaling-based approaches. More importantly, real-world robotic deployments confirm that StereoNav substantially improves navigation reliability in complex, unstructured environments. Project page: https://yunheng-wang.github.io/stereonav-public.github.io.

AI.tldr: StereoNav introduces Target-Location Priors and stereo vision to close the sim-to-real gap in Vision-Language Navigation, achieving state-of-the-art RGB performance with fewer parameters and data, and validated in real-world robotic deployments.
AI.motivation: VLN agents degrade when transferring from simulation to the real world due to perceptual instability and under-specified instructions; the bottleneck is robust spatial grounding and cross-domain priors, not merely larger models or more data.
AI.method: Propose StereoNav, a Vision-Language-Action framework that uses Target-Location Priors as a persistent cross-domain bridge and stereo vision to fuse semantic and geometric cues, enabling depth-aware action prediction and improved grounding.
AI.result: On R2R-CE and RxR-CE, StereoNav achieves state-of-the-art egocentric RGB performance with SR/SPL of 81.1% / 68.3% on R2R-CE and 67.5% / 52.0% on RxR-CE, using significantly fewer parameters and less training data; real-world robotic deployments show substantially improved navigation reliability in complex environments.
AI.conclusion: Target-Location Priors and stereo-depth grounding yield robust, domain-invariant guidance for VLN, reducing data/parameter requirements while delivering strong real-world performance.

title: Differentiable Learning of Lifted Action Schemas for Classical Planning
summary: Classical planners can effectively solve very large deterministic MDPs represented in STRIPS or PDDL where states are sets of atoms over objects and relations, and lifted action schemas add or delete these atoms. This compact representation yields strong search heuristics and provides an ideal setting for structural generalization, since lifted relations and action schemas give rise to infinitely many domain instances. A central challenge is to learn these relations and action schemas from data, and recent approaches have addressed this problem using different types of observations. In this work, we develop a novel neural network architecture for learning action schemas from traces where states are fully observed but action arguments are unobserved. The problem is a simplification but an important step towards learning planning domains from sequences of images and action labels, and we aim to solve this simplification in a nearly perfect manner. The challenge lies in learning the action schemas while simultaneously identifying the action arguments from observed state changes. Our approach yields a robust differentiable component that can then be integrated into larger neuro-symbolic models. We evaluate the architecture on various planning domains, where the learned lifted action schemas must recover the ground-truth structure. Additionally, we report experiments on robustness to observation noise and on a variation related to slot-based dynamics models.

AI.tldr: A neural architecture is proposed to learn lifted action schemas from traces where states are observed but action arguments are hidden, addressing a key step toward learning planning domains from data and integrating into neuro-symbolic models.
AI.motivation: To bridge symbolic planning with data-driven learning by recovering action schemas and their arguments from state-transition traces, enabling scalable, lifted representations that generalize across domain instances.
AI.method: A novel differentiable neural architecture that jointly infers action schemas (lifted predicates and predicates relations) and the hidden action arguments from observed state changes in traces. It targets a simplification (fully observed states, unobserved arguments) as a stepping stone toward learning planning domains from sequences of images and labels, and emphasizes robust integration as a modular component.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across several planning domains shows that the model recovers ground-truth lifted schemas, demonstrates robustness to observation noise, and performs on variations related to slot-based dynamics models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture provides a robust differentiable component for learning action schemas from traces, suitable for integration into larger neuro-symbolic systems and a meaningful advance toward data-driven learning of planning domains.

title: CRePE: Curved Ray Expectation Positional Encoding for Unified-Camera-Controlled Video Generation
summary: Camera-conditioned video generation requires positional encoding that remains reliable under changes in camera motion, lens configuration, and scene structure. However, existing attention-level camera encodings either provide ray-only camera signals or rely on pinhole camera geometry, limiting their applicability to general camera control under the Unified Camera Model, including wide-angle and fisheye lenses. To address this limitation, we propose Curved Ray Expectation Positional Encoding (CRePE). CRePE represents each image token as a depth-aware positional distribution along its source ray, providing a Unified Camera Model-compatible positional encoding that captures the projected-path geometry induced by wide-angle and fisheye cameras. CRePE is implemented through a Geometric Attention Adapter added to frozen video DiTs, injecting token-wise scene-distance information into selected attention layers and stabilizing it with pseudo supervision from a monocular geometry foundation model. This design leads to more stable camera control and improves several geometry-aware and perceptual-quality metrics, while remaining competitive on video-quality metrics. Controlled positional-encoding ablations show a better overall average rank than a RayRoPE-style endpoint PE baseline, demonstrating the effectiveness of UCM-aware projected-path integration across diverse camera models. Furthermore, by extending the same positional-encoding pathway to external geometry control through Radial MixForcing, CRePE supports external radial-map control for scene-geometry-conditioned generation and source-video motion transfer beyond camera control.

AI.tldr: Proposes CRePE, a depth-aware, curved-ray positional encoding compatible with the Unified Camera Model for camera-conditioned video generation, improving geometry-aware metrics and enabling external radial-map control via a Geometric Attention Adapter and pseudo supervision.
AI.motivation: Current camera encodings are limited to ray-only or pinhole models, failing under wide-angle/fisheye/distorted lenses and the Unified Camera Model. A geometry-aware, unified encoding is needed for robust camera control across diverse optics and scene structures.
AI.method: Represent each image token as a depth-aware probabilistic distribution along its source ray; integrate this via a Geometric Attention Adapter added to frozen video DiTs, injecting scene-distance information into selected attention layers and stabilized with pseudo supervision from a monocular geometry foundation model; extend to external radial-map control through Radial MixForcing.
AI.result: Improves geometry-aware and perceptual-quality metrics, with more stable camera control and competitive video quality; ablations show CRePE achieves a better average rank than RayRoPE-style endpoint PE; demonstrates effectiveness across diverse camera models and enables external radial-map control for scene-geometry-conditioned generation and motion transfer.
AI.conclusion: CRePE provides a Unified Camera Model–aware positional encoding by embedding projected-path geometry into token representations, enabling stable camera control and external geometry control (Radial MixForcing) across wide-angle and fisheye lenses while remaining competitive on video quality.

title: WriteSAE: Sparse Autoencoders for Recurrent State
comment: 26 pages, 14 figures, 21 tables; code at https://github.com/JackYoung27/writesae
summary: We introduce WriteSAE, the first sparse autoencoder that decomposes and edits the matrix cache write of state-space and hybrid recurrent language models, where residual SAEs cannot reach. Existing SAEs read residual streams, but Gated DeltaNet, Mamba-2, and RWKV-7 write to a $d_k \times d_v$ cache through rank-1 updates $k_t v_t^\top$ that no vector atom can replace. WriteSAE factors each decoder atom into the native write shape, exposes a closed form for the per-token logit shift, and trains under matched Frobenius norm so atoms swap one cache slot at a time. Atom substitution beats matched-norm ablation on 92.4% of $n=4{,}851$ firings at Qwen3.5-0.8B L9 H4, the 87-atom population test holds at 89.8%, the closed form predicts measured effects at $R^2=0.98$, and Mamba-2-370M substitutes at 88.1% over 2,500 firings. Sustained three-position installs at $3\times$ lift midrank target-in-continuation from 33.3% to 100% under greedy decoding, the first behavioral install at the matrix-recurrent write site.

AI.tldr: WriteSAE is a sparse autoencoder that directly models and edits the matrix cache writes in state-space/hybrid RNN language models, enabling atom-level substitutions and strong empirical gains; it provides a closed-form per-token logit shift and high predictive fidelity, outperforming residual SAEs in several settings.
AI.motivation: Existing residual SAEs read residual streams but cannot modify the rank-1 cache updates (k_t v_t^T) used by state-space and RWKV-style models; this limits effective editing of the computation at the cache-write level.
AI.method: Factor each decoder atom into the native write shape; derive a closed-form for the per-token logit shift; train under a matched Frobenius-norm objective to allow atoms to swap one cache slot at a time; evaluate atom substitution across multiple models/datasets and analyze midrank-target-in-continuation gains under greedy decoding.
AI.result: Atom substitution achieves 92.4% success over 4,851 firings on Qwen3.5-0.8B L9 H4; 87-atom population test holds at 89.8%; closed-form predictions achieve R^2 = 0.98; Mamba-2-370M substitutes at 88.1% over 2,500 firings; three-position installs yield a 3× lift of midrank target-in-continuation from 33.3% to 100% under greedy decoding.
AI.conclusion: WriteSAE effectively decomposes and edits matrix-recurrent writes, enabling robust atom-level substitutions with substantial empirical gains and a strong predictive closed form.

title: Exploring Human-Robot Collaboration: Analysis of Interaction Modalities in Challenging Tasks
summary: This work compares three interaction modalities for human-robot collaboration: passive, reactive, and proactive. We studied 18 participants assembling a seven-layer colored tower from memory while using nearby and distant blocks. In the passive modality participants worked alone; in the reactive modality a mobile robot helped only upon request; in the proactive modality it initiated brick delivery and error signaling without explicit requests. Although robot assistance increased completion time, most participants preferred collaboration: 67% preferred proactive behavior and 78% judged it most useful. These results suggest that timely proactive support can improve user experience in controlled collaborative tasks.

AI.tldr: Proactive robot assistance enhances user experience in a controlled HRC task despite increasing task time; participants favored proactive modality over passive or reactive.
AI.motivation: To examine how different interaction modalities (passive, reactive, proactive) affect task performance and user experience in human-robot collaboration.
AI.method: 18 participants assembled a seven-layer colored tower from memory using blocks located nearby or distantly. Three modalities were tested: passive (robot disengaged), reactive (robot assists only on request), and proactive (robot initiates brick delivery and error signaling without requests).
AI.result: Use of proactive modality increased completion time but was preferred by 67% of participants and judged most useful by 78%, suggesting proactive, timely support improves user experience in collaborative tasks.
AI.conclusion: Timely proactive robot support can improve user experience in controlled collaborative tasks, even at the cost of longer task completion times.

title: What properties of reasoning supervision are associated with improved downstream model quality?
comment: To appear in the Proceedings of the International Conference on Computational Science (ICCS) 2026
summary: Validating training data for reasoning models typically requires expensive trial-and-error fine-tuning cycles. In this work, we investigate whether the utility of a reasoning dataset can be reliably predicted prior to training using intrinsic data metrics. We propose a suite of quantitative measures and evaluate their predictive power by fine-tuning 8B and 11B models on semantically distinct variants of a Polish reasoning dataset. Our analysis reveals that these intrinsic metrics demonstrate strong and significant correlations with downstream model performance. Crucially, we find that the predictors of utility are scale-dependent: smaller models rely on alignment-focused metrics to ensure precision, whereas larger models benefit from high redundancy, utilizing verbose traces to solve complex tasks. These findings establish a scale-aware framework for validating reasoning data, enabling practitioners to select effective training sets without the need for exhaustive empirical testing.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic data metrics can predict the utility of reasoning datasets before training; predictors are scale-dependent, enabling a scale-aware data validation framework.
AI.motivation: Reduce costly trial-and-error fine-tuning by predicting dataset usefulness using intrinsic properties; examine how model size affects useful metrics.
AI.method: Propose a suite of intrinsic data metrics; empirically test by fine-tuning 8B and 11B Polish reasoning dataset variants; compute correlations between metrics and downstream performance; analyze scale-dependence.
AI.result: Intrinsic metrics exhibit strong correlations with downstream performance; small models rely on alignment-focused metrics for precision; large models benefit from high redundancy and verbose traces; results support a scale-aware data validation framework.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a scale-aware framework for validating reasoning data to select effective training sets without exhaustive empirical testing.

title: DirectTryOn: One-Step Virtual Try-On via Straightened Conditional Transport
summary: Recent diffusion- and flow-based VTON methods achieve strong results with pretrained generative models, but their reliance on multi-step sampling incurs high inference cost, while existing acceleration methods largely overlook the intrinsic structure of the try-on task. In this paper, we highlight a key observation: VTON outputs are highly constrained by the conditional inputs, suggesting that the conditional sampling trajectory can be much straighter than that in general image generation, making one-step generation a natural solution. However, limited task-specific data makes training from scratch impractical, forcing existing methods to fine-tune pretrained models whose objectives do not encourage such straight conditional trajectories. Thus, the deviation from an ideal straight path mainly comes from the mismatch between pretrained base models and the conditional nature of try-on generation, rather than from the task itself. Motivated by this insight, we encourage straighter VTON sampling trajectories through three targeted modifications: pure conditional transport, a garment preservation loss, and a self consistency loss. We further introduce a one-step distillation stage. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with one-step sampling, establishing a new standard for efficient and high-quality VTON.

AI.tldr: The paper argues for one-step VTON by exploiting the conditional structure of try-on tasks, introducing three targeted losses and a one-step distillation stage to enforce straight conditional sampling, achieving state-of-the-art results with significantly reduced inference cost.
AI.motivation: Multi-step diffusion/flow-based VTON methods incur high inference cost and often ignore the task's conditional structure. Training from scratch is impractical due to limited task-specific data, while fine-tuning pretrained models does not align with the ideal straight conditional trajectory.
AI.method: Introduce three targeted modifications to encourage straighter sampling: 1) pure conditional transport, 2) garment preservation loss, 3) self-consistency loss. Add a one-step distillation stage to train a model for one-step generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with one-step sampling, establishing a new standard for efficient and high-quality VTON.
AI.conclusion: Straighter conditional sampling trajectories are achievable for VTON via the proposed losses and distillation, enabling efficient one-step generation without sacrificing quality.

title: Graph-Based Financial Fraud Detection with Calibrated Risk Scoring and Structural Regularization
summary: Financial transaction fraud prevention faces challenges such as complex relationship structures, concealed behavioral patterns, and dynamically changing data distribution. Discrimination models relying solely on independent sample features are insufficient to fully characterize the risks of group collaboration and chain transfers within transaction networks. This paper proposes a graph neural network representation learning and risk discrimination framework for financial transaction fraud prevention. It integrates transaction records and identity information into node attributes and constructs a transaction graph based on shared attributes and interaction consistency to explicitly model inter-transaction relationships. In model design, a multi-layer message passing mechanism is employed to aggregate neighborhood information, learn node embedding representations containing structural context semantics, and output transaction-level fraud probability and risk scores through a lightweight risk discrimination head. A weighted supervision objective is introduced to mitigate training bias caused by class imbalance, and structural consistency regularization constraints are combined to suppress the impact of noisy edges on representation drift, thereby improving the stability and usability of risk characterization. Experiments are conducted on a publicly available financial transaction dataset, comparing various methods in the same direction and comprehensively evaluating them under a unified evaluation protocol. The results show that the proposed method outperforms other methods in risk ranking and probability calibration quality, validating the effectiveness of graph structure modeling and representation learning collaboration in financial transaction fraud prevention.

AI.tldr: GNN-based framework for financial transaction fraud prevention using a transaction graph with integrated transaction and identity features, multi-layer message passing, weighted loss for class imbalance, and structural consistency regularization; achieves improved risk ranking and calibration on a public dataset.
AI.motivation: Independent-sample features fail to capture relational patterns such as group collaboration and chain transfers in transaction networks; a relational graph approach is needed to enhance fraud risk discrimination.
AI.method: Construct a graph where nodes represent transactions with features from transaction records and identity information. Edges link transactions via shared attributes and interaction consistency. Apply multi-layer message passing to aggregate neighborhood information and learn node embeddings that capture structural context. Use a lightweight risk discrimination head to output per-transaction fraud probability and risk scores. Employ a weighted supervision objective to address class imbalance and structural consistency regularization to suppress noisy edges and reduce representation drift.
AI.result: Evaluated on a public financial transaction dataset under a unified evaluation protocol, the proposed method outperforms competing approaches in risk ranking and probability calibration, validating the effectiveness of graph-based representation learning and relational modeling for fraud prevention.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based relational modeling with representation learning improves fraud risk characterization and prevention performance, enabling more accurate and calibrated fraud assessment.

title: BlockVLA: Accelerating Autoregressive VLA via Block Diffusion Finetuning
summary: While autoregressive (AR) Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated formidable reasoning capabilities in robotic tasks, their sequential decoding process often incurs high inference latency and may amplify error accumulation during long-horizon execution. Discrete Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs) provide a promising alternative through parallel token refinement, but their practical deployment in robotics remains limited by repeated denoising function evaluations (NFEs) and the difficulty of directly applying standard KV caching to bidirectional iterative decoding. To bridge these paradigms, we propose BlockVLA, a framework that adapts pretrained AR backbones into an efficient discrete diffusion policy through a block diffusion paradigm. BlockVLA maintains autoregressive dependencies at the block level while enabling parallel denoising within each block, thereby combining global causal coherence with local parallel generation. This design enables prefix KV-cache reuse across completed blocks, reduces the effective cost of iterative denoising, and provides a smoother transition from AR pretraining to diffusion-based policy fine-tuning. We conduct extensive evaluations on the LIBERO and SimplerEnv benchmarks. Experimental results demonstrate that our BlockVLA achieves a 3.3$\times$ inference acceleration over standard discrete diffusion baselines. Furthermore, our model exhibits superior training efficiency, with success rates converging substantially faster than baselines, a gain that is particularly pronounced in complex, long-horizon tasks, where BlockVLA achieves significant performance gains in the early stages of training. This work establishes Block Diffusion as a robust bridge between large-scale pretrained AR models and efficient, high-frequency real-time robotic control.

AI.tldr: BlockVLA introduces a block diffusion framework that converts autoregressive VLA backbones into an efficient discrete diffusion policy by parallel denoising within blocks while preserving block-level autoregression; it achieves 3.3x inference speed-ups and faster training convergence on LIBERO and SimplerEnv, bridging AR pretraining with diffusion-based policy in robotic control.
AI.motivation: AR VLA models show strong reasoning but suffer high latency and error accumulation; discrete diffusion LMs enable parallel refinement but incur many denoising evaluations and KV-cache challenges for bidirectional decoding; a bridge between AR pretraining and diffusion-based control is needed for efficient, real-time robotics.
AI.method: Introduce BlockVLA with a block diffusion paradigm: preserve autoregressive dependencies at the block level and perform parallel denoising within each block. Enable prefix KV-cache reuse across completed blocks to reduce iterative denoising costs and facilitate a smooth transition from AR pretraining to diffusion fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LIBERO and SimplerEnv shows BlockVLA achieves about 3.3× inference acceleration versus standard discrete diffusion baselines. Training efficiency improves with faster convergence of success rates, particularly in long-horizon, complex tasks, indicating benefits during early training stages.
AI.conclusion: Block Diffusion serves as a robust bridge between large-scale pretrained AR models and efficient, high-frequency real-time robotic control, demonstrating that combining AR pretraining with diffusion-based policy learning yields both speed and accuracy gains in challenging robotic tasks.

title: Debunking Grad-ECLIP: A Comprehensive Study on Its Incorrectness and Fundamental Principles for Model Interpretation
summary: Grad-ECLIP is published at ICML 2024 and represents a new Transformer interpretation technical route (intermediate features-based). First, this paper demonstrates that the intermediate features-based technical route is not a novel one. Based on the existing attention-based route, we have developed Attention-ECLIP, which is completely equivalent to Grad-ECLIP but with simpler computation. Both through formal derivation and experimental validation, we prove that the intermediate feature-based route represented by Grad-ECLIP is actually an equivalent variant of the attention-based route. Next, this paper demonstrates that the Grad-ECLIP method is flawed. The model interpretation results obtained by Grad-ECLIP are not those of the original model, and the interpretation results are misaligned with the model's performance. We analyze the causes of Grad-ECLIP's flaws and propose, or rather, explicitly emphasize two fundamental principles that model interpretation should adhere to in order to avoid similar errors.

AI.tldr: Grad-ECLIP is not a novel intermediate-feature interpretation route; it is equivalent to the attention-based approach (Attention-ECLIP) and thus unnecessary complexity. Its interpretation results do not align with the original model's behavior, revealing flaws. The paper advocates two fundamental principles to ensure model interpretations reflect the model and avoid similar errors.
AI.motivation: Assess whether the intermediate-feature interpretation route claimed by Grad-ECLIP is truly novel and whether its interpretations faithfully reflect the model. Also to highlight pitfalls in model interpretability for Transformer-based models.
AI.method: Provide formal derivations to show equivalence between Grad-ECLIP and Attention-ECLIP; simplify computation via Attention-ECLIP; conduct experimental validation; diagnose causes of misalignment; articulate guiding principles for interpretation.
AI.result: Formal derivation and experiments show Grad-ECLIP is equivalent to Attention-ECLIP, with a simpler computation path. Grad-ECLIP’s interpretation results do not correspond to the original model’s behavior, indicating misalignment and flaws. The authors identify causes and emphasize two fundamental principles to avoid such errors.
AI.conclusion: The intermediate-feature route is not a novel interpretability method. Interpretability work must adhere to robust principles to ensure that explanations faithfully reflect the model’s behavior; the paper calls for principled guidelines to prevent misinterpretation.

title: ToolMol: Evolutionary Agentic Framework for Multi-objective Drug Discovery
summary: Advances in large language models (LLMs) have recently opened new and promising avenues for small-molecule drug discovery. Yet existing LLM-based approaches for molecular generation often suffer from high rates of invalid and low-quality ligand candidates, a result of the syntactic limitations of current models with regard to molecular strings. In this paper, we introduce $\texttt{ToolMol}$, an evolutionary agentic framework for de novo drug design. $\texttt{ToolMol}$ combines a multi-objective genetic algorithm with an agentic LLM operator that iteratively updates the ligand population. We build a comprehensive toolbox of RDKit-backed functions that allows our agentic operator to consisently make precise ligand modifications. $\texttt{ToolMol}$ achieves state-of-the-art performance on multi-objective property optimization tasks, discovering drug-like and synthesizable ligands that have $>10\%$ stronger predicted binding affinity compared to existing methods, evaluated on three protein targets. $\texttt{ToolMol}$ ligands additionally achieve state-of-the-art results in gold-standard Absolute Binding Free Energy scores, gaining over existing methods by over $35\%$.
