LLMs show recall-safety tradeoffs and anchoring biases; the benchmark aims to better assess and improve AI sequential decision-support in high-stakes care.
AI.motivation: ICU benchmarks often treat historical clinician actions as ground truth, but such actions are taken with incomplete information and context, risking suboptimal judgments. There is a need for clinically grounded evaluation of AI decision-support that reflects real informational constraints.
AI.method: RealICU creates hindsight annotations after full patient trajectories reviewed by senior physicians. It defines four physician-motivated tasks (assess Patient Status, Acute Problems, Recommended Actions, Red Flag actions). Trajectories are partitioned into 30-minute windows. Two datasets are released: RealICU-Gold (930 windows, 94 MIMIC-IV patients) and RealICU-Scale (11,862 windows) with Oracle, a physician-validated LLM hindsight labeler. Also ICU-Evo studies structured-memory agents for long-horizon reasoning.
AI.result: Existing LLMs, including memory-augmented models, perform poorly on RealICU, exposing two failure modes: a recall-safety tradeoff for clinical recommendations and anchoring bias toward early interpretations. ICU-Evo improves long-horizon reasoning but does not fully eliminate safety failures.
AI.conclusion: RealICU provides a clinically grounded testbed to measure and improve AI sequential decision-support in high-stakes ICU care and enables evaluation of memory-enhanced approaches like ICU-Evo.

title: Uncertainty-aware Spatial-Frequency Registration and Fusion for Infrared and Visible Images
summary: Infrared and Visible Image Fusion (IVIF) has shown promise in visual tasks under challenging environments, but fusion under unregistered conditions faces inherent misalignments. Current studies to solve them either predict the deformation parameters coarse-to-fine (i.e., coarse registration and fine registration) or estimate the deformation fields in multi-scales for registration. Though straightforward, they overlook the cumulative errors in registration, which contaminate the fusion stage and severely deteriorate the resulting images. We introduce the Spatial-Frequency Registration and Fusion (SFRF) framework, which incorporates uncertainty estimation and infrared thermal radiation distribution consistency into a unified pipeline to handle the error accumulation for robust registration and fusion across both spatial and frequency domains. Specifically, SFRF constructs a Multi-scale Iterative Registration (MIR) framework that iteratively refines the deformation field across scales, leveraging uncertainty estimation at each stage to mitigate error accumulation and enhance alignment accuracy dynamically. To ensure the accurate alignment of infrared thermal distributions during registration, thermal radiation distribution consistency is employed as a frequency-domain supervisory signal, promoting global consistency in the frequency domain. Based on the spatial-frequency alignment, SFRF further adopts a Dual-branch Spatial-Frequency Fusion (DSFF) module, which incorporates spatial geometric features and frequency distribution information to reconstruct visually appealing images. SFRF achieves impressive performance across diverse datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes SFRF: a unified spatial-frequency framework for robust unregistered infrared-visible image fusion, combining Multi-scale Iterative Registration with uncertainty estimation and frequency-domain supervision, and a Dual-branch Spatial-Frequency Fusion module to achieve robust alignment and high-quality fusion across datasets.
AI.motivation: Unregistered IVIF suffers from misalignment and cumulative errors that degrade fusion; existing coarse-to-fine or multi-scale registration methods do not adequately mitigate error accumulation; there is a need for a unified approach that handles spatial and frequency-domain consistency.
AI.method: Introduce MIR for iterative, multi-scale deformation refinement with uncertainty estimation; apply thermal radiation distribution consistency as a frequency-domain supervisory signal; develop DSFF module that fuses using spatial geometry features and frequency information.
AI.result: Demonstrates impressive performance across diverse datasets, indicating improved robustness of registration and quality of fused images.
AI.conclusion: SFRF provides a robust, unified pipeline that suppresses error accumulation in IVIF through spatial-frequency alignment and uncertainty-aware registration, achieving reliable fusion across challenging conditions.

title: Discrete Stochastic Localization for Non-autoregressive Generation
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2602.16169
summary: Continuous diffusion is a natural framework for non-autoregressive generation but has generally lagged behind masked discrete diffusion models (MDMs) on discrete sequence generation. We argue that the bottleneck is not continuity itself, but a representation in which denoising depends on timestep-indexed noise regimes. We introduce \emph{Discrete Stochastic Localization} (DSL), a continuous-state framework with unit-sphere token embeddings whose Bayes-optimal denoiser is invariant to the nominal signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) under the localization channel. One trained network then supports an entire family of per-token SNR paths, with endpoint masked-diffusion paths as a special case. Fine-tuning a pretrained MDLM checkpoint with DSL substantially improves distributional faithfulness (MAUVE) on OpenWebText across all step budgets from $T{=}128$ to $T{=}1024$, and the same checkpoint supports random-order autoregressive sampling, as well as a hybrid continuous-then-discrete sampler using as few as T=48 total steps -- without distillation or retraining.

AI.tldr: Discrete Stochastic Localization (DSL) provides a continuous-state diffusion for discrete tokens with unit-sphere embeddings, enabling a single model to support multiple per-token SNR paths and offering improved fidelity and flexible sampling (including random-order AR and hybrid continuous-discrete sampling) with minimal retraining.
AI.motivation: Continuous diffusion lags MDMs on discrete sequences; the bottleneck is a representation where denoising depends on timestep-indexed noise regimes. A representation with SNR-invariant denoisers could unify and improve discrete generation under diffusion.
AI.method: Introduce Discrete Stochastic Localization (DSL): a continuous-state framework with unit-sphere token embeddings. The Bayes-optimal denoiser is invariant to nominal SNR under the localization channel. Train a single network to support a family of per-token SNR paths; endpoint paths include masked-diffusion. Fine-tune a pretrained MDLM checkpoint with DSL to improve distributional faithfulness; enable random-order autoregressive sampling and a hybrid continuous-then-discrete sampler using as few as T=48 steps, without distillation or retraining.
AI.result: MAUVE-based fidelity on OpenWebText improves across step budgets from T=128 to T=1024 after fine-tuning with DSL. The same checkpoint supports random-order autoregressive sampling and a hybrid continuous-then-discrete sampler at very low step counts (as low as T=48), without distillation or retraining.
AI.conclusion: DSL provides a unified, efficient diffusion framework for discrete generation, improving fidelity and enabling flexible sampling strategies with minimal retraining.

title: Manipulation Planning for Construction Activities with Repetitive Tasks
summary: In this paper, we study the problem of manipulation skill acquisition for performing construction activities consisting of repetitive tasks (e.g., building a wall or installing ceiling tiles). Our approach involves setting up a simulated construction activity in a Virtual Reality (VR) environment, where the user can provide demonstrations of the object manipulation skills needed to perform the construction activity. We then exploit the screw geometry of motion to approximate the demonstrated motion as a sequence of constant screw motions. For performing the construction activity, we generate the sequence of manipulation task instances and then compute the joint space motion plan corresponding to each instance using Screw Linear Interpolation (ScLERP) and Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC). We evaluate our framework by executing two representative construction tasks: constructing brick walls and installing multiple ceiling tiles. Each task is performed using only a single demonstration, a pick-and-place action for the bricks, and a single ceiling tile installation. Our experiments with a 7-DoF robot in both simulation and hardware demonstrate that the approach generalizes robustly to arbitrarily long construction activities that involve repetitive motions and demand precision, even when provided with just one demonstration. For instance, we can construct walls of arbitrary layout and length by leveraging a single demonstration of placing one brick on top of another.

AI.tldr: An imitation-learning framework for repetitive construction tasks using VR demonstrations and screw-motion interpolation (ScLERP) to plan joint-space trajectories; demonstrates robust generalization from a single demonstration to arbitrarily long walls/ tile layouts using a 7-DoF robot in simulation and hardware.
AI.motivation: Tackle repetitive, precision-intensive construction activities with limited training data by leveraging screw-motion geometry and VR demonstrations to synthesize scalable manipulation policies.
AI.method: Capture demonstrations in VR; model motion as constant screw motions; generate task-instances; compute joint-space trajectories via ScLERP and Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC); apply to 7-DoF robot in simulation and hardware; validate with two tasks (brick-wall construction and ceiling-tile installation) using single demonstrations per task (bricks via pick-and-place, tiles via simple placement).
AI.result: The approach generalizes robustly to long, repetitive construction sequences, enabling walls of arbitrary layout/length using one demonstration; successful execution in both simulation and hardware.
AI.conclusion: A VR-based imitation framework with screw-motion decomposition can learn scalable, accurate manipulation policies for repetitive construction activities from minimal demonstrations, offering practical generalization to extended tasks.

title: Learning Local Constraints for Reinforcement-Learned Content Generators
summary: Constraint-based game content generators that learn local constraints from existing content, such as Wave Function Collapse (WFC), can generate visually satisfying game levels but face challenges in guaranteeing global properties, such as playability. On the other hand, reinforcement-learning trained generators can guarantee global properties -- because such properties can easily be included in reward functions -- but the results can be visually dissatisfying. In this paper, we explore ways to combine these methods. Specifically, we constrain the action space of a PCGRL generator with constraints learned by WFC, effectively allowing the PCGRL generator to achieve global properties while forced to adhere to local constraints. To better analyze how this hybrid content generation method operates, we vary the number and type of inputs, and we test whether to randomly collapse the starting state and exclude rare patterns. While the method is sensitive to hyperparameter tuning, the best of our trained generators produce visually satisfying and playable puzzle-platform game levels -- such as Lode Runner levels -- with desired global properties.

AI.tldr: A hybrid approach combines WFC-learned local constraints to constrain a PCGRL generator's action space, achieving global playability while preserving local visual quality; ablations vary inputs, starting-state collapse, and rare-pattern exclusion; the best models yield visually satisfying and playable levels (e.g., Lode Runner) with desired global properties.
AI.motivation: To resolve the trade-off between constraint-based generators (high visual fidelity, weak global guarantees) and reinforcement-learning generators (strong global guarantees via rewards, potentially poor aesthetics) by integrating them into a single framework.
AI.method: Constrain the PCGRL generator's action space using constraints learned by WFC. Conduct ablations by varying input types, randomly collapsing the starting state, and excluding rare patterns to assess their impact on performance.
AI.result: Hybrid method can produce levels that are both visually appealing and playable. The best trained generators meet the desired global properties, with examples including Lode Runner-inspired levels.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid synthesis can reconcile local constraints with global playability, but performance is sensitive to hyperparameters. This approach shows promise and warrants further refinement and broader evaluation.

title: BrainAnytime: Anatomy-Aware Cross-Modal Pretraining for Brain Image Analysis with Arbitrary Modality Availability
summary: Clinical diagnostic workups typically follow a modality escalation pathway: after initial clinical evaluation, clinicians begin with routine structural imaging (e.g., MRI), selectively add sequences such as FLAIR or T2 to refine the differential, and reserve molecular imaging (e.g., amyloid-PET) for cases that remain uncertain after standard evaluation. Consequently, patients are observed with heterogeneous and often incomplete modality subsets. However, most current AI models assume fixed data modalities as the model inputs. In this paper, we present BrainAnytime, a unified pretraining framework pretrained on 34,899 3D brain scans from five datasets that support brain image analysis under arbitrary modality availability spanning multi-sequence MRI and amyloid-PET. A single model accepts whatever imaging is available, from a lone T1 scan to a full multimodal workup. Pretraining learns structural-molecular correspondences between MRI and PET via cross-modal distillation (RCMD) and prioritizes disease-vulnerable anatomy via atlas-guided curriculum masking (PACM), all within a shared 3D masked autoencoder (Multi-MAE3D). Across four downstream tasks and five clinically motivated modality settings, BrainAnytime largely outperforms modality-specific models, missing-modality baselines, and large-scale brain MRI pretrained foundation models on most modality settings. Notably, it surpasses the strongest missing-modality baselines with relative improvements of 6.2% and 7.0% in average accuracy on CN vs. AD and CN vs. MCI classification, respectively. Code is available at https://github.com/SDH-Lab/BrainAnytime.

AI.tldr: BrainAnytime is a unified pretraining framework enabling brain image analysis with arbitrary modality availability (multi-sequence MRI and amyloid-PET) using a 3D masked autoencoder, cross-modal distillation, and atlas-guided curriculum masking; outperforms modality-specific and missing-modality baselines across tasks.
AI.motivation: Clinical diagnostic workups follow a modality escalation pathway and many AI models assume fixed modalities. A robust model that can operate with partial or missing modalities would improve practicality and performance.
AI.method: Pretrain on 34,899 3D brain scans from five datasets using a shared 3D masked autoencoder (Multi-MAE3D). Introduce cross-modal distillation (RCMD) to learn MRI-PET correspondences and atlas-guided curriculum masking (PACM) to emphasize disease-vulnerable anatomy, enabling arbitrary modality input (from single T1 to full multimodal). Evaluate across four downstream tasks and five modality settings.
AI.result: BrainAnytime largely outperforms modality-specific models, missing-modality baselines, and large-scale brain MRI pretrained models on most settings. Notable gains: relative improvements of 6.2% (CN vs AD) and 7.0% (CN vs MCI) in average accuracy. Code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates strong robustness to incomplete modality inputs and broad generalization across modality configurations, suggesting practical benefits for clinical deployment and potential for cross-modality pretraining benefits.

title: Bayesian Model Merging
summary: Model merging aims to combine multiple task-specific expert models into a single model without joint retraining, offering a practical alternative to multi-task learning when data access or computational budget is limited. Existing methods, however, face two key limitations: (1) they overlook the valuable inductive bias of strong anchor models and estimate the merged weights from scratch, and (2) they rely on a shared hyperparameter setting across different modules of the network, lacking a global optimization strategy. This paper introduces Bayesian Model Merging (BMM), a plug-and-play bi-level optimization framework, where the inner level formulates the model merging as an activation-based Bayesian regression under a strong prior induced by an anchor model, yielding an efficient closed-form solution; and the outer level leverages a Bayesian optimization procedure to search module-specific hyperparameters globally based on a small validation set. Furthermore, we reveal a key alignment between activation statistics and task vectors, enabling us to derive a data-free variant of BMM that estimates the Gram matrix for regression without any auxiliary data. Across extensive benchmarks, including up to 20-task merging in vision and 5-task merging in language, BMM consistently outperforms all plug-and-play anchor baselines (e.g., TA, WUDI-Merging, and TSV). In particular, on the ViT-L/14 benchmark for 8-task merging, a single merged model reaches 95.1, closely matching the average performance of eight task-specific experts (95.8).

AI.tldr: Bayesian Model Merging (BMM) is a plug-and-play bi-level framework that merges task-specific experts using an anchor prior and activation-based Bayesian regression, with a global hyperparameter optimization step; it has a data-free variant and strong empirical results across vision and language benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Overcome two main limitations in existing model merging: (i) neglecting the anchor model's inductive bias and estimating merged weights from scratch, (ii) relying on a fixed, shared hyperparameter setting across modules without a global optimization strategy.
AI.method: Inner level: activation-based Bayesian regression with a strong prior from an anchor model, yielding a closed-form solution. Outer level: Bayesian optimization to search module-specific hyperparameters using a small validation set. Data-free variant: aligns activation statistics with task vectors to estimate the Gram matrix for regression without data.
AI.result: BMM consistently outperforms plug-and-play anchor baselines (TA, WUDI-Merging, TSV) across up to 20-task vision and 5-task language benchmarks. On ViT-L/14 for 8-task merging, a single merged model achieves 95.1, close to the 95.8 average of eight task-specific experts.
AI.conclusion: BMM provides an effective, anchor-informed merging framework with a global hyperparameter optimization strategy and a data-free variant, enabling strong multi-task performance with limited data and computation.

title: FrameSkip: Learning from Fewer but More Informative Frames in VLA Training
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies are commonly trained from dense robot demonstration trajectories, often collected through teleoperation, by sampling every recorded frame as if it provided equally useful supervision. We argue that this convention creates a temporal supervision imbalance: long low-change segments dominate the training stream, while manipulation-critical transitions such as alignment, contact, grasping, and release appear only sparsely. We introduce FrameSkip, a data-layer frame selection framework that scores trajectory frames using action variation, visual-action coherence, task-progress priors, and gripper-transition preservation, then remaps training samples toward high-importance frames under a target retention ratio. Because FrameSkip operates only in the dataloader, it leaves the VLA architecture, action head, training objective, and inference procedure unchanged. Across RoboCasa-GR1, SimplerEnv, and LIBERO, FrameSkip improves the success-retention trade-off over full-frame training and simpler frame selection variants, achieving a macro-average success rate of 76.15% across the three benchmarks compared with 66.50% for full-frame training while using a compressed trajectory view that retains 20% of unique frames in the main setting.

AI.tldr: FrameSkip prioritizes high-importance frames during VLA training by scoring frames on action variation, visual-action coherence, task progress priors, and gripper transitions, enabling a smaller retained frame set without changing model architecture; improves success-retention trade-off.
AI.motivation: Dense demonstrations produce temporal supervision imbalance: long low-change intervals dominate training while critical manipulation events are sparse. Reducing redundant frames without losing essential signals can improve learning efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Data-layer frame scoring and selective sampling: compute per-frame scores from action variation, visual-action coherence, task-progress priors, and gripper-transition preservation; remap training samples toward high-importance frames under a target retention ratio. FrameSkip operates solely in the dataloader, leaving architecture, loss, and inference unchanged.
AI.result: On RoboCasa-GR1, SimplerEnv, and LIBERO, FrameSkip achieves a macro-average success rate of 76.15% compared with 66.50% for full-frame training, while retaining only 20% of unique frames.
AI.conclusion: FrameSkip improves the success-retention trade-off for Vision-Language-Action policies by intelligent frame selection without altering model design, suggesting broad applicability to VLA training pipelines.

title: Position: Assistive Agents Need Accessibility Alignment
summary: Assistive agents for Blind and Visually Impaired (BVI) users require accessibility alignment as a first-class design objective. Despite rapid progress in agentic AI, most systems are designed and evaluated under assumptions of sighted interaction, low-cost verification, and tolerable trial-and-error, leading to systematic failures in assistive scenarios that cannot be resolved by model scaling or post-hoc interface adaptations alone. Drawing on an analysis of 778 assistance task instances from prior work, we show that current agentic AI remain prone to failure in assistive scenarios due to mismatches between sighted-user design assumptions and the verification, risk, and interaction constraints faced by BVI users. We argue that accessibility should be treated as an alignment problem rather than a peripheral usability concern. To this end, we introduce accessibility alignment and propose a lifecycle-oriented design pipeline for accessibility-aligned assistive agents, spanning user research, system design, deployment and post-deployment iteration. We conclude that BVI-centered assistive tasks provide a critical stress test for agentic AI and motivate a broader shift toward inclusive agent design.

AI.tldr: Accessibility alignment is essential for assistive agents; sighted-user assumptions cause failures; propose a lifecycle pipeline to integrate accessibility early; BVI tasks serve as stress tests for agentic AI.
AI.motivation: To shift accessibility from peripheral usability to core alignment, addressing fundamental mismatches between how assistant agents are designed/tested and the needs/constraints of blind and visually impaired users.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of 778 assistance task instances from prior work; followed by a design proposal: a lifecycle-oriented pipeline for accessibility-aligned assistive agents including user research, system design, deployment, and post-deployment iteration.
AI.result: Evidence that current agentic AI exhibit mismatches with BVI constraints, leading to failures in assistive tasks; introduction of an accessibility-aligned framework and lifecycle approach.
AI.conclusion: BVI-centered assistive tasks function as robust stress tests for agentic AI and support a broader shift toward inclusive agent design; accessibility should be treated as an alignment problem.

title: Edit-Compass & EditReward-Compass: A Unified Benchmark for Image Editing and Reward Modeling
summary: Recent image editing models have achieved remarkable progress in instruction following, multimodal understanding, and complex visual editing. However, existing benchmarks often fail to faithfully reflect human judgment, especially for strong frontier models, due to limited task difficulty and coarse-grained evaluation protocols. In parallel, reward models have become increasingly important for RL-based image editing optimization, yet existing reward model benchmarks still rely on unrealistic evaluation settings that deviate from practical RL scenarios. These limitations hinder reliable assessment of both image editing models and reward models. To address these challenges, we introduce Edit-Compass and EditReward-Compass, a unified evaluation suite for image editing and reward modeling. Edit-Compass contains 2,388 carefully annotated instances spanning six progressively challenging task categories, covering capabilities such as world knowledge reasoning, visual reasoning, and multi-image editing. Beyond broad task coverage, Edit-Compass adopts a fine-grained multidimensional evaluation framework based on structured reasoning and carefully designed scoring rubrics. In parallel, EditReward-Compass contains 2,251 preference pairs that simulate realistic reward modeling scenarios during RL optimization.

AI.tldr: Two unified evaluation suites (Edit-Compass and EditReward-Compass) with large, diverse datasets and fine-grained scoring to benchmark image editing and RL reward modeling realistically.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks underrepresent task difficulty and rely on coarse evaluation, failing to reflect human judgment for strong editors and using unrealistic reward-model setups. A more challenging, multilayered, and RL-aligned evaluation is needed.
AI.method: Edit-Compass: 2,388 annotated instances across six progressively challenging task categories, including world knowledge reasoning, visual reasoning, and multi-image editing, with structured reasoning and detailed scoring rubrics. EditReward-Compass: 2,251 preference pairs simulating realistic reward-modeling scenarios during RL optimization.
AI.result: Provides broad task coverage and a fine-grained, multidimensional evaluation framework, enabling more reliable benchmarking of both image editing models and reward models in settings closer to practical RL.
AI.conclusion: The suites offer robust benchmarks to drive progress and more faithful assessment of capabilities and rewards in image editing research.

title: Multitask Multimodal Fusion with Tabular Foundation Models for Peak and Durability Prediction of Pertussis Booster Response
comment: 22 pages, 8 figures, 4 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Divya1205/cmi-pb-multitask
summary: Pertussis booster vaccination produces immune responses that vary widely across individuals in both peak magnitude and long-term durability. These two phases are governed by partly distinct biological compartments:peak reflects acute B-cell activation and antibody secretion, while durability reflects the establishment of long-term humoral memory. Yet most computational models target only one, missing the full boost-and-wane trajectory. Jointly predicting both is non-trivial because the two endpoints are biologically dissociated rather than redundant; samples are small, modalities are heterogeneous with structured missingness, and the two tasks rely on different measurement windows. We propose a multi-task contrastive multimodal fusion architecture combining frozen TabPFN-v2 per-modality encoders, a dual-label supervised contrastive loss that treats two subjects as a positive pair if they agree on the Task 1 label or the Task 2 label, modality dropout calibrated to empirical missingness, and missingness-masked attention fusion. Applied to a curated subset of the CMI-PB pertussis booster dataset (n = 158 subjects, four modalities, 44.9% with at least one modality missing; Spearman r = -0.58 between peak and durability, n = 96), the model achieves test AUROC 0.797 (95% CI [0.621, 0.948]) for peak response and 0.755 (95% CI [0.519, 0.945]) for durability, with both significant under joint label permutation (N = 1000; p = 0.002 and p = 0.045). Across logistic regression, XGBoost, and MLP baselines on raw features and on TabPFN embeddings, the proposed model is the only one whose 95% CIs lie above chance on both tasks simultaneously. Per-modality contribution analyses recover task-specific modality contributions consistent with the underlying immunology: peak prediction is carried by cytokine signatures, while durability is carried by baseline antibody features.

AI.tldr: A multi-task contrastive multimodal fusion model predicts both peak immune response and long-term durability after pertussis booster, handling small sample size and structured missingness; achieves cross-task improvement and per-modality interpretability.
AI.motivation: Biological endpoints peak and durability are dissociated; existing models typically target a single endpoint. A joint model is needed to capture the full booster response trajectory under multimodal, partially missing data.
AI.method: A multi-task framework using frozen TabPFN-v2 encoders per modality; a dual-label supervised contrastive loss where pairs are positive if they share Task 1 or Task 2 label; modality dropout aligned to empirical missingness; missingness-masked attention fusion. Dataset: CMI-PB pertussis booster subset (n=158, four modalities, 44.9% with at least one modality missing). Evaluation: test AUROC for peak and durability; permutation tests for joint significance; baselines include logistic regression, XGBoost, MLP on raw features and on TabPFN embeddings; per-modality contribution analysis.
AI.result: Test AUROC: peak 0.797 (95% CI 0.621–0.948); durability 0.755 (95% CI 0.519–0.945). Both tasks significantly above chance under joint label permutation (p=0.002 for peak, p=0.045 for durability). Across baselines, the proposed model uniquely has 95% CIs above chance on both tasks. Per-modality analysis shows peak driven by cytokine signatures; durability by baseline antibody features.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-task multimodal fusion approach effectively models the heal-and-wane trajectory of vaccine response, handles missing modalities, and yields interpretable modality-level insights aligned with immunology; promising for joint modeling in vaccinology, though limited by small sample and wide CIs.

title: RoboEvolve: Co-Evolving Planner-Simulator for Robotic Manipulation with Limited Data
summary: The scalability of robotic manipulation is fundamentally bottlenecked by the scarcity of task-aligned physical interaction data. While vision-language models (VLMs) and video generation models (VGMs) hold promise for autonomous data synthesis, they suffer from semantic-spatial misalignment and physical hallucinations, respectively. To bridge this gap, we introduce RoboEvolve, a novel framework that couples a VLM planner and a VGM simulator into a mutually reinforcing co-evolutionary loop. Operating purely on unlabeled seed images, RoboEvolve leverages a cognitive-inspired dual-phase mechanism: (i) daytime exploration fosters physically grounded behavioral discovery through a semantic-controlled multi-granular reward, and (ii) nighttime consolidation mines "near-miss" failures to stabilize policy optimization. Guided by an autonomous progressive curriculum, the system naturally scales from simple atomic actions to complex tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RoboEvolve (I) achieves superior effectiveness, elevating base planners by 30 absolute points and amplifying simulator success by 48% on average; (II) exhibits extreme data efficiency, surpassing fully supervised baselines with merely 500 unlabeled seeds--a 50x reduction; and (III) demonstrates robust continual learning without catastrophic forgetting.

AI.tldr: RoboEvolve couples a VLM planner and a VGM simulator in a co-evolutionary loop to generate task-aligned data from unlabeled seeds, with daytime exploration and nighttime consolidation, achieving data efficiency and continual learning.
AI.motivation: Address scarcity of task-aligned physical interaction data for scalable robotic manipulation; existing VLMs and VGMs suffer from semantic misalignment and physical hallucinations.
AI.method: Mutually reinforcing loop between VLM planner and VGM simulator. Daytime exploration with semantic-controlled multi-granular reward to discover physically grounded behaviors; nighttime consolidation of near-miss failures to stabilize policy optimization; progressive autonomous curriculum from simple to complex tasks.
AI.result: Improved performance: base planners +30 absolute points; simulator success +48% average; data efficiency: effective with 500 unlabeled seeds (≈50x reduction vs fully supervised); robust continual learning without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: RoboEvolve offers a scalable, data-efficient, continual-learning framework for robotic manipulation data synthesis, mitigating semantic-spatial misalignment and physical hallucinations via co-evolution of planning and simulation.

title: Unweighted ranking for value-based decision making with uncertainty
summary: As intelligent systems are increasingly implemented in our society to make autonomous decisions, their commitment to human values raises serious concerns. Their alignment with human values remains a critical challenge because it can jeopardise the integrity and security of citizens. For this reason, an innovative human-centred and values-driven approach to decision making is required. In this work, we introduce the Fuzzy-Unweighted Value-Based Decision Making (FUW-VBDM) framework, where agents incorporate both quantitative and qualitative criteria to generate human-centred decisions. We also address the normative bias introduced by stakeholders with arbitrary weights by removing prior weights and introducing a fuzzy domain of decision variables defined for a score function. This concept allows us to generalise any VBDM problem as the search for feasible solutions when optimising the score in the weight domain. To provide a solution to FUW-VBDM, we present Rankzzy, a customizable unweighted ranking method that integrates fuzzy-based reasoning to quantify uncertainty. We mathematically prove the consistency of the Rankzzy for any admissible configuration selected by stakeholders. We show the applicability of our method through an illustrative case study, which we also use as a running example. The evaluation conducted indicates a reduced computational cost in large-scale value-based decision-making problems and a strong rank performance regarding existing approaches when employing the aggregation via Pythagorean means.

AI.tldr: FUW-VBDM introduces an unweighted, fuzzy, value-based decision-making framework (FUW-VBDM) to mitigate normative bias from stakeholder weights, via a fuzzy decision-variable domain and a Rankzzy ranking method with fuzzy reasoning. It includes a consistency proof for Rankzzy, a case study, and shows reduced computational cost and strong ranking performance with Pythagorean-mean aggregation compared to existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Align autonomous decision-making with human values while mitigating normative bias from arbitrary weights; enable scalable, human-centered value-based decisions with reduced computational cost.
AI.method: Propose FUW-VBDM framework that removes prior weights and defines a fuzzy domain of decision variables for a score function; introduce Rankzzy, a customizable unweighted ranking method with fuzzy reasoning; provide mathematical consistency proof for Rankzzy across admissible stakeholder configurations; illustrate with a running case study.
AI.result: Demonstrates reduced computational cost in large-scale VBDM and strong ranking performance versus existing methods when using Pythagorean means for aggregation.
AI.conclusion: FUW-VBDM with Rankzzy offers a principled, unweighted, fuzzy approach to value-based decision making, showing consistency guarantees, efficiency gains, and robust ranking performance in case studies.

title: HarmoGS: Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting in the Wild via Conflict-Aware Gradient Harmonization
summary: In-the-wild 3D Gaussian Splatting remains challenging due to transient distractors and illumination-induced cross-view appearance inconsistencies. Existing methods mainly rely on image-level masking to suppress unreliable supervision, but masking alone cannot fully eliminate residual occlusions or resolve illumination-induced inconsistencies, both of which can introduce conflicting cross-view gradients. These unresolved conflicts may destabilize Gaussian optimization and lead to visible reconstruction artifacts. We propose a conflict-aware 3DGS framework that addresses this problem from both image-space supervision and gradient-level optimization. Semantic Consistency-Guided Masking learns pixel-wise consistency scores to adaptively refine prior masks and suppress unreliable supervision before gradient formation. A dual-view Conflict-Aware Gradient Harmonization strategy further reconciles view-specific gradients by mutually rotating them into an orthogonal configuration, reducing negative directional interference across views. We also introduce conflict-aware densification and pruning to stabilize Gaussian growth and remove persistently conflicting primitives. Extensive experiments on standard in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality under complex transient distractors and cross-view inconsistencies.

AI.tldr: A conflict-aware 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) framework addresses transient distractors and illumination-induced cross-view inconsistencies by (1) semantic consistency-guided masking to refine supervision before gradient formation, and (2) dual-view gradient harmonization that orthogonally rotates gradients to reduce inter-view interference, plus conflict-aware densification/pruning. It achieves state-of-the-art rendering under complex in-the-wild conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing image-level masking cannot eliminate residual occlusions or resolve illumination-induced cross-view gradient conflicts during Gaussian optimization, leading to unstable updates and artifacts in in-the-wild 3DGS.
AI.method: A two-part approach: (1) Semantic Consistency-Guided Masking that learns per-pixel consistency scores to adaptively refine masks and suppress unreliable supervision prior to gradient formation. (2) Conflict-Aware Gradient Harmonization that rotates multi-view gradients into an orthogonal configuration to mitigate negative directional interference; plus conflict-aware densification and pruning to stabilize Gaussian growth and remove persistently conflicting primitives.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard in-the-wild benchmarks show state-of-the-art rendering quality under complex transient distractors and cross-view inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively mitigates both image-space supervision issues and gradient-level optimization conflicts, enabling more robust and visually faithful 3D Gaussian splatting in challenging wild scenes.

title: Realtime-VLA FLASH: Speculative Inference Framework for Diffusion-based VLAs
summary: Diffusion-based vision-language-action models (dVLAs) are promising for embodied intelligence but are fundamentally limited in real-time deployment by the high latency of full inference. We propose Realtime-VLA FLASH, a speculative inference framework that eliminates most full inference calls during replanning by introducing a lightweight draft model with parallel verification via the main model's Action Expert and a phase-aware fallback mechanism that reverts to the full inference pipeline when needed. This design enables low-latency, high-frequency replanning without sacrificing reliability. Experiments show that on LIBERO, FLASH largely preserves task performance by replacing many 58.0 ms full-inference rounds with speculative rounds as fast as 7.8 ms, lowering task-level average inference latency to 19.1 ms (3.04x speedup). We additionally demonstrate effectiveness on real-world conveyor-belt sorting, highlighting its practical impact for latency-critical embodied tasks.

AI.tldr: Realtime-VLA FLASH enables speculative inference to cut latency in diffusion-based vision-language-action models by using a lightweight draft model with parallel verification and a phase-aware fallback, achieving ~3x speedups (7.8 ms speculative rounds vs 58 ms full-inference rounds) and 19.1 ms task latency on LIBERO, with real-world conveyor-belt validation.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents require high-frequency, low-latency replanning. Full-inference rounds in diffusion-based VLA systems create a bottleneck; a speculative, verifiable approach can reduce latency without sacrificing reliability.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight draft model for speculative inference, enable parallel verification by the main model's Action Expert, and implement a phase-aware fallback to revert to full inference when needed. This minimizes full-inference calls while preserving correctness through verification and fallback mechanisms.
AI.result: On LIBERO, speculative rounds replace most 58.0 ms full-inference rounds, achieving speculative latencies as fast as 7.8 ms and reducing task-level average latency to 19.1 ms (3.04x speedup). Demonstrated effectiveness on a real-world conveyor-belt sorting task, showing practical impact in latency-critical embodied tasks.
AI.conclusion: FLASH provides low-latency, high-frequency replanning for diffusion-based VLA systems with maintained reliability through verification and controlled fallback, enabling practical deployment in latency-sensitive embodied applications.

title: How to Interpret Agent Behavior
summary: Autonomous agents such as Claude Code and Codex now operate for hours or even days. Understanding their runtime behavior has become critical for downstream tasks such as diagnosing inefficiencies, fixing bugs, and ensuring better oversight. A primary way to gain this understanding is analyzing the reasoning trajectories and execution traces these agents generate. Yet such data remains in unstructured natural-language form, making it difficult for humans to interpret at scale. We introduce ACT*ONOMY (a combination of Action and Taxonomy), a taxonomy for describing and analyzing agent behavior at runtime. ACT*ONOMY has two components: (1) the taxonomy itself, developed through Grounded Theory and structured as a three-level hierarchy of 10 actions, 46 subactions, and 120 leaf categories; and (2) an open repository that hosts the living taxonomy, provides an automated analysis pipeline that applies it to agent trajectories analysis, and defines an extension protocol for customization and growth. Our experiments show that ACTONOMY can compare behavioral profiles across agents and characterize a single agent's behavior across diverse trajectories, surfacing patterns indicative of failure modes. By providing a shared vocabulary, ACT*ONOMY helps researchers, agent designers, and end users interpret agent behavior more consistently, enabling better oversight and control.

AI.tldr: ACT*ONOMY provides a runtime-behavior taxonomy for autonomous agents and an open repository with an automated analysis pipeline; it enables cross-agent behavioral profiling and detection of failure patterns.
AI.motivation: To understand and oversee long-running agents by analyzing their reasoning trajectories and execution traces, which are currently unstructured, enabling diagnosis of inefficiencies and bugs.
AI.method: Developed via Grounded Theory into a three-level taxonomy (10 actions, 46 subactions, 120 leaf categories). Created an open repository hosting the taxonomy, an automated analysis pipeline, and a protocol for extensions and customization.
AI.result: The taxonomy allows comparison of behavioral profiles across agents and characterization of individual agent behavior over diverse trajectories, surfacing patterns that indicate failure modes.
AI.conclusion: ACT*ONOMY provides a shared vocabulary for consistent interpretation of agent behavior, aiding oversight, control, and collaborative analysis among researchers, designers, and end users.

title: Learning to See What You Need: Gaze Attention for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: When humans describe a visual scene, they do not process the entire image uniformly; instead, they selectively fixate on regions relevant to their intended description. In contrast, current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) attend to all visual tokens at each generation step, leading to diluted focus and unnecessary computational overhead. In this work, we introduce Gaze Attention, a novel mechanism that enables MLLMs to selectively attend to task-relevant visual regions during generation. Specifically, we spatially group visual embeddings-stored as key-value caches-into compact gaze regions, each represented by a lightweight descriptor. At each decoding step, the model dynamically selects the most relevant regions and restricts attention to them, reducing redundant computation while enhancing focus. To mitigate the loss of global context caused by localized attention, we further propose learnable context tokens appended to each image or frame, allowing the model to maintain holistic visual awareness. Extensive experiments on image and video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Gaze Attention matches or surpasses dense-attention baselines, while using up to 90% fewer visual KV entries in the attention computation.

AI.tldr: Gaze Attention enables multimodal LLMs to attend only to task-relevant visual regions via compact gaze-region descriptors, reducing computation by up to 90% while preserving or improving performance.
AI.motivation: Humans describe scenes by fixating on relevant regions, whereas current MLLMs attend to all visual tokens, causing wasted computation and diluted focus.
AI.method: Visual embeddings are grouped into compact gaze regions stored as key-value caches. At each decoding step, the model dynamically selects relevant regions and restricts attention to them. Learnable context tokens are appended to each image/frame to retain global visual awareness despite localized attention.
AI.result: Experiments on image and video understanding benchmarks show Gaze Attention matches or surpasses dense-attention baselines while using up to 90% fewer visual KV entries in the attention computation.
AI.conclusion: Gaze Attention provides a more efficient and focused attention mechanism for multimodal generation, achieving similar or better performance with substantially reduced visual-token computations while preserving holistic context.

title: Descriptive Collision in Sparse Autoencoder Auto-Interpretability: When One Explanation Describes Many Features
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are now standard tools for decomposing language model activations into interpretable features, and automated interpretability pipelines routinely assign each feature a short natural-language explanation. Existing critiques of this practice focus on polysemanticity -- one feature with many meanings -- or on whether explanations predict activations. We identify a complementary, structurally distinct problem we call descriptive collision: many distinct SAE features admit the same explanation. Reanalyzing the largest publicly-available dataset of human-annotated SAE features (Marks et al., 2025), comprising 722 annotated features across Gemma 2 2B and Pythia 70M, we find that the mean annotation string is reused across 3.07 features; 82.1% of features share their annotation with at least one other feature; and the single most common annotation string ("plural nouns") labels 101 distinct features spanning 18 layers and four model components. Information-theoretically, the average annotation resolves only 70% of feature identity. We formalize a property called discrimination, prove that current detection-style auto-interpretability scoring is invariant to collision, and propose two complementary corrective metrics -- collision-adjusted detection and discrimination scoring -- that explicitly penalize explanations that fail to distinguish a feature from its neighbors. The collision problem is independent of, and additive with, previously identified failure modes of auto-interpretability; ignoring it inflates reported feature interpretability by a quantity equal to roughly one-third of the bits required to identify a feature.

AI.tldr: Descriptive collision in SAE interpretability: many distinct features share the same explanations; introduces discrimination and collision-adjusted metrics; collision inflates interpretability claims.
AI.motivation: Identify a complementary failure mode of auto-interpretability beyond polysemanticity by examining whether distinct SAE features can share identical explanations, and quantify its impact on interpretability assessments.
AI.method: Reanalyze the Marks et al. (2025) dataset (722 features across Gemma 2 2B and Pythia 70M); quantify annotation reuse; formalize 'discrimination'; prove invariance of detection-style scoring to collision; propose collision-adjusted detection and discrimination scoring metrics.
AI.result: Observed high descriptive collision: mean annotation reused across 3.07 features; 82.1% of features share an annotation with at least one other; the most common string ('plural nouns') labels 101 features across 18 layers and four model components; information-theoretic annotation resolves only 70% of feature identity; collision-adjusted metrics proposed; inflation of interpretability by ~one-third of bits.
AI.conclusion: Descriptive collision is an independent/additive failure mode for auto-interpretability; ignoring it overstates interpretability; recommended adoption of discrimination-aware metrics to accurately assess feature explainability.

title: LMPath: Language-Mediated Priors and Path Generation for Aerial Exploration
comment: Poster at 2026 AI-Driven Safe Aerial Robotics Workshop
summary: Traditional autonomous UAV search missions rely on geometric coverage patterns that ignore the semantic context of the target, leading to significant time waste in large-scale environments. In this paper we present LMPath, a pipeline for generating language-mediated exploration priors for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) search missions that leverages semantics. Given a basic geofence and an object of interest prompt, LMPath uses generative language models to determine what regions of the environment should contain that object and a foundation vision model ran over satellite imagery to segment sub-regions that form the exploration prior. This prior can then be used to generate UAV paths with various objectives, such as minimizing the expected time to locate the object of interest, maximizing the probability that the object is found given a limited travel distance, or narrowing down the search space to sub-regions that are most likely to contain the object. To demonstrate it's capabilities, we used LMPath to generate various UAV paths and ran them using a real UAV over large-scale environments. We also ran simulations to demonstrate how paths generated using LMPath outperform traditional path planning approaches for search missions.

AI.tldr: LMPath leverages language models and foundation vision models to create semantic exploration priors for UAV search, guiding path planning to improve efficiency over geometric patterns.
AI.motivation: Reduce time and energy waste in large-scale UAV search by incorporating semantic context about likely object locations.
AI.method: Given a geofence and object prompt, use LMs to infer regions containing the object; apply a foundation vision model to satellite imagery to segment sub-regions; build exploration priors; generate UAV paths optimizing objectives (minimize time to locate, maximize probability given distance, etc.); validate via real UAV flights and simulations.
AI.result: LMPath-generated paths outperform traditional planning in simulations; successful real UAV demonstrations in large environments.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-driven priors based on language and vision can enhance UAV search efficiency and flexibility across objectives; approach demonstrates practicality and potential for real-world deployment.

title: Adaptive mine planning under geological uncertainty: A POMDP framework for sequential decision-making
summary: Strategic mine production scheduling under geological uncertainty is conventionally formulated as a stochastic optimization problem in which a fixed extraction sequence and routing decisions are computed ex ante. This plan-driven paradigm treats uncertainty as passive: decisions are hedged across geological scenarios, but planning does not anticipate how future observations will inform future decisions. We propose a different perspective by formulating mine scheduling as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), in which extraction and routing decisions are made sequentially with planning explicitly integrating the expectation of future belief updates. To achieve computational tractability, we introduce a hybrid SA-POMDP architecture that combines simulated annealing-based (SA) value approximation with ensemble-based belief updating via ensemble smoother with multiple data assimilation (ES-MDA). At each decision epoch, candidate actions are evaluated through their expected long-term value under the current belief, and the belief is updated as mining observations are assimilated. This yields an adaptive policy rather than a fixed plan. We evaluate the framework on a copper-gold open-pit mining complex with multiple processing destinations. Under a statistically consistent prior, the SA-POMDP reduces the expectation-reality gap from 22.3% to 4.6%, improving realized NPV by USD8.4M relative to one-shot stochastic optimization. Under systematic prior misspecification of 10%, the adaptive framework outperforms static planning by up to USD44.6M (36.9%), demonstrating structural robustness beyond scenario hedging. These results show that sequential belief updating transforms geological uncertainty from a passive constraint into an active component of value creation.

AI.tldr: POMDP-based adaptive mine scheduling with a SA-POMDP hybrid and ES-MDA belief updating significantly improves NPVs and robustness versus fixed-plan stochastic optimization.
AI.motivation: Conventional plan-driven mine scheduling hedges uncertainty but does not use information from future observations. A sequential, belief-updating decision framework could transform uncertainty into an active, value-generating component.
AI.method: Formulate mining scheduling as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). Use a hybrid SA-POMDP architecture combining simulated annealing–based value approximation with ensemble smoother using multiple data assimilation (ES-MDA) for belief updates. At each epoch, evaluate candidate actions by their expected long-term value under the current belief and update beliefs as mining observations are assimilated.
AI.result: With a consistent prior, the approach reduces the expectation-reality gap from 22.3% to 4.6% and increases realized NPV by USD 8.4M versus one-shot stochastic optimization. Under 10% prior misspecification, it outperforms static planning by up to USD 44.6M (36.9%), showing robustness beyond hedging.
AI.conclusion: Sequential belief updating reframes geological uncertainty as an active contributor to value creation and yields adaptive policies with superior economic performance and robustness compared with static planning.

title: PRA-PoE: Robust Alzheimer's Diagnosis with Arbitrary Missing Modalities
summary: Missing modalities are prevalent in real-world Alzheimer's disease (AD) assessment and pose a significant challenge to multimodal learning, particularly when the distribution of observed modality subsets differs between training and deployment. Such missingness pattern mismatch induces a conditional representation shift across modality subsets. Existing approaches that rely on implicit imputation or modality synthesis often fail to explicitly model modality availability and uncertainty, leading to overconfident dependence on synthesized features, reduced robustness, and miscalibrated uncertainty estimates. To address these limitations, we propose PRA-PoE, an incomplete multimodal learning framework that is equipped with Prototype-anchored Representation Alignment (PRA) and an Uncertainty-aware Product of Experts (UA-PoE) fusion mechanism. First, PRA uses learnable global prototypes and availability-conditioned tokens to encode modality availability, distinguish observed from missing modalities, re-synthesize features for missing modalities, and adaptively refine observed representations to align latent spaces across modality subsets, with the goal of reducing representation shift under varying missingness patterns. Second, UA-PoE models each modality as a Gaussian expert and performs closed-form Product of Experts fusion, where experts with higher uncertainty are automatically down-weighted via lower precision, improving uncertainty reliability. We evaluate PRA-PoE under a clinically realistic protocol by training with naturally missing data and testing on all non-empty modality combinations. PRA-PoE consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art across datasets, achieving a 5.4% relative improvement in average accuracy on ADNI and a 10.9% relative gain in average F1 on OASIS-3 over the strongest baseline across all non-empty modality subsets.

AI.tldr: PRA-PoE is a novel incomplete multimodal learning framework for Alzheimer's disease assessment that explicitly handles missing modalities via Prototype-anchored Representation Alignment and an Uncertainty-aware Product of Experts, yielding stronger accuracy and F1 than state-of-the-art under realistic missingness protocols.
AI.motivation: Missing data and differing observed modality subsets between training and deployment create representation shifts and uncertain predictions. Existing implicit imputation approaches may over-rely on synthesized features, leading to overconfidence and poor uncertainty calibration.
AI.method: Prototype-anchored Representation Alignment (PRA): learnable global prototypes plus availability-conditioned tokens to encode modality availability, distinguish observed vs missing modalities, re-synthesize missing features, and align latent spaces across modality subsets. Uncertainty-aware Product of Experts (UA-PoE): model each modality as a Gaussian expert; closed-form PoE fusion with higher-uncertainty experts down-weighted via lower precision to improve uncertainty reliability.
AI.result: Evaluated under a clinically realistic protocol (naturally missing data during training; testing on all non-empty modality subsets). PRA-PoE outperforms state-of-the-art across datasets, with 5.4% relative improvement in average accuracy on ADNI and 10.9% relative gain in average F1 on OASIS-3 across all non-empty modality subsets.
AI.conclusion: PRA-PoE provides robust handling of incomplete multimodal data with improved predictive performance and better calibrated uncertainty, demonstrating practical promise for AD assessment and other domains with missing modalities.

title: Certified Robustness under Heterogeneous Perturbations via Hybrid Randomized Smoothing
summary: Randomized smoothing provides strong, model-agnostic robustness certificates, but existing guarantees are limited to single modalities, treating continuous and discrete inputs in isolation. This limitation becomes critical in multimodal models, where decisions depend on cross-modal semantics and adversaries can jointly perturb heterogeneous inputs, rendering unimodal certificates insufficient. We introduce a unified randomized smoothing framework for mixed discrete--continuous inputs based on an analytically tractable Neyman--Pearson formulation of the joint worst-case problem. By analyzing the joint likelihood ordering induced by factorized discrete and continuous noise, our approach yields a closed-form, one-dimensional certificate that strictly generalizes both Gaussian (image-only) and discrete (text-only) randomized smoothing. We validate the framework on multimodal safety filtering, providing, to our knowledge, the first model-agnostic Neyman--Pearson certificate for joint discrete-token and continuous-image perturbations in interaction-dependent text--image safety filtering.

AI.tldr: Unified randomized smoothing for mixed discrete-continuous inputs using a Neyman–Pearson formulation, producing a closed-form 1D robustness certificate that generalizes Gaussian (image-only) and discrete (text-only) smoothing, validated on multimodal safety filtering.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in model-agnostic robustness certificates for multimodal models where cross-modal perturbations can occur; unimodal certificates fail to capture joint adversarial perturbations across discrete tokens and continuous images.
AI.method: Formulate a Neyman–Pearson joint worst-case problem with factorized discrete and continuous noise. Derive likelihood ordering and obtain a tractable, one-dimensional certificate. The approach recovers Gaussian smoothing for images and discrete smoothing for text as special cases and extends to joint modalities.
AI.result: A closed-form, one-dimensional robustness certificate that applies to joint discrete-token and continuous-image perturbations. Claims the first model-agnostic Neyman–Pearson certificate for interaction-dependent text–image safety filtering.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, generalizable framework for multimodal robustness certification, unifying and extending existing smoothing guarantees and enabling safe deployment of multimodal systems under joint input perturbations.

title: Loiter UAV Reinsertion Guidance for Fixed-wing UAV Corridors
summary: This paper considers fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) corridors comprising a main lane, a circular loiter lane for managing traffic congestion, and transit lanes connecting the two. In particular, we address the problem of conflict-free reinsertion of UAVs from the loiter lane back into the main lane. The loiter lane contains a fixed number of equidistant virtual slots that UAVs can occupy. Reinsertion of loiter UAVs into the main lane becomes essential either due to reduced traffic in the main lane or due to a loiter UAV needing to reach its destination urgently. Given the total number of loiter slots, UAV speed limits, and the minimum safety distance, a guidance algorithm is developed to compute the required speed of a loiter UAV in the transit lane to ensure safe reinsertion. The proposed guidance and automation strategies are validated through numerical simulations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a fixed-wing UAV corridor framework with a main flight lane, a circular loiter lane with predefined virtual slots, and transit lanes for reentry. Develops a guidance algorithm to compute the loiter UAV’s speed in the transit lane to ensure conflict-free reinsertion into the main lane, given speed limits and safety distance, and validates the approach via numerical simulations.
AI.motivation: Improve safety and efficiency of UAV traffic management by enabling reliable, conflict-free reinsertion of loitering UAVs into the main traffic lane, leveraging a bounded loitering region and predefined slots to manage congestion.
AI.method: Define a corridor with a main lane, a circular loiter lane containing equidistant virtual slots. Develop a guidance algorithm that, using the total number of loiter slots, aircraft speed limits, and a minimum safety distance, computes the required speed of a loiter UAV in the transit lane to achieve safe reinsertion into the main lane. The approach is evaluated through numerical simulations to demonstrate feasibility and performance.
AI.result: The guidance algorithm enables safe reinsertion of loiter UAVs into the main lane and the automation strategies are validated through numerical simulations, demonstrating the feasibility of conflict-free reinsertion and the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a viable framework for autonomous management of fixed-wing UAV corridors with a loiter lane, allowing reentry into the main lane from loiter when conditions permit, and validates the proposed guidance and automation through simulations.

title: ScioMind: Cognitively Grounded Multi-Agent Social Simulation with Anchoring-Based Belief Dynamics and Dynamic Profiles
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent simulation offers a powerful testbed for studying social opinion dynamics. Yet current approaches often adopt two contrasting methods: either relying on fixed update rules with limited cognitive grounding or delegating belief change largely to unconstrained LLM interaction. We introduce ScioMind, a cognitively grounded simulation framework that bridges these paradigms by combining structured opinion dynamics with LLM-based agent reasoning. ScioMind integrates three key components: 1) a memory-anchored belief update rule that modulates susceptibility to influence via personality-conditioned anchoring strength; 2) a hierarchical memory architecture that supports persistent, experience-driven belief formation; and 3) dynamic agent profiles derived from a corpus-grounded retrieval pipeline, enabling heterogeneous personalities, rationales, and evolving internal states. We evaluate ScioMind on multiple case studies in a real-world policy debate scenario. Across metrics including polarisation, diversity, extremization, and trajectory stability, the proposed components consistently yield improvements in behavioural realism. In particular, dynamic profiles increase opinion diversity, memory and reflection reduce unstable oscillation, and anchoring induces persistent belief trajectories that better align with patterns reported in political psychology. These results suggest that our cognitively grounded design provides a novel solution to LLM-based social simulation that improves both stable and behavioural realism

AI.tldr: Cognitively grounded LLM-based social simulation (ScioMind) with memory-anchored belief updates, hierarchical memory, and corpus-grounded dynamic profiles; shows improved realism and stability in policy-debate scenarios.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between fixed-rule influence models and unconstrained LLM interactions by providing cognitive grounding for belief dynamics.
AI.method: Three components: (1) memory-anchored belief update with personality-conditioned anchoring strength; (2) hierarchical memory for persistent, experience-driven beliefs; (3) corpus-grounded retrieval to create heterogeneous, evolving agent profiles. Evaluated on real-world policy-debate case studies using metrics on polarization, diversity, extremization, and trajectory stability.
AI.result: Across metrics, components yield improvements in behavioural realism: dynamic profiles boost opinion diversity; memory/reflection reduces unstable oscillations; anchoring creates persistent belief trajectories that align with patterns in political psychology.
AI.conclusion: A cognitively grounded design for LLM-based social simulation improves both stability and behavioural realism, offering a novel approach that blends structured opinion dynamics with LLM reasoning.

title: RoSplat: Robust Feed-Forward Pixel-wise Gaussian Splatting for Varying Input Views and High-Resolution Rendering
summary: Generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting has recently emerged as an efficient approach for novel-view synthesis, enabling feed-forward synthesis from only a few input views. However, existing pixel-wise feed-forward methods suffer from over-bright renderings when the number of input views varies during inference, as well as insufficient supervision for accurate Gaussian scale estimation, which leads to hole artifacts, particularly in high-resolution renderings. To address these issues, we identify that the over-brightness is caused by the varying number of overlapping Gaussians and propose a simple alpha normalization strategy to maintain brightness consistency across different number of input views. In addition, we introduce an auxiliary 3D sampling-based regularizer to improve Gaussian scale estimation, thereby mitigating hole artifacts in high-resolution rendering. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method significantly improves baseline models under varying input-view and high-resolution rendering settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes alpha normalization to stabilize brightness across varying numbers of input views in generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting and adds a 3D sampling-based regularizer to improve Gaussian scale estimation, addressing over-brightness and hole artifacts in high-resolution rendering; shows improvements on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Over-bright renderings and hole artifacts in feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting arise when the number of input views varies and supervision for Gaussian scale is weak. There is a need for brightness consistency and more reliable scale estimation to improve high-resolution rendering.
AI.method: Introduce an alpha normalization strategy to keep brightness constant as the number of overlapping Gaussians changes with different input views. Add an auxiliary 3D sampling-based regularizer to enhance Gaussian scale estimation, reducing hole artifacts during high-resolution rendering. Integrate these into the feed-forward rendering pipeline for generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on benchmark datasets demonstrates significant improvements of the proposed method over baselines under varying input-view counts and high-resolution rendering conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach mitigates over-brightness and holes in high-resolution renderings, enhancing robustness and rendering quality of generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting across variable input views.

title: ASAP: Amortized Doubly-Stochastic Attention via Sliced Dual Projection
summary: Doubly-stochastic attention has emerged as a transport-based alternative to row-softmax attention, with recent Transformer variants using it to reduce attention sinks and rank collapse while improving performance. In this family, the standard approach is Sinkhorn scaling, which trains more efficiently but still repeats matrix scaling in every inference forward pass. Sliced-transport attention removes the online iteration, but its soft sorting approximation materializes dense tensors for each slice, requiring substantially more training resources than Sinkhorn attention. We introduce ASAP: Amortized Doubly-Stochastic Attention via Sliced Dual Projection, a train-then-compile method that trains the doubly-stochastic layer with Sinkhorn, then replaces the iterative scaling loop at inference with a fixed sliced-dual operator. It learns a lightweight parametric map from exact one-dimensional Kantorovich potentials to the Sinkhorn query-side dual, then reconstructs the attention plan with a two-sided entropic c-transform. Across language and vision benchmarks, ASAP keeps the cheaper training setup and remains highly competitive with recent baselines. In the main frozen-layer benchmark, ASAP is 5.3 faster than the trained Sinkhorn teacher while matching its accuracy; in downstream replacements, ASAP recovers most of the teacher performance without any retraining.

AI.tldr: Introduces ASAP, a train-then-compile approach for doubly-stochastic attention that trains with Sinkhorn and replaces online scaling with a fixed sliced-dual operator at inference, achieving speedups with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Doubly-stochastic attention reduces attention sinks and rank collapse; online Sinkhorn is expensive at inference; sliced-transport methods are resource-heavy during training; need a cheaper inference-time implementation without retraining.
AI.method: Train using Sinkhorn scaling; learn a lightweight parametric map from exact 1D Kantorovich potentials to the Sinkhorn query dual; reconstruct attention plan via two-sided entropic c-transform; at inference replace iterative scaling with fixed sliced-dual operator; amortized compile.
AI.result: ASAP is 5.3x faster than trained Sinkhorn teacher in the main frozen-layer benchmark while matching its accuracy; in downstream replacements, ASAP recovers most of the teacher performance without retraining; keeps cheaper training setup and remains competitive across language and vision benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ASAP provides a practical, efficient alternative to online Sinkhorn inference by amortizing scaling into a learnable map and a fixed operator, achieving speedups with minimal loss in accuracy.

title: Senses Wide Shut: A Representation-Action Gap in Omnimodal LLMs
summary: When an omnimodal large language model accepts a question whose textual premise contradicts what it actually sees or hears, does the failure lie in perception or in action? Recent omnimodal models are positioned as perception-grounded agents that jointly process video, audio, and text, yet a basic form of grounding remains untested: catching a textual claim that conflicts with the model's own sensory input. We introduce IMAVB, a curated 500-clip benchmark of long-form movies with a 2x2 design crossing target modality (vision, audio) and premise condition (standard, misleading), which lets us measure conflict detection separately from ordinary multimodal comprehension. Across eight open-source omnimodal LLMs and Gemini 3.1 Pro, we document a Representation-Action Gap: hidden states reliably encode premise-perception mismatches even when the same models almost never reject the false claim in their outputs. Behaviorally, models fall into two failure modes: under-rejection, in which they answer misleading questions as if the false premise were true; and over-rejection, in which they reject more often but also reject standard questions, sacrificing ordinary comprehension accuracy. The gap is modality-asymmetric (audio grounding underperforms vision) and prompt-resistant across seven variants. As an initial diagnostic intervention, a probe-guided logit adjustment (PGLA) re-injects the encoded mismatch signal into decoding and consistently improves rejection behavior. Together, these results suggest the bottleneck for omnimodal grounding lies in translation, not perception.

AI.tldr: IMAVB tests whether omnimodal LLMs detect textual premises that conflict with perceptual input, revealing a Representation-Action Gap where internal signals reflect mismatches but outputs often do not; audio grounding is weaker than vision; a probe-guided logit adjustment (PGLA) improves rejection by re-injecting mismatch into decoding, suggesting translation (decoding) rather than perception as the bottleneck in grounding.
AI.motivation: To disentangle perception from action in omnimodal grounding when premises contradict perceptual input, and to quantify whether models detect conflicts internally and translate them into refused answers.
AI.method: Create IMAVB, a 500-clip long-form movie benchmark with a 2x2 design: target modality (vision vs audio) and premise condition (standard vs misleading). Evaluate eight open-source omnimodal LLMs and Gemini 3.1 Pro. Measure conflict detection in hidden representations separate from answering, identify failure modes, assess prompt resilience across seven variants, and test a diagnostic intervention (PGLA) that re-injects mismatch signals into decoding.
AI.result: Hidden representations encode premise-perception mismatches, but outputs largely do not reject false premises. Two failure modes emerge: under-rejection (accepting misleading premises) and over-rejection (rejecting even correct premises). The gap is modality-asymmetric (audio grounding weaker than vision) and prompt-resistant (seven variants). PGLA consistently improves rejection by reintroducing mismatch into decoding, indicating the main bottleneck is translation/decoding rather than perception.
AI.conclusion: The bottleneck for omnimodal grounding lies in translating perceptual-mismatch signals into the decoder, not in perception. A targeted decoding-level intervention (PGLA) can mitigate misaligned outputs; further work should explore robust decoding strategies and how to reduce the representation-to-output gap across modalities.

title: Flow Augmentation and Knowledge Distillation for Lightweight Face Presentation Attack Detection
comment: Accepted at 2026 International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG)
summary: Face presentation attack detection (FacePAD) remains challenging under diverse spoofing representation, including 2D print and replay, 3D mask-based spoofing, makeup-induced appearance manipulation, and physical occlusions, as well as under varying capture conditions. Motion cues are highly discriminative for FacePAD but typically require explicit optical flow estimation, which introduces substantial computational overhead and limits real-time deployment. In this work, we leverage optical flow to enhance motion representation during training while eliminating the need for flow computation at inference. We propose a dual-branch teacher model that fuses appearance cues from RGB frames with motion cues derived from colorwheel-encoded optical flow, enabling effective modeling of micro-motions and temporal consistency. To enable efficient deployment, we introduce a knowledge distillation framework that transfers motion-aware knowledge from the flow-augmented teacher to a lightweight RGB-only student via logit distillation. As a result, the student implicitly learns motion-sensitive representations without requiring explicit flow estimation or additional feature extraction blocks at inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong performance across multiple benchmarks, achieving 0.0% HTER on Replay-Attack and Replay-Mobile, 0.94% HTER on ROSE-Youtu, 5.65% HTER on SiW-Mv2, and 0.42% ACER on OULU-NPU. The distilled student achieves performance comparable to or better than the teacher while significantly reducing parameters and FLOPs, achieving 52 FPS on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, indicating its suitability for real-time and resource-constrained FacePAD deployment.

AI.tldr: Flow-augmented teacher-student distillation enables RGB-only FacePAD with motion cues for improved robustness and real-time deployment.
AI.motivation: FacePAD faces diverse spoofing modalities and capture conditions; motion cues are highly discriminative but require optical flow computation; aim to leverage flow information during training while avoiding inference overhead.
