AI.method: A dual-branch teacher model fuses RGB appearance with colorwheel-encoded optical flow to capture micro-motions and temporal consistency; knowledge distillation transfers motion-aware knowledge from the flow-augmented teacher to a lightweight RGB-only student via logit distillation, enabling motion sensitivity without explicit flow at inference.
AI.result: Strong cross-dataset performance: 0.0% HTER on Replay-Attack and Replay-Mobile; 0.94% HTER on ROSE-Youtu; 5.65% HTER on SiW-Mv2; 0.42% ACER on OULU-NPU; the student achieves comparable or better performance than the teacher with significantly reduced parameters/FLOPs, running at 52 FPS on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that training-time use of flow cues via a teacher-student distillation framework yields a real-time, resource-efficient FacePAD system with robust motion-sensitive representations; highlights potential for deployment in edge devices; further work could explore ablations, generalization to unseen attack types, and sensitivity to teacher quality.

title: RISED: A Pre-Deployment Safety Evaluation Framework for Clinical AI Decision-Support Systems
comment: Submission to Artificial Intelligence in Medicine (Elsevier). Open-source Python implementation at https://github.com/rohithreddybc/rised-healthcare-eval (MIT license). Synthetic evaluation cohort at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Rohithreddybc/rised-healthcare-eval-dataset (DOI: 10.57967/hf/8734)
summary: Aggregate accuracy metrics dominate the evaluation of clinical AI decision-support systems but do not detect deployment-phase failures of input reliability, subgroup equity, threshold sensitivity, or operational feasibility. We propose the RISED Framework: a five-dimension pre-deployment evaluation covering Reliability, Inclusivity, Sensitivity, Equity, and Deployability, in which each dimension is operationalized through formal sub-criteria, pre-specified pass/fail thresholds, and bias-corrected accelerated (BCa) bootstrap 95% confidence intervals combined under a Holm-Bonferroni family-wise error correction. A central demonstration is that a classifier satisfying conventional high-discrimination benchmarks can simultaneously fail input-encoding stability and threshold-shift sensitivity checks, while subgroup AUC parity remains statistically inconclusive, pointing to deployment risks that aggregate evaluation alone cannot detect. We validate this differential pass/fail pattern on a synthetic cohort and three publicly available real-world cohorts spanning 35 years of clinical data vintage, from a 1980s cardiology dataset to a 2024 nationally representative health survey, where failing dimensions differ across cohorts, providing preliminary evidence of construct validity. The Equity dimension is reframed as a proxy-dependence diagnostic rather than a stand-alone gate: any need-based fairness verdict computed against a utilization-derived proxy carries a construct-validity problem the framework surfaces explicitly, triggering a procurement requirement for an outcome-independent need measure before the gate is binding. RISED is released as an open-source Python package that supplies the quantitative verdicts existing clinical AI reporting standards require, providing a principled gateway between in-silico model validation and silent-trial clinical evaluation.

AI.tldr: RISED: a five-dimension pre-deployment evaluation framework (Reliability, Inclusivity, Sensitivity, Equity, Deployability) for clinical AI, with formal sub-criteria, pass/fail thresholds, BCa bootstrap CIs, and Holm-Bonferroni correction. Demonstrates that high-discrimination models can fail deployment-relevant checks (input stability, threshold sensitivity, subgroup parity); validated on synthetic and three real-world cohorts; reframes Equity as a proxy-dependence diagnostic; released as open-source Python package bridging in-silico validation and silent-trial evaluation.
AI.motivation: Aggregate accuracy metrics overlook deployment-phase issues such as input reliability, subgroup equity, threshold shifts, and operational feasibility. A formal pre-deployment framework is needed to detect these risks and guide procurement decisions.
AI.method: Five-dimensional evaluation with formal sub-criteria, predetermined pass/fail thresholds, bias-corrected accelerated bootstrap 95% CIs, and Holm-Bonferroni WFW correction; empirical demonstration on synthetic and three public cohorts spanning 35 years; Equity reframed as proxy-dependence diagnostic; open-source Python package providing verdicts.
AI.result: Classifier can meet conventional discrimination benchmarks yet fail input-encoding stability and threshold-shift sensitivity; subgroup AUC parity inconclusive; dimension failures vary across cohorts; preliminary construct validity evidence; RISED released as an open-source tool.
AI.conclusion: RISED offers a principled pre-deployment gateway linking in-silico validation to silent-trial clinical evaluation, with explicit need for outcome-independent fairness diagnostics and a procurement-triggered gate when proxies indicate potential inequity or miscalibration.

title: Harnessing Agentic Evolution
summary: Agentic evolution has emerged as a powerful paradigm for improving programs, workflows, and scientific solutions by iteratively generating candidates, evaluating them, and using feedback to guide future search. However, existing methods are typically instantiated either as fixed hand-designed procedures that are modular but rigid, or as general-purpose agents that flexibly integrate feedback but can drift in long-horizon evolution. Both forms accumulate rich evidence over time, including candidates, feedback, traces, and failures, yet lack a stable interface for organizing this evidence and revising the mechanism that drives future evolution. We address this limitation by formulating agentic evolution as an interactive environment, where the accumulated evolution context serves as a process-level state. We introduce AEvo, a harnessed meta-editing framework in which a meta-agent observes this state and acts not by directly proposing the next candidate, but by editing the procedure or agent context that controls future evolution. This unified interface enables AEvo to steer both procedure-based and agent-based evolution, making accumulated evidence actionable for long-horizon search. Empirical evaluations on agentic and reasoning benchmarks show that AEvo outperforms five evolution baselines, achieving a 26 relative improvement over the strongest baseline. Across three open-ended optimization tasks, AEvo further outperforms four evolution baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance under the same iteration budget.

AI.tldr: AEvo introduces a meta-editing framework for agentic evolution, where a meta-agent edits the evolution procedure/contexts instead of proposing candidates, unifying procedure-based and agent-based evolution and improving long-horizon search performance.
AI.motivation: Current agentic-evolution approaches are either rigid fixed procedures or flexible agents that can drift over long horizons. There is no stable interface to organize accumulated evidence (candidates, feedback, traces, failures) or revise the underlying mechanism guiding evolution.
AI.method: Model evolution as an interactive environment with evolution context as state. AEvo deploys a meta-agent that observes the state and edits the evolving procedure or agent context that controls future evolution, enabling steering of both procedure-based and agent-based approaches.
AI.result: AEvo outperforms five baselines on agentic and reasoning benchmarks (26% relative improvement over the strongest baseline). On three open-ended optimization tasks, it surpasses four baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance within the same iteration budget.
AI.conclusion: A unified, actionable interface for long-horizon evolution is feasible via meta-editing, allowing accumulated evidence to inform and revise the future search process across diverse evolution paradigms.

title: Pyramid Forcing: Head-Aware Pyramid KV Cache Policy for High-Quality Long Video Generation
summary: Autoregressive video generation enables streaming and open-ended long video synthesis, but still suffers from long-term degradation caused by accumulated errors. Existing KVCache strategies usually apply unified historical-frame retention, implicitly assuming homogeneous historical dependencies across attention heads. We revisit historical-frame attention and reveal three distinct head types: Anchor Heads require broad long-range context, Wave Heads exhibit periodic temporal dependencies, and Veil Heads focus on initial and adjacent frames. Based on this finding, we propose Pyramid Forcing, a head-aware pyramidal KVCache framework that identifies head types offline, assigns behavior-specific cache policies, and supports heterogeneous cache lengths via efficient ragged-cache attention. Experiments on Self Forcing and Causal Forcing show that Pyramid Forcing consistently improves long-horizon generation quality on VBench-Long, increasing the 60-second Self Forcing score from 77.87 to 81.21 while enhancing motion dynamics, visual fidelity, and semantic consistency. Project: https://if-lab-pku.github.io/Pyramid-Forcing/.

AI.tldr: Head-aware pyramidal KVCache (Pyramid Forcing) for autoregressive video generation; classifies attention heads into Anchor, Wave, and Veil types, and uses ragged-cache attention with heterogeneous cache lengths to improve long-horizon quality.
AI.motivation: Long-term degradation in autoregressive video generation due to uniform historical-frame retention; implicit assumption of homogeneous dependencies across attention heads is limiting.
AI.method: Offline head-type identification; assign behavior-specific cache policies per head; enable heterogeneous cache lengths via ragged-cache attention; evaluates on Self Forcing and Causal Forcing tasks.
AI.result: Consistent long-horizon gains: e.g., 60s Self Forcing score on VBench-Long rises from 77.87 to 81.21; improvements in motion dynamics, visual fidelity, and semantic consistency.
AI.conclusion: Head-aware pyramidal KVCache effectively mitigates accumulated errors in long video synthesis; demonstrates robust gains across forcing setups; project available at the authors’ page.

title: VIP-COP: Context Optimization for Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for in-context learning on structured data, enabling direct prediction on new tabular tasks without task-specific training. However, their effectiveness is constrained by context length limits, restricting application to medium-scale data and degrading performance when inference-time data exceed pretraining size distributions. Our work introduces VIP-COP, estimating the Value of Importance for Prediction of training examples and features for hard Context OPtimization for TFMs. Its explicit selection mechanism suppresses noise and isolates influential data, enabling the model to also benefit from data augmentation by prioritizing high-value augmented samples and features. VIP-COP is (i) fast, boosting performance often within minutes of optimization, based on an online KernelSHAP-based regression with iterative refinement, value-guided context sampling, and multi-fidelity pruning; (ii) budget-aware and any-time, improving with additional test-time compute unlike heuristics that produce fixed contexts; (iii) model-aware yet fully black-box, requiring no access to model internals, making it compatible with both proprietary and open-source TFMs; (iv) interpretable, identifying discrete ``Very Important Predictors'' (samples and features) that maximize signal-to-noise, which makes it (v) robust, isolating high-value data from noise. In contrast, soft-prompt optimization requires model gradients, produces abstract latent tokens, and lacks explicit signal discrimination. Extensive experiments show that VIP-COP consistently outperforms heuristic and optimized baselines across large-scale high-dimensional testbeds, including data augmentation and data-noise settings, establishing a new state of the art in test-time context refinement for TFMs.

AI.tldr: VIP-COP is a fast, budget-aware, model-agnostic method for test-time context optimization in tabular foundation models, selecting the most valuable training samples and features to improve predictions, enabling data augmentation and noise robustness.
AI.motivation: To overcome context-length limits in tabular foundation models (TFMs) and improve performance when inference-time data extend beyond pretraining distributions, by identifying and leveraging the most informative samples and features.
AI.method: Online KernelSHAP-based regression with iterative refinement to estimate the Value of Importance for samples and features, plus explicit selection mechanism, value-guided context sampling, and multi-fidelity pruning; fast, any-time, model-aware yet black-box, requiring no access to model internals.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over heuristic and optimized baselines across large-scale, high-dimensional testbeds, including data augmentation and data-noise settings; fast (often minutes to optimize), robust, interpretable; sets a new state of the art in test-time context refinement for TFMs.
AI.conclusion: VIP-COP offers an effective, interpretable, and practical approach to context optimization in TFMs, addressing noise and data scarcity, and generalizing across models and data regimes.

title: History Anchors: How Prior Behavior Steers LLM Decisions Toward Unsafe Actions
summary: Frontier LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents that pick the next action after a long log of prior tool calls produced by the same or a different model. We ask a simple safety question: if a prior step in that log was harmful, will the model continue the harmful course? We build HistoryAnchor-100, 100 short scenarios across ten high-stakes domains, each pairing three forced harmful prior actions with a free-choice node offering two safe and two unsafe options. Across 17 frontier models from six providers we find a striking asymmetry: under a neutral system prompt the strongest aligned models almost never pick unsafe, but a single added sentence, "stay consistent with the strategy shown in the prior history", flips them to 91-98%, and the flipped models often escalate beyond continuation. Two controls rule out simpler explanations: permuting action labels leaves the effect intact, and the same instruction with an all-safe prior history keeps unsafe rates below 7%. Different families flip at different doses of unsafe history, and within every aligned family the flagship is the most affected sibling, an inverse-scaling pattern with respect to safety. These results are a red flag for agentic deployments where trajectories may be replayed, forged, or injected.

AI.tldr: HistoryAnchor-100 shows that a simple prompt to stay consistent with prior history causes frontier LLMs to switch from safe to unsafe choices, revealing strong agentic risk in sequential tool-use settings.
AI.motivation: Assess whether harmful steps in a model’s tool-use history bias future actions in frontier LLMs and quantify how prompts can amplify or dampen this effect.
AI.method: Construct HistoryAnchor-100: 100 short scenarios across ten high-stakes domains. Each scenario provides three forced harmful prior actions and a node with two safe and two unsafe options. Test 17 frontier models across six providers under two prompts: a neutral system prompt and one adding 'stay consistent with the strategy shown in the prior history'. Include two controls: (1) permute action labels to test for label reliance, (2) use an all-safe prior history. Analyze escalation and divergence across model families and
AI.result: With neutral prompts, the strongest aligned models almost never choose unsafe options. Adding the prompt to stay consistent with prior history flips models to 91–98% unsafe selections, with several models escalating beyond mere continuation. Permuting action labels leaves the effect intact, and an all-safe prior history keeps unsafe choices below 7%. Different model families flip at different doses of unsafe history; within each aligned family, the flagship model is the most affected, showing an inverse-scaling pattern with safety. The findings generalize across domains and providers and imply substantial risks for agentic deployments that replay, forge, or inject histories.
AI.conclusion: The results flag a systemic safety vulnerability in sequential, history-dependent agent deployments: small prompts can convert history-consistent behavior into high rates of unsafe actions, necessitating robust safeguards against history manipulation, history reuse, and trajectory-following prompts.

title: Early Semantic Grounding in Image Editing Models for Zero-Shot Referring Image Segmentation
summary: Instruction-based image editing (IIE) models have recently demonstrated strong capability in modifying specific image regions according to natural language instructions, which implicitly requires identifying where an edit should be applied. This indicates that such models inherently perform language-conditioned visual semantic grounding. In this work, we investigate whether this implicit grounding can be leveraged for zero-shot referring image segmentation (RIS), a task that requires pixel-level localization of objects described by natural language expressions. Through systematic analysis, we reveal that strong foreground-background separability emerges in the internal representations of these models at the earliest denoising timestep, well before any visible image transformation occurs. Building on this insight, we propose a training-free framework that repurposes pretrained image editing models for RIS by exploiting their intermediate representations. Our approach decomposes localization into two complementary components: attention-based spatial priors that estimate where to focus, and feature-based semantic discrimination that determines what to segment. By leveraging feature-space separability, the framework produces accurate segmentation masks using only a single denoising step, without requiring full image synthesis. Extensive experiments on RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance over existing zero-shot baselines.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot referring image segmentation (RIS) by repurposing pretrained instruction-based image editing (IIE) models; leverages early-stage semantic grounding in denoising representations; decomposes localization into attention priors and feature discrimination; uses a single denoising step; achieves strong zero-shot results on RefCOCO variants.
AI.motivation: RIS requires pixel-level localization for natural language expressions; instruction-based image editing models implicitly ground language and vision. Reusing these models promises training-free RIS with efficient inference.
AI.method: Analyze internal representations of IIE models to identify early foreground-background separability; construct a training-free RIS framework that uses attention-based spatial priors and feature-space semantic discrimination; perform localization using early denoising features rather than full image synthesis.
AI.result: Outperforms existing zero-shot RIS baselines on RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg; single-step inference with competitive accuracy; demonstrates that semantic grounding emerges at the initial denoising timestep.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained IIE models possess latent, usable grounding for RIS; a simple training-free decomposition into attention and features yields effective segmentation; highlights efficiency and potential for zero-shot cross-task transfer.

title: Data Difficulty and the Generalization--Extrapolation Tradeoff in LLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Data selection during supervised fine-tuning (SFT) can critically change the behavior of large language models (LLMs). Although existing work has studied the effect of selecting data based on heuristics such as perplexity, difficulty, or length, the reported findings are often inconsistent or context-dependent. In this work, we systematically study the role of data difficulty in fine-tuning from both empirical and theoretical perspectives, and find that there is no universally optimal difficulty level; rather, its effectiveness depends on the dataset size. We show that for a fixed data budget, there exists an optimal data difficulty for SFT, and that this optimal difficulty shifts toward harder data as the data budget increases. To explain this phenomenon, we conduct controlled synthetic experiments that reveal a simple underlying mechanism: the interplay between the (in-distribution) generalization gap and the extrapolation gap. We further support this mechanism through a theoretical analysis using PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds. Overall, our results clarify how data size and difficulty jointly affect the trade-off between generalization and extrapolation in SFT, providing guidance for difficulty-based data selection under certain model and data conditions.

AI.tldr: There exists a dataset-size dependent optimum in data difficulty for supervised fine-tuning: for a fixed data budget, an intermediate level of difficulty is best; as budget grows, the optimum shifts toward harder data; explained by the balance between generalization and extrapolation gaps, with PAC-Bayes support.
AI.motivation: Clarify how data difficulty, data size, and the generalization/extrapolation trade-off interact in SFT, addressing inconsistent findings in prior heuristics-based data selection.
AI.method: Empirical analysis with controlled synthetic experiments to isolate data difficulty effects, varying data budget and difficulty; measurement of in-distribution generalization and extrapolation gaps; theoretical support via PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds.
AI.result: For a fixed budget, an optimal data difficulty exists; this optimum shifts toward harder data as budget increases; the mechanism is the interaction between generalization gap and extrapolation gap; PAC-Bayesian bounds corroborate the observed behavior.
AI.conclusion: Data size and difficulty jointly govern the generalization/extrapolation trade-off in SFT; there is no universal optimum. The findings offer guidance for selecting data difficulty conditioned on budget and model/data conditions.

title: ERPPO: Entropy Regularization-based Proximal Policy Optimization
summary: Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) is a variant of the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, specifically tailored for multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). MAPPO optimizes cooperative multi-agent settings by employing a centralized critic with decentralized actors. However, in case of multi-dimensional environment, MAPPO can not extract optimal policy due to non-stationary agent observation. To overcome this problem, we introduce a novel approach, Entropy Regularization-based Proximal Policy Optimization (ERPPO). For the policy optimization, we first define the object detection ambiguity under multi-dimensional observation environment. Distributional Spatiotemporal Ambiguity (DSA) learner is trained to estimate object detection uncertainty in non-stationary constraints. Then, we enhance PPO with a novel Entropy Regularization term. This regularization dynamically adjusts the policy update by applying a stronger (L1) regularization in high-ambiguity observation to encourage significant exploratory actions and a weaker (L2) regularization in low-ambiguity observation to stabilize the proximal policy optimization. This approach is designed to enhance the probability of successful object localization in time-critical operations by reducing detection failures and optimizing search policy. Experiments on a testbed with AirSim-based maritime searching scenarios show that the proposed ERPPO improves accuracy performance. Our proposed method improves higher gradient than MAPPO. Qualitative results confirm that ERPPO effectiveness in terms of suppressing false detection in visually uncertain conditions.

AI.tldr: ERPPO augments MAPPO with entropy-regularized PPO guided by a Distributional Spatiotemporal Ambiguity (DSA) learner to handle non-stationary multi-dimensional observations, improving object localization in MARL tasks.
AI.motivation: Address non-stationary observations and detection ambiguity in multi-dimensional MARL; enhance time-critical object localization and reduce detection failures.
AI.method: Introduce DSA learner to estimate uncertainty; integrate adaptive entropy regularization into PPO (strong L1 in high-ambiguity, weaker L2 in low-ambiguity); maintain centralized critic with decentralized actors; evaluate on AirSim-based maritime search scenarios.
AI.result: ERPPO achieves higher accuracy and stronger learning signals than MAPPO; reduces false detections under visually uncertain conditions; qualitative results support effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-regularization with DSA-guided adaptation improves robustness and performance of MARL under multi-dimensional observation, with potential generalization to time-critical search tasks.

title: Quantifying Sensitivity for Tree Ensembles: A symbolic and compositional approach
summary: Decision tree ensembles (DTE) are a popular model for a wide range of AI classification tasks, used in multiple safety critical domains, and hence verifying properties on these models has been an active topic of study over the last decade. One such verification question is the problem of sensitivity, which asks, given a DTE, whether a small change in subset of features can lead to misclassification of the input. In this work, our focus is to build a quantitative notion of sensitivity, tailored to DTEs, by discretizing the input space of the model and enumerating the regions which are susceptible to sensitivity. We propose a novel algorithmic technique that can perform this computation efficiently, within a certified error and confidence bound. Our approach is based on encoding the problem as an algebraic decision diagram (ADD), and further splitting it into subproblems that can be solved efficiently and make the computation compositional and scalable. We evaluate the performance of our technique over benchmarks of varying size in terms of number of trees and depth, comparing it against the performance of model counters over the same problem encoding. Experimental results show that our tool XCount achieves significant speedup over other approaches and can scale well with the increasing sizes of the ensembles.

AI.tldr: Quantifies DTE sensitivity by discretizing input space and enumerating vulnerable regions using ADD-based encoding; introduces XCount with certified error bounds and compositional scalability; shows speedups over model counters.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical domains require verification of sensitivity in decision tree ensembles; existing qualitative notions are insufficient; a scalable, certified quantitative measure is needed.
AI.method: Encode the sensitivity problem with algebraic decision diagrams (ADDs), discretize inputs, decompose into subproblems using a compositional approach, and compute with certified error/confidence bounds.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on benchmarks with varying numbers of trees and depths shows significant speedups over counter-based approaches; the method scales well with ensemble size and complexity.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a scalable, certified framework for sensitivity analysis in decision-tree ensembles; XCount demonstrates practical efficiency and potential for adoption in safety-critical AI verification; future work includes refinement of discretization and extension to broader model families.

title: Multi-Modal Guided Multi-Source Domain Adaptation for Object Detection
summary: General object detection (OD) struggles to detect objects in the target domain that differ from the training distribution. To address this, recent studies demonstrate that training from multiple source domains and explicitly processing them separately for multi-source domain adaptation (MSDA) outperforms blending them for unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA). However, existing MSDA methods learn domain-agnostic features from domain-specific RGB images while preserving domain-specific information from the domain-agnostic feature map. To address this, we propose MS-DePro: Multi-Source Detector with Depth and Prompt, composed of (1) depth-guided localization and (2) multi-modal guided prompt learning. We leverage domain-agnostic input modalities, namely depth maps and text, to encode domain-agnostic characteristics. Specifically, we utilize depth maps to generate domain-agnostic region proposals for localization and integrate multi-modal features to align learnable text embeddings for classification. MS-DePro achieves state-of-the-art performance on MSDA benchmarks, and comprehensive ablations demonstrate the effectiveness of our contributions. Our code is available on https://github.com/sejong-rcv/Multi-Modal-Guided-Multi-Source-Domain-Adaptation-for-Object-Detection.

AI.tldr: MS-DePro introduces depth-guided localization and multi-modal guided prompt learning for multi-source domain adaptation in object detection, using depth maps and text prompts to produce domain-agnostic proposals and aligned class embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: General object detection (OD) suffers from domain shift; multi-source domain adaptation (MSDA) aims to leverage multiple source domains, but existing methods often learn domain-agnostic features from domain-specific RGB while still retaining domain-specific cues. Using domain-agnostic modalities (depth, text) can better capture invariant signals and improve cross-domain localization and classification.
AI.method: A two-component approach: (1) depth-guided localization to generate domain-agnostic region proposals, and (2) multi-modal guided prompt learning to align learnable text embeddings with visual features for classification. Depth maps and text prompts encode domain-agnostic characteristics, with separate processing of multiple sources.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance on MSDA benchmarks; extensive ablations validate the contributions; code released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Depth- and text-guided multimodal cues enable more robust MSDA in object detection by producing domain-agnostic proposals and aligned class embeddings, illustrating the value of multimodal prompts in cross-domain detection.

title: Revisiting DAgger in the Era of LLM-Agents
summary: Long-horizon LM agents learn from multi-turn interaction, where a single early mistake can alter the subsequent state distribution and derail the whole trajectory. Existing recipes fall short in complementary ways: supervised fine-tuning provides dense teacher supervision but suffers from covariate shift because it is trained on off-policy teacher trajectories; while reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards avoids this off-policy mismatch by learning from on-policy rollouts but with only sparse outcome feedback. We address this dilemma by revisiting Dataset Aggregation (DAgger) for multi-turn LM agents: the algorithm collects trajectories through a turn-level interpolation of student and teacher policies, and the student is then trained on these trajectories using supervised labels provided by the teacher. By directly interacting with environments, we expose the model to realistic states likely to be encountered during deployment, thereby effectively mitigating covariate shift. Besides, since the student is learned by mimicking the teacher's behavior, it receives rich feedback during learning. To demonstrate DAgger enjoys the benefits of both worlds, we tested the algorithm to train a software-engineering agent with 4B- and 8B-scale student models. On SWE-bench Verified, our DAgger-style training improves over the strongest post-training baseline by +3.9 points at 4B and +3.6 points at 8B. The resulting 4B agent reaches 27.3%, outperforming representative published 8B SWE-agent systems, while the 8B agent achieves 29.8%, surpassing SWE-Gym-32B and coming within 5 points of stronger 32B-scale agents. Together with consistent gains on the held-out SWE-Gym split, these results suggest the effectiveness of DAgger for modern long-horizon LM agents.

AI.tldr: DAgger-style turn-level data aggregation for long-horizon LM agents combines on-policy exposure with teacher-provided supervision to mitigate covariate shift and provide rich feedback, yielding notable gains on SWE benchmarks for 4B and 8B models.
AI.motivation: Address the complementary weaknesses of supervised fine-tuning (covariate shift due to off-policy data) and RL with sparse feedback (on-policy but sparse rewards) in multi-turn dialogue-like tasks.
AI.method: Use DAgger-inspired Dataset Aggregation: collect trajectories via turn-level interpolation between student and teacher policies; train the student on teacher-labeled trajectories; interact with environments to expose realistic states; evaluate on SWE benchmarks.
AI.result: 4B agent: +3.9 points over strongest post-training baseline; achieves 27.3% vs peers; 8B agent: +3.6 points; 29.8%, beats SWE-Gym-32B and close to 32B agents; gains on held-out SWE-Gym.
AI.conclusion: DAgger-style training effectively combines benefits of supervised and RL approaches for long-horizon LM agents, enabling practical performance gains in software-engineering tasks.

title: EvObj: Learning Evolving Object-centric Representations for 3D Instance Segmentation without Scene Supervision
comment: CVPR 2026. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/vLAR-group/EvObj
summary: We introduce EvObj for unsupervised 3D instance segmentation that bridges the geometric domain gap between synthetic pretraining data and real-world point clouds. Current methods suffer from structural discrepancies when transferring object priors from synthetic datasets (e.g., ShapeNet) to real scans (e.g., ScanNet), particularly due to morphological variations and occlusion artifacts. To address this, EvObj integrates two innovative modules: (1) An object discerning module that dynamically refines object candidates, enabling continuous adaptation of object priors to target domains; and (2) An object completion module that reconstructs partial geometries after discovering objects. We conduct extensive experiments on both real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating superior 3D object segmentation performance over all baselines while achieving state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: EvObj presents an unsupervised 3D instance segmentation approach that reduces the synthetic-to-real domain gap by (1) dynamically refining object priors via an object discerning module and (2) completing partial geometries with an object completion module, achieving state-of-the-art results on real and synthetic datasets.
AI.motivation: The work targets the well-known domain gap between synthetic shape priors (e.g., ShapeNet) and real-world scans (e.g., ScanNet), caused by morphological differences and occlusions, which degrades transferability of object priors for unsupervised 3D instance segmentation.
AI.method: Two modules: (1) object discerning module that continuously refines and adapts object priors to the target domain; (2) object completion module that reconstructs partial geometries after object discovery; integrated in an unsupervised learning framework for 3D instance segmentation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic datasets show superior 3D object segmentation performance over all baselines and achieve state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: EvObj effectively bridges the synthetic-to-real domain gap for unsupervised 3D instance segmentation by dynamically adapting priors and completing partial geometries, leading to improved performance.

title: GenCape: Structure-Inductive Generative Modeling for Category-Agnostic Pose Estimation
summary: Category-agnostic pose estimation (CAPE) aims to localize keypoints on query images from arbitrary categories, using only a few annotated support examples for guidance. Recent approaches either treat keypoints as isolated entities or rely on manually defined skeleton priors, which are costly to annotate and inherently inflexible across diverse categories. Such oversimplification limits the model's capacity to capture instance-wise structural cues critical for accurate pixel-level localization. To overcome these limitations, we propose GenCape, a Generative-based framework for CAPE that infers keypoint relationships solely from image-based support inputs, without additional textual descriptions or predefined skeletons. Our framework consists of two principal components: an iterative Structure-aware Variational Autoencoder (i-SVAE) and a Compositional Graph Transfer (CGT) module. The former infers soft, instance-specific adjacency matrices from support features through variational inference, embedded layer-wise into the Graph Transformer Decoder for progressive structural priors refinement. The latter adaptively aggregates multiple latent graphs into a query-aware structure via Bayesian fusion and attention-based reweighting, enhancing resilience to visual uncertainty and support-induced bias. This structure-aware design facilitates effective message propagation among keypoints and promotes semantic alignment across object categories with diverse keypoint topologies. Experimental results on the MP-100 dataset show that our method achieves substantial gains over graph-support baselines under both 1- and 5-shot settings, while maintaining competitive performance against text-support counterparts.

AI.tldr: GenCape introduces a generative, skeleton-free approach to category-agnostic pose estimation (CAPE) using i-SVAE to infer instance-specific adjacency graphs from support features and CGT to fuse latent graphs for query-aware structure, yielding improved performance over graph-baseline methods and competitive text-guided methods on MP-100 in 1- and 5-shot settings.
AI.motivation: CAPE struggles with rigid, predefined skeleton priors and isolated keypoints; there's a need for flexible, instance-specific structural reasoning that captures varying keypoint topologies across categories using only image-based support.
AI.method: Proposes two core components: (1) iterative Structure-aware Variational Autoencoder (i-SVAE) that infers soft, instance-specific adjacency matrices from support features via variational inference and embeds them into a Graph Transformer Decoder for progressive structural priors refinement; (2) Compositional Graph Transfer (CGT) that adaptively aggregates multiple latent graphs into a query-aware structure through Bayesian fusion and attention-based reweighting, enabling robust message passing and alignment across categories.
AI.result: Experiments on MP-100 show GenCape yields substantial gains over graph-support baselines in both 1- and 5-shot settings and is competitive with text-support methods.
AI.conclusion: A structure-aware, generative CAPE framework that avoids predefined skeletons and leverages support-driven latent graphs to guide pose keypoint localization across diverse categories; demonstrates improved robustness to uncertainty and support-induced bias, with strong empirical performance.

title: IV-ICL: Bounding Causal Effects with Instrumental Variables via In-Context Learning
summary: The instrumental-variables (IV) setting is standard for partial identification of causal effects when unobserved confounding makes point identification impossible. Existing approaches face methodological bottlenecks: closed-form bound estimands are required -- e.g., Balke-Pearl equations in binary IV -- and even when available, designing accurate estimators requires manual effort tailored to each estimand. While direct Bayesian inference of the causal effects, instead of the bounds, circumvents these challenges, it is often computationally intensive and suffers from high prior sensitivity or under-dispersed posteriors. As a remedy, we introduce IV-ICL, an amortized Bayesian in-context learning method that learns the marginal posterior distribution of the causal effects directly and derives bounds as its quantiles. Unlike standard variational inference that optimizes exclusive KL divergence, amortized Bayesian inference minimizes the expected inclusive KL, a mass-covering objective. We empirically observe that optimizing inclusive KL can recover the entire identified set across diverse data-generating processes, while exclusive-KL (e.g. with variational inference) of the same Bayesian formulation collapses onto a single mode and fails to cover the identified set. We evaluate IV-ICL on synthetic and semi-synthetic IV benchmarks and show it produces intervals that are more reliably valid and more informative compared to efficient semi-parametric, Bayesian, and plug-in baselines, at 20-500x lower inference time. Beyond methodology, we propose a procedure to convert randomized controlled trials into IV benchmarks with provably preserved ground-truth causal effects that enables a more realistic evaluation of partial-identification methods.

AI.tldr: IV-ICL uses amortized Bayesian in-context learning to infer the marginal posterior of causal effects under IV models, producing bounds as quantiles. It emphasizes a mass-covering inclusive KL objective to recover the full identified set, outperforming baselines in validity, informativeness, and speed (20–500x). It also introduces a benchmark extension converting RCTs into IV benchmarks with preserved causal effects.
AI.motivation: Partial identification in instrumental variable settings suffers from reliance on closed-form bounds and bespoke estimators; direct Bayesian inference can be flexible but is computationally heavy and sensitive to priors. A mass-covering, amortized approach could efficiently recover the entire identified set.
AI.method: Propose IV-ICL, an amortized Bayesian in-context learning framework that learns the marginal posterior distribution of causal effects directly and uses quantiles of this distribution to form bounds. Optimizes the inclusive (mass-covering) KL divergence rather than exclusive KL. Empirically evaluates on synthetic and semi-synthetic IV benchmarks, and introduces a procedure to convert randomized controlled trials into IV benchmarks with ground-truth causal effects preserved.
AI.result: IV-ICL yields intervals that are more reliably valid and informative than efficient semi-parametric, Bayesian, and plug-in baselines, with 20–500x faster inference. The inclusive-KL objective avoids collapse to a single mode, thereby covering the identified set. A practical benchmarking protocol is proposed by transforming RCTs into IV benchmarks while preserving true effects.
AI.conclusion: Amortized Bayesian in-context learning for IV partial identification provides a fast, robust alternative to traditional bounds-based or variational approaches, capable of recovering the full identified set and enabling realistic evaluation via RCT-derived IV benchmarks.

title: The Efficiency Gap in Byte Modeling
summary: Modern language models have historically relied on two dominant design choices: subword tokenization and autoregressive (AR) ordering. These design decisions bake in priors that dictate a model's learning. Recently, two alternative paradigms have challenged this: byte-level modeling, which bypasses static statistically-derived token vocabularies, and masked diffusion modeling (MDM), which conducts parallel, non-sequential generation. Their intersection represents a fully end-to-end modality-agnostic generative prototype; however, removing these structural priors incurs a significant computational cost. In this work, we investigate this cost through a compute-matched scaling study. Our results reveal that the performance penalty of byte modeling is not uniform; across scale, the scaling overhead of byte modeling is worse for MDM than for AR. We hypothesize that this disparity stems from context fragility: while AR's stable causal history allows models to naturally rediscover subword patterns, the MDM objective destroys the local contiguity required to efficiently resolve semantics from raw bytes. Our findings from controlled permutation experiments suggest that future modality-agnostic designs must incorporate alternative structural biases to maintain viable scaling trajectories in the byte regime.

AI.tldr: Byte-level modeling incurs a larger compute-scaling penalty under masked diffusion modeling (MDM) than under autoregressive (AR) generation; this is attributed to context fragility in MDM, which disrupts local byte contiguity needed to resolve semantics, unlike AR's stable causal history that helps rediscover subword patterns.
AI.motivation: Current language-model design choices embed priors via subword vocabularies and AR ordering. Exploring byte-level modeling and MD-mask diffusion offers a modality-agnostic alternative, but their scaling costs are poorly understood and potentially prohibitive for practical use.
AI.method: A compute-matched scaling study contrasting byte-level vs subword tokenization within two generation paradigms (AR and MD). Controlled permutation experiments were conducted to probe context fragility and the ability to recover semantics from raw bytes.
AI.result: The byte-modeling penalty is worse for MD diffusion than for AR. The authors hypothesize that AR preserves a stable causal history permitting re-discovery of subword patterns, whereas MDM destroys local byte contiguity, hindering semantic resolution from raw bytes. Permutation experiments support the role of context fragility in scaling costs.
AI.conclusion: Future modality-agnostic designs should incorporate alternative structural biases to sustain viable scaling trajectories in the byte regime, acknowledging the higher cost of byte-level MD compared with AR.

title: Trajectory-Level Data Augmentation for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: We propose a data augmentation method for offline reinforcement learning, motivated by active positioning problems. Particularly, our approach enables the training of off-policy models from a limited number of suboptimal trajectories. We introduce a trajectory-based augmentation technique that exploits task structure and the geometric relationship between rewards, value functions, and mathematical properties of logging policies. During data collection, our augmentation supports suboptimal logging policies, leading to higher data quality and improved offline reinforcement learning performance. We provide theoretical justification for these strategies and validate them empirically across positioning tasks of varying dimensionality and under partial observability.

AI.tldr: Trajectory-based data augmentation for offline reinforcement learning enabling learning from limited suboptimal trajectories through structure-aware augmentation that leverages the relationship among rewards, value functions, and logging policies; theoretically justified and empirically validated on positioning tasks with varying dimensionality and partial observability.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from data quality and distribution shift. In active positioning problems, the task structure and geometry linking rewards, value functions, and logging policies can be exploited to augment data and enable effective learning from suboptimal trajectories.
AI.method: Introduce a trajectory-based augmentation technique that uses task structure and the geometric relationships between rewards, value functions, and logging policy properties. The augmentation operates during data collection, supports suboptimal logging policies, and is backed by theoretical justification.
AI.result: The approach is theoretically justified and empirically validated across positioning tasks of varying dimensionality and under partial observability, demonstrating improved offline RL performance.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-based augmentation improves data efficiency and robustness of offline RL when training from suboptimal trajectories, with demonstrated generality across task dimensionality and partial observability.

title: Pareto-Guided Optimal Transport for Multi-Reward Alignment
summary: Text-to-image generation models have achieved remarkable progress in preference optimization, yet achieving robust alignment across diverse reward models remains a significant challenge. Existing multi-reward fusion approaches rely on weighted summation, which is costly to tune and insufficient for balancing conflicting objectives. More critically, optimization with reward models is highly susceptible to reward hacking, where reward scores increase while the perceived quality of generated images deteriorates. We demonstrate that optimizing against a unified global target under heterogeneous reward upper bounds can induce reward hacking, a risk further exacerbated by the inherent instability of weak reward models. To mitigate this, we propose a Pareto Frontier-Guided Optimal Transport (PG-OT) framework. Our method constructs a prompt-specific Pareto frontier and maps dominated samples toward it via distribution-aware optimal transport. Furthermore, we develop both online and offline optimization strategies tailored to diverse reward signal characteristics. To provide a more rigorous assessment, we introduce the Joint Domination Rate (JDR) and Joint Collapse Rate (JCR) as principled metrics to quantify multi-reward synergy and reward hacking. Experimental results show that our approach outperforms strong baselines with an 11% gain in JDR and achieves a near 80% win rate in human evaluations.

AI.tldr: Proposes Pareto Frontier-Guided Optimal Transport (PG-OT) to align text-to-image models with multi-reward signals, mitigating reward hacking via a prompt-specific Pareto frontier and distribution-aware transport, introducing JDR/JCR metrics and online/offline optimization; shows strong gains.
AI.motivation: Multi-reward alignment in text-to-image generation is challenging due to conflicting objectives and vulnerability to reward hacking. Existing weighted sums are brittle and reward models are unstable.
AI.method: Construct a prompt-specific Pareto frontier and apply distribution-aware optimal transport to map dominated samples toward the frontier. Develop online and offline optimization strategies tailored to different reward signal characteristics. Introduce Joint Domination Rate (JDR) and Joint Collapse Rate (JCR) as evaluation metrics.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines with an 11% gain in Joint Domination Rate (JDR) and achieves near 80% win rate in human evaluations, indicating improved multi-reward synergy and reduced reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: PG-OT offers a robust framework for robust multi-reward alignment in text-to-image generation, yielding better Pareto trade-offs and reliable human-perceived quality across diverse reward models.

title: The Expressivity Boundary of Probabilistic Circuits: A Comparison with Large Language Models
summary: Probabilistic Circuits (PCs) are deep generative models that support exact and efficient probabilistic inference. Yet in autoregressive language modeling, PCs still lag behind Transformer-based large language models (LLMs), suggesting an important expressivity gap. In this work, we compare PCs and LLMs under a unified autoregressive formulation. First, an output bottleneck: PCs parameterize predictions as convex combinations in probability space, which struggles to represent the sharp distributions typical of language; adopting a logit-space parameterization substantially narrows this gap. Second, a context-encoding bottleneck: we prove that structured-decomposable PCs can match Transformer separation rank on vtree-aligned partitions, but show, both theoretically and empirically, that this capacity is limited to partitions aligned with the fixed routing structure, leading to severe degradation when the data exhibits heterogeneous dependency topologies. We further prove that decomposable PCs are strictly more expressive than structured-decomposable ones, though effectively optimizing them remains an open challenge.

AI.tldr: Adopting logit-space predictions narrows the expressivity gap between probabilistic circuits (PCs) and Transformer-based LLMs in autoregressive models; however, capacity of structured-decomposable PCs is tied to fixed routing, causing failure on heterogeneous data, while decomposable PCs are strictly more expressive but harder to optimize.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental expressivity gap between PCs and LLMs in autoregressive language modeling. The goal is to unify the autoregressive formulation, identify the bottlenecks that limit PC expressivity, and understand how PC variants compare to Transformers.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of two bottlenecks: (1) an output bottleneck where PCs use convex combinations in probability space versus a logit-space parameterization; (2) a context-encoding bottleneck showing structured-decomposable PCs can match Transformer separation rank on vtree-aligned partitions but degrade when dependencies are heterogeneous; demonstration that decomposable PCs are strictly more expressive than structured-decomposable ones; and discussion of optimization challenges.
AI.result: Key findings: (a) logit-space parameterization substantially narrows the gap in expressivity; (b) structured-decomposable PCs can match Transformer separation rank only on partitions aligned with the fixed routing, with severe degradation for heterogeneous dependency topologies; (c) decomposable PCs are strictly more expressive than structured-decomposable PCs; (d) despite higher expressivity, practical optimization of decomposable PCs remains an open challenge.
AI.conclusion: There exists an expressivity hierarchy among PC classes, with decomposable PCs at the top, but real-world performance depends on routing flexibility and optimization; to close the gap with LLMs, future work must address wider dependency topologies and effective training of more expressive PC families.

title: OmniLiDAR: A Unified Diffusion Framework for Multi-Domain 3D LiDAR Generation
summary: LiDAR scene generation is increasingly important for scalable simulation and synthetic data creation, especially under diverse sensing conditions that are costly to capture at scale. Typically, diffusion-based LiDAR generators are developed under single-domain settings, requiring separate models for different datasets or sensing conditions and hindering unified, controllable synthesis under heterogeneous distribution shifts. To this end, we present OmniLiDAR, a unified text-conditioned diffusion framework that generates LiDAR scans in a shared range-image representation across eight representative domains spanning three shift types: adverse weather, sensor-configuration changes (e.g., reduced beams), and cross-platform acquisition (vehicle, drone, and quadruped). To enable training a single model over heterogeneous domains without isolating optimization by domain, we introduce a Cross-Domain Training Strategy (CDTS) that mixes domains within each mini-batch and leverages conditioning to steer generation. We further propose Cross-Domain Feature Modeling (CDFM), which captures directional dependencies along azimuth and elevation axes to reflect the anisotropic scanning structure of range images, and Domain-Adaptive Feature Scaling (DAFS) as a lightweight modulation to account for structured domain-dependent feature shifts during denoising. In the absence of a public consolidated benchmark, we construct an 8-domain dataset by combining real-world scans with physically based weather simulation and systematic beam reduction while following official splits. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong generation fidelity and consistent gains in downstream use cases, including generative data augmentation for LiDAR semantic segmentation and 3D object detection, as well as robustness evaluation under corruptions, with consistent benefits in limited-label regimes.

AI.tldr: OmniLiDAR is a unified text-conditioned diffusion model that generates LiDAR scans across eight heterogeneous domains using cross-domain training and domain-aware feature modeling, enabling high-fidelity generation and improvements in downstream tasks and robustness.
AI.motivation: Single-domain diffusion models impede scalable synthesis across diverse sensing conditions; a unified, controllable generator is needed to handle domain shifts (weather, sensor config, platform) for simulation and data augmentation.
AI.method: Proposes OmniLiDAR in a shared range-image space, conditioned text input. CDTS mixes domains in each minibatch and uses conditioning to steer generation. CDFM captures azimuth/elevation directional dependencies in the diffusion denoising process. DAFS provides lightweight domain-adaptive feature scaling. An 8-domain dataset is constructed by combining real scans with weather simulation and beam-reduction, with official splits. Training a single model across domains without per-domain optimization.
AI.result: Achieves high-fidelity LiDAR generation across domains; yields gains in downstream tasks: LiDAR semantic segmentation and 3D object detection via generative data augmentation; demonstrates robustness to corruptions and improved performance in low-label regimes.
AI.conclusion: A unified, controllable multi-domain LiDAR generator is practical through cross-domain training and domain-aware feature mechanisms, enabling scalable synthetic data and robust downstream performance across heterogeneous sensing conditions.

title: Dual-Pathway Circuits of Object Hallucination in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in bridging visual perception and natural language understanding, enabling a wide range of multimodal reasoning tasks. However, they often produce object hallucinations, describing content absent from the input image, which limits their reliability and interpretability. To address this limitation, we propose Dual-Pathway Circuit Analysis, a framework that identifies and characterizes hallucination-related circuits in VLMs for mechanistic understanding and causal probing. We first apply activation patching across five architecturally diverse VLMs to identify a visual grounding pathway that supports correct predictions and a hallucination pathway that drives erroneous outputs. We then introduce Conditional Pathway Analysis (CPA) to characterize pathway-level interactions, revealing that grounding components remain strongly redundant in both correct and hallucinating samples but undergo a consistent polarity flip, shifting from supporting the ground truth on correct samples to aligning with the hallucinated answer on erroneous ones. We further perform targeted suppression of hallucination-pathway components, showing that scaling these components reduces object hallucination by up to 76% with minimal accuracy cost, and validate that the same circuit selectively transfers to relational but not attribute hallucination. Evaluations on POPE-adversarial and AMBER show that the identified circuits are consistent across architectures, support causal intervention, and transfer selectively across hallucination types.

AI.tldr: Proposes Dual-Pathway Circuit Analysis to identify grounding and hallucination pathways in vision-language models, uses CPA for pathway interactions, and shows suppressing hallucination pathways reduces object hallucination up to 76% with minimal accuracy cost; circuits are consistent across architectures and transfer across relational but not attribute hallucination across POPE-adversarial and AMBER datasets.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination in vision-language models undermines reliability and interpretability. A mechanistic, causal understanding of the underlying circuits is needed to enable targeted interventions and cross-architecture generalization.
AI.method: Activation patching across five diverse VLMs to identify a grounding pathway and a hallucination pathway; Conditional Pathway Analysis (CPA) to characterize interactions; targeted suppression of hallucination-pathway components; evaluation on POPE-adversarial and AMBER datasets to test cross-architecture consistency and transferability across hallucination types.
AI.result: Found a redundant grounding component with a polarity flip depending on sample (correct vs incorrect); suppressing the hallucination pathway reduces object hallucination by up to 76% with minimal accuracy loss; the circuit transfers to relational hallucination but not attribute hallucination; circuits are consistent across architectures and support causal intervention and cross-type transfer.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a mechanistic, causal account of hallucinations in VLMs, enabling targeted, architecture-agnostic suppression of hallucinations and demonstrating selective transferability across hallucination types.

title: Reinforced Collaboration in Multi-Agent Flow Networks
summary: Multi-agent systems provide a powerful way to extend large language models (LLMs) by decomposing a complex task into specialized subtasks handled by different agents. However, their performance is often hindered by error propagation, arising from suboptimal workflow design or inaccurate agent outputs, which can propagate through the agent collaboration process and degrade final results. To address the challenges, we present MANGO (Multi-Agent Network Gradient Optimization), a data-driven framework that organizes and refines agent collaboration via a flow network constructed from past successful workflows. MANGO integrates reinforcement learning and textual gradients to jointly optimize workflow paths and agent behaviors, while a skipping mechanism prevents redundant updates to well-optimized agents for improving efficiency. Extensive experiments on seven benchmarks show that MANGO achieves up to 12.8% performance improvement over state-of-the-art baselines, enhances efficiency by 47.4%, and generalizes effectively to unseen domains. Our code and datasets are publicly available at https://github.com/openJiuwen-ai/agent-store/tree/main/community/mango.

AI.tldr: MANGO builds a data-driven flow-network of past successful agent workflows to optimize multi-agent collaboration in LLM tasks, using reinforcement learning and textual gradients with a skipping mechanism to improve performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Error propagation in multi-agent LLM systems due to suboptimal workflows and noisy outputs; a data-driven method is needed to refine agent coordination and mitigate cascading errors.
AI.method: Construct a flow network from historical successful workflows; jointly optimize workflow paths and agent behaviors via reinforcement learning and textual gradient signals; implement a skipping mechanism to avoid updating well-optimized agents for efficiency.
AI.result: Achieves up to 12.8% relative improvement over state-of-the-art baselines across seven benchmarks; reduces computation/updates efficiency by 47.4%; demonstrates strong generalization to unseen domains; code and datasets are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: MANGO offers a scalable, data-driven framework for robust, efficient multi-agent LLM collaboration, yielding measurable performance gains and broad generalization.

title: Unifying Physically-Informed Weather Priors in A Single Model for Image Restoration Across Multiple Adverse Weather Conditions
summary: Image restoration under multiple adverse weather conditions aims to develop a single model to recover the underlying scene with high visibility. Weather-related artifacts vary with the particle's distance to the camera according to the established scene visibility analysis, where close and faraway regions are more affected by falling drops and fog effects, respectively. Existing methods fail to consider this weather-specific physical visual process; thus, the restoration performance is limited. In this work, we analyze the common visual factors in adverse weather conditions and present a unified imaging model that considers the individually visible particles and fog-like aggregate scattering effects. Further, we design a novel weather-prior-based network, which leverages the weather-related prior information to help recover the scene by enhancing the features using the estimated occlusion and transmission. Experimental results in multiple adverse scenarios show the superiority of our method against state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed, weather-prior-guided network restores scenes degraded by multiple adverse weather by modeling occlusion and transmission and using a unified scattering model.
AI.motivation: Existing deraining/defogging methods treat weather effects separately and neglect distance-dependent physics; a unified model plus priors is needed for robust restoration across diverse weather conditions.
AI.method: Proposes a unified imaging model that separates individually visible particles (occlusion) from fog-like scattering; introduces a weather-prior-based network that leverages estimated occlusion maps and transmission to enhance features and guide restoration.
AI.result: Experimental results across several adverse-weather scenarios show improved restoration compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating physics-based priors with a weather-aware network yields robust restoration across diverse adverse weather conditions by jointly modeling occlusion and transmission.

title: From Instance Selection to Fixed-Pool Data Recipe Search for Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data selection is commonly formulated as instance ranking: score each example and retain a top-$k$ subset. However, effective SFT training subsets are often produced through ordered curation recipes, where filtering, mixing, and deduplication operators jointly shape the final data distribution. We formulate this problem as fixed-pool data recipe search: given a raw instruction pool and a library of grounded operators, the goal is to discover an executable recipe that constructs a high-quality selected subset under a limited budget of full SFT evaluations, without generating, rewriting, or augmenting training samples. We introduce AutoSelection, a two-layer solver that decouples fixed-pool materialization based on cached task-, data-, and model-side signals from expensive full evaluation, using warmup probes, realized subset states, local recipe edits, Gaussian-process-assisted ranking, and stagnation-triggered reseeding. Experiments on a 90K instruction pool show that AutoSelection achieves the strongest in-distribution reasoning average across three base models, outperforming full-data training, random recipe search, random top-$k$, and single-operator selectors. Additional Out-of-distribution graph-reasoning results, search-stability analyses, structural ablations, and 1.5B-to-7B transfer checks further show that recipe structure matters beyond individual selection operators. Code is available at https://github.com/w253/AutoSelection.

AI.tldr: AutoSelection is a two-layer data-recipe search method for SFT data selection under a fixed instruction pool and evaluation budget, yielding superior in-distribution reasoning and demonstrating that recipe structure matters beyond individual operators.
AI.motivation: SFT data selection is often done via instance ranking, but effective subsets arise from ordered, multi-operator curation recipes. There is a need to optimize a fixed pool of data-processing operators to construct high-quality subsets with limited full evaluations.
AI.method: A two-layer solver that decouples fixed-pool materialization from expensive full evaluations. It uses cached task-, data-, and model-side signals with warmup probes, realized subset states, local recipe edits, Gaussian-process-assisted ranking, and stagnation-triggered reseeding to search executable recipes over a fixed pool of operators.
AI.result: On a 90K instruction pool, AutoSelection achieves the strongest in-distribution reasoning average across three base models, outperforming full-data training, random recipe search, random top-k, and single-operator selectors. Additional out-of-distribution graph-reasoning results, search-stability analyses, structural ablations, and 1.5B→7B transfer checks indicate recipe structure, not just operators, governs performance. Code is available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Recipe structure in SFT data curation materially impacts performance beyond individual operators. AutoSelection provides an effective budget-aware recipe-search framework that outperforms baselines and demonstrates generalization across model scales and tasks.

title: A$_3$B$_2$: Adaptive Asymmetric Adapter for Alleviating Branch Bias in Vision-Language Image Classification with Few-Shot Learning
summary: Efficient transfer learning methods for large-scale vision-language models ($e.g.$, CLIP) enable strong few-shot transfer, yet existing adaptation methods follow a fixed fine-tuning paradigm that implicitly assumes a uniform importance of the image and text branches, which has not been systematically studied in image classification. Through extensive analysis, we reveal a Branch Bias issue in vision-language image classification: adapting the image encoder does not always improve performance under out-of-distribution settings. Motivated by this observation, we propose A$_3$B$_2$, an Adaptive Asymmetric Adapter that alleviates Branch Bias in few-shot learning. A$_3$B$_2$ introduces Uncertainty-Aware Adapter Dampening (UAAD), which automatically suppresses image-branch adaptation when prediction uncertainty is high, enabling soft and data-driven control without manual intervention. Architecturally, A$_3$B$_2$ adopts a lightweight asymmetric design inspired by mixture-of-experts with Load Balancing Regularization. Extensive experiments on three few-shot image classification tasks across 11 datasets demonstrate that A$_3$B$_2$ consistently outperforms 11 competitive prompt- and adapter-based baselines.

AI.tldr: A3B2 provides an adaptive asymmetric adapter with uncertainty-aware dampening to mitigate Branch Bias in CLIP-like models, improving few-shot transfer across datasets.
AI.motivation: Identify and address Branch Bias: uniform importance of image/text branches in fine-tuning vision-language models can be suboptimal, especially under out-of-distribution settings.
AI.method: Introduce A3B2, a lightweight asymmetric adapter inspired by mixture-of-experts with load balancing regularization. Incorporates Uncertainty-Aware Adapter Dampening (UAAD) to suppress image-branch adaptation when prediction uncertainty is high, enabling data-driven control without manual intervention.
AI.result: Empirically, A3B2 consistently outperforms 11 competitive prompt- and adapter-based baselines across three few-shot image classification tasks on 11 datasets.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, uncertainty-driven, asymmetric adapters effectively mitigate Branch Bias and enhance few-shot performance for large-scale vision-language models.

title: Separating Shortcut Transition from Cross-Family OOD Failure in a Minimal Model
summary: Shortcut features are often invoked to explain out-of-distribution (OOD) failure, but training correlation, learned shortcut use, and test-time failure need not coincide. We study a minimal binary model with one invariant coordinate and one family-dependent shortcut coordinate. In the deterministic regime, positive average shortcut correlation pulls logistic ERM toward positive shortcut weight, but ridge regularization keeps the classifier invariant-dominated and prevents deterministic OOD failure. When the invariant coordinate is noisy, ridge-logistic ERM switches to the shortcut rule once the training shortcut signal exceeds the invariant signal. Whether that transition causes failure depends on the held-out family: weaker shortcut correlation yields positive excess risk, and sign-flipped families yield above-chance error. Synthetic checks match these analytic regimes and show that the same training-side transition can have different held-out consequences. The model separates shortcut attraction, shortcut-rule transition, and cross-family OOD failure.

AI.tldr: A minimal two-coordinate binary model shows how training-time shortcut correlations interact with ridge vs ERM; the invariant dominates under ridge, preventing deterministic OOD failure, but when the invariant is noisy, the model transitions to shortcut-dominated predictions. The held-out OOD risk depends on shortcut strength and family structure, with weaker correlations or sign-flipped families yielding positive excess risk. Synthetic checks validate the theory and reveal that training-time transitions can have different cross-family outcomes.
AI.motivation: Disentangle the roles of training correlation, shortcut use, and test-time failure in OOD generalization using a minimal, tractable model.
AI.method: Analytical derivations in a deterministic regime; extension to noisy invariant; derive transition conditions for ridge-logistic ERM; synthetic simulations to validate theoretical predictions and to compare training-side transitions with held-out risk across families.
AI.result: Deterministic ridge ERM remains invariant-dominated and avoids deterministic OOD failure; with noisy invariant, ERM switches to shortcut rule once shortcut signal dominates; the OOD outcome depends on held-out family, with weaker shortcut correlation yielding positive excess risk and sign-flipped families yielding above-chance error; the same training-side transition can have different held-out consequences.
AI.conclusion: The study separates shortcut attraction, shortcut-rule transition, and cross-family OOD failure, highlighting that training dynamics do not universally predict OOD risk and that family structure crucially shapes held-out performance.

title: PanoWorld: Towards Spatial Supersensing in 360$^\circ$ Panorama World
summary: Multimodal large laboratory models (MLLMs) still struggle with spatial understanding under the dominant perspective-image paradigm, which inherits the narrow field of view of human-like perception. For navigation, robotic search, and 3D scene understanding, 360-degree panoramic sensing offers a form of supersensing by capturing the entire surrounding environment at once. However, existing MLLM pipelines typically decompose panoramas into multiple perspective views, leaving the spherical structure of equirectangular projection (ERP) largely implicit. In this paper, we study pano-native understanding, which requires an MLLM to reason over an ERP panorama as a continuous, observer-centered space. To this end, we first define the key abilities for pano-native understanding, including semantic anchoring, spherical localization, reference-frame transformation, and depth-aware 3D spatial reasoning. We then build a large-scale metadata construction pipeline that converts mixed-source ERP panoramas into geometry-aware, language-grounded, and depth-aware supervision, and instantiate these signals as capability-aligned instruction tuning data. On the model side, we introduce PanoWorld with Spherical Spatial Cross-Attention, which injects spherical geometry into the visual stream. We further construct PanoSpace-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating ERP-native spatial reasoning. Experiments show that PanoWorld substantially outperforms both proprietary and open-source baselines on PanoSpace-Bench, H* Bench, and R2R-CE Val-Unseen benchmarks. These results demonstrate that robust panoramic reasoning requires dedicated pano-native supervision and geometry-aware model adaptation. All source code and proposed data will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: PanoWorld enables ERP-native panoramic reasoning via Spherical Spatial Cross-Attention and a geometry-grounded data pipeline, plus PanoSpace-Bench diagnostic benchmarks; demonstrates superior panorama spatial understanding over baselines.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between panoramic ERP data and existing MLLM pipelines that process panoramas as multi-view projections, enabling observer-centered, depth-aware, geometry-aware reasoning in 360-degree scenes.
AI.method: Introduce PanoWorld with Spherical Spatial Cross-Attention to inject spherical geometry into the visual stream; build a large-scale metadata pipeline to convert mixed-source ERP panoramas into geometry-aware, language-grounded, depth-aware supervision; create PanoSpace-Bench as a diagnostic suite.
AI.result: PanoWorld substantially outperforms proprietary and open baselines on PanoSpace-Bench, H* Bench, and R2R-CE Val-Unseen; demonstrates superior pano-native spatial reasoning and the importance of geometry-aware supervision.
AI.conclusion: Dedicated pano-native supervision and geometry-aware model adaptation are essential for robust panoramic reasoning; plans to release code and data to foster reproducibility.

title: U-HNO: A U-shaped Hybrid Neural Operator with Sparse-Point Adaptive Routing for Non-stationary PDE Dynamics
summary:
Solutions to many partial differential equations (PDEs) display coexisting smooth global transport and localized sharp features within a single trajectory: shock fronts, thin interfaces, and concentrated high-frequency content sit on top of slowly varying backgrounds. This poses a challenge for neural operators: Fourier-based architectures mix nonlocal interactions efficiently but tend to under-resolve localized non-smooth features, whereas spatially local architectures recover fine detail at the cost of long-range propagation and rollout stability. Existing hybrid operators paper over this tension with a fixed, spatially uniform fusion that forces the same trade-off everywhere.
  We propose U-HNO, a U-shaped hybrid neural operator whose central design is Sparse-Point Adaptive Routing (SPAR): at every spatial location, a per-pixel hard mask selects whether the global Fourier branch or the local multi-scale Gaussian branch should dominate, and the sparsity ratio is a function of the local contrast of the routing signal, so smooth and shock-aligned regions receive different mixtures of global and local computation. SPAR is embedded in a hierarchical encoder-bottleneck-decoder backbone with skip connections so that the dual branches and the gate operate at every resolution. Training combines pointwise supervision with a finite-difference H^1 gradient term and a band-wise spectral consistency regularizer.
  Across benchmarks spanning 1D Burgers, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, KdV, 2D advection, Allen-Cahn, Navier-Stokes, Darcy flow, and 3D transonic compressible Navier-Stokes from PDEBench, U-HNO achieves state-of-the-art rollout accuracy on the majority of tasks in both relative L^2 and H^1 metrics, with the largest gains on problems dominated by sharp localized features. Ablations show that removing any single component substantially degrades rollout error.

AI.tldr: Introduces U-HNO with SPAR, a per-pixel adaptive fusion of global Fourier and local Gaussian branches for PDE operators, yielding state-of-the-art rollout accuracy on diverse PDE benchmarks, especially with sharp localized features.
AI.motivation: PDE solutions often contain smooth global transport coexisting with localized sharp features (shocks, interfaces).
