Fourier-based neural operators under-resolve non-smooth regions and local operators struggle with long-range propagation; existing hybrids use fixed, uniform fusion, which cannot adapt to local variability.
AI.method: A U-shaped hybrid neural operator (U-HNO) with Sparse-Point Adaptive Routing (SPAR): at each spatial location, a per-pixel hard mask selects the dominant branch (global Fourier vs local multi-scale Gaussian). The sparsity ratio depends on the local contrast of the routing signal, enabling smooth/shock-aligned regions to receive different mixtures. The architecture is a hierarchical encoder-bottleneck-decoder with skip connections; SPAR operates at every resolution. Training combines pointwise supervision with a finite-difference H^1 gradient term and a band-wise spectral consistency regularizer.
AI.result: Across PDEBench benchmarks (1D Burgers, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, KdV, 2D advection, Allen-Cahn, Navier-Stokes, Darcy flow, 3D transonic compressible NS), U-HNO achieves state-of-the-art rollout accuracy on the majority of tasks in relative L^2 and H^1 metrics, with largest gains on problems dominated by sharp localized features. Ablations show removing any single component substantially degrades rollout error.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, per-location fusion via SPAR enables effective handling of mixed smooth and sharp features in PDEs, with the proposed architecture and loss terms yielding robust rollout performance and spectral consistency across diverse PDE tasks.

title: CLIP Tricks You: Training-free Token Pruning for Efficient Pixel Grounding in Large VIsion-Language Models
summary: In large vision-language models, visual tokens typically constitute the majority of input tokens, leading to substantial computational overhead. To address this, recent studies have explored pruning redundant or less informative visual tokens for image understanding tasks. However, these methods struggle with pixel grounding tasks, where token importance is highly contingent on the input text. Through an in-depth analysis of CLIP, we observe that visual tokens located within referent regions often exhibit low similarity to the textual representation. Motivated by this insight, we introduce LiteLVLM, a training-free, text-guided token pruning strategy for efficient pixel grounding inference. By reversing the ranking of CLIP's visual-text similarity, LiteLVLM effectively retains visual tokens covering the referent regions, while recovering context tokens to enable clear foreground-background separation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LiteLVLM significantly outperforms existing methods by over 5% across diverse token budgets. Without any training or fine-tuning, LiteLVLM maintains 90\% of the original performance with a 22% speedup and a 2.3x memory reduction. Our code is available at https://github.com/sejong-rcv/LiteLVLM.

AI.tldr: Training-free, text-guided token pruning for pixel grounding in large vision-language models; inverts CLIP visual-text similarity to preserve referent-region tokens, achieving ~22% speedup, 2.3× memory reduction, and ~90% of original accuracy, outperforming prior methods by >5% across token budgets.
AI.motivation: Visual tokens dominate input, causing high compute costs. Pixel grounding requires text-conditioned token importance, which existing pruning strategies struggle to preserve referent regions. A training-free approach that aligns token selection with the text guidance is needed.
AI.method: Analyze CLIP similarities and reverse the ranking to retain tokens in referent regions while dropping less-relevant context; reintroduce context tokens to maintain clear foreground-background separation; no training or fine-tuning required; employs CLIP as a guiding signal for token pruning during inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show LiteLVLM outperforms existing pruning methods by over 5% across diverse token budgets; maintains ~90% of original performance with 22% speedup and 2.3× memory reduction; code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: LiteLVLM offers an effective, training-free solution for efficient pixel grounding in vision-language models by leveraging inverted CLIP token-similarity guidance to preserve referent-region tokens and restore context tokens, delivering practical speed and memory benefits.

title: Controlling Logical Collapse in LLMs via Algebraic Ontology Projection over F2
summary:
Do large language models internally encode ontological relations in a formally verifiable algebraic structure? We introduce Algebraic Ontology Projection (AOP), which projects LLM hidden states into the Galois Field F2 under Liskov Substitution Principle constraints, using only 42 relational pairs as algebraic keys. AOP achieves up to 93.33% zero-shot inclusion accuracy on unseen concept pairs (Gemma-2 Instruct with optimized prompt), with consistent 86.67% accuracy observed across multiple model families -- with no model tuning, but through prompt alone.
  This algebraic structure is strongly layer-dependent. We introduce Semantic Crystallisation (SC), a metric that quantifies F2 constraint satisfaction relative to a random baseline and predicts zero-shot accuracy without held-out data. System prompts act as algebraic boundary conditions: only their combination with instruction tuning prevents Late-layer Collapse -- a systematic degradation of logical consistency in the final layers, observed in 7 of 10 conditions. These findings reframe forward computation as an iterative process of algebraic organisation, and open a path toward LLMs whose logical structure is not merely approximated, but formally accessible.

AI.tldr: Proposes Algebraic Ontology Projection (AOP) projecting LLM hidden states into GF(2) under Liskov Substitution Principle constraints using 42 algebraic keys; reports high zero-shot accuracy across models; introduces Semantic Crystallisation (SC) to predict accuracy and quantify constraint satisfaction; system prompts plus instruction tuning mitigate late-layer collapse; frames forward computation as algebraic organization for verifiably accessible logic.
AI.motivation: Test whether LLMs encode ontological relations in a verifiable algebraic structure and move toward formal accessibility of their internal logical organization.
AI.method: Project hidden states into the Galois Field F2 under LSP constraints using 42 relational keys as algebraic operators; evaluate zero-shot inclusion accuracy on unseen concept pairs (Gemma-2 Instruct with optimized prompt); assess across multiple model families with no tuning; introduce Semantic Crystallisation (SC) metric; analyze the role of system prompts as boundary conditions and their interaction with instruction tuning; examine late-layer collapse across conditions.
AI.result: Up to 93.33% zero-shot inclusion accuracy on unseen concept pairs; 86.67% accuracy across multiple model families; no model tuning required, prompts suffice; SC predicts zero-shot accuracy without held-out data; system prompts act as boundary conditions; only their combination with instruction tuning prevents late-layer collapse, observed in 7 of 10 conditions.
AI.conclusion: Frames forward computation as iterative algebraic organization and outlines a path to LLMs whose logical structure is formally accessible, via AOP and SC, under principled prompting and tuning.

title: Does Engram Do Memory Retrieval in Autoregressive Image Generation?
summary: The Engram module -- a hash-keyed, O(1) associative memory injected into Transformer layers -- was recently shown to improve large language model pretraining, with the appealing interpretation that it provides a content-addressed shortcut to recurring local token patterns. We ask whether this interpretation transfers to autoregressive (AR) image generation, or whether the observed gains, if any, come from a different mechanism. We adapt the Engram module to vision with 2D spatial $n$-gram hashing, gated fusion, and KV-cache-compatible incremental inference, and inject it into a class-conditional AR generator trained on ImageNet 256x256. Across a sweep of backbone-to-memory budget ratios $ρ{\in}[0.17, 0.90]$, every Engram-augmented variant trails the pure AR baseline in FID, indicating that the module saves backbone FLOPs but does not, by itself, improve sample quality. We then probe how the module is used. A gate-clamp sweep shows that disabling the Engram pathway entirely is catastrophic, yet a tiny constant gate (g=0.10) matches or beats the learned gate -- inconsistent with a heavily content-addressed recall mechanism. A donor-probe experiment shows that swapping the hash inputs for matched, adversarial, or random same-class exemplars produces statistically indistinguishable next-token distributions, while collapsing or randomising the table degrades them by two to three orders of magnitude. Finally, training a model from scratch with the entire memory table frozen to $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ noise costs only $Δ\text{FID}{=}0.10$ and actually raises Inception Score. Together, these findings indicate that the Engram in AR image generation behaves not as a content-addressed retriever but as a gated architectural side-pathway: a hash-keyed residual stream whose benefit is dominated by the pathway itself, with the learned table contributing only a small distributional refinement.

AI.tldr: Engram in autoregressive image generation does not function as a content-addressed retriever; it mostly acts as a gated residual pathway, offering limited refinement and no clear retrieval benefit; training with fixed/noisy memory yields only small or zero gains, and model quality is not improved by the memory.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether Engram's content-addressed recall benefits observed in language-model pretraining transfer to autoregressive image generation, and uncover the actual mechanism when used in vision.
AI.method: Adapt Engram to vision with 2D spatial n-gram hashing, gated fusion, and KV-cache-compatible incremental inference; inject into a class-conditional AR generator trained on ImageNet 256x256. Explore a range of backbone-to-memory budgets ρ in [0.17, 0.90]. Perform a gate-clamp sweep (vary gate g) to assess reliance on the Engram path. Conduct a donor-probe by swapping hash inputs with matched, adversarial, or random same-class exemplars to test content-specific retrieval. Deactivate or randomize the memory table and retrain from scratch to test necessity of the stored content. Compare FID and Inception Score to a pure AR baseline.
AI.result: Across the ρ sweep, Engram-augmented variants underperform the pure AR baseline in FID, indicating memory reduces backbone FLOPs but does not improve sample quality. A tiny gate (g=0.10) matches or beats the learned gate, suggesting no heavy content-addressed recall. Donor-probe experiments show swapped hash inputs yield statistically indistinguishable next-token distributions, while collapsing or randomizing the table degrades performance by 2–3 orders of magnitude. Freezing the memory table to Gaussian noise and training from scratch changes FID by only ΔFID=0.10 and can increase Inception Score. Overall, Engram behaves as a gated architectural side-pathway rather than a content-addressed retriever; the learned table provides only minor distributional refinement.
AI.conclusion: In AR image generation, Engram functions as a gated residual path; gains observed in language-model pretraining do not transfer to vision AR. Memory content plays a limited role, and design should favor architectural gating over retrieval-based mechanisms for such tasks.

title: Revisiting Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards from a Contrastive Perspective
summary: RLVR has become a widely adopted paradigm for improving LLMs' reasoning capabilities, and GRPO is one of its most representative algorithms. In this paper, we first show that GRPO admits an equivalent discriminative reformulation as a weighted positive-negative score difference. Under this view, GRPO increases sequence-level scores of verified positive rollouts and decreases those of negative rollouts, where the scores are averages of clipped token-level importance sampling ratios. This reformulation reveals two structural limitations of GRPO: likelihood-misaligned scoring, where clipped ratio-based surrogate scores are optimized instead of generation likelihoods, and score-insensitive credit assignment, where rollout-level credit is assigned without accounting for relative score gaps between positive and negative rollouts in the same group. To address these limitations, we propose ConSPO, a framework for Contrastive Sequence-level Policy Optimization in RLVR. ConSPO replaces GRPO's clipped ratio-based scores with length-normalized sequence log-probabilities, aligning the optimized rollout scores with the likelihoods used in autoregressive generation. It then optimizes a group-wise InfoNCE-style objective that contrasts each positive rollout against negative distractors from the same group, enabling credit assignment to depend on their relative scores. This contrastive formulation amplifies updates for poorly separated positives while concentrating suppressive updates on high-scoring negatives. Moreover, ConSPO introduces a curriculum-scheduled margin, guiding optimization from coarse positive-negative ordering in early training toward stronger separation in later stages. Extensive evaluations across diverse backbone models, parameter scales, and training datasets show that ConSPO consistently outperforms several strong RLVR baselines on challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ConSPO: a contrastive, sequence-level RLVR method that fixes GRPO's misalignment and credit-assignment issues, improving math-reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address two structural limitations of GRPO in RLVR: (1) likelihood-misaligned scoring where surrogate scores do not reflect generation likelihoods, and (2) score-insensitive credit assignment that ignores relative score gaps among rollouts within a group.
AI.method: Replace GRPO's clipped ratio-based scores with length-normalized sequence log-probabilities; optimize a group-wise InfoNCE-style contrastive objective contrasting each positive rollout against negative distractors within the same group; employ a curriculum-scheduled margin to gradually enforce stronger positive–negative separation.
AI.result: ConSPO consistently outperforms strong RLVR baselines across diverse backbones, parameter scales, and training datasets on challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Aligning rollout scores with autoregressive likelihoods and enabling contrastive, group-aware credit assignment yields robust improvements in RLVR for LLM reasoning, particularly in math-intensive tasks.

title: DiffST: Spatiotemporal-Aware Diffusion for Real-World Space-Time Video Super-Resolution
summary: Diffusion-based models have shown strong performance in video super-resolution (VSR) and video frame interpolation (VFI). However, their role in the coupled space-time video super-resolution (STVSR) setting remains limited. Existing diffusion-based STVSR approaches suffer from two issues: (1) low inference efficiency and (2) insufficient utilization of spatiotemporal information. These limitations impede deployment. To address these issues, we introduce DiffST, an efficient spatiotemporal-aware video diffusion framework for real-world STVSR. To improve efficiency, we adapt a pre-trained diffusion model for one-step sampling and process the entire video directly rather than operating on individual frames. Furthermore, to enhance spatiotemporal information utilization, we introduce cross-frame context aggregation (CFCA) and video representation guidance (VRG). The CFCA module aggregates information across multiple keyframes to produce intermediate frames. The VRG module extracts video-level global features to guide the diffusion process. Extensive experiments show that DiffST obtains leading results on real-world STVSR tasks. It also maintains high inference efficiency, running about 17$\times$ faster than previous diffusion-based STVSR methods. Code is available at: https://github.com/zhengchen1999/DiffST.

AI.tldr: DiffST presents an efficient, spatiotemporal-aware diffusion framework for real-world STVSR. It adapts a pre-trained diffusion model for one-step sampling, processes whole videos, and introduces cross-frame context aggregation (CFCA) and video representation guidance (VRG). It achieves leading results while being about 17x faster than prior diffusion-based STVSR methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome two main limitations of diffusion-based STVSR: (i) low inference efficiency and (ii) insufficient exploitation of spatiotemporal information, hindering real-world deployment.
AI.method: Adapt a pre-trained diffusion model for one-step sampling and process the entire video as a sequence. Introduce CFCA to aggregate information across multiple keyframes for intermediate frame synthesis and VRG to leverage video-level global features to guide diffusion.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on real-world STVSR tasks with substantially improved inference speed, achieving roughly 17× faster runtime than previous diffusion-based methods.
AI.conclusion: DiffST demonstrates that efficient, spatiotemporal-aware diffusion for STVSR is feasible, enabling practical deployment in real-world scenarios.

title: CoRe-Gen: Robust Spectrum-to-Structure Generation under Imperfect Fingerprint Conditions
summary:
Molecular structure elucidation from tandem mass spectra (MS/MS) remains challenging, particularly for de novo generation beyond database coverage. A common approach decomposes the task into spectrum-to-fingerprint prediction followed by fingerprint-to-structure decoding, enabling the use of large-scale molecular corpora. However, at deployment, the decoder relies on predicted rather than oracle fingerprints, introducing structured errors that propagate into generation. This results in a fundamental condition mismatch, where models trained on clean inputs must operate under noisy, biased predictions, especially for long-tail substructures.
  We present CoRe-Gen that explicitly addresses this gap. CoRe-Gen improves the intermediate condition via synthetic-spectrum pretraining of the encoder, matches deployment-time noise through frequency-aware fingerprint corruption during decoder training, and mitigates residual errors using structure-aware autoregressive decoding with compositional SELFIES representations, auxiliary structural supervision, and lightweight chemical constraints. Experiments on standard benchmarks show that CoRe-Gen establishes a new state of the art on NPLIB1, achieving 19.54\% Top-1 and 29.92\% Top-10 exact-match accuracy, while remaining competitive on the more challenging MassSpecGym benchmark. Importantly, CoRe-Gen preserves the efficiency advantages of autoregressive decoding, providing a practical and scalable solution for robust spectrum-to-structure generation under realistic conditions.

AI.tldr: CoRe-Gen addresses the training-deployment mismatch in spectrum-to-structure generation by (i) synthetic-spectrum encoder pretraining, (ii) frequency-aware fingerprint corruption during decoder training to simulate realistic noise, and (iii) structure-aware autoregressive decoding with compositional SELFIES, supervisory signals and chemical constraints. It achieves state-of-the-art on NPLIB1 (Top-1 19.54%, Top-10 29.92%) while remaining competitive on MassSpecGym, preserving autoregressive efficiency.
AI.motivation: The task suffers from a gap between clean training inputs (oracle fingerprints) and noisy, biased predictions at deployment, especially for long-tail substructures, leading to error propagation and a fundamental condition mismatch.
AI.method: A three-pronged approach: (1) synthetic-spectrum pretraining to make the encoder robust to spectrum variability; (2) frequency-aware fingerprint corruption during decoder training to mimic deployment-time noise; (3) structure-aware autoregressive decoding using compositional SELFIES, augmented with auxiliary structural supervision and lightweight chemical constraints to mitigate residual errors.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance on NPLIB1 (Top-1: 19.54%; Top-10: 29.92% exact-match). MassSpecGym remains challenging but CoRe-Gen remains competitive. The method preserves the efficiency benefits of autoregressive decoding, offering a practical solution for robust spectrum-to-structure generation under realistic conditions.
AI.conclusion: CoRe-Gen effectively closes the training-deployment gap in MS/MS structure elucidation by robust encoder pretraining, noise-aware decoding training, and chemistry-informed autoregressive decoding, delivering robust, scalable performance with practical inference efficiency.

title: FIKA-Bench: From Fine-grained Recognition to Fine-Grained Knowledge Acquisition
summary: Fine-grained recognition in everyday life is often not a closed-book classification problem: when encountering unfamiliar objects, humans actively search, compare visual details, and verify evidence before deciding. Existing benchmarks primarily evaluate visually recognition, leaving this active external knowledge acquisition ability underexplored. We study fine-grained knowledge acquisition, where a system must seek, verify, and use external evidence to answer open-ended fine-grained recognition questions. We introduce FIKA-Bench, a leakage-aware and evidence-grounded collection of 311 public-source and real-life instances. To ensure high quality, every example is filtered against frontier closed-book models to remove memorized cases and audited to eliminate image-answer leakage, retaining only samples supported by verified evidence. Our evaluation of latest Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) and agents reveals that the task remains a formidable challenge: the best system reaches only 25.1% accuracy, with no model exceeding 30%. Crucially, we find that merely equipping models with tools is insufficient to bridge this gap; agent failures are predominantly driven by wrong entity retrieval and poor visual judgement. These results show that reliable knowledge acquisition needs better agent designs that focus on fine-grained recognition.

AI.tldr: FIKA-Bench introduces a leakage-aware, evidence-grounded benchmark for open-ended fine-grained recognition via external knowledge acquisition, revealing that current LMMs and agents struggle (best ~25.1% accuracy) and that tool use alone is insufficient due to retrieval and visual-judgment errors.
AI.motivation: To evaluate fine-grained recognition in an open-world setting where the model must seek, verify, and use external evidence rather than rely on closed-book memory, addressing a gap in existing benchmarks that focus on recognition accuracy without external information gathering.
AI.method: Create FIKA-Bench with 311 real-world instances derived from public sources, applying leakage-filtering to remove memorized cases and auditing to prevent image-answer leakage, and grounding each sample in verified evidence; evaluate state-of-the-art LMMs and agents on the task and analyze failure modes.
AI.result: Best performing system achieves 25.1% accuracy, with no model exceeding 30%; results indicate that simply equipping models with tools does not close the gap, as failures are mainly due to incorrect entity retrieval and poor visual judgment.
AI.conclusion: Advancing reliable knowledge acquisition requires improved agent designs that focus on fine-grained recognition, rather than relying solely on tool-enabled retrieval or external evidence; the task remains a substantial challenge for current models.

title: Decision Tree Learning on Product Spaces
summary: Decision tree learning has long been a central topic in theoretical computer science, driven by its practical importance. A fundamental and widely used method for decision tree construction is the top-down greedy heuristic, which recursively splits on the most influential variable. Despite its empirical success, theoretical analysis of this heuristic has been limited. A recent breakthrough by Blanc et al. (ITCS, 2020) provided the first rigorous theoretical guarantees for the greedy approach, but only under the uniform distribution. We extend this analysis to the more general and practically relevant setting of arbitrary product distributions. Our main result shows that for any function $f$ computable by an optimal decision tree of size $s$, maximum depth $D_{\text{opt}}$, and average depth $Δ_{\text{opt}}$, the greedy heuristic constructs an $ε$-approximating tree whose size grows at most with $\exp\bigl(Δ_{\text{opt}} D_{\text{opt}} \log(e/ε)\bigr)$. In the special case where the optimal tree is a full binary tree, this bound improves upon the bound of Blanc et al. and holds under a strictly broader class of distributions. Moreover, we present an algorithm based on the top-down greedy heuristic that is entirely parameter-free -- it requires no prior knowledge of the optimal tree's size or depth -- offering a practical advantage over Blanc et al.'s method.

AI.tldr: Greedy top-down decision-tree learning achieves ε-approximation under arbitrary product distributions with a size bound exp(Δ_opt D_opt log(e/ε)); extends Blanc et al. (ITCS 2020) beyond uniform distributions; includes a parameter-free algorithm and a stronger bound for full binary trees.
AI.motivation: To obtain rigorous performance guarantees for the widely used greedy top-down decision-tree heuristic under realistic input distributions (product distributions), extending beyond the uniform distribution case.
AI.method: Extend the prior uniform-distribution analysis to arbitrary product distributions. Relate the greedy split quality to the optimal tree’s size s, maximum depth D_opt, and average depth Δ_opt. Derive an ε-approximation size bound of exp(Δ_opt D_opt log(e/ε)). Special-case analysis yields a tighter bound for full binary trees and a broader class of distributions. Propose a parameter-free, practical variant of the top-down greedy algorithm that does not require prior knowledge of the optimal tree’s size or depth.
AI.result: Main bound: the ε-approximate tree produced by the greedy method has size at most exp(Δ_opt D_opt log(e/ε)). In the full binary-tree special case, the bound improves and applies to a broader distribution class than previously shown by Blanc et al. A parameter-free greedy algorithm is also introduced, enhancing practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that the greedy top-down decision-tree heuristic admits provable guarantees beyond the uniform distribution, with a tight (in certain cases) size bound and a practical, parameter-free algorithm. This broadens theoretical understanding and informs practical deployment of greedy decision-tree induction.

title: STAR: Semantic-Temporal Adaptive Representation Learning for Few-Shot Action Recognition
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology (TCSVT)
summary: Few-shot action recognition (FSAR) requires models to generalize to novel action categories from only a handful of annotated samples. Despite progress with vision-language models, existing approaches still suffer from semantic-temporal misalignment, where static textual prompts fail to capture decisive visual cues that appear sparsely across sequences, and from inadequate modeling of multi-scale temporal dynamics, as short-term discriminative cues and long-range dependencies are often either oversmoothed or fragmented. To address these challenges, we propose Semantic Temporal Adaptive Representation Learning (STAR), a unified framework, consisting of a semantic-alignment component and a temporal-aware component, effectively bridging the semantic and temporal gaps and transferring the sequence modeling capability of Mamba into the FSAR. The semantic alignment module introduces a Temporal Semantic Attention (TSA) mechanism, which performs frame-level cross-modal alignment with textual cues, ensuring fine-grained semantic-temporal consistency. The temporal-aware module incorporates a Semantic Temporal Prototype Refiner (STPR) that integrates semantic-guided Mamba blocks with multi-frequency temporal sampling and bidirectional state-space refinement, yielding semantically aligned prototypes with enhanced discriminative fidelity and temporal consistency. Furthermore, temporally dependent class descriptors derived from large language models (LLMs) provide long-range semantic guidance. Extensive experiments on five FSAR benchmarks demonstrate the consistent superiority of STAR over state-of-the-art methods. For instance, STAR achieves up to 8.1% and 6.7% gains on the SSv2-Full and SSv2-Small datasets under the 1-shot setting, and 7.3% on HMDB51, validating its effectiveness under limited supervision. The code is available at https://github.com/HongliLiu1/STAR-main.

AI.tldr: STAR introduces Semantic Temporal Adaptive Representation Learning for few-shot action recognition, combining semantic alignment and temporal modeling to address semantic-temporal misalignment and multi-scale dynamics, leveraging semantic-guided Mamba-style blocks and LLM-derived descriptors for improved performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In FSAR, static textual prompts fail to capture decisive visual cues that appear sparsely across sequences, and multi-scale temporal dynamics are inadequately modeled, leading to semantic-temporal misalignment and fragmented long-range dependencies. There is a need to bridge semantic and temporal gaps and to transfer sequence modeling capabilities from advanced architectures (e.g., Mamba) to FSAR, possibly enriched by LLM-based long-range guidance.
AI.method: Semantic Alignment module with Temporal Semantic Attention (TSA) for frame-level cross-modal alignment with textual cues; Temporal-aware module with Semantic Temporal Prototype Refiner (STPR) that fuses semantic-guided Mamba blocks, multi-frequency temporal sampling, and bidirectional state-space refinement to produce semantically aligned prototypes; incorporates temporally dependent class descriptors from large language models for long-range semantic guidance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five FSAR benchmarks show STAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with gains of up to 8.1% on SSv2-Full and 6.7% on SSv2-Small in 1-shot, and 7.3% on HMDB51, validating effectiveness under limited supervision. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: STAR effectively bridges semantic and temporal gaps in FSAR, improving discriminative fidelity and temporal consistency through semantic temporal alignment and adaptive representation learning, with LLM-guided semantic cues further enhancing performance.

title: Not Just RLHF: Why Alignment Alone Won't Fix Multi-Agent Sycophancy
summary: LLM-based multi-agent pipelines flip from correct to incorrect answers under simulated peer disagreement at rates we term yield, a vulnerability widely attributed to RLHF-induced sycophancy. We test this attribution across four model families and find it largely wrong: pretrained base models exhibit the same substitution pattern as their Instruct variants, averaging higher yield than Instruct. Using activation patching, we localize the corruption to a narrow mid-layer window where attention carries the causal weight and MLP contribution is negligible; patching above this window restores 96% of the clean-to-pressured P(correct) gap. The attack surface decomposes into two independent factors (channel framing and consensus strength) whose interaction produces a 47.5 percentage-point yield gap at majority consensus, preserved across jury sizes $N \in \{4, 5, 6\}$. Two converging activation-space interventions show that pressure suppresses clean-reasoning features rather than activating a new sycophancy circuit. A single correctly-arguing dissenter reduces yield by 54-73 percentage points across all framings tested, whereas the strongest prompt-level defense fails on attack variants outside its design surface. Mitigations should target the mechanism, structured dissent at the pipeline level, rather than prompt-level defenses.

AI.tldr: Peer disagreement yields incorrect answers across model families; the vulnerability localizes to a mid-layer, attentional window and is not solely due to RLHF sycophancy. Structural (pipeline-level) interventions and dissent reduce yield, while prompt-level defenses fail on variant attacks.
AI.motivation: Clarify whether RLHF-induced sycophancy explains yield under simulated peer disagreement and identify mechanistic loci and effective mitigations.
AI.method: Activation patching to localize corruption, cross-model family evaluation, analysis of channel framing and consensus strength, testing different jury sizes, two activation-space interventions, assessing a dissenting voice, and evaluating a prompt-level defense.
AI.result: Substitution patterns occur in pretrained base models and Instruct variants; corruption localized to a narrow mid-layer window where attention dominates; patching above this window restores ~96% of the clean-vs-pressured P(correct) gap. The attack decomposes into channel framing and consensus strength, yielding a 47.5 percentage-point gap at majority consensus across N=4,5,6. Pressure suppresses clean-reasoning features rather than activating a new sycophancy circuit. A single correctly-arguing dissenter reduces yield by 54-73 percentage points; strongest prompt-level defense fails on attack variants outside its design surface.
AI.conclusion: Mitigations should target the underlying mechanism and implement structured dissent at the pipeline level rather than rely on prompt-level defenses.

title: Skill-Aligned Annotation for Reliable Evaluation in Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) generation has advanced rapidly, making reliable evaluation critical as performance differences between models narrow. Existing evaluation practices typically apply uniform annotation mechanisms, such as Likert-scale or binary question answering (BQA), across heterogeneous evaluation skills, despite fundamental differences in their nature. In this work, we revisit T2I evaluation through the lens of skill-aligned annotation, where annotation strategies reflect the underlying characteristics of each evaluation skill. We systematically compare skill-aligned annotation against uniform baselines and show that it produces more consistent evaluation signals, with higher inter-annotator agreement and improved stability across models. Finally, we present an automated pipeline that instantiates the proposed evaluation protocol, enabling scalable and fine-grained evaluation with spatially grounded feedback. Our work highlights that improving the foundations of image evaluation can increase reliability and efficiency without simply scaling annotation effort. We hope this motivates further research on refining evaluation protocols as a central component of reliable model assessment.

AI.tldr: Skill-aligned annotation improves T2I evaluation reliability and scalability, outperforming uniform baselines and enabling spatially grounded feedback.
AI.motivation: Reliable evaluation is critical as T2I models narrow performance gaps; conventional uniform annotation conflates heterogeneous evaluation skills, hampering reliability.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of skill-aligned annotation against uniform baselines; empirical evaluation across models; development of an automated pipeline for scalable, fine-grained evaluation with spatially grounded feedback.
AI.result: Skill-aligned annotation yields higher inter-annotator agreement and more consistent evaluation signals; improved stability across models; automated pipeline for scalable evaluation with spatial feedback.
AI.conclusion: Refining evaluation protocols via skill-aligned annotation enhances reliability without increasing annotation effort; advocates further research into foundations of image evaluation as central to model assessment.

title: DP-Muon: Differentially Private Optimization via Matrix-Orthogonalized Momentum
summary: We study differentially private (DP) training with Muon, a matrix-valued optimizer that updates hidden-layer weights using momentum followed by Newton--Schulz orthogonalization. While DP-SGD is well understood, the interaction between per-example clipping, Gaussian noise, momentum, and nonlinear orthogonalization in Muon has not been systematically analyzed. We formulate DP-Muon, a private Muon procedure that clips per-example matrix gradients, adds Gaussian noise to the clipped lot average, and then applies momentum and Newton--Schulz orthogonalization as post-processing. We prove that DP-Muon inherits the privacy guarantee certified by the corresponding same-lot subsampled Gaussian accountant, with no additional privacy cost from Muon-specific post-processing. On the optimization side, we establish finite-horizon and vanishing stationarity guarantees under per-matrix clipping, with bounds that separate optimization error, clipping residual, privacy noise, and Newton--Schulz approximation error. We further show that the DP-induced bias in Muon arises not in the linear momentum buffer itself, but after the nonlinear Newton--Schulz map, where Gaussian noise induces a matrix-valued heat-smoothing bias. This motivates DP-MuonBC, a bias-corrected variant that removes the leading output-level bias term while preserving the same privacy guarantee. Experiments on E2E and DART show that Muon-style matrix updates improve private fine-tuning, and that DP-MuonBC further improves utility without increasing the privacy budget.

AI.tldr: DP-Muon is a differentially private matrix-valued optimizer that applies per-example gradient clipping, Gaussian noise, then momentum and Newton–Schulz orthogonalization; it preserves privacy via the same-lot Gaussian accountant and provides finite-horizon and vanishing-stationarity guarantees; it identifies a DP-induced bias arising after the nonlinear Newton–Schulz map and introduces a bias-corrected variant (DP-MuonBC); experiments on E2E and DART show improved private fine-tuning, with DP-MuonBC further improving utility without increasing the privacy budget.
AI.motivation: Analyzing the interaction of per-example clipping, Gaussian noise, momentum, and nonlinear orthogonalization in a matrix-valued optimizer (Muon) under differential privacy is nontrivial. The goal is to formalize a privacy-preserving Muon variant, establish privacy guarantees, and understand/update the optimization bias and utility.
AI.method: Define DP-Muon: clip per-example matrix gradients, add Gaussian noise to the clipped lot average, then apply momentum followed by Newton–Schulz orthogonalization as a post-processing step. Prove privacy via the same-lot subsampled Gaussian accountant with no extra cost from post-processing. Provide optimization analysis with finite-horizon and vanishing stationarity bounds, decomposing error into optimization, clipping residual, privacy noise, and Newton–Schulz approximation terms. Analyze that DP-induced bias arises after the nonlinear map, due to Gaussian-noise-induced matrix heat-smoothing, motivating a bias-corrected variant (DP-MuonBC) that removes the leading bias term while preserving privacy.
AI.result: DP-Muon preserves the privacy guarantee and yields explicit bounds that separate optimization error, clipping residual, privacy noise, and Newton–Schulz approximation error. The DP-induced bias is shown to occur after the Newton–Schulz map, leading to a correction (DP-MuonBC) that removes the main output-level bias without increasing the privacy budget.
AI.conclusion: DP-Muon is a principled DP optimizer for matrix-valued updates with solid privacy guarantees and optimality bounds; the nonlinear Newton–Schulz step introduces bias that can be mitigated by DP-MuonBC, resulting in improved private fine-tuning as demonstrated on E2E and DART datasets.

title: ReTool-Video: Recursive Tool-Using Video Agents with Meta-Augmented Tool Grounding
summary: Video understanding requires active evidence seeking, motivating tool-augmented video agents for temporal reasoning, cross-modal understanding, and complex question answering. Existing video agents have improved video reasoning with retrieval, memory, frame inspection, and verifier tools, but they still face two limitations: (1) a coarse tool space that lacks fine-grained operations for compositional reasoning; and (2) a flat action space that forces high-level video intents into primitive executable tool calls. In this paper, we address these challenges with two complementary designs. First, we construct a MetaAug-Video Tool Library (MVTL), an extensible tool library with 134 registered tools, including 26 base tools for general multimodal signal processing and 108 meta tools for filtering, aggregation, reranking, formatting, and other intermediate-result operations. MVTL supports dual-level access to both structured video information and raw modal evidence, enabling diverse video reasoning scenarios. Second, we propose ReTool-Video, a recursive tool-using method that grounds high-level video intents into executable tool chains. In ReTool-Video, matched actions are executed directly, while unmatched intents are delegated to a resolver for parameter repair, tool substitution, or decomposition. This allows abstract actions such as temporal merging, cross-modal verification, or repeated-event aggregation to be progressively translated into concrete multimodal operations at runtime. Experiments on MVBench, MLVU, and Video-MME w/o sub. show that ReTool-Video consistently outperforms strong baselines. Further analysis demonstrates that recursive grounding and fine-grained meta tools improve the stability and effectiveness of complex video understanding.

AI.tldr: Proposes MVTL, a large extensible tool library with 134 tools (26 base, 108 meta) enabling fine-grained, dual-level access to structured video information and raw evidence. Introduces ReTool-Video, a recursive grounding method that translates high-level intents into executable tool chains via direct matched actions or a resolver for repair/substitution/decomposition. Demonstrates consistent improvements over baselines on MVBench, MLVU, and Video-MME (w/o sub), with ablations showing gains from recursive grounding and meta-tools in complex video understanding.
AI.motivation: Current video agents use limited tool spaces and flat action sets, hindering compositional, multi-step reasoning over video. There is a need for a fine-grained, extensible tool library and a mechanism to ground high-level intents into executable tool chains.
AI.method: Build MVTL: an extensible library with 134 tools (26 base, 108 meta) enabling dual access to structured video info and raw evidence, supporting filtering, aggregation, reranking, formatting, etc. Propose ReTool-Video: recursively ground high-level intents; matched actions execute; unresolved intents are handled by a resolver for parameter repair, tool substitution, or decomposition; runtime translation of abstract actions into concrete multimodal operations.
AI.result: Empirical results show ReTool-Video consistently outperforms strong baselines on MVBench, MLVU, and Video-MME without sub, with ablation analyses indicating improved stability and effectiveness due to recursive grounding and fine-grained meta-tools.
AI.conclusion: Dual-level, extensible tool library together with recursive grounding enables fine-grained, compositional video reasoning and robust problem solving; the framework improves performance and provides interpretable, modular tool-usage for video understanding.

title: F-GRPO: Factorized Group-Relative Policy Optimization for Unified Candidate Generation and Ranking
summary: Traditional retrieval pipelines optimize utility through stages of candidate retrieval and reranking, where ranking operates over a predefined candidate set. Large Language Models (LLMs) broaden this into a generative process: given a candidate pool, an LLM can generate a subset and order it within a single autoregressive pass. However, this flexibility introduces a new optimization challenge: the model must search a combinatorial output space while receiving utility feedback only after the full ranked list is generated. Because this feedback is defined over the completed sequence, it cannot distinguish whether a poor result arises from failing to generate a relevant subset or from failing to rank that subset correctly. This credit assignment gap makes end-to-end optimization unstable and sample-inefficient. Existing systems often address this by separating candidate generation from ranking. However, such decoupling remains misaligned with downstream utility because ranking is limited by the candidate set it receives. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified framework that performs both within a single autoregressive rollout and optimizes them end-to-end via factorized group-relative policy optimization (F-GRPO). Our framework factorizes the policy into candidate generation and ranking while sharing a single LLM backbone, and jointly trains them with an order-invariant coverage reward and a position-aware utility reward. To address the resulting phase-specific credit assignment problem, we use separate group-relative advantages for generation and ranking within a two-phase sequence-level objective. Across sequential recommendation and multi-hop question answering benchmarks, F-GRPO improves top-ranked performance over GRPO and decoupled baselines, outperforms supervised alternatives, and remains competitive with strong zero-shot rerankers, with no architectural changes at inference time.

AI.tldr: A unified, end-to-end framework (F-GRPO) combines candidate generation and ranking in a single autoregressive LLM rollout via a factorized policy, addressing credit assignment gaps with separate group-relative advantages and two-phase objectives; demonstrates improved top-ranked performance on sequential recommendation and multi-hop QA benchmarks versus GRPO and decoupled baselines, competitive with zero-shot rerankers, without changing inference architecture.
AI.motivation: The credit assignment gap in end-to-end LLM-based retrieval, where feedback on the full ranked list cannot distinguish whether errors come from subset generation or ranking; misalignment from decoupled generation-ranking pipelines limits downstream utility.
AI.method: Factorize the policy into generation and ranking with a shared LLM backbone; optimize end-to-end using an order-invariant coverage reward and a position-aware utility reward; employ a two-phase sequence-level objective with separate group-relative advantages for generation and ranking to address phase-specific credit assignment.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on sequential recommendation and multi-hop question answering shows that F-GRPO improves top-ranked performance over GRPO and decoupled baselines, beats supervised alternatives, and remains competitive with strong zero-shot rerankers; no architectural changes are needed at inference.
AI.conclusion: F-GRPO effectively bridges generation and ranking in a unified autoregressive framework, enabling stable, efficient end-to-end optimization and improved top-ranked performance across multiple benchmarks without changing inference-time architecture.

title: X-Restormer++: 1st Place Solution for the UG2+ CVPR 2026 All-Weather Restoration Challenge
summary: In this work, we present our winning solution for the 8th UG2+ Challenge (CVPR 2026) Track 1: Image Restoration under All-weather Conditions. Our method is built upon the strong baseline framework X-Restormer, which effectively captures both channel-wise global dependencies and spatially-local structural information through its dual-attention design (Multi-DConv Head Transposed Attention and Overlapping Cross-Attention). To further boost the restoration performance, we propose several key improvements. First, we integrate the spatially-adaptive input scaling mechanism from Restormer-Plus to dynamically adjust the spatial weights of the input image, enhancing spatial adaptability. Second, to better preserve structural details and edge information, we introduce a novel Gradient-Guided Edge-Aware (GGEA) loss, which is combined with L1 and Multi-Scale SSIM losses in a unified training objective. Third, we significantly expand the training data by incorporating an extra 24,500 degraded-clean image pairs from FoundIR and WeatherBench alongside the original WeatherStream dataset. With these strategies, our proposed method successfully ranks the 1st place in the challenge.

AI.tldr: A winning CVPR 2026 image restoration method that extends X-Restormer with dual-attention, spatially-adaptive input scaling, a gradient-guided edge-aware loss, and expanded training data, achieving 1st place in UG2+ Challenge Track 1.
AI.motivation: To enhance image restoration under all-weather conditions by preserving edges and structural details while leveraging both channel-wise global dependencies and local spatial information, and by increasing data diversity.
AI.method: Base framework: X-Restormer with dual-attention (Multi-DConv Head Transposed Attention and Overlapping Cross-Attention). Enhancements: (1) Spatially-adaptive input scaling from Restormer-Plus; (2) Gradient-Guided Edge-Aware (GGEA) loss combined with L1 and Multi-Scale SSIM; (3) expanded training data with 24,500 degraded-clean pairs from FoundIR and WeatherBench, added to WeatherStream dataset.
AI.result: Achieved 1st place in the UG2+ Challenge, Track 1: Image Restoration under All-weather Conditions.
AI.conclusion: Shows that integrating powerful attention-based backbones with adaptive input weighting, edge-aware gradient loss, and larger, more diverse training data yields state-of-the-art restoration performance under challenging weather conditions.

title: Frequency Bias and OOD Generalization in Neural Operators under a Variable-Coefficient Wave Equation
summary: Neural operators learn to map initial conditions to the terminal solution of partial differential equations (PDEs), providing a surrogate for the full operator mapping. This enables rapid prediction across different input configurations. While recent neural operator architectures have demonstrated strong performance on diverse PDE tasks, their behavior under structured distribution shifts remains insufficiently understood. To investigate this, we study operator learning in a wave propagation setting governed by a one-dimensional variable-coefficient wave equation, using two representative architectures, the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) and the Deep Operator Network (DeepONet). To examine their generalization under distribution shifts, we consider structured out-of-distribution (OOD) settings that independently vary input frequency and coefficient smoothness. The results show that under smoothness shifts, both models maintain stable performance, with FNO achieving lower error. In contrast, under frequency shifts, FNO exhibits a sharp increase in error under unseen high-frequency inputs, whereas DeepONet shows milder degradation despite higher overall error. Our analysis reveals that these differences arise from how each architecture represents and responds to variations in frequency structure. Together, these findings highlight a fundamental gap between strong in-distribution performance and generalization under distribution shifts in operator learning, underscoring the role of architectural representation bias in developing more reliable neural operators for physics-based PDE simulations beyond the training distribution.

AI.tldr: FNO and DeepONet are compared on a 1D variable-coefficient wave equation to test generalization under structured OOD shifts. FNO handles smoothness shifts well but fails with unseen high-frequency inputs; DeepONet degrades more moderately in frequency shifts but has higher baseline error. The study attributes differences to architectural frequency representations, highlighting a gap between in-distribution performance and out-of-distribution generalization in neural operators.
AI.motivation: Assess how neural operator architectures generalize under structured distribution shifts in physics-based PDEs, focusing on input frequency and coefficient smoothness, to guide robust operator design.
AI.method: Evaluate Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) and DeepONet on a 1D variable-coefficient wave equation. Introduce structured OOD settings by independently varying input frequency and coefficient smoothness. Compare performance under shifts and analyze representational biases.
AI.result: Smoothness shifts: both models stable; FNO achieves lower error. Frequency shifts: FNO error sharply increases for unseen high-frequency inputs; DeepONet shows milder degradation but higher overall error. The differences are linked to how each architecture represents/factors in frequency structure.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental gap between strong in-distribution performance and generalization under distribution shifts in operator learning. Architectural representation bias plays a key role in developing more reliable neural operators for physics-based PDE simulations beyond training distribution.

title: Img2CADSeq: Image-to-CAD Generation via Sequence-Based Diffusion
summary: Boundary Representation (BRep) is the standard format for Computer-Aided Design (CAD), yet reconstructing high-quality BReps from single-view images remains challenging due to the complexity of topological constraints and operation sequences. We present Img2CADSeq, a multi-stage pipeline that overcomes these limitations by encoding CAD sequences into a three-level hierarchical codebook. Guided by an importance prioritization, this strategy values profiles over details, compressing long sequences into a stable discrete latent space. To bridge the modality gap, we leverage a coarse-to-fine point cloud intermediate, aligning 2D visual features with 3D CAD sequences via contrastive learning to condition a VQ-Diffusion model. Supported by newly introduced CAD-220K and PrintCAD datasets, our approach ensures robust industrial domain adaptation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Img2CADSeq significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, producing standard STEP files that can be directly used in commercial CAD software.

AI.tldr: Proposes Img2CADSeq, a multi-stage pipeline that encodes CAD sequences into a three-level hierarchical codebook to reconstruct high-quality BReps from single-view images, bridging 2D and 3D with a coarse-to-fine point cloud and contrastive learning, aided by new CAD-220K and PrintCAD datasets; claims state-of-the-art results yielding direct STEP outputs.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing high-quality boundary representations (BReps) from single-view images is difficult due to topological constraints and long operation sequences. A robust, discrete latent representation and cross-modal alignment are needed for reliable CAD sequence reconstruction and industrial deployment.
AI.method: A three-level hierarchical codebook encodes CAD sequences into discrete latent space with importance prioritization that favors profiles over details; a multi-stage pipeline uses a coarse-to-fine point cloud intermediate to align 2D visual features with 3D CAD sequences via contrastive learning, conditioning a VQ-Diffusion model to generate CAD sequences. Two new datasets, CAD-220K and PrintCAD, support industrial-domain adaptation.
AI.result: Experiments show Img2CADSeq significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches and outputs standard STEP files directly usable in commercial CAD software.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical discrete coding, cross-modal alignment, and diffusion-based generation enable robust single-view CAD reconstruction with direct usability in industry, supported by large-scale datasets for domain adaptation.

title: \emph{DRIFT}: A Benchmark for Task-Free Continual Graph Learning with Continuous Distribution Shifts
summary: Continual graph learning (CGL) aims to learn from dynamically evolving graphs while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Existing CGL approaches typically adopt a task-based formulation, where the data stream is partitioned into a sequence of discrete tasks with pre-defined boundaries. However, such assumptions rarely hold in real-world environments, where data distributions evolve continuously and task identity is often unavailable. To better reflect realistic non-stationary environments, we revisit continual graph learning from a task-free perspective. We propose a unified formulation that models the data stream as a time-varying mixture of latent task distributions, enabling continuous modeling of distribution drift. Based on this formulation, we construct DRIFT, a benchmark that spans a spectrum of transition dynamics ranging from hard task switches to smooth distributional drift through a Gaussian parameterization. We evaluate representative continual learning methods under this task-free setting and observe substantial performance degradation compared to traditional task-based protocols. Our findings indicate that many existing approaches implicitly rely on task boundary information and struggle under realistic task-free graph streams. This work highlights the importance of studying continual graph learning under realistic non-stationary conditions and provides a benchmark for future research in this direction. Our code is available at https://github.com/gqBond/DRIFT.

AI.tldr: Proposes a task-free continual graph learning (CGL) framework by modeling data streams as time-varying mixtures of latent task distributions; introduces DRIFT benchmark across hard switches to smooth drift; shows existing CL methods degrade without task boundaries; enables future research in realistic non-stationary graphs; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Real-world graphs evolve continuously without clear task boundaries; task-based CGL is brittle; need robust, task-free evaluation and modeling of distribution drift.
AI.method: Unified task-free formulation; time-varying mixture of latent task distributions; Gaussian parameterization to generate DRIFT benchmarking scenarios; evaluate representative CL methods on this setting.
AI.result: Significant performance degradation of task-based CL methods under task-free DRIFT; some methods rely on task boundary information; DRIFT spans a spectrum of dynamics; provides benchmark and baseline results.
AI.conclusion: Task-free continual graph learning is essential; current methods insufficient; DRIFT benchmark will drive future work; code released.

title: Color Constancy in Hyperspectral Imaging via Reduced Spectral Spaces
summary: Illuminant estimation aims to infer scene illumination from image measurements despite intrinsic ambiguities between surface reflectance and lighting. Most existing methods operate on trichromatic RGB images and are therefore fundamentally limited by the restricted spectral information available. Hyperspectral imaging provides a much richer representation of scene radiance and has the potential to alleviate these ambiguities. However, its high dimensionality poses computational and statistical challenges. In this work, we systematically study the effect of spectral dimensionality and representation choice on illuminant estimation performance using hyperspectral data. We adopt the practical and effective Color-by-Correlation (CbC) framework as the estimation backbone and analyze its behavior under different spectral dimensionality reduction strategies. Our results offer practical insights into how hyperspectral information can be efficiently exploited for illuminant estimation and identify conditions under which compact spectral representations outperform conventional RGB-based approaches. The code is available at https://github.com/IVRL/Reduced-Spectral-Color-Constancy.

AI.tldr: Hyperspectral-based illuminant estimation using Color-by-Correlation (CbC) is studied across spectral dimensionality reduction schemes to identify when compact representations outperform RGB methods; practical guidelines and code are provided.
AI.motivation: To overcome the spectral limitations of RGB illumination estimation by leveraging hyperspectral data, while addressing the computational/statistical challenges of high dimensionality and understanding how dimensionality reduction affects performance.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of spectral dimensionality and representation choices within the Color-by-Correlation (CbC) framework for illuminant estimation, evaluating different spectral reduction strategies using hyperspectral data.
AI.result: The study yields practical insights on exploiting hyperspectral information for illuminant estimation and identifies conditions under which compact spectral representations can surpass conventional RGB-based approaches, with code available for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Hyperspectral information, when coupled with appropriate dimensionality reduction within a robust estimator like CbC, can be efficiently exploited for illuminant estimation; effective representations depend on the reduction strategy and the target performance relative to RGB baselines.

title: JEDI: Joint Embedding Diffusion World Model for Online Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
summary: Diffusion world models have recently become competitive for online model-based reinforcement learning, but current approaches expose a tension: pixel diffusion is effective but computationally expensive while the latest latent diffusion approach improves efficiency yet performs subpar. The latter also relies on separately trained latents rather than the end-to-end world-model objectives that have driven much of modern MBRL progress. In particular, JEPA-style predictive representation learning has emerged as an especially promising direction for world modeling and MBRL. Concurrently, diffusion-style objectives have gained traction across multiple domains, with iterative refinement as a promising approach for multimodal and stochastic targets. Taken together, these trends motivate Joint Embedding DIffusion (JEDI), the first online end-to-end latent diffusion world model. JEDI learns its latent space directly from the diffusion denoising loss with a JEPA framework, using denoising to learn and predict future latents rather than relying on reconstruction and pretrained models. We provide a theoretical motivation showing that conventional JEPA objectives induce a predictive information bottleneck, and that conditional diffusion denoising admits a closely related predictive-compression decomposition. Empirically, JEDI is competitive on Atari100k and outperforms the baseline with seperately trained latents where directly comparable. Relative to the pixel diffusion baseline, JEDI uses 43% less VRAM, over 3$\times$ faster world-model sampling, and 2.5$\times$ faster training. JEDI also exhibits a markedly different task-level performance profile from the pixel baseline, suggesting that end-to-end predictive latents change more than compute alone.

AI.tldr: JEDI introduces an online, end-to-end latent diffusion world model for MBRL that leverages JEPA-style predictive representations learned through diffusion denoising, achieving faster, more memory-efficient training with competitive Atari100k performance.
AI.motivation: There is a tension in diffusion-based MBRL: pixel diffusion is accurate but computationally heavy, while latent diffusion (with pretrained latents) is efficient but suboptimal and not end-to-end. An online, end-to-end latent diffusion model guided by JEPA predictive learning can combine efficiency with strong predictive modeling.
AI.method: Joint Embedding DIffusion (JEDI) learns its latent space directly from the diffusion denoising loss within a JEPA framework, using denoising to learn and predict future latents rather than reconstruction or pretrained models. The work provides theoretical motivation linking JEPA objectives to a predictive information bottleneck and shows a close relation between conditional diffusion denoising and predictive-compression decomposition.
AI.result: Empirically, JEDI is competitive on Atari100k and outperforms baselines that use separately trained latents where directly comparable. Relative to a pixel diffusion baseline, JEDI uses 43% less VRAM, more than 3× faster world-model sampling, and 2.5× faster training. It also exhibits a notably different task-level performance profile from the pixel baseline, indicating end-to-end predictive latents influence task behavior beyond compute savings.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end latent diffusion via JEDI demonstrates that predictive latent representations learned through diffusion denoising can achieve strong MBRL performance with substantial efficiency gains. This supports a shift toward end-to-end diffusion-based world models and justifies the integration of JEPA-style predictive learning with diffusion objectives.

title: Test-time Sparsity for Extreme Fast Action Diffusion
summary: Action diffusion excels at high-fidelity action generation but incurs heavy computational costs owing to its iterative denoising nature. Despite current technologies showing promise in accelerating diffusion transformers by reusing the cached features, they struggle to adapt to policy dynamics arising from diverse perceptions and multi-round rollout iterations in open environments. We propose test-time sparsity to tackle this challenge, which aims to accelerate action diffusion by dynamically predicting prunable residual computations for each model forward at test time. However, two bottlenecks remain in this paradigm: 1) repetitive conditional encoding and pruning offset most potential speed gains, and 2) the features cached from previous denoising timesteps cannot constrain large pruning errors under aggressive sparsity. To address the first bottleneck, we design a highly parallelized inference pipeline that minimizes the non-decoder delay to milliseconds. Specifically, we first design a lightweight pruner that shares the encoder with the diffusion transformer. Then, we decouple the encoding and pruning from the autoregressive denoising loop by processing all denoising timesteps in parallel, and overlap the pruner with the decoder forward inference through asynchronism. To overcome the second bottleneck, we introduce an omnidirectional reusing strategy, which achieves 95% sparsity by selectively reusing features cached from the current forward, previous denoising timesteps, and earlier rollout iterations. To learn the rollout-level reusing strategies, we sample a few action trajectories to supervise the sparsified diffusion step by step. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method reduces FLOPs by 92% and accelerates action generation by 5x, achieving lossless performance with an inference frequency of 47.5 Hz. Our code is available at https://github.com/ky-ji/Test-time-Sparsity.

AI.tldr: Test-time sparsity accelerates action diffusion with a shared-encoder pruner, parallelized denoising, and omnidirectional feature reuse to reach 95% sparsity, 5x speed, and 47.5 Hz, with lossless performance and 92% FLOP reduction.
AI.motivation: Action diffusion yields high-fidelity action generation but is computationally expensive due to iterative denoising. Existing acceleration via cached features helps transformers but struggles with policy dynamics, perception variability, and multi-round rollouts in open environments. There is a need for test-time methods that adapt to dynamic rollout trajectories without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce test-time sparsity: a lightweight pruner that shares encoder with the diffusion transformer, decouple encoding/pruning from the autoregressive loop to process all denoising timesteps in parallel, and overlap pruning with decoding via asynchronous execution. Implement an omnidirectional reuse strategy that selectively reuses features from the current forward pass, previous denoising timesteps, and earlier rollout iterations to reach up to 95% sparsity. Learn rollout-level reuse strategies by supervising sparsified steps on a few sampled action trajectories.
AI.result: FLOPs reduced by 92%, action generation accelerated by 5x, achieving 47.5 Hz inference with lossless performance on the tested tasks. Code released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Test-time sparsity with a shared-encoder pruner, parallel denoising, asynchronous execution, and omnidirectional feature reuse can dramatically accelerate action diffusion without sacrificing performance in open-environment rollout settings. This approach enables near-real-time action generation and reduces compute requirements substantially.

title: Rethinking Efficient Graph Coarsening via a Non-Selfishness Principle
summary: Graph coarsening is a graph dimensionality reduction technique that aims to construct a smaller and more tractable graph while preserving the essential structural and semantic properties of the original graph. However, most existing methods rely on pair-wise similarity matching, where each node independently searches for its best partner based on global information. This selfishness matching paradigm incurs substantial computational and memory overhead. To address this problem, we shift to a non-selfishness principle that prioritizes the collective interference of neighborhood in coarsening, and propose an efficient method named NOPE, which achieves linear memory consumption and near-linear computational complexity in the number of nodes. Furthermore, we derive a faster variant NOPE*, which reduces O(δ\dot d) interference evaluation to O(d) based on the local isotropy assumption, and consequently alleviates the computational bottleneck for high-degree nodes. Experimental results show that NOPE* achieves 1.8-10\times speedup over NOPE and surpass almost all baselines with 1-3 orders of magnitude acceleration. Meanwhile, learning on coarsened graphs yields comparable performance to original graphs, and can even show superior performance over LLM-based graph reasoning owing to compact graph information. The code can be available at https://github.com/dazonglian/NOPE-main.

AI.tldr: NOPE proposes a non-selfish graph coarsening framework with linear memory and near-linear time, plus a faster NOPE* variant via local isotropy; shows large speedups and competitive downstream learning on coarsened graphs; code released.
AI.motivation: Overcome the high computational/memory cost of pairwise selfish node matching in traditional graph coarsening by adopting a collective, neighborhood-interference-based approach.
AI.method: Define NOPE with a non-selfish principle; design algorithm achieving linear memory; introduce NOPE* deriving from local isotropy to reduce interference evaluation from O(δ·d) to O(d).
AI.result: NOPE* yields 1.8–10× speedup over NOPE; surpasses baselines by 1–3 orders magnitude acceleration; learning on coarsened graphs comparable or better than original graphs and can surpass LLM-based reasoning in some cases; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Non-selfish graph coarsening is effective and scalable; the NOPE family provides practical, efficient graph hierarchy construction that preserves semantics, enabling efficient downstream learning.

title: KamonBench: A Grammar-Based Dataset for Evaluating Compositional Factor Recovery in Vision-Language Models
summary: Kamon (family crests) are an important part of Japanese culture and a natural test case for compositional visual recognition: each crest combines a small number of symbolic choices, but the space of possible descriptions is sparse. We introduce KamonBench, a grammar-based image-to-structure benchmark with 20,000 synthetic composite crests and auxiliary component examples. Each composite crest is paired with a formal kamon description language - "kamon yōgo" - description, a segmented Japanese analysis, an English translation, and a non-linguistic program code. Because each synthetic crest is generated from known factors, namely container, modifier, and motif, KamonBench supports evaluation beyond caption-level accuracy: direct program-code factor metrics, controlled factor-pair recombination splits, counterfactual motif-sensitivity groups under fixed container-modifier contexts, and linear probes of factor accessibility. We include baseline results for a ViT encoder/Transformer decoder and two VGG n-gram decoders, with and without learned positional masks. KamonBench therefore provides a controlled testbed for sparse compositional visual recognition and factor recovery in vision-language models.

AI.tldr: KamonBench is a grammar-based, 20k-sample synthetic crest benchmark that enables factor-level evaluation of sparse compositional recognition in vision-language models.
AI.motivation: To move beyond caption-level accuracy in crest recognition by enabling controlled, factor-aware evaluation (container/modifier/motif) and causal/ablation-style analyses.
AI.method: Generate 20,000 synthetic composite kamon crests from known factors (container, modifier, motif); provide kamon yōgo descriptions, segmented Japanese analysis, English translations, and non-linguistic program code. Create evaluation pipelines: direct program-code factor metrics, controlled factor-pair recombination splits, counterfactual motif-sensitivity groups under fixed contexts, and linear probes of factor accessibility. Baselines include ViT encoder/Transformer decoder and two VGG n-gram decoders, with/without learned positional masks.
AI.result: Baseline results demonstrate the feasibility of factor-level evaluation on a large synthetic corpus and provide a starting point for assessing factor recovery; the benchmark offers controlled tests beyond caption accuracy.
AI.conclusion: KamonBench provides a controlled testbed for sparse compositional visual recognition and factor recovery in vision-language models, enabling targeted analysis of how models use discrete factors to compose descriptions.

title: Offline Two-Player Zero-Sum Markov Games with KL Regularization
summary: We study the problem of learning Nash equilibria in offline two-player zero-sum Markov games. While existing approaches often rely on explicit pessimism to address distribution shift, we show that KL regularization alone suffices to stabilize learning and guarantee convergence. We first introduce Regularized Offline Sequential Equilibrium (ROSE), a theoretical framework that achieves a fast $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/n)$ convergence rate under \textit{unilateral concentrability}, improving over the standard $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/\sqrt{n})$ rates in unregularized settings. We then propose Sequential Offline Self-play Mirror Descent (SOS-MD), a practical model-free algorithm based on least-squares value estimation and iterative self-play updates. We prove that the last iterate of SOS-MD attains the same $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/n)$ statistical rate up to a vanishing optimization error of order $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/\sqrt{T})$ in the number of self-play iterations $T$.

AI.tldr: KL regularization alone stabilizes offline two-player zero-sum Markov game learning, achieving fast last-iterate convergence O(1/n) under unilateral concentrability via ROSE; SOS-MD is a practical model-free algorithm whose last iterate inherits the same rate up to an O(1/√T) optimization error.
AI.motivation: Address distribution shift and sample efficiency in offline multi-agent reinforcement learning without relying on explicit pessimism; establish conditions under which KL regularization yields fast convergence to Nash equilibria.
AI.method: Define Regularized Offline Sequential Equilibrium (ROSE) as a KL-regularized theoretical framework; develop Sequential Offline Self-play Mirror Descent (SOS-MD) using least-squares value estimation and iterative self-play updates.
AI.result: ROSE achieves a fast tilde O(1/n) convergence rate under unilateral concentrability, improving over the typical tilde O(1/√n) in unregularized offline settings. SOS-MD is model-free; the last iterate attains the same tilde O(1/n) rate up to a vanishing optimization error of order tilde O(1/√T) as the number of self-play iterations increases.
AI.conclusion: KL regularization suffices to stabilize offline equilibrium learning in two-player zero-sum Markov games, offering faster rates without explicit pessimism; SOS-MD provides a practical path to attain these rates, with potential extensions to broader settings and empirical validation.

title: Stylized Text-to-Motion Generation via Hypernetwork-Driven Low-Rank Adaptation
summary: Text-driven motion diffusion models are capable of generating realistic human motions, but text alone often struggles to express fine-level nuances of motion, commonly referred to as style. Recent approaches have tackled this challenge by attaching a style injection mechanism to a pretrained text-driven diffusion model. Existing stylization methods, however, either require style-specific fine-tuning of existing models or rely on heavy ControlNet-based architectures, limiting efficiency and generalization to unseen styles. We propose a lightweight style conditioning framework that dynamically modulates a pretrained diffusion model through hypernetwork-generated LoRA parameters. A style reference motion is encoded into a global style embedding, which is mapped by a hypernetwork to low-rank updates applied at each denoising step of the diffusion model. By structuring the style latent space with a supervised contrastive loss, our framework reliably captures diverse stylistic attributes, improves generalization to unseen styles, and supports optimization-based guidance without requiring predefined style categories. Experiments on the HumanML3D and 100STYLE datasets show state-of-the-art stylization results, while achieving improved stylization for unseen styles.

AI.tldr: A lightweight style conditioning framework uses a hypernetwork to generate LoRA-style updates for a pretrained diffusion model, enabling robust, generalizable text-driven motion stylization with a structured style latent space; achieves state-of-the-art results on HumanML3D and 100STYLE and better unseen-style generalization.
AI.motivation: Text-driven motion models struggle to express fine-grained style. Existing stylization approaches require heavy fine-tuning or large control networks, limiting efficiency and generalization. A lightweight, generalizable mechanism is needed.
AI.method: Encode a style reference motion into a global style embedding. A hypernetwork maps this embedding to low-rank LoRA updates applied at each denoising step of a pretrained diffusion model. Training enforces a structured style latent space via a supervised contrastive loss. The framework supports optimization-based guidance without predefined style categories.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art stylization on HumanML3D and 100STYLE datasets and shows improved generalization to unseen styles. The method is efficient due to low-rank, per-step updates and avoids heavy architectures like ControlNet.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, flexible solution for style-conditioned text-driven motion synthesis, improving generalization to unseen styles while maintaining efficiency and avoiding extensive fine-tuning of base models.

title: Understanding and Accelerating the Training of Masked Diffusion Language Models
