summary: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models (ARMs) for language modeling. However, MDMs are known to learn substantially more slowly than ARMs, which may become problematic when scaling MDMs to larger models. Therefore, we ask the following question: how can we accelerate standard MDM training while maintaining its final performance? To this end, we first provide a detailed analysis of why MDM training is slow. We find that the main factor is the locality bias of language: the predictive information for a token is concentrated in nearby positions. We further investigate how this bias slows learning and suggest a simple yet effective remedy: bell-shaped time sampling as a training strategy. Notably, MDMs trained with our training recipe reach the same validation negative log-likelihood (NLL) up to $\sim4\times$ faster than standard training on One Billion Word Benchmark (LM1B). We also show faster improvements in generative perplexity, zero-shot perplexity, and downstream task performance on various benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Bell-shaped time sampling speeds up masked diffusion model (MDM) training by mitigating locality bias, achieving ~4x faster convergence on LM1B while matching final validation NLL and improving perplexity and downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: MDMs train significantly slower than autoregressive models (ARMs); predictive information for a token is highly localized, slowing learning. A remedy is needed to accelerate MDM training without sacrificing final performance.
AI.method: 1) Analyze causes of slow MDM training, identifying locality bias as the main factor. 2) Propose bell-shaped time sampling as a training strategy to counteract this bias. 3) Empirically evaluate on language modeling benchmarks (e.g., LM1B) and assess gains in NLL, generative perplexity, zero-shot perplexity, and downstream tasks.
AI.result: MDMs trained with the proposed training recipe reach the same validation NLL about 4× faster than standard training on LM1B. Also observe faster improvements in generative perplexity, zero-shot perplexity, and downstream task performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Bell-shaped time sampling effectively mitigates the locality bias, enabling substantially faster MDM training without compromising final performance, thereby facilitating scaling of MDMs to larger models and broader application.

title: Drag within Prior Distribution: Text-Conditioned Point-Based Image Editing within Distribution Constraints
summary: Diffusion-based point editing methods have gained significant traction in image editing tasks due to their ability to manipulate image semantics and fine details by applying localized perturbations on the manifold of noise latent. However, these approaches face several limitations. Traditional point-based editing relies on pairs of handle and target points to define motion trajectories, which can introduce ambiguity or unnecessary alterations. Furthermore, when the distance between the handle and target points is large, the accumulated perturbations often cause the noise latent deviation from inversion score trajectory, resulting in unnatural artifacts. To address these issues in global editing tasks, we introduce a CLIP-based model to evaluate and guide intermediate editing steps, ensuring that the generated results remain both semantically aligned. Additionally, we propose a prior-preservation loss that constrains the optimized latent code to stay within the sampling space of the diffusion prior, improving consistency with the original data distribution, to ensure the model generates images along a familiar score trajectory. For fine-grained tasks, we present a directionally-weighted point tracking mechanism that steers the editing process toward the target direction within similar feature regions. This improves both the tracking accuracy and generation quality, while also reducing the editing time.

AI.tldr: Proposes CLIP-guided diffusion point editing with prior-preservation loss and directionally weighted point tracking to handle global and fine-grained edits, addressing ambiguity and artifact issues.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of standard diffusion point editing: ambiguity from handle-target trajectories, large perturbations causing latent-score drift artifacts, and the need for semantic consistency and data-distribution alignment in editing tasks.
AI.method: Introduce a CLIP-based evaluator to guide intermediate edits for semantic alignment; add a prior-preservation loss to keep the latent code within the diffusion prior's sampling space; implement a directionally-weighted point tracking mechanism for fine-grained edits to steer edits toward the target direction within similar feature regions.
AI.result: The abstract does not provide quantitative results; it claims improved semantic alignment, reduced artifacts, better tracking accuracy, and faster editing, with better consistency with the original data distribution.
AI.conclusion: The work presents an integrated framework combining semantic guidance (CLIP), latent-space constraints, and directional tracking to enhance both global and fine-grained diffusion-based point editing.

title: FeatCal: Feature Calibration for Post-Merging Models
summary: Model merging combines task experts into one model and avoids joint training, retraining, or deploying many expert models, but the merged model often still underperforms task experts. We study this performance gap through feature drift, the difference between features produced by the merged model and by the expert on the same input. Our theory decomposes this drift into upstream propagation and local mismatch, tracks how it propagates and combines through later layers in forward order, and links final feature drift to output drift. This view motivates FeatCal, which uses a small calibration set to calibrate the merged model weights layer by layer in forward order, reducing feature drift while staying close to merged weights and preserving the benefits of model merging. FeatCal uses an efficient closed-form solution to update model weights, with no gradient descent, iterative optimization, or extra modules. On the main CLIP and GLUE benchmarks, FeatCal beats Surgery and ProbSurgery, the closest post-merging calibration baselines: 85.5% vs. 77.0%/78.8% on CLIP-ViT-B/32 Task Arithmetic (TA) and 85.2% vs. 83.7%/82.2% on FLAN-T5-base GLUE. On CLIP-ViT-B/32, 8 examples per task reach 82.9%, and 256 examples per task take 53 seconds, about 4x faster than both baselines, showing better sample efficiency and lower calibration cost.

AI.tldr: FeatCal is a new post-merging calibration method that reduces feature drift by layer-wise forward-order calibration using a small calibration set, via a closed-form weight update, achieving strong performance improvements on CLIP and GLUE with faster calibration than baselines.
AI.motivation: Merged models often underperform task experts due to feature drift between the merged model and task-specific experts. Reducing this drift without retraining aims to preserve the benefits of model merging while closing the performance gap.
AI.method: The authors decompose feature drift into upstream propagation and local mismatch, analyze its forward propagation through layers, and propose FeatCal: a forward-order, layer-by-layer calibration using a small labeled set. Weight updates are computed in closed-form (no gradients) to minimize drift while staying close to the merged weights.
AI.result: FeatCal outperforms Surgery and ProbSurgery baselines on main CLIP and GLUE benchmarks. Specific gains: CLIP-ViT-B/32 Task Arithmetic: 85.5% vs 77.0%/78.8%; FLAN-T5-base GLUE: 85.2% vs 83.7%/82.2%. Efficiency: at 8 examples per task, 82.9% accuracy; 256 examples per task, 53 seconds, ~4x faster than baselines, indicating better sample efficiency and lower calibration cost.
AI.conclusion: FeatCal provides an efficient, non-gradient-based, layer-wise calibration method to mitigate feature drift in merged models, preserving merged weights and delivering practical performance gains on vision-language and NLP tasks.

title: Neural Surrogate Forward Modelling For Electrocardiology Without Explicit Intracellular Conductivity Tensor
comment: Accepted into the 9th International Conference on Computational and Mathematical Biomedical Engineering (CMBE2026)
summary: Accurate forward modelling is essential for non-invasive cardiac electrophysiology, particularly in atrial fibrillation, where electrical activation is highly disorganised. Conventional physics-based forward models require explicit specification of intracellular conductivity tensors, which are not directly measurable in clinical practice and introduce structural modelling errors. This proof-of-concept study presents a deep learning approach that learns a direct mapping from left atrial intracellular electrical potentials to far-field ECGs without requiring explicit intracellular conductivity inputs at inference time. Despite training only on 74 subjects, the model achieved an R2 of 0.949 \pm 0.037, highlighting potential to reduce structural uncertainty and improve non-invasive AF assessment.

AI.tldr: A deep learning model maps left atrial intracellular potentials directly to far-field ECGs, eliminating the need for explicit intracellular conductivity inputs at inference; trained on 74 subjects, achieving R2 ≈ 0.95.
AI.motivation: Reduce structural modelling uncertainty in forward models of atrial electrophysiology by removing reliance on unmeasurable intracellular conductivities, potentially improving non-invasive AF assessment.
AI.method: Supervised deep learning approach that takes left atrial intracellular electrical potentials as input and predicts far-field ECGs, with no conductivity inputs required at inference; trained on 74 subjects.
AI.result: R2 = 0.949 ± 0.037 on validation/test performance, indicating strong predictive accuracy for the forward mapping.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a DL-based forward model can bypass explicit conductivity inputs while preserving predictive fidelity, but requires further validation and larger datasets for clinical translation.

title: What Information Matters? Graph Out-of-Distribution Detection via Tri-Component Information Decomposition
summary: Graph neural networks are widely used for node classification, but they remain vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts in node features and graph structure. Prior work established that methods trained with standard supervised learning (SL) objectives tend to capture spurious signals from either features and/or structure, leaving the model fragile under distributional changes. To address this, we propose textscTide, a textbfnovel and effective underlineTri-Component underlineInformation underlineDecomposition framework that textbfexplicitly decomposes information into textitfeature-specific, structure-specific and joint components. textscTide aims to textbfpreserve only the label-relevant part of the joint information while textbffiltering out spurious feature- and structure-specific information, thereby enhancing the separation between in-distribution (ID) and OOD nodes. Beyond the framework, we provide theoretical and empirical analyses showing that an information bottleneck objective is preferable to standard SL for graph OOD detection, with higher ID confidence and a greater entropy gap between ID and OOD data. Extensive experiments across seven datasets confirm the efficacy of textscTide, achieving up to a 34% improvement in FPR95 over strong baselines while maintaining competitive ID accuracy.

AI.tldr: Tri-component information decomposition for GNNs (feature-specific, structure-specific, and joint). Uses an information bottleneck objective to filter spurious signals and preserve label-relevant joint information, improving OOD detection (FPR95 up to 34% better) while maintaining ID accuracy.
AI.motivation: GNNs trained with standard SL objectives capture spurious signals from features or structure, making them fragile under distributional shifts (OOD). A decomposition that preserves label-relevant joint information and filters out spurious signals can improve robustness.
AI.method: Introduce TextscTide (Tri-Component Information Decomposition) for graphs. Decomposes information into feature-specific, structure-specific, and joint components and optimizes an information bottleneck objective to retain label-relevant joint information while discarding spurious signals.
AI.result: Theoretical and empirical analyses show the information bottleneck is preferable to standard SL for graph OOD detection, yielding higher ID confidence and a larger entropy gap between ID and OOD. Empirically, across seven datasets, up to 34% improvement in FPR95 over strong baselines, with competitive ID accuracy.
AI.conclusion: TextscTide effectively enhances GNN OOD robustness by combining tri-component information decomposition with an information bottleneck objective, improving OOD detection without compromising ID performance.

title: GRIP-VLM: Group-Relative Importance Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language Models
summary: In Vision-Language Models (VLMs), processing a massive number of visual tokens incurs prohibitive computational overhead. While recent training-aware pruning methods attempt to selectively discard redundant tokens, they largely rely on continuous-gradient relaxations. However, visual token pruning is inherently a discrete, non-convex combinatorial problem; consequently, these continuous approximations frequently trap the optimization in sub-optimal local minima, especially under aggressive compression budgets. To overcome this fundamental bottleneck, we propose GRIP-VLM, a Group-Relative Importance Pruning framework driven by Reinforcement Learning. Rather than relying on smooth-gradient assumptions, GRIP-VLM formulates pruning as a Markov Decision Process, employing a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) paradigm anchored by supervised warm-up to directly explore the discrete selection space. Integrated with a budget-aware scorer, our lightweight agent dynamically evaluates per-token importance and adapts to arbitrary compression ratios without retraining. Extensive experiments across diverse multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that GRIP-VLM consistently outperforms heuristic and supervised-learning baselines, achieving a superior Pareto frontier and delivering up to a 15\% inference speedup at equal accuracy.

AI.tldr: Discrete RL-based token pruning for Vision-Language Models via Group Relative Policy Optimization; achieves up to 15% faster inference at equal accuracy with flexible compression budgets.
AI.motivation: Continuous-gradient relaxations used in prior pruning policies are ill-suited for inherently discrete, non-convex token selection in VLMs and can trap optimization under aggressive compression.
AI.method: Formulates token pruning as a Markov Decision Process and optimizes with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) coupled with supervised warm-up. A budget-aware scorer guides a lightweight RL agent to evaluate per-token importance and adapt to arbitrary compression ratios without retraining.
AI.result: GRIP-VLM consistently outperforms heuristic and supervised-learning baselines across diverse multimodal benchmarks, achieving a superior Pareto frontier and delivering up to 15% inference speedup at equal accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Discrete, budget-aware RL-based pruning is effective for VLM token reduction, enabling flexible, retrain-free adaptation to various compression targets while maintaining performance.

title: Large Language Models Lack Temporal Awareness of Medical Knowledge
summary: The existing methods for evaluating the medical knowledge of Large Language Models (LLMs) are largely based on atemporal examination-style benchmarks, while in reality, medical knowledge is inherently dynamic and continuously evolves as new evidence emerges and treatments are approved. Consequently, evaluating medical knowledge without a temporal context may provide an incomplete assessment of whether LLMs can accurately reason about time-specific medical knowledge. Moreover, most medical data are historical, requiring the models not only to recall the correct knowledge, but also to know when that knowledge is correct. To bridge the gap, we built TempoMed-Bench, the first-of-its-kind benchmark for evaluating the temporal awareness of the LLMs in the medical domain through evolving guideline knowledge. Based on the TempoMed-Bench, our evaluation analysis first reveals that LLMs lack temporal awareness in medical knowledge through the key findings: (1) model performance on up-to-date medical knowledge exhibits a gradual linear decline over time rather than a sharp knowledge-cutoff behavior, suggesting that parametric medical knowledge is not strictly bounded by knowledge cutoffs; (2) LLMs consistently struggle more with recalling outdated historical medical knowledge than with up-to-date recommendations: accuracy of historical knowledge is only 25.37%-53.89% of up-to-date knowledge, indicating potential knowledge forgetting effects during training; and (3) LLMs often exhibit temporally inconsistent behaviors, where predictions fluctuate irregularly across neighboring years. We also show that the temporal awareness problem is a challenge that cannot be easily solved when integrated with agentic search tools (-3.15%-14.14%). This work highlights an important yet underexplored challenge and motivates future research on developing LLMs that can better encode time-specific medical knowledge.

AI.tldr: TempoMed-Bench creates a temporal benchmark for medical knowledge to assess LLMs' temporal awareness; findings show gradual time-based accuracy decline, weak recall of historical knowledge versus current knowledge, and temporal inconsistencies; adding agentic search yields limited or negative gains.
AI.motivation: Medical knowledge evolves continuously; static benchmarks with fixed cutoffs fail to capture when knowledge is correct, prompting the need for time-aware evaluation in LLMs.
AI.method: Construct TempoMed-Bench built on evolving medical guideline knowledge; evaluate LLMs across time, comparing up-to-date versus historical knowledge; analyze temporal consistency and the impact of agentic search tools.
AI.result: LLMs exhibit a linear-like decline in up-to-date medical knowledge over time (rather than a sharp cutoff); historical knowledge recall is substantially worse, at 25.37%-53.89% of up-to-date accuracy; predictions fluctuate across neighboring years, showing temporal instability; integrating agentic search yields modest to negative improvements (-3.15% to -14.14%).
AI.conclusion: Temporal awareness in medical knowledge is an important, underexplored challenge. Parametric knowledge is not strictly bounded by static cutoffs, and future work should enable LLMs to encode and reason with time-specific information.

title: Backbone is All You Need: Assessing Vulnerabilities of Frozen Foundation Models in Synthetic Image Forensics
summary: As AI-generated synthetic images become increasingly realistic, Vision Transformers (ViTs) have emerged as a cornerstone of modern deepfake detection. However, the prevailing reliance on frozen, pre-trained backbones introduces a subtle yet critical vulnerability. In this work, we present the Surrogate Iterative Adversarial Attack (SIAA), a gray-box attack that exploits knowledge of the detector's ViT backbone alone and operates entirely within the target detector's feature space to craft highly effective adversarial examples. Through our experiments, involving multiple ViT-based detectors and diverse gray-box scenarios, including few-shot learning, complete training misalignment and attack transferability tests, we demonstrate that this vulnerability consistently yields high attack success rates, often approaching white-box performance. By doing so, we reveal that backbone knowledge alone is sufficient to undermine detector reliability, highlighting the urgent need for more resilient defenses in adversarial multimedia forensics.

AI.tldr: Gray-box backone-based adversarial attack (SIAA) targets ViT-based detectors by crafting perturbations in the detector's feature space using only backbone knowledge, achieving high success rates and exposing robustness weaknesses.
AI.motivation: Assess the robustness of ViT-based deepfake detectors that rely on frozen, pre-trained backbones and identify vulnerabilities that persist under partial (gray-box) knowledge.
AI.method: Introduce Surrogate Iterative Adversarial Attack (SIAA), a gray-box attack operating within the target detector's feature space and using only knowledge of the ViT backbone. The attack is evaluated across multiple ViT-based detectors under few-shot, training misalignment, and transferability scenarios.
AI.result: SIAA achieves high attack success rates, often close to white-box performance, across diverse gray-box conditions, demonstrating backbone knowledge alone is sufficient to compromise detector reliability.
AI.conclusion: Backbone-only information can undermine ViT-based deepfake detectors, underscoring the need for more robust defenses that do not rely solely on frozen backbones and for defenses addressing feature-space vulnerabilities.

title: Bridging Domain Gaps with Target-Aligned Generation for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cross-domain offline reinforcement learning aims to adapt a policy from a source domain to a target domain using only pre-collected datasets, where environment dynamics may differ. A key challenge is to leverage source data while reducing distributional mismatch, particularly when the target dataset is extremely limited. To address this, we propose Target-aligned Coverage Expansion (TCE), a framework that decides how source data should be used, either by directly incorporating target-near transitions or by expanding state coverage through target-aligned generation, guided by theoretical analysis. TCE builds on a dual score-based generative model to synthesize target-consistent transitions over an expanded state region. Extensive experiments across diverse cross-domain environments show that TCE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-domain offline RL baselines.

AI.tldr: A cross-domain offline RL method (TCE) uses a dual score-based generator to align source data with a target domain by either using target-near transitions or expanding coverage via target-aligned generation, guided by theory; it outperforms existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Mitigate distributional mismatch in cross-domain offline RL when target data are extremely scarce, by leveraging source data while ensuring target-consistency.
AI.method: Target-aligned Coverage Expansion (TCE) framework that selects how to use source data. It employs a dual score-based generative model to synthesize target-consistent transitions over an expanded state region, enabling both direct use of target-near transitions and generation-based coverage expansion; decisions are guided by theoretical analysis.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse cross-domain environments show that TCE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-domain offline RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: TCE provides a principled, theory-guided mechanism to leverage source data for cross-domain offline RL under severe target-data scarcity, achieving robust improvements over existing methods.

title: PreFIQs: Face Image Quality Is What Survives Pruning
summary: Face Image Quality Assessment (FIQA) evaluates the utility of a face image for automated face recognition (FR) systems. In this work, we propose PreFIQs, an unsupervised and training-free FIQA framework grounded in the Pruning Identified Exemplar (PIE) hypothesis. We hypothesize that low-utility face images rely disproportionately on fragile network parameters, resulting in larger geometric displacement of their embeddings under model sparsification. Accordingly, PreFIQs quantifies image utility as the Euclidean distance between L2-normalized embeddings extracted from a pre-trained FR model and its pruned counterpart. We provide a first-order theoretical justification via a Jacobian-vector product analysis, demonstrating that this empirical drift serves as a computationally efficient approximation of the exact geometric sensitivity of the latent embedding manifold. Extensive experiments across eight benchmarks and four FR models demonstrate that PreFIQs achieves competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art FIQA methods, including establishing new state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks, without any training or supervision. These results validate parameter sparsification as a principled and practically efficient signal for face image utility, and demonstrate that quality is, in essence, what survives pruning.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised, training-free FIQA using pruning-induced embedding drift (PreFIQs); achieves competitive or SOTA performance across benchmarks without training.
AI.motivation: Address the need for training-free FIQA signals by leveraging model sparsification; hypothesize that low-utility images rely on fragile parameters, causing larger embedding drift under pruning.
AI.method: Extract L2-normalized embeddings from a pre-trained FR model, prune the model, extract embeddings again, and measure Euclidean distance between the full and pruned embeddings as image quality. Provides first-order justification via Jacobian-vector product analysis. No training or supervision; evaluated on eight benchmarks and four FR models.
AI.result: PreFIQs achieves competitive or superior performance relative to state-of-the-art FIQA methods and establishes new SOTA on several benchmarks, without any training or supervision.
AI.conclusion: Parameter sparsification offers a principled, efficient signal for face image utility; quality is essentially what survives pruning; the approach is training-free and unsupervised.

title: Ergodic Trajectory Design by Learned Pushforward Maps: Provable Coverage via Conditional Flow Matching
summary: Designing continuous trajectories whose time-averaged occupancy provably matches a prescribed spatial density (the \emph{ergodic coverage} problem) is central to UAV-assisted data collection and sensing, robotic exploration, and mobile monitoring. For flying agents in particular, this challenge is acute: trajectories must balance coverage fidelity against tight energy budgets, no-fly zones, and acceleration limits. Existing methods either re-optimize each trajectory online (with cost growing in the horizon and re-running for every target, agent, and realization) or rely on bespoke analytical constructions that must be re-derived for each new constraint. We propose a \emph{epushforward} framework that decouples ergodicity from density matching: an analytic latent trajectory provides exact uniform ergodicity on a simple annular domain, and a single map, learned offline by optimal-transport conditional flow matching, transports this latent occupancy onto the prescribed target density. The composed trajectory is then asymptotically ergodic with respect to the learned pushforward distribution, with deviation from the target controlled by the flow-matching training loss. Once trained for a given target density and constraint set, the map serves an unbounded number of trajectories and a multi-agent fleet without per-agent retraining, and many differentiable operational constraints (no-fly zones, acceleration ceilings, or fairness penalties) enter as additive soft penalties in the training loss without re-deriving the design. We prove three results (an acceleration-energy bound, an $O(1/\sqrt{K})$ ergodic convergence rate in the number of trajectory cycles $K$, and an approximation-error bound) that combine into an end-to-end coverage bound estimable from CFM training diagnostics (certified given an architectural Lipschitz bound on $v_θ$).

AI.tldr: An epushforward framework decouples ergodicity from density matching for ergodic coverage. It uses an analytic latent trajectory with exact uniform ergodicity on an annulus, and a single offline-learnt transport map (via optimal-transport conditional flow matching) to push this latent occupancy onto a target density. The resulting trajectory is asymptotically ergodic w.r.t. the pushforward distribution; constraints enter as soft penalties in training. The approach yields an acceleration-energy bound, an O(1/√K) ergodic convergence rate, and an approximation-error bound, enabling an end-to-end, diagnosable coverage bound. This map reusably serves many trajectories and agents without retraining.
AI.motivation: To address ergodic coverage for UAVs balancing fidelity with energy, constraints (no-fly zones, acceleration limits), and scalability, by avoiding online re-optimization or bespoke designs for each constraint.
AI.method: Introduce a latent analytic trajectory with uniform ergodicity on a simple annulus. Learn a single transport map offline using optimal-transport conditional flow matching (CFM) to push the latent occupancy to the target density. Compose the latent trajectory with the learned map. Treat differentiable constraints as additive soft penalties in the training loss. Prove three results: an acceleration-energy bound, an O(1/√K) ergodic convergence rate in trajectory cycles K, and an approximation-error bound, yielding an end-to-end, diagnosable coverage bound via CFM diagnostics under a Lipschitz bound on v_theta.
AI.result: The composed trajectory is asymptotically ergodic w.r.t. the learned pushforward distribution. After training for a target density and constraints, the map is reusable for unlimited trajectories and multi-agent fleets. The theoretical bounds combine to give a verifiable end-to-end coverage bound.
AI.conclusion: The epushforward framework decouples ergodicity from density matching, enabling offline, reusable design that accommodates multiple agents and constraints with soft penalties, and provides certifiable coverage guarantees through training diagnostics.

title: Fast and Compact Graph Cuts for the Boykov-Kolmogorov Algorithm
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures, submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Computing a minimum $s$-$t$ cut in a graph is a solution to a wide range of computer vision problems, and is often done using the Boykov-Kolmogorov (BK) algorithm. In this paper, we revisit the BK algorithm from both a theoretical and practical point of view. We improve the analysis of the time complexity of the BK algorithm to $O(mn|C|)$ and propose a new algorithm, the fast and compact BK (fcBK) algorithm, with a time complexity of $O(m|C|)$, where $m$, $n$, and $|C|$ are the number of edges, number of vertices, and the capacity of the cut, respectively. We additionally propose a compact graph representation that allows our implementation to find a minimum $s$-$t$ cut in a graph with upwards of $10^9$ vertices and $10^{10}$ edges on a machine with 128 GB of memory. We find our implementation of the BK algorithm to be the fastest available implementation of the BK algorithm when evaluating on a comprehensive set of benchmark datasets, highlighting the importance of memory-efficient implementations. We make our implementations publicly available for further research and implementation development within minimum $s$-$t$ cut algorithms.

AI.tldr: Reanalyzes the Boykov–Kolmogorov (BK) min s-t cut algorithm, tightening the theoretical bound to O(mn|C|) and introducing a fast and compact BK (fcBK) with O(m|C|) time; proposes a compact graph representation enabling extremely large graphs; reports that the implementation is the fastest BK variant on benchmarks and releases the code.
AI.motivation: BK is a cornerstone for many vision problems but suffers from heavy theoretical bounds and memory inefficiency. There is a need for tighter analysis, faster practical variants, and memory-efficient representations to scale to very large graphs.
AI.method: Provide a tighter theoretical analysis of BK to obtain O(mn|C|); develop fcBK with O(m|C|) time; design a compact graph representation; implement and benchmark across diverse datasets; release publicly.
AI.result: Theoretical bound improved to O(mn|C|); fcBK achieves O(m|C|) time; compact graph representation enables extremely large graphs (up to 10^9 vertices and 10^10 edges) on 128 GB memory; implementation reportedly the fastest BK variant on benchmarks; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: fcBK, together with the compact graph representation, offers scalable and memory-efficient minimum s-t cut computation for large-scale vision problems, with practical implications for real-world datasets and future research.

title: Local Inverse Geometry Can Be Amortized
comment: Preprint. 21 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/AadityaKachhadiya/deceptron
summary: Nonlinear inverse problems often trade inexpensive but fragile first-order updates against curvature-aware methods such as Gauss-Newton and Levenberg-Marquardt, which obtain stronger directions by repeatedly solving Jacobian-based linearized systems. We propose a learned alternative: amortize local inverse geometry into a reusable reverse operator. Our framework learns a bidirectional surrogate, Deceptron, and deploys it through D-IPG (Deceptron Inverse-Preconditioned Gradient), an iterative solver that pulls residual-corrected measurement-space proposals back to latent space. The key mechanism is a Jacobian Composition Penalty (JCP), which trains the reverse Jacobian to act as a local left inverse of the forward Jacobian; its runtime counterpart, RJCP, measures the same inverse-consistency error along optimization trajectories. We prove that D-IPG is first-order equivalent to damped Gauss-Newton under local pseudoinverse consistency, with deviation controlled by composition error and conditioning. Across seven PDE inverse-problem benchmarks, D-IPG outperforms standard baselines, achieves 94.8% mean success across the six-problem reliability suite, and reaches comparable or better recovery quality at up to 77x lower inference-time solve cost on the main benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Learned inverse solver: Deceptron with D-IPG amortizes local inverse geometry via a bidirectional surrogate, using Jacobian Composition Penalty (JCP) and its runtime RJCP to enforce a local left inverse of the forward Jacobian. The authors prove first-order equivalence to damped Gauss-Newton under local pseudoinverse consistency and demonstrate strong empirical gains (94.8% mean success, up to 77x faster solves) across seven PDE inverse problems.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear inverse problems typically trade cheap first-order updates for expensive curvature-aware methods. An amortized, reusable reverse operator could accelerate convergence while preserving accuracy by approximating the local inverse geometry of the forward model.
AI.method: Introduce Deceptron, a bidirectional surrogate; deploy D-IPG (Deceptron Inverse-Preconditioned Gradient) as an iterative solver that maps residual-corrected measurement-space proposals back to latent space. Train reverse Jacobian via Jacobian Composition Penalty (JCP) to act as a local left inverse of the forward Jacobian. RJCP measures inverse-consistency error along optimization trajectories at runtime.
AI.result: Theoretically, D-IPG is first-order equivalent to damped Gauss-Newton under local pseudoinverse consistency, with deviation governed by composition error and conditioning. Empirically, across seven PDE inverse-problem benchmarks, D-IPG outperforms standard baselines, achieves 94.8% mean success over six problems, and attains comparable or better recovery with up to 77x lower inference-time solve cost on main benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Amortizing local inverse geometry via a learned bidirectional surrogate yields competitive accuracy and substantial speedups in nonlinear inverse problems, validating a practical path for fast, reliable learned solvers built on forward Jacobian structure.

title: Bayesian In Vivo Tracking of Synapses using Joint Poisson Deconvolution and Diffeomorphic Registration
summary: Synapses are densely packed submicron structures that dynamically reorganize during learning and memory formation. Longitudinal \textit{in vivo} imaging of fluorescently tagged synaptic receptors offers a promising opportunity to study large-scale synaptic dynamics and how these processes are disrupted in neurological disease. However, in vivo imaging with 2-photon microscopy uses low laser power and therefore suffers from low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and high shot noise, nonlinear tissue motion between days, nonstationary fluctuations in synaptic fluorescence, and significant blur induced by the microscope point spread function (PSF). Together, these factors make it challenging to detect and track synapses, especially in regions with high synaptic density. This paper presents a novel template-based framework for modeling synapses as varying luminance point sources that move under a nonlinear tissue deformation. Taking a unified Bayesian approach, we apply this model to microscopy data by deriving a posterior that incorporates a diffeomorphic mapping for domain warping, a Gaussian point spread function for the imaging process, and a Poisson observation model for raw photon counts. The Bayesian solution simultaneously: (1) Constructs a probabilistic template of synapse locations, (2) denoises and deconvolves the image data, (3) infers fluorescence intensities, (4) performs diffeomorphic image registration to correct for tissue motion, and (5) provides confidence regions for these parameter estimates. We demonstrate the framework on both a 2D+t simulated dataset and a 3D+t longitudinal \textit{in vivo} microscopy dataset of fluorescent synapses imaged in a mouse over two weeks.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian, template-based framework for in vivo 2-photon synapse imaging that models synapses as moving luminance point sources under nonlinear tissue deformation, PSF blur, and Poisson noise, enabling joint template construction, denoising/deconvolution, intensity estimation, motion correction, and uncertainty quantification; validated on 2D+t simulated and 3D+t in vivo mouse data.
AI.motivation: High-density synaptic imaging via 2P in vivo suffers from low SNR, shot noise, motion, fluorescence fluctuations, and PSF blur, complicating detection/tracking of synapses, especially in dense regions.
AI.method: Unified Bayesian approach: model synapses as varying luminance point sources with diffeomorphic warping; observation model combines Gaussian PSF and Poisson photon counts; compute posterior to jointly estimate synapse template, denoise/deconvolve, infer intensities, perform motion correction, and quantify uncertainty.
AI.result: Posterior yields probabilistic synapse template, denoised/deconvolved images, inferred fluorescence intensities, diffeomorphic registration, and confidence regions; demonstrated on 2D+t simulated data and 3D+t in vivo mouse data over two weeks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a comprehensive framework for robust synapse detection/tracking under challenging imaging conditions and provides uncertainty estimates, enabling more reliable studies of synaptic dynamics in learning and disease.

title: Spectral Flattening Is All Muon Needs: How Orthogonalization Controls Learning Rate and Convergence
summary: Muon orthogonalizes the momentum buffer before each update, replacing its singular values with ones via Newton-Schulz iterations. This simple change lets Muon tolerate far larger learning rates and converge faster than other optimizers, but why? We show that the mechanism is spectral flattening, and develop two results around it. First, we prove that Muon's maximal stable step size scales with the average singular value of the gradient rather than the largest, which bottlenecks standard gradient descent. Second, we recast Muon as a preconditioned gradient method and show, under a Kronecker-factored curvature model, that it improves the effective convergence factor, with the improvement controlled by the spectrum of the gradient covariance. Extensive experiments validate both results: Muon remains stable at learning rates that cause SGD to diverge within the first few iterations, and reaches accuracy milestones several epochs earlier even at identical step sizes. Taken together, our results offer a principled, geometric explanation for Muon's empirical success.

AI.tldr: Muon stabilizes training by spectral flattening: it forces the momentum buffer to have uniform singular values, enabling much larger learning rates and faster convergence. Theoretical results show the maximal stable step scales with the average gradient singular value, and Muon can be viewed as a preconditioned gradient method under a Kronecker-factored curvature model; improvements depend on the gradient covariance spectrum. Experiments confirm stability beyond SGD and earlier attainment of accuracy milestones.
AI.motivation: Explain Muon's empirical success and provide a principled, geometric understanding of why spectral flattening enables larger steps and faster convergence.
AI.method: Orthogonalize and spectral-flatten the momentum buffer by replacing its singular values with ones using Newton-Schulz iterations; prove two results: (i) step-size stability scales with average gradient singular value; (ii) reinterpret Muon as a preconditioned gradient method under a Kronecker-factored curvature model, with improvement governed by gradient-covariance spectrum; validate with extensive experiments.
AI.result: Muon remains stable at learning rates that cause SGD to diverge early; reaches accuracy milestones several epochs earlier at identical step sizes; the variance in performance aligns with the gradient spectrum, supporting the proposed mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Spectral flattening provides a principled geometric explanation for Muon's empirical success and suggests a design principle for optimizers: shaping the spectrum of the momentum/gradient interaction can dramatically affect stability and convergence speed.

title: OP4KSR: One-Step Patch-Free 4K Super-Resolution with Periodic Artifact Suppression
summary: Diffusion-based real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR) has achieved remarkable perceptual quality; however, directly super-resolving images to 4K remains limited by extreme memory consumption. Consequently, prior methods adopt patch-based inference, sacrificing global context and introducing semantic confusion, spatial inconsistency, and severe latency. We propose OP4KSR, a one-step patch-free 4K SR approach built upon the powerful Flux backbone. By leveraging the extreme-compression F16 VAE, OP4KSR makes 4K SR inference tractable under practical GPU budgets, preserving global spatial-semantic coherence while enabling highly efficient inference. However, adapting this one-step architecture intrinsically triggers severe periodic artifacts. We trace this to a RoPE base frequency allocation mismatch and intra-token spatial ambiguity, both exacerbated by the lack of iterative refinement. To suppress these artifacts, we couple RoPE base frequency rescaling (RFR) with an autocorrelation-based periodicity loss ($\mathcal{L}_\text{AP}$). Furthermore, we curate a dedicated training dataset alongside three benchmarks (one synthetic and two real-world) to advance 4K SR research. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OP4KSR achieves competitive perceptual quality with efficient inference, generating a $4096\times4096$ output in only 5.75 seconds on a single NVIDIA H20 GPU.

AI.tldr: OP4KSR is a one-step, patch-free 4K SR method that uses Flux backbone and an extreme-compression F16 VAE to enable memory-efficient inference, with RoPE base frequency rescaling and an autocorrelation-based periodicity loss to suppress artifacts; includes a dedicated training dataset and three benchmarks; achieves 4K output in 5.75s on a single H20 GPU and reports competitive perceptual quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome memory bottlenecks in diffusion-based Real-ISR when producing 4K outputs, which patch-based methods handle poorly due to loss of global context, by proposing a patch-free, single-pass approach that preserves global spatial-semantic coherence.
AI.method: One-step patch-free SR architecture built on the Flux backbone; uses extreme-compression F16 VAE to render 4K inference tractable on practical GPUs; artifact suppression via RoPE base frequency rescaling (RFR) combined with an autocorrelation-based periodicity loss (L_AP); curated training dataset and three benchmarks (1 synthetic, 2 real-world).
AI.result: Demonstrates competitive perceptual quality with significantly efficient inference; capable of generating 4096×4096 outputs in about 5.75 seconds on a single NVIDIA H20 GPU across evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: OP4KSR shows that a patch-free, single-pass 4K SR pipeline is viable and can maintain global coherence while delivering practical inference speed; introduces RFR and L_AP to mitigate diffusion-induced periodic artifacts and contributes new training data and benchmarks to advance 4K SR research.

title: Bayesian Nonparametric Mixed-Effect ODEs with Gaussian Processes
summary: Dynamical modelling is central to many scientific domains, including pharmacometrics, systems biology, physiology, and epidemiology. In these settings, heterogeneity is often intrinsic: different subjects or units follow related but distinct continuous-time dynamics. Classical nonlinear mixed-effects Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) models address this by combining population-level structure with subject-specific effects, but they rely on a parametric vector field and are therefore vulnerable to structural misspecification and unmodelled mechanisms. This motivates nonparametric approaches that can retain principled uncertainty quantification, yet existing nonparametric ODE methods typically assume a single shared dynamical system rather than an explicit mixed-effect hierarchy over subject-specific dynamics. We propose MEGPODE, a Bayesian nonparametric mixed-effect ODE model in which each subject's vector field is decomposed into a shared population component and a subject-specific deviation, both endowed with Gaussian process (GP) priors. To avoid repeated ODE solves per subject during training, we combine state-space GP trajectory priors with virtual collocation observations, yielding Kalman-smoothing trajectory updates and closed-form regressions for the vector fields. Across controlled heterogeneous ODE benchmarks spanning oscillatory, biomedical systems, MEGPODE improves population-field recovery and subject-level trajectory prediction relative to strong baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces MEGPODE, a Bayesian nonparametric mixed-effect ODE model using Gaussian process priors to capture population-level and subject-specific dynamics, with efficient training via state-space GP trajectory priors and virtual collocation (Kalman smoothing) to avoid per-subject ODE solves.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity in continuous-time dynamics across subjects, overcoming structural misspecification in parametric nonlinear mixed-effects ODEs and the rigidity of single-shared nonparametric ODEs.
AI.method: Subject-specific vector fields are decomposed into a shared population component plus a subject-specific deviation, both with GP priors. Training uses state-space GP trajectory priors and virtual collocation observations, enabling Kalman-smoothing trajectory updates and closed-form regressions for the vector fields, avoiding repeated ODE solves.
AI.result: On controlled heterogeneous ODE benchmarks (including oscillatory and biomedical systems), MEGPODE achieves improved population-field recovery and subject-level trajectory prediction compared with strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, well-calibrated nonparametric mixed-effects framework for dynamic systems, improving robustness to misspecification and enabling principled uncertainty quantification while maintaining computational efficiency through Kalman-based updates.

title: Z-Order Transformer for Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting
summary: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled significant progress in photorealistic novel view synthesis. However, traditional 3DGS relies on a slow, iterative optimization process, which limits its use in scenarios demanding real-time results. To overcome this bottleneck, recent feed-forward methods aim to predict Gaussian attributes directly from images, but they often struggle with the redundancy of Gaussian primitives and rendering quality. In this work, we introduce a transformer-based architecture specifically designed for feed-forward Gaussian Splatting. Our key insight is that spatial and semantic relationships among Gaussians can be effectively captured through a sparse attention mechanism, enabled by a Z-order strategy that organizes the unstructured Gaussian set into a spatially coherent sequence. Furthermore, we incorporate this Z-order strategy to adaptively suppress redundancy while preserving critical structural details. This allows the transformer to efficiently model context, compress Gaussian primitives, and predict Gaussian attributes in a single forward pass. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves fast and high-quality novel view synthesis with fewer Gaussian primitives.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based feed-forward pipeline for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) using sparse attention and a Z-order ordering to organize Gaussians. It aims to suppress redundancy, capture spatial-semantic context, and predict Gaussian attributes in one forward pass for fast, high-quality novel view synthesis with fewer Gaussians.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiency of slow iterative optimization in traditional 3DGS and the redundancy/quality trade-offs in prior feed-forward methods, enabling real-time rendering with compact Gaussian representations.
AI.method: Introduce a transformer architecture that uses a sparse attention mechanism guided by a Z-order-based organization of an unstructured Gaussian set into a spatially coherent sequence. The Z-order strategy is used to adaptively suppress redundancy and preserve structure, allowing single-pass prediction of Gaussian attributes and efficient context modeling and compression of primitives.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate fast, high-quality novel view synthesis with a reduced number of Gaussian primitives compared to prior methods, demonstrating both efficiency and fidelity improvements in feed-forward 3DGS.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Z-order-aware transformer for feed-forward Gaussian Splatting improves efficiency and rendering quality by effectively modeling spatial-semantic relations among Gaussians and suppressing redundancy, enabling single-pass prediction with fewer primitives.

title: Margin-calibrated Classifier Guidance for Property-driven Synthesis Planning
summary: Synthesis planning seeks an efficient sequence of chemical reactions that produce a target molecule. Typically, a pretrained single-step (autoregressive) retrosynthesis model is repeatedly invoked to generate such a sequence. Classifier guidance can, in principle, help steer the output of single-step model toward reactions that satisfy specific constraints or accommodate chemist's preferences during inference without having to retrain the autoregressive generator. We expose the insufficiency of auxiliary classifiers trained with cross-entropy loss to override the unconditional token-level distributions learned from typical sparse single-disconnection reaction datasets. We overcome this issue with a novel method called Sequence Completion Ranking (SCR), which employs contrastive argumentation and a margin-based loss to calibrate the classifier so that it can meaningfully discriminate between continuations during decoding. We formally establish that margin-calibrated classifiers can expand the set of property-satisfying sequences reachable under guided beam search. Empirically, on USPTO-190, given chemist-specified guidance targets, SCR substantially improves multi-step solve rates from $16.8\%$ (unguided generator) to $78.4\%$ with reaction-type guidance and $95.3\%$ with Tanimoto guidance, unlocking valid routes for 33 targets ($17.4\%$) previously unsolvable with baselines. Our method also effectively closes the long-standing diversity gap between template-free and template-based methods.

AI.tldr: SCR improves guided retrosynthesis by margin-calibrated, contrastive classifier training to steer a single-step generator without retraining. It expands reachable, constraint-satisfying sequences during decoding and narrows the diversity gap between template-free and template-based methods.
AI.motivation: Conventional auxiliary classifiers trained with cross-entropy fail to override the unconditional token-level distributions learned from sparse disconnection reaction datasets. There is a need to steer autoregressive retrosynthesis models toward constraint- or preference-satisfying reactions during inference without retraining the generator.
AI.method: Introduce Sequence Completion Ranking (SCR) that uses contrastive argumentation and a margin-based loss to calibrate the classifier so that it can discriminate between continuation options during decoding. Formal results show that margin-calibrated classifiers expand the set of property-satisfying sequences under guided beam search.
AI.result: Empirically on USPTO-190, with chemist-specified guidance targets, SCR raises multi-step solve rates from 16.8% (unguided) to 78.4% with reaction-type guidance and 95.3% with Tanimoto guidance, enabling valid routes for 33 targets (17.4%) previously unsolvable. It also reduces the diversity gap between template-free and template-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Margin-calibrated SCR enables effective constraint-guided retrosynthesis without retraining the autoregressive generator, improving solve rates and expanding accessible routes while closing the diversity gap.

title: FedHPro: Federated Hyper-Prototype Learning via Gradient Matching
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative training of distributed clients while protecting privacy. To enhance generalization capability in FL, prototype-based FL is in the spotlight, since shared global prototypes offer semantic anchors for aligning client-specific local prototypes. However, existing methods update global prototypes at the prototype-level via averaging local prototypes or refining global anchors, which often leads to semantic drift across clients and subsequently yields a misaligned global signal. To alleviate this issue, we introduce hyper-prototypes, defined by a set of learnable global class-wise prototypes to preserve underlying semantic knowledge across clients. The hyper-prototypes are optimized via gradient matching to align with class-relevant characteristics distilled directly from clients' real samples, rather than prototype-level descriptors. We further propose FedHPro, a Federated Hyper-Prototype Learning framework, to leverage hyper-prototypes to promote inter-class separability via mutual-contrastive learning with client-specific margin, while encouraging intra-class uniformity through a consistency penalty. Comprehensive experiments under diverse heterogeneous scenarios confirm that 1) hyper-prototypes produce a more semantically consistent global signal, and 2) FedHPro achieves state-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/mala-lab/FedHPro}{https://github.com/mala-lab/FedHPro}.

AI.tldr: Introduces hyper-prototypes—learnable global class-wise prototypes—within FedHPro to reduce semantic drift in federated learning and achieve state-of-the-art generalization.
AI.motivation: Prototype-level aggregation causes semantic drift across clients; a semantically consistent global signal is needed to improve cross-client generalization.
AI.method: Hyper-prototypes are learned via gradient matching from clients' real samples; FedHPro uses mutual-contrastive learning with client-specific margins to enhance inter-class separation and a consistency penalty to promote intra-class uniformity.
AI.result: Empirical results under heterogeneous FL show improved semantic consistency of the global signal and state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Hyper-prototypes effectively preserve semantic knowledge and FedHPro delivers superior generalization in federated learning under heterogeneous settings.

title: MLGIB: Multi-Label Graph Information Bottleneck for Expressive and Robust Message Passing
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) suffer from over-squashing in deep message passing, where information from exponentially growing neighborhoods is compressed into fixed-dimensional representations. We show that this issue becomes a distinct failure mode in multi-label graphs: neighboring nodes often share only limited labels while differing across many irrelevant ones, causing predictive signals to be diluted by noisy label information. To address this challenge, we propose the Multi-Label Graph Information Bottleneck (MLGIB), which formulates multi-label message passing as constrained information transmission under irrelevant label noise. MLGIB balances expressiveness and robustness by preserving predictive label signals while suppressing irrelevant noise. Specifically, it constructs a Markovian dependence space and derives tractable variational bounds, where the lower bound maximizes mutual information with target labels and the upper bound constrains redundant source information. These bounds lead to an end-to-end label-aware message-passing architecture. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over existing methods, validating the effectiveness and generality of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: MLGIB introduces a label-aware information bottleneck for multi-label graph networks to combat over-squashing. It filters noisy label information during message passing via variational bounds, enabling robust, end-to-end learning.
AI.motivation: Over-squashing in deep GNNs is aggravated in multi-label graphs where neighboring nodes share few labels but differ in irrelevant ones; noise from unrelated labels dilutes predictive signals.
AI.method: Propose Multi-Label Graph Information Bottleneck (MLGIB) that defines a Markovian dependence space and derives tractable variational bounds: a lower bound maximizing mutual information with target labels and an upper bound constraining redundant (irrelevant) source information. This yields an end-to-end, label-aware message-passing architecture.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks show consistent improvements over existing methods, indicating effectiveness and generality of the framework in mitigating label-noise-induced degradation.
AI.conclusion: MLGIB provides a principled, scalable approach to preserving predictive label signals while suppressing irrelevant label noise in multi-label graphs, with broad applicability to graph representation learning.

title: Neural Video Compression with Domain Transfer
comment: Accepted to ISCAS 2026 as an oral paper
summary: Content-adaptive compression has always been a key direction in neural video coding (NVC), aiming to mitigate the domain gap between training and testing data. Such gaps often arise from distributional discrepancies between training and inference data, which may cause noticeable performance degradation when the testing content differs from the training distribution. To tackle this challenge, we propose DCVC-DT, a domain transfer enhanced neural video compression framework. Specifically, we design a lightweight online domain transfer (DT) mechanism that dynamically adapts the encoded latent representation during inference, effectively bridging the domain gap without modifying the encoder or decoder parameters. In addition, we develop a frame-level dynamic RD (Rate and Distortion) adjustment scheme that actively regulates the ratio of R and D in the loss function based on quality fluctuation, thereby improving rate-distortion performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DCVC-DT achieves up to 6.21% bitrate savings over the baseline DCVC-DC, while significantly enhancing generalization to unseen testing data and alleviating error propagation. Our code is available at https://github.com/SunnyMass/DCVC-DT.

AI.tldr: DCVC-DT introduces online domain transfer and frame-level dynamic RD adjustment to bridge training-testing domain gaps in neural video compression, achieving bitrate savings and better generalization without modifying enc/dec parameters.
AI.motivation: Domain shifts between training and testing data in neural video coding cause performance degradation; there is a need for content-adaptive, generalizable compression without expensive retraining.
AI.method: Lightweight online domain transfer (DT) that adapts the encoded latent representation during inference without changing encoder/decoder parameters; frame-level dynamic rate-distortion (RD) adjustment that tunes the R/D ratio in the loss based on observed quality fluctuations.
AI.result: Reported up to 6.21% bitrate savings over DCVC-DC baseline; improved generalization to unseen content and reduced error propagation; code available.
AI.conclusion: DCVC-DT effectively mitigates domain gaps in neural video compression, offering improved rate-distortion performance and generalization with minimal overhead.

title: PhysEditBench: A Protocol-Conditioned Benchmark for Dense Physical-Map Prediction with Image Editors
comment: 48 pages, 12 figures, including references, appendix, and supplementary benchmark details
summary: Can general-purpose image editors predict physical maps from a single RGB image? General-purpose image editors differ from standard task-specific dense-prediction models: they do not directly take an image and output a physical map. Instead, they must be guided by prompts, examples, or image-based textual cues. To this end, we introduce PhysEditBench, a novel protocol-conditioned benchmark to evaluate and standardize image editors in dense physical-map prediction that covers five targets: depth, normal, albedo, roughness, and metallic maps. For evaluation data, we build a target-dependent benchmark substrate. We use OpenRooms-FF for depth, surface normal, albedo, and roughness, InteriorVerse as an additional source for depth, normal, albedo, and a new procedurally generated source for metallic maps. We curate the data with quality checks, valid-region masks, scene-level sampling, and lighting-based stress subsets to ensure reliable and diverse evaluation. For each target, PhysEditBench defines a fixed protocol that specifies the allowed input, expected output format, and scoring procedure. Each score, therefore, reflects the performance of a model under a specified protocol, rather than its best possible performance under all prompts or interaction modes. Experimental results show that specialized models remain much stronger on depth, normal, and albedo, and stronger image editors can produce more reasonable map-like outputs. For roughness and metallic, image editors can match or outperform specialized baselines on some scalar metrics, but they still suffer from structural errors, sparsity effects, and sensitivity to lighting.

AI.tldr: PhysEditBench benchmarks general-purpose image editors on dense physical-map prediction (depth, normal, albedo, roughness, metallic) with fixed protocols, showing specialized models stay strong for some maps while editors can yield plausible outputs for roughness/metallic but with structural and lighting sensitivities.
AI.motivation: To assess how general-purpose image editors perform on dense physical-map tasks and provide a standardized evaluation protocol across prompts and interaction modalities.
AI.method: Introduce PhysEditBench, five targets with fixed input/output protocols; use curated datasets (OpenRooms-FF, InteriorVerse, procedural metallic data); quality checks, valid-region masks, scene-level sampling, and lighting-based stress subsets; per-target scoring.
AI.result: Specialized models outperform editors on depth, normal, albedo; editors can match/outperform on some roughness/metallic metrics but suffer from structural errors, sparsity, and lighting sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: PhysEditBench reveals both limitations and potential of image editors for physical-map prediction; highlights the need for standardized datasets and protocol-driven evaluation; editors can produce reasonable map-like outputs, but further work is needed to reduce artifacts.

title: KAST-BAR: Knowledge-Anchored Semantically-Dynamic Topology Brain Autoregressive Modeling for Universal Neural Interpretation
summary: While EEG foundation models have shown significant potential in universal neural decoding across tasks, their advancement remains constrained by the inadequacy modeling of complex spatiotemporal topology, as well as the inherent modality gap between low-level physiological signals and high-level textual semantics. To address these challenges, we propose a Knowledge-Anchored Semantically-Dynamic Topology Brain Autoregressive Model (KAST-BAR), which dynamically aligns physiological representations derived from multi-level brain topology with an expert-level semantic space. Specifically, we design a Dual-Stream Hierarchical Attention (DSHA) encoder that accurately captures the brain's intrinsic non-Euclidean topology by modeling local temporal dynamics with global spatial contexts. On this basis, a Knowledge-Anchored Semantic Profiler (KASP) is proposed to synthesize physically-grounded and instance-level textual profiles, which subsequently drive a Semantic Text-Aware Refiner (STAR) to dynamically reconstruct EEG representations using Latent Expert Queries. By conducting large-scale pre-training on 21 diverse datasets to build a foundation model, KAST-BAR effectively integrates expert-level medical knowledge into EEG signal representations, consistently achieving superior performance across six downstream tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/KAST-BAR/KAST-BAR

AI.tldr: Proposes KAST-BAR, a brain autoregressive foundation model for EEG that aligns multi-level brain topology with semantic space via a DSHA encoder, KASP, and STAR, trained on 21 datasets to achieve state-of-the-art across six tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the limited modeling of complex spatiotemporal EEG topology and the modality gap between raw physiological signals and high-level textual semantics, aiming to integrate expert medical knowledge into EEG representations.
AI.method: DSHA encoder captures non-Euclidean brain topology by modeling local temporal dynamics with global spatial context; Knowledge-Anchored Semantic Profiler (KASP) produces physically-grounded, instance-level textual profiles; Semantic Text-Aware Refiner (STAR) reconstructs EEG representations using Latent Expert Queries; large-scale pre-training on 21 diverse datasets.
AI.result: Consistently superior performance across six downstream tasks with a foundation model trained on 21 datasets; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective integration of expert-level medical knowledge into EEG signal representations, enabling dynamic topology-aware decoding and improved universal EEG understanding.

title: ArcVQ-VAE: A Spherical Vector Quantization Framework with ArcCosine Additive Margin
comment: To appear in Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) has become a fundamental framework for learning discrete representations in image modeling. However, VQ-VAE models must tokenize entire images using a finite set of codebook vectors, and this capacity limitation restricts their ability to capture rich and diverse representations. In this paper, we propose ArcCosine Additive Margin VQ-VAE (ArcVQ-VAE), a novel vector quantization framework that introduces a spherical angular-margin prior (SAMP) for the codebook of a conventional VQ-VAE. The proposed SAMP consists of Ball-Bounded Norm Regularization, which constrains all codebook vectors within a time-dependent Euclidean ball, and ArcCosine Additive Margin Loss, which encourages greater angular separability among latent vectors. This formulation promotes more discriminative and uniformly dispersed latent representations within the constrained space, thereby improving effective latent-space coverage and leading to improved codebook utilization. Experimental results on standard image reconstruction and generation tasks show that ArcVQ-VAE achieves competitive performance against baseline models in terms of reconstruction accuracy, representation diversity, and sample quality. The code is available at: https://github.com/goals4292/ArcVQ-VAE

AI.tldr: ArcVQ-VAE introduces a spherical angular-margin prior (SAMP) for VQ-VAE by constraining codebook vectors within a time-dependent Euclidean ball and applying an ArcCosine Additive Margin Loss to enforce angular separability, improving latent-space coverage, representation diversity, and codebook utilization with competitive reconstruction and generation results.
AI.motivation: Address capacity limits and poor latent-space dispersion in standard VQ-VAE by promoting discriminative, uniformly dispersed latent representations within a constrained codebook space.
AI.method: Integrate Ball-Bounded Norm Regularization (time-dependent radius) to bound codebook vectors in Euclidean space, and apply ArcCosine Additive Margin Loss to encourage larger angular margins between latent vectors, within the VQ-VAE training framework.
AI.result: Empirical results on image reconstruction and generation show competitive performance with baseline models in reconstruction accuracy, representation diversity, and sample quality; code is available for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: SAMP improves effective latent-space coverage and codebook utilization in VQ-VAE, yielding a competitive alternative that achieves more discriminative latent geometry and better use of the codebook.

title: Collaborating in Multi-Armed Bandits with Strategic Agents
summary:
We study collaborative learning in multi-agent Bayesian bandit problems, where strategic agents collectively solve the same bandit instance. While multiple agents can accelerate learning by sharing information, strategic agents might prefer to free-ride and avoid exploration. We consider a setting with persistent agents that participate in multiple time periods. This is in contrast to most previous works on incentives in multi-agent MAB, which assume short-lived agents, namely each agent has a single decision to make and optimizes their expected reward in that single decision. As in the multi-agent MAB model with incentives, our model does not have monetary transfers, and the only incentives are through information sharing.
  We propose \texttt{CAOS}, a mechanism that sustains collaboration as a Nash equilibrium while achieving strong regret guarantees. Our results demonstrate that collaborative exploration can be sustained purely through information sharing, achieving performance close to that of fully cooperative systems despite strategic behavior.

AI.tldr: CAOS sustains collaboration in persistent multi-agent Bayesian bandits via information-sharing incentives, achieving near-cooperative regret.
AI.motivation: Address strategic, persistent agents who may free-ride on exploration in multi-agent Bayesian bandits; extend beyond short-lived agent models; remove monetary transfers and rely on information sharing as the sole incentive.
AI.method: Introduce the CAOS mechanism that aligns incentives through information sharing to sustain collaboration at a Nash equilibrium; provide regret guarantees.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong regret guarantees close to fully cooperative performance, showing collaborative exploration can be sustained purely by information sharing despite strategic behavior.
AI.conclusion: Information-sharing incentives suffice to sustain collaboration among persistent agents in multi-agent Bayesian bandits; CAOS effectively closes the gap to fully cooperative systems.

title: Towards Unified Surgical Scene Understanding:Bridging Reasoning and Grounding via MLLMs
summary: Surgical scene understanding is a cornerstone of computer-assisted intervention. While recent advances, particularly in surgical image segmentation, have driven progress, real-world clinical applications require a more holistic understanding that jointly captures procedural context, semantic reasoning, and precise visual grounding. However, existing approaches typically address these components in isolation, leading to fragmented representations and limited semantic consistency. To address this limitation, we propose SurgMLLM, a unified surgical scene understanding framework that bridges high-level reasoning and low-level visual grounding within a single model. Given surgical videos, SurgMLLM fine-tunes a multimodal large language model (MLLM) to support structured interpretability reasoning, which is used to jointly model phases, instrument-verb-target (IVT) triplets, and triplet-entity segmentation tokens. These tokens are then temporally aggregated and serve as prompts for a segmentation network, enabling accurate pixel-wise grounding of triplet instruments and targets. The entire framework is trained end-to-end with a unified objective that couples language-based reasoning supervision with visual grounding losses, promoting coherent cross-task learning and clinically consistent scene representations. To facilitate unified evaluation, we introduce CholecT45-Scene, extending CholecT45 dataset with 64,299 frames of pixel-level mask annotations for instruments and targets, aligned with existing triplet labels. Extensive experiments show that SurgMLLM significantly advances surgical scene understanding, improving the primary triplet recognition metric AP_IVT from 40.7% to 46.0% and consistently outperforming prior methods in phase recognition and segmentation. These results highlight the effectiveness of unified reasoning-and-grounding for reliable, context-aware surgical assistance.

AI.tldr: Unified surgical scene understanding via SurgMLLM that links high-level reasoning and pixel-level grounding, plus a CholecT45-Scene dataset; shows notable gains in IVT triplet recognition and segmentation.
AI.motivation: Current methods treat reasoning, triplet extraction, and grounding separately, causing fragmented, inconsistent representations and suboptimal clinical usefulness.
AI.method: Fine-tune a multimodal large language model to perform structured interpretability reasoning over phases, IVT triplets, and triplet-entity segmentation tokens; temporally aggregate tokens as prompts for a segmentation network; end-to-end training with supervision for reasoning and grounding losses; release CholecT45-Scene with 64k+ frames annotated for instruments and targets aligned with triplets.
AI.result: AP_IVT improves from 40.7% to 46.0%; improvements in phase recognition and segmentation; outperforms prior methods; demonstrates coherence between reasoning and grounding.
AI.conclusion: Unified reasoning-and-grounding in a single model yields more coherent, context-aware surgical scene representations and potentially more reliable intraoperative assistance.

title: Understanding Generalization through Decision Pattern Shift
summary: Understanding why deep neural networks (DNNs) fail to generalize to unseen samples remains a long-standing challenge. Existing studies mainly examine changes in externally observable factors such as data, representations, or outputs, yet offer limited insight into how a model's internal decision mechanism evolves from training to test. To address this gap, we introduce Decision Pattern Shift (DPS), a new perspective that defines generalization through the stability of internal decision patterns and quantifies failure as their deviation from those learned during training. Specifically, we represent each sample's decision pattern as a GradCAM-based channel-contribution vector, which captures how feature channels collectively support a prediction, and we propose the DPS metric to measure its discrepancy from the class-average pattern. Empirical analyses across multiple datasets and architectures show that, (i) decision patterns form a highly structured, class-consistent space with strong intra-class cohesion and low inter-class confusion, enabling direct analysis of a model's decision logic; (ii) the DPS magnitude correlates linearly with the generalization gap (nearly all Pearson r > 0.8), revealing generalization as a systematic drift in the model's internal decision mechanism; (iii) the DPS spectrum organizes diverse generalization degradation scenarios (covering ideal generalization, in-distribution degradation, domain shift, out-of-distribution, and shortcut learning) into a continuous trajectory, providing a unified explanation of their failure modes. These findings open up new possibilities for early generalization-risk detection, failure-mode diagnosis, and channel-level defect localization.

AI.tldr: Introduces Decision Pattern Shift (DPS), a GradCAM-based analysis of internal decision patterns to quantify generalization failure as drift from training patterns; DPS correlates with generalization gap and forms a spectrum across degradation scenarios.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in understanding generalization by examining internal decision mechanisms, not only data, representations, or outputs.
AI.method: Represent each sample's decision pattern as a GradCAM-based channel-contribution vector; define DPS as the discrepancy between a sample's pattern and the class-average pattern; analyze across datasets/architectures; interpret a spectrum of degradation scenarios.
AI.result: Decision patterns exhibit class-consistent, structured space with strong intra-class cohesion; DPS magnitude correlates linearly with the generalization gap (Pearson r > 0.8); the DPS spectrum maps various failure modes (in-distribution degradation, domain shift, out-of-distribution, shortcut learning) onto a continuous trajectory.
AI.conclusion: Presents a framework for early generalization-risk detection, diagnosis of failure modes, and localization of channel-level defects, linking internal decision dynamics to generalization performance.

title: CA-GCL: Cross-Anatomy Global-Local Contrastive Learning for Robust 3D Medical Image Understanding
summary: Fine-grained Vision-Language Pre-training (FVLP) demonstrates significant potential in 3D medical image understanding by aligning anatomy-level visual representations with corresponding textual descriptions. However, existing FVLP paradigms often suffer from severe representation collapse in the textual embedding space, where text embeddings of distinct anatomical structures become highly clustered and indistinguishable. This distributional degeneracy renders the model hypersensitive to prompt variations, hindering reliable clinical deployment. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Cross-Anatomy Global-Local Contrastive Learning framework (CA-GCL).
