CA-GCL introduces a global contrastive objective that enforces separation between anatomical categories in the latent space, effectively counteracting the aggregation tendency induced by local alignment. Furthermore, we incorporate a clinical-aware text augmentation strategy based on permutation invariance and partial completeness to enhance robustness against descriptive incompleteness. Extensive evaluations on the CT-RATE and Rad-ChestCT datasets demonstrate that CA-GCL consistently outperforms existing VLP paradigms in zero-shot abnormality detection, achieving superior performance while exhibiting strong cross-dataset generalization. Crucially, CA-GCL reduces performance variance across diverse prompt templates, transforming the collapsed textual similarity distribution into a bell-shaped distribution. These results validate CA-GCL as an effective framework for robust 3D medical image understanding.

AI.tldr: CA-GCL introduces a global contrastive objective and clinically-informed text augmentation to combat textual embedding collapse in fine-grained Vision-Language Pre-training for 3D medical imaging, yielding improved zero-shot abnormality detection and cross-dataset generalization with reduced prompt sensitivity.
AI.motivation: FVLP methods often cause textual embedding collapse, where distinct anatomical terms become indistinguishable, making models highly sensitive to prompt variations and hindering clinical deployment.
AI.method: Propose Cross-Anatomy Global-Local Contrastive Learning (CA-GCL) with a global contrastive objective to separate anatomical categories in latent space, plus clinical-aware text augmentation based on permutation invariance and partial completeness. Trains and evaluates on CT-RATE and Rad-ChestCT for zero-shot abnormality detection and cross-dataset generalization; analyzes robustness to prompt templates.
AI.result: CA-GCL outperforms existing VLP methods in zero-shot abnormality detection and cross-dataset generalization; reduces variance across prompt templates, transforming collapsed textual similarity distributions into bell-shaped distributions.
AI.conclusion: CA-GCL is an effective framework for robust 3D medical image understanding, mitigating textual embedding collapse and enhancing robustness to descriptive incompleteness.

title: Continual Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models via Program Memory
summary: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), particularly Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), has become a standard approach for adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) under limited compute. However, in continual settings where models are updated sequentially with small datasets, conventional LoRA updates struggle to balance rapid adaptation and knowledge retention. Existing methods typically treat the low-rank space as a homogeneous update region, lacking mechanisms to regulate how short-term updates are consolidated over time. We propose a continual LoRA framework with \textbf{Pro}gram memory, inspired by \textbf{C}omplementary \textbf{L}earning Systems in neuroscience. Our approach, dubbed \textbf{ProCL}, organizes LoRA adapters into structured program memory slots that are dynamically retrieved through input-conditioned attention. This enables rapid and localized adaptation, encouraging similar inputs to reuse shared adapter regions while reserving unused capacity for future data. The slots are then combined with the underlying adapter, which maintains a distributed representation that gradually accumulates knowledge across tasks to balance plasticity and stability. Our method operates entirely within the LoRA parameterization and incurs no additional inference cost. Experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate improved retention and reduced catastrophic forgetting over other continual LoRA strategies.

AI.tldr: ProCL introduces a continual LoRA framework with program memory slots that dynamically retrieve adapter regions via input-conditioned attention, enabling rapid adaptation while preventing forgetting, with no inference overhead.
AI.motivation: In continual learning, standard LoRA updates struggle to balance fast adaptation with knowledge retention; treating low-rank space as uniform fails to regulate consolidation over tasks.
AI.method: Organize LoRA adapters into structured program memory slots; dynamic retrieval via input-conditioned attention; similar inputs reuse common regions; reserve unused capacity; combine with underlying adapter; supports distributed representation; full LoRA parameterization; no extra inference cost.
AI.result: Empirical results on diverse benchmarks show improved retention and reduced catastrophic forgetting compared to other continual LoRA methods.
AI.conclusion: ProCL demonstrates effective continual adaptation by partitioning and reusing adapter regions while accumulating knowledge, achieving a balance between plasticity and stability within the LoRA framework.

title: Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0 Technical Report
summary: We present Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0, a suite of high-compression Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) that achieve significant advances in both reconstruction fidelity and diffusability. To address the reconstruction bottlenecks of high compression, we adopt an improved architecture featuring Global Skip Connections (GSC) and expanded latent channels. Moreover, we scale training to billions of images and incorporate a synthetic rendering engine to improve performance in text-rich scenarios. To tackle the convergence challenges of high-dimensional latent space, we implement an enhanced semantic alignment strategy to make the latent space highly amenable to diffusion modeling. To optimize computational efficiency, we leverage an asymmetric and attention-free encoder-decoder backbone to minimize encoding overhead. We present a comprehensive evaluation of Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0 on public reconstruction benchmarks. To evaluate performance in text-rich scenarios, we propose OmniDoc-TokenBench, a new benchmark comprising a diverse collection of real-world documents coupled with specialized OCR-based evaluation metrics. Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0 achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction performance, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in both general domains and text-rich scenarios at high compression ratio. Furthermore, downstream DiT experiments reveal our models possess superior diffusability, significantly accelerating convergence compared to existing high-compression baselines. These establish Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0 as a leading model with high compression, superior reconstruction, and exceptional diffusability.

AI.tldr: Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0 delivers high-compression VAEs with improved reconstruction and diffusion compatibility, using Global Skip Connections, larger latent channels, billions-scale training, a text-rendering enhancer, and an asymmetric encoder-decoder; introduces OmniDoc-TokenBench for OCR-centric evaluation; achieves state-of-the-art results in reconstruction and faster diffusion convergence.
AI.motivation: Address reconstruction bottlenecks and convergence difficulties in high-compression VAEs and diffusion models, especially for text-rich content, while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.method: Architectural enhancements (Global Skip Connections, expanded latent channels), billions-scale training with a synthetic rendering engine for text-rich data, enhanced semantic alignment for diffusion compatibility, asymmetric, attention-free encoder-decoder backbone for efficiency; new OmniDoc-TokenBench for OCR-based evaluation; evaluation on public reconstruction benchmarks and downstream DiT diffusion experiments.
AI.result: State-of-the-art reconstruction at high compression ratios; superior diffusability with significantly accelerated diffusion convergence compared to existing high-compression baselines.
AI.conclusion: Qwen-Image-VAE-2.0 achieves leading performance across high compression, reconstruction fidelity, and diffusion speed, positioning it as a top-tier model for image compression and diffusion-ready generative tasks in text-rich domains.

title: Finding the Weakest Link: Adversarial Attack against Multi-Agent Communications
comment: Full version of the Extended Abstract presented at AAMAS 2026
summary: Multi-agent systems rely on communication for information sharing and action coordination, which exposes a vulnerability to attacks. We investigate single-victim communication perturbation attacks against Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning-trained systems and propose methods that use gradient information from the Jacobian to identify which messages, agent, and timesteps are most susceptible to attack and have the greatest impact on the system. We enhance these methods with two proposed adversarial loss functions that trade-off attack success for attack impact which also create more effective perturbations. We empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods against two different multi-agent communication methods in navigation, PredatorPrey, and TrafficJunction environments. Our results show that our novel message selection method achieves a similar or greater impact than random message selection across almost all tested scenarios. Our victim selection, message selection, tempo, and loss functions improve attack effectiveness in half of the thirty scenarios we tested.

AI.tldr: Gradient-based single-victim perturbations target messages, agents, and timesteps in MARL communications; introduces two adversarial losses balancing attack success and impact; evaluated across navigation, PredatorPrey, and TrafficJunction with two communication schemes; message selection often matches or exceeds random; overall improvements in 15 of 30 scenarios.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent communication in reinforcement learning creates a vulnerability surface to perturbations. The study aims to identify critical communication components (which messages, which agents, and when) that maximize disruption using gradient (Jacobian) information, and to enhance perturbations with adversarial losses that trade off attack success against impact.
AI.method: Compute the Jacobian of agent/model outputs with respect to communicated messages to locate sensitive messages, victims, and timesteps. Develop two adversarial loss functions that trade-off attack success and impact. Apply these attacks to two MARL communication methods across three environments (navigation, PredatorPrey, TrafficJunction). Compare against random message selection.
AI.result: The proposed message selection approach achieves similar or greater disruption than random selection in most scenarios. Incorporating victim selection, refined message selection, attack tempo, and the two loss functions yields higher attack effectiveness in about 15 of 30 tested scenarios, demonstrating the viability of single-victim, gradient-guided perturbations in MARL communications.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-guided single-victim perturbations can substantially degrade MARL performance through targeted communication perturbations. The two adversarial losses enhance perturbation quality, indicating a need for robust defense mechanisms in MARL communication protocols.

title: HIR-ALIGN: Enhancing Hyperspectral Image Restoration via Diffusion-Based Data Generation
summary: Hyperspectral image (HSI) restoration is crucial for reliable analysis, as real HSIs suffer from degradations like noise, blur, and resolution loss. However, existing models trained on source data often fail on target domains lacking clean references, a common occurrence in practice. To address this issue, we present HIR-ALIGN, a plug-and-play target-adaptive augmentation framework that enhances hyperspectral image restoration by augmenting limited training images with synthetic data that closely matches the target distribution using no extra data. It consists of three stages: (i) proxy generation, where off-the-shelf restoration models restore degraded target observations to produce semantics-preserving proxy HSIs that approximate target-domain clean images; (ii) distribution-adaptive synthesis, where a blur-robust unCLIP diffusion model generates target-aligned RGBs from proxy RGBs, with prompt conditioning and embedding-space noise initialization. Then, a warp-based spectral transfer module synthesizes HSIs by aligning each generated RGB with the proxy RGB, estimating soft patch-wise transport weights, and applying these weights and learnable local interpolation kernels to the proxy HSI; and (iii) aligned supervised finetuning, where restoration networks pretrained on the source distribution are finetuned using both the proxy HSIs and synthesized target-aligned HSIs, and are then deployed on degraded target images. We further provide theoretical analysis showing that augmentation-based finetuning can achieve lower target-domain restoration risk by jointly improving target distribution coverage and controlling spectral bias. Extensive experiments on simulated and real datasets across denoising and super-resolution tasks demonstrate that HIR-ALIGN consistently improves source-only supervised baselines, outperforming both source-only counterparts and representative unsupervised methods.

AI.tldr: HIR-ALIGN enables target-adaptive augmentation for hyperspectral restoration via a three-stage pipeline (proxy generation, distribution-adaptive synthesis, aligned finetuning), improving cross-domain performance without extra data.
AI.motivation: Domain shift and lack of clean target references in HSI restoration; need data-efficient, target-aligned augmentation to bridge source-target gap.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (i) proxy generation from degraded target to semantics-preserving proxies using existing restoration models; (ii) distribution-adaptive synthesis by a blur-robust unCLIP diffusion model generating target-aligned RGBs from proxies; warp-based spectral transfer aligning generated RGBs with proxy RGBs to synthesize HSIs; (iii) aligned supervised finetuning of source-trained restorers using proxy HSIs and synthesized target-aligned HSIs.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over source-only baselines and competitive against unsupervised methods on both denoising and super-resolution tasks across simulated and real HSIs.
AI.conclusion: Augmentation-based finetuning broadens target distribution coverage and mitigates spectral bias, enabling effective, plug-and-play target adaptation for HSI restoration.

title: Do Heavy Tails Help Diffusion? On the Subtle Trade-off Between Initialization and Training
summary: Recent works have proposed incorporating heavy-tailed (HT) noise into diffusion- and flow-based generative models, with the goals of better recovering the tails of target distributions and improving generative diversity. This motivation is intuitive: if the data are heavy-tailed, HT noise may appear better matched than light-tailed (LT) Gaussian noise. However, replacing Gaussian noise by HT noise also changes the underlying estimation problem. In this paper, we revisit this paradigm through a combined theoretical and empirical study, establishing sampling-error bounds for two representative diffusion models driven by HT and LT noise. We show that HT noise makes the statistical estimation problem harder, leading to less favorable sampling-error bounds. We support these findings with experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, empirically recovering the predicted error trade-off. Our results call into question a growing design trend in generative modeling and challenge the use of HT noise to improve rare-region exploration.

AI.tldr: Replacing Gaussian noise with heavy-tailed (HT) noise in diffusion/flow models makes sampling harder: HT-driven models have worse sampling-error bounds and offer no practical gain in rare-region exploration, challenging the design trend of using HT noise for diversity.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether aligning noise tails with data tails (heavy-tailed data) by using HT noise improves tail recovery and diversity, and to understand its effect on the statistical estimation problem underlying diffusion/flow models.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation of sampling-error bounds for two representative diffusion models under HT and LT noise, followed by empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets to observe the predicted trade-offs between tail accuracy and estimation difficulty.
AI.result: HT noise degrades the statistical estimation problem, yielding less favorable sampling-error bounds compared to LT (Gaussian) noise; experiments corroborate the predicted trade-off between tail fidelity and sampling error, offering limited if any benefit for rare-region exploration.
AI.conclusion: Using HT noise to enhance tail modeling or diversity in diffusion/flow models is not universally beneficial; the observed estimation difficulty calls into question this design choice and suggests careful consideration of the tail-noise alignment with the data and the associated sampling error.

title: Phy-CoSF: Physics-Guided Continuous Spectral Fields Reconstruction and Super-Resolution for Snapshot Compressive Imaging
summary: Recent advances have demonstrated that coded aperture snapshot spectral imaging (CASSI) systems show great potential for capturing 3D hyperspectral images (HSIs) from a single 2D measurement. Despite the inherent spectral continuity of scenes captured by CASSI, most existing reconstruction methods are restricted to fixed, discrete spectral outputs, thereby precluding continuous spectral reconstruction or spectral super-resolution. To address this challenge, we propose Phy-CoSF, which synergizes deep unfolding networks with implicit neural representations, establishing a new paradigm for continuous spectral reconstruction and super-resolution in CASSI. Specifically, we propose a two-phase architecture that bridges discrete-wavelength training with continuous spectral rendering, enabling the synthesis of high-fidelity HSIs at arbitrary target wavelengths. At the core of our framework lies the continuous spectral fields (CoSF) module, embedded within each unfolding stage as a dynamic prior, which comprises a triple-branch cross-domain feature mixer for comprehensive spatial-frequency-channel feature fusion, alongside a spectral synthesis head that generates spectral intensities by querying continuous wavelength coordinates. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that Phy-CoSF not only achieves continuous modeling at arbitrary spectral resolutions but also outperforms many state-of-the-art methods in both reconstruction fidelity and spectral detail preservation. Our code and more results are available at: https://github.com/PaiDii/Phy-CoSF.git.

AI.tldr: Phy-CoSF offers continuous spectral reconstruction and super-resolution for CASSI by integrating deep unfolding with implicit neural representations via CoSF, enabling arbitrary-wavelength HSIs with improved fidelity.
AI.motivation: Overcome discrete spectral limitations in CASSI and exploit spectral continuity to enable reconstruction at arbitrary wavelengths and spectral super-resolution.
AI.method: Two-phase architecture that couples deep unfolding networks with implicit neural representations. Introduces continuous spectral fields (CoSF) embedded in each unfolding stage as a dynamic prior, featuring a triple-branch cross-domain feature mixer for fusion and a spectral synthesis head that queries continuous wavelength coordinates.
AI.result: Demonstrates continuous modeling at arbitrary spectral resolutions and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reconstruction fidelity and spectral detail preservation.
AI.conclusion: Presents a new paradigm combining physics-informed reconstruction with implicit spectral rendering, enabling continuous HSIs in CASSI; code is publicly available.

title: Stable Attention Response for Reliable Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Precipitation nowcasting remains challenging due to the highly localized, rapidly evolving, and heterogeneous nature of atmospheric dynamics. Although recent methods increasingly adopt attention-based architectures in both unimodal and multimodal settings, they mainly emphasize stronger representation learning and prediction capacity, while paying less attention to the stability of attention responses across samples. In this work, we show that cross-sample instability of attention-response energy is an important and previously underexplored source of forecasting unreliability. Empirically, inaccurate forecasts are associated with larger attention-response energy variance across heads and layers. Theoretically, we show that cross-sample variability can propagate through self-attention, and enlarge a lower bound on prediction error. Based on this insight, we propose HARECast, a Head-wise Attention Response Energy-regulated framework for precipitation nowcasting. HARECast explicitly models head-wise attention-response energy and stabilizes it through a group-wise regularization objective that reduces cross-sample fluctuations. The proposed formulation is generic and applicable to both unimodal and multimodal nowcasting architectures. We instantiate HARECast in a standard forecasting pipeline with reconstruction branches and a diffusion-based predictor, and evaluate it on commonly used benchmarks--SEVIR and MeteoNet. Experimental results demonstrate that HARECast achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Cross-sample instability in attention responses degrades precipitation nowcasting; HARECast introduces head-wise attention response energy regulation via group-wise regularization to stabilize attention across samples, achieving state-of-the-art on SEVIR and MeteoNet.
AI.motivation: Attention-based models for nowcasting exhibit cross-sample variability in attention-energy, leading to unreliable forecasts; stabilizing this energy can improve robustness and accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce HARECast: a framework that models head-wise attention-response energy and stabilizes it with a group-wise regularization objective to reduce cross-sample fluctuations; applicable to unimodal and multimodal architectures; implemented within a forecasting pipeline using reconstruction branches and a diffusion-based predictor.
AI.result: Empirical: HARECast achieves state-of-the-art performance on SEVIR and MeteoNet; attention-energy variance across heads/layers correlates with forecast error; theoretical: cross-sample variability can propagate through self-attention and enlarge a lower bound on prediction error.
AI.conclusion: Stabilizing head-wise attention-response energy improves accuracy and reliability of precipitation nowcasting; the approach is generic and readily integrable into existing architectures.

title: HetScene: Heterogeneity-Aware Diffusion for Dense Indoor Scene Generation
summary: Generating controllable and physically plausible indoor scenes is a pivotal prerequisite for constructing high-fidelity simulation environments for embodied AI. However, existing deeplearning-based methods usually treat all objects as homogeneous instances within a unified generation process. While effective for sparse and simplistic layouts, they struggle to model realistic layouts with dense object arrangements and complex spatial dependencies, leadingto limited scalability and degraded physical plausibility. To deal with these challenges, we revisit indoor layout generation from the perspective of structural heterogeneity and decompose the objects into primary objects and secondary objects according to their distinct roles in shaping a scene. Based on this decomposition, we propose HetScene, a heterogeneous two-stage generation framework that decouples indoor layout synthesis into Structural Layout Generation (SLG) and Contextual Layout Generation (CLG). SLG first generates globally coherent structural layouts with only primary objects conditioned on text descriptions, top-down binary room masks, and spatial relation graphs, establishing a stable global macro-skeleton of large core furniture.

AI.tldr: HetScene introduces a heterogeneous two-stage indoor scene generation framework that separates primary and secondary objects and uses Structural Layout Generation (SLG) to build a global macro-skeleton from text, room masks, and spatial relations, followed by Contextual Layout Generation (CLG) to place contextual objects.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of homogeneous, single-stage scene generation methods in producing dense, realistic indoor layouts with complex spatial dependencies and physical plausibility.
AI.method: Decompose objects into primary and secondary roles. HetScene employs a two-stage pipeline: SLG (Structural Layout Generation) creates a globally coherent layout using only primary objects conditioned on text descriptions, top-down binary room masks, and spatial relation graphs to form a stable macro-skeleton of large core furniture; CLG (Contextual Layout Generation) (implied) places secondary/contextual objects conditioned on the primary layout.
AI.result: Not stated in the provided abstract excerpt.
AI.conclusion: The framework targets improved controllability and plausibility in indoor scene generation by leveraging object heterogeneity, with SLG establishing a macro-skeleton and CLG filling in contextual details.

title: N-vium: Mixture-of-Exits Transformer for Accelerated Exact Generation
summary: Improving the inference efficiency of autoregressive transformers typically means reducing FLOPs per token, usually through approximations that degrade model quality. We introduce N-vium, a mixture-of-exits transformer that partially parallelizes computation across depth on standard hardware, increasing effective FLOPs per second rather than minimizing compute per token. N-vium attaches prediction heads at multiple depths and defines the next-token distribution as a learned mixture over these exits, with token-adaptive routing. This formulation strictly generalizes the standard transformer, which is recovered exactly when routing assigns zero mass to all intermediate heads. Sampling from the mixture is exact, and complete KV caches are recovered by deferring the upper-layer computation and batching it with later tokens. We pretrain N-vium at scales up to 1.5B parameters. Our largest model reaches 57.9% wall-clock speedup over a parameter- and data-matched standard transformer at no perplexity cost.

AI.tldr: Mixture-of-exits transformer (N-vium) improves inference throughput by partially parallelizing compute across depth, using token-adaptive routing to multiple exits, yielding a speedup of 57.9% on a 1.5B model with no perplexity loss; standard transformer is recovered when routing assigns zero weight to intermediate exits.
AI.motivation: Increase inference efficiency of autoregressive transformers by raising effective FLOPs-per-second through partial depth parallelism and conditional computation, rather than strictly minimizing per-token FLOPs.
AI.method: Attach prediction heads at multiple depths and use a learned, token-adaptive mixture for the next-token distribution; exact sampling; defer upper-layer computation to batch with later tokens to preserve complete KV caches; pretrain models up to 1.5B parameters.
AI.result: Achieved 57.9% wall-clock speedup over a parameter- and data-matched standard transformer, with no loss in perplexity.
AI.conclusion: N-vium generalizes standard transformers; provides exact sampling and robust throughput gains by distributing work across depths and leveraging complete KV caching; scales to large models.

title: Real2Sim: A Physics-driven and Editable Gaussian Splatting Framework for Autonomous Driving Scenes
summary: Reliable autonomous driving relies on large-scale, well-labeled data and robust models. However, manual data collection is resource-intensive, and traditional simulation suffers from a persistent reality gap. While recent generative frameworks and radiance-field methods improve visual fidelity, they still struggle with temporal and spatial consistency and cannot ensure physics-aware behavior, limiting their applicability to driving scenario generation. To address these challenges, we propose Real2Sim, an unified framework that combines 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) with a differentiable Material Point Method (MPM) solver. Real2Sim explicitly reconstructs dynamic driving scenes as temporally continuous Gaussian primitives, supports instance-level editing, and simulates realistic object-object and object-environment interactions. This framework enables physics-aware, high-fidelity synthesis of diverse, editable scenarios, including challenging corner cases such as collisions and post-impact trajectories. Experiments on the Waymo Open Dataset validate Real2Sim's capabilities in rendering, reconstruction, editing, and physics simulation, demonstrating its potential as a scalable tool for data generation in downstream tasks such as perception, tracking, trajectory prediction, and end-to-end policy learning.

AI.tldr: Real2Sim unifies 4D Gaussian Splatting with a differentiable Material Point Method (MPM) solver to reconstruct dynamic driving scenes as temporally continuous Gaussian primitives, enabling physics-aware, editable, high-fidelity scene synthesis for data generation in autonomous driving tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the data-hungry nature of autonomous driving and the reality gap between real data and synthetic data. Existing generative and radiance-field methods struggle with temporal/spatial consistency and physics-aware behavior needed for driving scenarios.
AI.method: Represent dynamic scenes as temporally continuous 4D Gaussian primitives using 4D Gaussian Splatting; integrate a differentiable MPM solver to simulate object-object and object-environment interactions; enable instance-level editing and physics-aware synthesis of diverse scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments on the Waymo Open Dataset show successful rendering, reconstruction, editing, and physics simulation, validating the framework's capability to generate diverse, editable, and physically plausible driving scenes, including challenging cases like collisions.
AI.conclusion: Real2Sim provides a scalable, physics-aware, high-fidelity data-generation framework for downstream tasks (perception, tracking, trajectory prediction, policy learning) by unifying advanced scene representation with differentiable physics, addressing both realism and editability.

title: ECG-NAT: A Self-supervised Neighborhood Attention Transformer for Multi-lead Electrocardiogram Classification
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) arrhythmia classification remains challenging due to signal variability, noise, limited labeled data, and the difficulty in achieving both accuracy and efficiency in models. While self-supervised learning reduces label dependency, most methods target either global contextual features or local morphological patterns, but rarely implement hierarchical multi-scale feature extraction. ECG signals require architectures that simultaneously capture fine-grained beat-level morphology and broader rhythm-level dependencies with computational efficiency. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes the Electrocardiogram Neighborhood Attention Transformer (ECG-NAT), a novel self-supervised learning approach tailored for multi-lead ECG classification. Our two-stage approach begins with generative pretraining, using a masked autoencoder to reconstruct partially masked ECG signals across multiple diverse datasets, enabling the model to learn robust, domain-invariant representations from unlabeled data. This is followed by discriminative fine-tuning with a dual-loss function that combines supervised contrastive and cross-entropy losses, aligning representation learning with label prediction. The hierarchical attention mechanism efficiently captures multi-scale temporal features from localized beat morphology to broader rhythm patterns at low computational cost. ECG-NAT achieves robust performance on benchmark datasets, with 88.1\% accuracy using only 1\% labeled data, demonstrating strong efficacy in low-resource settings. The framework combines superior classification performance with computational efficiency, making it practical for real-time ECG diagnosis. The code will be made available upon acceptance at: https://github.com/Mahsagazeran/ECG-NAT.

AI.tldr: ECG-NAT is a two-stage self-supervised transformer for multi-lead ECG classification using masked autoencoder pretraining and dual supervised contrastive + cross-entropy fine-tuning, featuring hierarchical neighborhood attention for multi-scale temporal features; achieves strong, label-efficient performance (88.1% accuracy with 1% labeled data) and is designed for real-time use.
AI.motivation: ECG classification faces variability, noise, limited labeled data, and the challenge of extracting both fine-grained beat-level morphology and broader rhythm-level dependencies efficiently. Existing SSL approaches usually target either local or global features, lacking hierarchical multi-scale feature extraction.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) generative pretraining with a masked autoencoder to reconstruct partially masked multi-lead ECG signals across diverse datasets, learning robust, domain-invariant representations; (2) discriminative fine-tuning with a dual loss (supervised contrastive and cross-entropy) to align representations with label prediction. The model uses a hierarchical neighborhood attention mechanism to efficiently capture multi-scale temporal features from local morphology to broader rhythms with low computation.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust performance on benchmark datasets, achieving 88.1% accuracy using only 1% of labeled data; claims computational efficiency suitable for real-time diagnosis; code will be released upon acceptance (GitHub link provided).
AI.conclusion: ECG-NAT offers strong accuracy and efficiency by combining self-supervised pretraining with hierarchical multi-scale attention, enabling effective low-label ECG classification and practical deployment.

title: Sparse Code Uplifting for Efficient 3D Language Gaussian Splatting
comment: 18 pages (9 pages main paper), 10 figures, preprint
summary: 3D Language Gaussian Splatting (3DLGS) augments 3D Gaussian Splatting with language-aligned visual features for open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding. A core challenge is efficiently associating high-dimensional vision-language embeddings with millions of 3D Gaussians while preserving efficient feature rendering for text-based querying. Existing methods either store dense features directly on Gaussians, causing high storage costs and slow rendering, or learn compact representations through expensive per-scene optimization with repeated feature rasterization. No existing method simultaneously achieves fast 3D semantic reconstruction, efficient storage, and fast rendering. We propose SCOUP (Sparse COde UPlifting), which addresses all three by decoupling language representation learning from 3D Gaussian optimization. Rather than working directly in 3D, we learn sparse codebook-based representations entirely using features associated with 2D image regions, associating each region with a sparse set of codebook coefficients. We then uplift these coefficients to 3D Gaussians with our weighted sparse aggregation using Gaussian-to-pixel associations, where each Gaussian accumulates coefficients over codebook atoms across views. Top-$K$ filtering then extracts the most dominant multi-view coefficients per Gaussian, enabling efficient storage and fast rendering. Our method achieves up to $400\times$ training speedup while being $3\times$ more memory efficient during training compared to the state-of-the-art in rendering speed. Across multiple benchmarks, SCOUP matches or outperforms existing methods in open-vocabulary querying accuracy.

AI.tldr: SCOUP decouples language learning from 3D optimization by learning sparse codebook representations from 2D image regions and uplifting them to 3D Gaussians via weighted, multi-view aggregation with Top-K pruning, enabling fast training, compact storage, and fast rendering for open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding.
AI.motivation: The core challenge is to achieve fast 3D semantic reconstruction, efficient storage, and fast rendering for open-vocabulary querying. Existing methods either store dense per-Gaussian features with high storage/slow rendering or require expensive per-scene optimization, failing to meet all three goals simultaneously.
AI.method: Learn sparse codebook-based representations using 2D image-region features; associate each region with a sparse coefficient set; uplift coefficients to 3D Gaussians via weighted sparse aggregation using Gaussian-to-pixel correspondences across views; accumulate codebook atoms per Gaussian; apply Top-K filtering to keep dominant multi-view coefficients, enabling compact storage and fast rendering. This decouples language representation learning from 3D Gaussian optimization.
AI.result: Achieves up to 400x training speedup and 3x memory efficiency during training compared with state-of-the-art rendering speed; SCOUP matches or exceeds existing methods on open-vocabulary querying accuracy across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling language learning from 3D optimization and using sparse codebooks with weighted aggregation and Top-K pruning, SCOUP delivers fast training, compact storage, and efficient rendering without sacrificing open-vocabulary 3D semantic performance.

title: McCast: Memory-Guided Latent Drift Correction for Long-Horizon Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Existing precipitation nowcasting methods typically adopt an autoregressive formulation, where future states are predicted from previous outputs. However, such an approach accumulates errors over long rollouts, causing forecasts to drift away from physically plausible evolution trajectories. Although various studies have attempted to alleviate this problem by improving step-wise prediction accuracy, they largely neglect the global temporal evolution of meteorological systems and lack mechanisms to actively correct drift during rollouts. To address this issue, we propose McCast, a memory-guided latent drift correction method for precipitation nowcasting. Rather than treating memory as an unordered dictionary of latent states for passive conditioning, McCast leverages temporally organized memory to actively correct autoregressive latent evolution. Specifically, McCast introduces a Drift-Corrective Memory Bank (DCBank) that explicitly estimates the temporally consistent drift corrections to calibrate the divergent trajectory. DCBank performs drift correction in two stages: a Corrective Latent Extractor first predicts an initial correction from the current prediction and a reference latent state, and a Correction-Aware Memory Retrieval module then refines the initial correction using temporally organized historical memory. By explicitly correcting latent evolution, instead of improving step-wise prediction accuracy only, McCast produces more temporally coherent and reliable long-horizon forecasts. Experiments on two widely used benchmarks, SEVIR and MeteoNet, show that McCast achieves state-of-the-art performance, particularly in challenging long-horizon forecasting scenarios.

AI.tldr: McCast introduces a memory-guided latent drift correction framework for precipitation nowcasting, using a Drift-Corrective Memory Bank (DCBank) to actively rectify autoregressive latent evolution and achieve state-of-the-art long-horizon forecasts on SEVIR and MeteoNet.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive precipitation nowcasting suffers from cumulative drift over long rollouts and lacks mechanisms to actively correct temporal evolution. A memory-guided approach leveraging temporal coherence can constrain forecast trajectories.
AI.method: McCast uses a Drift-Corrective Memory Bank (DCBank) with two stages: (1) Corrective Latent Extractor predicts an initial latent drift correction from the current prediction and a reference latent state, and (2) Correction-Aware Memory Retrieval refines this correction using temporally organized historical memory to enforce temporally consistent drift corrections in latent space.
AI.result: Experiments on SEVIR and MeteoNet show state-of-the-art performance, especially in challenging long-horizon forecasting scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Explicit latent drift correction via memory-guided components produces more temporally coherent and reliable long-horizon precipitation nowcasts, outperforming step-wise improvements that do not address global temporal evolution.

title: Rethinking Graph Convolution for 2D-to-3D Hand Pose Lifting
summary: Graph convolutional networks (GCNs) are widely used for 3D hand pose estimation, where the hand skeleton is encoded as a fixed adjacency graph. We revisit whether this is the most effective way to incorporate hand topology in 2D-to-3D lifting. In this paper, we perform controlled, parameter-matched ablations on the FPHA benchmark and show that standard multi-head self-attention consistently outperforms GCN baselines. Even when the GCN is strengthened with multi-hop adjacency and matched parameter count, self-attention reduces MPJPE from 12.36 mm to 10.09 mm. A skeleton-constrained graph attention network recovers most of this gap, indicating that input-dependent aggregation is a major source of improvement, while fully connected attention yields additional gains. We further show that hand topology is most effective when introduced as a soft structural prior through graph-distance positional encoding, rather than as a hard adjacency constraint. These results suggest that, for hand pose lifting, adaptive spatial attention is a more effective inductive bias than fixed graph convolution.

AI.tldr: Adaptive spatial attention (self-attention) outperforms fixed GCNs for 2D-to-3D hand pose lifting on FPHA; soft graph-distance encoding beats hard adjacency; parameter-matched ablations show gains from attention and fully connected variants.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether hand topology should be encoded via fixed graphs (GCNs) or learned attention mechanisms, and how topology encoding affects 2D-to-3D hand pose lifting accuracy.
AI.method: Controlled, parameter-matched ablations on FPHA comparing standard GCNs (including multi-hop adjacency) to multi-head self-attention and to skeleton-constrained graph attention networks; also evaluate graph-distance positional encoding as a soft topology prior; compare with fully connected attention.
AI.result: Self-attention consistently outperforms GCN baselines; MPJPE improves from 12.36 mm to 10.09 mm; skeleton-constrained GAT recovers most of the gap; fully connected attention yields additional gains; soft topology prior via graph-distance encoding is more effective than hard adjacency.
AI.conclusion: For hand pose lifting from 2D to 3D, adaptive spatial attention is a more effective inductive bias than fixed graph convolution; input-dependent aggregation is the main contributor to performance improvements.

title: A Hybrid Tucker-LSTM Tensor Network Model for SOC Prediction in Electric Vehicles
summary: Accurate state of charge estimation is critical for the success of electric vehicle battery management strategies, but it is well known that conventional estimators suffer from two fundamental shortcomings: cumulative errors that grow over time and reliance on simplified battery models that do not reflect real world dynamics. Therefore, this paper presents a novel hybrid approach combining Tucker tensor decomposition with LSTM networks, using full - lifecycle EV field data for SOC prediction. The inputs are charge status, mileage, voltage, current, cell differentials, and temporal features. Tucker decomposition is skillfully used to reduce dimensionality while maintaining the temporal structure, hence allowing a direct, fair comparison with standard LSTM. The result is unequivocal: Tucker - LSTM outperforms the baseline on all metrics, with MSE dropping 70.5\% (from 21.07 to 6.22 ), MAE improving 48.7\% (from 3.37\% to 1.73\%), RMSE falling from 4.59\% to 2.49\%, and $R^2$ rising from 0.918 to 0.976. Since the experimental results demonstrably demonstrate that tensor decomposition compresses high-dimensional battery data very well without loss of predictive fidelity, this paper naturally opens up a new direction for tensor-based analytics in electric vehicle battery management.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Tucker-LSTM for SOC estimation using full lifecycle EV data shows strong performance gains over a baseline LSTM.
AI.motivation: Addresses persistent SOC estimation errors and the mismatch of simple battery models with real-world dynamics by leveraging tensor structure to handle high-dimensional, temporally rich data.
AI.method: Apply Tucker tensor decomposition to reduce dimensionality while preserving temporal structure, enabling a direct comparison with standard LSTM. Input features include charge status, mileage, voltage, current, cell differentials, and temporal features, using full lifecycle EV field data.
AI.result: MSE decreases from 21.07 to 6.22 (−70.5%), MAE from 3.37% to 1.73% (−48.7%), RMSE from 4.59% to 2.49%, and R^2 increases from 0.918 to 0.976.
AI.conclusion: Tensor decomposition can compress high-dimensional battery data without loss of predictive fidelity, suggesting a promising new direction for tensor-based analytics in EV battery management.

title: WD-FQDet: Multispectral Detection Transformer via Wavelet Decomposition and Frequency-aware Query Learning
summary: Infrared-visible object detection improves detection performance by combining complementary features from multispectral images. Existing backbone-specific and backbone-shared approaches still suffer from the problems of severe bias of modality-shared features and the insufficiency of modality-specific features. To address these issues, we propose a novel detection framework WD-FQDet that explicitly decouples modality-shared and modality-specific information from infrared and visible modalities in the new view of low- and high-frequency domains, allowing fusion strategies tailored to their frequency characteristics. Specifically, a low-frequency homogeneity alignment module is proposed to align modality-shared features across modalities via a cross-modal attention mechanism, and a high-frequency specificity retention module is proposed to preserve modality-specific features through the multi-scale gradient consistency loss. To reinforce the feature representation in the frequency domain, we propose a hybrid feature enhancement module that incorporates spatial cues. Furthermore, considering that the contributions of homogeneous and modality-specific features to object detection vary across scenarios, we propose a frequency-aware query selection module to dynamically regulate their contributions. Experimental results on the FLIR, LLVIP, and M3FD datasets demonstrate that WD-FQDet achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: WD-FQDet introduces a frequency-domain decoupled fusion framework for infrared-visible object detection, separating modality-shared and modality-specific information to tailor low- and high-frequency fusion, with modules for alignment, retention, enhancement, and dynamic query weighting, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard datasets.
AI.motivation: The approach targets the core limitations in prior multispectral detectors: excessive bias towards shared features across modalities and insufficient preservation of modality-specific cues. By operating in low- and high-frequency domains, it seeks to exploit complementary information more effectively.
AI.method: Proposes: (1) low-frequency homogeneity alignment with cross-modal attention to align shared features; (2) high-frequency specificity retention with multi-scale gradient consistency loss to preserve modality-specific cues; (3) hybrid feature enhancement incorporating spatial cues to strengthen frequency-domain representations; (4) frequency-aware query selection to dynamically regulate contributions of shared vs. specific features; (5) fusion in the frequency domain with tailored strategies.
AI.result: Experimental results on FLIR, LLVIP, and M3FD show state-of-the-art performance across multiple metrics, validating the effectiveness of frequency-domain decoupling and the proposed modules.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling modality-shared and modality-specific information in the frequency domain with dedicated alignment, retention, and weighting mechanisms improves infrared-visible object detection, with performance gains demonstrated on standard benchmarks.

title: Switching Successor Measures for Hierarchical Zero-shot Reinforcement Learning
summary: Hierarchical reinforcement learning can improve generalization by decomposing long-horizon decision-making into simpler subproblems. However, existing approaches often rely on restrictive design choices, such as fixed temporal abstractions or goal-conditioned objectives, which largely confine them to goal-reaching tasks and limit their applicability to general reward functions. In this paper, we introduce switching successor measures, an extension of successor measures that enables hierarchical control in zero-shot reinforcement learning without additional supervision, fixed horizons, or manually designed subgoals. We show that switching successor measures arise naturally from classical successor measures while preserving their underlying structure. Building on this result, we propose FB $π$-Switch, an algorithm that extracts both a high-level subgoal-selection policy and a low-level control policy directly from forward-backward (FB) representations, allowing hierarchical behavior to emerge from a single learned representation. Experiments on both goal-conditioned and general reward-based tasks show that FB $π$-Switch improves over non-hierarchical baselines and matches state-of-the-art hierarchical methods in goal-conditioned settings. These results demonstrate that structured successor representations provide a flexible foundation for hierarchical zero-shot reinforcement learning beyond goal-reaching tasks. Our project website is available at: https://stestokth.github.io/switching-successors/.

AI.tldr: Switching successor measures extend classical successor representations to enable hierarchical control in zero-shot RL without fixed horizons or handcrafted subgoals. The FB π-Switch algorithm learns a forward-backward representation to derive both high-level subgoal selection and low-level control directly from a single representation, enabling hierarchy for both goal-conditioned and general reward tasks. Empirically, it outperforms non-hierarchical baselines and matches state-of-the-art HRL in goal-conditioned tasks, illustrating a flexible foundation for hierarchical zero-shot RL beyond goal-reaching tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the rigidity of existing hierarchical RL approaches that rely on fixed temporal abstractions or manually designed subgoals, which limit applicability to general reward functions and non-goal-reaching tasks.
AI.method: Introduce switching successor measures as an extension of successor representations. Propose FB π-Switch which extracts a high-level subgoal policy and a low-level controller from forward-backward representations, enabling hierarchical behavior from a single learned representation without extra supervision, fixed horizons, or subgoal design.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on goal-conditioned and general reward-based tasks shows performance gains over non-hierarchical baselines and parity with state-of-the-art hierarchical methods on goal-conditioned tasks.
AI.conclusion: Structured successor representations provide a flexible foundation for hierarchical zero-shot reinforcement learning that extends beyond goal-reaching tasks.

title: HADAR-Based Thermal Infrared Hyperspectral Image Restoration
summary: Thermal-infrared (TIR) hyperspectral imagery (HSI) provides critical scene information for various applications. However, its practical utility is severely limited by unique sensor degradations beyond the capabilities of existing restoration methods, which are ignorant of underlying thermal physics. Here, we propose HAIR (HADAR-based Image Restoration) as a physics-driven framework for ground-based TIR-HSI restoration. HAIR utilizes the HADAR rendering equation (HRE) and combines it with the atmospheric downwelling radiative transfer equation (RTE) to model TIR-HSI using temperature, emissivity, and texture (TeX) physical triplets. This physical model leads to a TeX decompose-synthesize strategy that guarantees physical consistency and spatio-spectral noise resilience, in stark contrast to existing approaches. Moreover, our framework uses a forward-modeled atmospheric downwelling reference, along with spectral smoothness of emissivity and blackbody radiation, to enable spectral calibration and generation that would otherwise be elusive. Our extensive experiments on the outdoor DARPA Invisible Headlights dataset and in-lab FTIR measurements show that HAIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across denoising, inpainting, spectral calibration, and spectral super-resolution, establishing a benchmark in objective accuracy and visual quality.

AI.tldr: HAIR: a physics-driven TeX-based restoration for ground-based TIR-HSI using HADAR and atmospheric downwelling; achieves superior denoising, inpainting, calibration, and super-resolution with physical consistency.
AI.motivation: TIR-HSI restoration is hindered by sensor degradations not captured by current methods. A physics-informed framework leveraging the HADAR rendering equation and atmospheric RTE can model temperature, emissivity, and texture (TeX) triplets to ensure physical consistency and noise resilience.
AI.method: Introduce TeX decomposition of TIR-HSI into temperature, emissivity, and texture; forward-model HADAR rendering plus atmospheric downwelling RTE to synthesize data; enforce spectral smoothness of emissivity and blackbody radiation; use forward-modeled reference to enable spectral calibration/generation; denoise, inpaint, calibrate, and perform spectral super-resolution via a TeX decompose-synthesize pipeline.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on outdoor DARPA Invisible Headlights dataset and in-lab FTIR measurements shows HAIR outperforms state-of-the-art methods across denoising, inpainting, spectral calibration, and spectral super-resolution, establishing a benchmark in objective accuracy and visual quality.
AI.conclusion: A physics-driven framework guarantees physical consistency and spatio-spectral noise resilience; enables spectral calibration and generation that would otherwise be elusive; sets a new benchmark for TIR-HSI restoration.

title: Machine Learning-Driven Multimodal Spectroscopic Liquid Biopsy for Early Multicancer Detection
summary: Cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide, making the development of rapid, minimally invasive, label-free and scalable diagnostic strategies a major challenge in modern oncology. In this context, spectroscopic liquid biopsy has emerged as a promising alternative, as it enables the holistic characterization of biochemical alterations in biological fluids. In this work, we propose a multimodal spectroscopic liquid biopsy framework for multicancer detection based on the combination of Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and Excitation-Emission Matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy together with Machine Learning (ML) methodologies. Serum samples from breast cancer patients, colorectal cancer patients, and healthy controls were analyzed through the three spectroscopic modalities. After modality-specific preprocessing, low-level data fusion (LLDF) was employed to integrate the complementary biochemical information encoded within the different spectroscopic measurements, and classification was performed using XGBoost models. Seven experimental configurations were evaluated, including the three unimodal approaches, all pairwise bimodal configurations, and the full multimodal approach of FTIR, Raman, and EEM fluorescence. The results show that although several individual modalities achieved high discrimination performance, the multimodal fusion provided the most balanced overall results, reaching a ROC-AUC of 0.997 for breast cancer and 0.994 for colorectal cancer, together with highly balanced sensitivity and specificity values.

AI.tldr: A multimodal spectroscopic liquid biopsy framework integrating FTIR, Raman, and EEM fluorescence with low-level data fusion and XGBoost demonstrates high accuracy for multicancer detection (breast and colorectal cancer) using serum samples.
AI.motivation: Address the need for rapid, minimally invasive, label-free, and scalable cancer diagnostics by leveraging complementary biochemical information from multiple spectroscopic modalities in a liquid biopsy setting.
AI.method: Collect serum from breast cancer patients, colorectal cancer patients, and healthy controls. Apply modality-specific preprocessing to FTIR, Raman, and EEM data, perform low-level data fusion to combine modalities, and classify with XGBoost. Evaluate seven configurations: unimodal, all pairwise bimodal, and full multimodal FTIR+Raman+EEM.
AI.result: Multimodal fusion yielded the best-balanced performance among configurations, achieving ROC-AUCs of 0.997 for breast cancer and 0.994 for colorectal cancer, with high sensitivity and specificity.
AI.conclusion: Integrating FTIR, Raman, and EEM spectroscopy with LLDF and XGBoost enables highly accurate multicancer detection from serum, outperforming individual modalities and supporting the potential of multimodal spectroscopic liquid biopsy for cancer diagnostics.

title: SceneGraphVLM: Dynamic Scene Graph Generation from Video with Vision-Language Models
summary: Scene graph generation provides a compact structured representation for visual perception, but accurate and fast graph prediction from images and videos remains challenging. Recent VLM-based methods can generate scene graphs end-to-end as structured text, yet often produce long outputs with irrelevant objects and relations. We present SceneGraphVLM, a compact method for image and video scene graph generation with small visual language models. SceneGraphVLM serializes graphs in a token-efficient TOON format and trains the model in two stages: supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with hallucination-aware rewards that balance relation coverage and precision while penalizing unsupported objects and relations. For videos, the model can optionally condition each frame on the previously generated graph, providing lightweight short-term context without tracking or post-processing. We evaluate SceneGraphVLM on PSG, PVSG, and Action Genome. With compact VLMs and vLLM-accelerated decoding, SceneGraphVLM achieves a strong quality-speed trade-off, improves precision-oriented SGG metrics while preserving reasonable recall, and generates complete scene graphs with approximately one-second latency. Code and implementation details are available at: https://github.com/markus0440/SceneGraphVLM.git.

AI.tldr: A compact, two-stage VLM-based pipeline for image/video scene graph generation that uses a token-efficient graph serialization (TOON) and reinforcement learning with hallucination-aware rewards, achieving fast (~1s) and accurate graphs with optional short-term video context.
AI.motivation: Scene graph generation (SGG) enables structured visual understanding but current VLM-based approaches often produce long, noisy graphs or rely on heavy models. A compact representation and efficient training objective are needed to improve precision, speed, and applicability to video.
AI.method: Serialize graphs in a token-efficient TOON format. Train in two stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning on graph data; (2) reinforcement learning with hallucination-aware rewards that balance relation coverage and precision while penalizing unsupported objects/relations. For videos, optionally condition each frame on the previously generated graph to provide lightweight short-term context. Use compact visual language models with vLLM-accelerated decoding. Evaluated on PSG, PVSG, and Action Genome.
AI.result: Achieves a strong quality-speed trade-off: improves precision-oriented SGG metrics while preserving recall and generates complete scene graphs with approximately 1-second latency. Demonstrates effectiveness for both image and video SGG with compact VLMs.
AI.conclusion: SceneGraphVLM offers an efficient, high-quality solution for image and video scene graph generation, leveraging a token-efficient serialization and RL-based optimization. Code is available at the provided repository.

title: Mix, Don't Tune: Bilingual Pre-Training Outperforms Hyperparameter Search in Data-Constrained Settings
summary: For most languages of the world, language model pre-training operates in a data-constrained regime where models must repeat their training data many times, degrading generalization. Two remedies exist: aggressive hyperparameter tuning such as high weight decay, and mixing in data from a high-resource auxiliary language to directly aid the low-resource target. While hyperparameter tuning regularizes the model by shrinking weights to restrict network capacity, auxiliary data mixing uses a tunable mixing ratio to expand the training distribution and diversify the training signal with new knowledge. Both offer a principled way to improve training in a data-constrained domain. We compare these levers systematically across four model scales from 150M to 1.43B parameters, using Arabic as the low-resource target and English as the auxiliary, over approximately 1000 pre-training runs. Three findings emerge. First, mixing yields larger improvements than hyperparameter tuning on both validation loss and downstream task accuracy, and the gap grows with model size. Second, we quantify how much mixing helps: it boosts performance by an amount equivalent to 2--3$\times$ the unique target data on validation loss and 2--13$\times$ on downstream task accuracy, with the gain scaling steeply with model size. Third, this divergence reveals that target-language validation loss systematically underestimates mixing's value. Mixing regularizes by diversifying the training signal and contributes knowledge the repeated target corpus cannot supply; validation loss captures only the first effect. Our practical recommendations are: mix in a high-resource language, prioritize the mixing ratio over hyperparameter tuning, and transfer hyperparameters from a small proxy model via $μ$P.

AI.tldr: Mixing in a high-resource auxiliary language outperforms hyperparameter tuning for data-constrained language model pre-training; gains grow with model size; target validation loss underestimates mixing's value; practical guidance: mix languages, prioritize mixing ratio, and transfer hyperparameters via μP.
AI.motivation: In data-constrained LM pre-training, models risk overfitting the scarce target-language data. The study compares two remedies—aggressive weight decay (hyperparameter tuning) and data mixing with a high-resource auxiliary language—to understand their effectiveness across model scales and to quantify the value of mixing.
AI.method: Systematic experimental comparison across four model scales (≈150M to 1.43B parameters) using Arabic as the low-resource target and English as the auxiliary. Approximately 1000 pre-training runs manipulated mixing ratio and hyperparameters, evaluating on validation loss and downstream tasks.
AI.result: Mixing yields larger gains than hyperparameter tuning, with the gap widening as model size increases. Mixing improves performance by about 2–3x the amount of unique target data on validation loss and 2–13x on downstream task accuracy, with gains scaling steeply with model size. Target-language validation loss underestimates mixing’s value because it captures only the regularization effect, not the knowledge transfer from the auxiliary language.
AI.conclusion: Prioritize data mixing with a high-resource language over hyperparameter tuning in data-constrained LM pre-training. Use a larger mixing ratio, and transfer hyperparameters from small proxy models via μP to stabilize training.

title: Pattern-Enhanced RT-DETR for Multi-Class Battery Detection
summary: Accurate and efficient battery detection is increasingly important for applications in electronic waste recycling, industrial quality control, and automated sorting systems. In this paper, we present both a comprehensive benchmark and a novel method for multi-class battery detection. We systematically compare three CNN-based detectors (YOLOv8n, YOLOv8s, YOLO11n) and two transformer-based detectors (RT-DETR-L, RT-DETR-X) on a publicly available dataset of approximately 8,591 annotated images under identical experimental conditions, and further propose PaQ-RT-DETR, which introduces pattern-based dynamic query generation into RT-DETR to alleviate query activation imbalance with negligible computational overhead. Among baselines, YOLO11n achieves the best CNN-based accuracy (mAP@50: 0.779) at only 2.6M parameters, while YOLOv8n delivers the fastest inference at ~1,667 FPS. PaQ-RT-DETR-X achieves the highest overall mAP@50 of 0.782, surpassing RT-DETR-X by +2.8% with consistent per-class gains across all six battery categories including the data-scarce Bike Battery class. Our findings provide practical guidance for selecting object detection models in battery-related industrial applications.

AI.tldr: Benchmark-driven evaluation of CNN and transformer detectors for multi-class battery detection, introducing PaQ-RT-DETR with pattern-based dynamic query generation; achieves state-of-the-art mAP@50 of 0.782 (PaQ-RT-DETR-X) and strong baselines (YOLOv11n mAP@50 0.779; YOLOv8n 1,667 FPS).
AI.motivation: Need for accurate and efficient battery recognition across diverse categories for e-waste recycling, industrial QC, and automated sorting; absence of a unified benchmark and limited effectiveness of existing detectors on multi-class battery images with data scarcity in some categories.
AI.method: Systematic benchmarking of three CNN-based detectors (YOLOv8n, YOLOv8s, YOLO11n) and two transformer-based detectors (RT-DETR-L, RT-DETR-X) on ~8,591 annotated images under identical conditions, with a novel PaQ-RT-DETR that adds pattern-based dynamic query generation into RT-DETR to mitigate query activation imbalance with negligible overhead.
AI.result: CNN baseline: YOLO11n achieved best accuracy (mAP@50: 0.779) with 2.6M parameters; YOLOv8n fastest (~1,667 FPS). Transformer baseline: RT-DETR-X and RT-DETR-L; proposed PaQ-RT-DETR-X achieved highest mAP@50 of 0.782, surpassing RT-DETR-X by 2.8% with consistent gains across all six battery categories, including data-scarce Bike Battery class.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidance for selecting object detectors in battery-related industrial tasks and demonstrates that pattern-based dynamic query generation in RT-DETR yields measurable gains with minimal computational cost.

title: Teacher-Guided Policy Optimization for LLM Distillation
summary: The convergence of reinforcement learning and imitation learning has positioned Reverse KL (RKL) as a promising paradigm for on-policy LLM distillation, aiming to unify exploration with teacher supervision. However, we identify a critical limitation: when the student and teacher distributions diverge significantly, standard RKL often fails to yield meaningful improvement due to uninformative negative feedback. To address this inefficiency, we propose Teacher-Guided Policy Optimization (TGPO), an on-policy algorithm that incorporates dense directional guidance by leveraging teacher predictions conditioned on the student's rollout. Because TGPO remains on-policy, the algorithm integrates seamlessly with existing RLVR frameworks without requiring additional data annotation. Experiments on complex reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TGPO significantly outperforms standard baselines and is robust to different teachers.

AI.tldr: TGPO is an on-policy distillation method for LLMs that uses teacher-guided, dense directional feedback conditioned on the student’s rollout to overcome ineffective negative feedback from standard Reverse KL, improving performance and robustness across teachers.
AI.motivation: Reverse KL distillation struggles when student and teacher distributions diverge, yielding uninformative negative feedback and limited improvement.
AI.method: Introduce Teacher-Guided Policy Optimization (TGPO), an on-policy algorithm that furnishes dense directional guidance by conditioning teacher predictions on the student’s rollout. TGPO remains on-policy, integrates with existing RLVR frameworks without extra annotation, and uses teacher guidance to shape policy updates.
AI.result: Experiments on complex reasoning benchmarks show TGPO significantly outperforms standard baselines and is robust to different teachers.
AI.conclusion: TGPO provides an effective, on-policy approach to teacher-guided distillation, improving sample efficiency and performance while maintaining compatibility with current RLVR pipelines.

title: SpurAudio: A Benchmark for Studying Shortcut Learning in Few-Shot Audio Classification
summary: Few-shot classification (FSC) is widely used for learning from limited labeled data, yet most evaluations implicitly assume that target concepts are independent of contextual cues. In real-world settings, however, examples often appear within rich contexts, allowing models to exploit spurious correlations between foreground content and background signals. While such effects have been studied in few-shot image classification, their role in few-shot audio classification remains largely unexplored, and existing audio benchmarks offer limited control over contextual structure. We introduce SpurAudio, a benchmark that leverages the natural separability of foreground events and background environments in audio to enable controlled, multi-level evaluation of contextual shifts across support and query sets. Using this benchmark, we show that many state-of-the-art few-shot methods suffer severe performance degradation when background correlations are disrupted, despite achieving similar accuracy under standard evaluation protocols. Crucially, this vulnerability persists even in large pretrained audio foundation models, ruling out limited backbone capacity as an explanation. Moreover, methods that appear comparable under conventional benchmarks can exhibit markedly different sensitivity to spurious correlations, revealing systematic algorithmic strengths and vulnerabilities tied to how feature representations interact with classifier heads at inference time. These findings provide new insight into the behavior of few-shot methods in audio and highlight the need for benchmarks that explicitly probe context dependence when evaluating FSC models.

AI.tldr: SpurAudio shows that contextual background cues can severely impair FSC in audio; current FSC methods (even large models) rely on background-content correlations; SpurAudio enables controlled multi-level context evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address gap: FSC in audio often evaluated without controlling context; foreground/background separability in audio can introduce spurious correlations; need benchmarks to diagnose reliance on context.
AI.method: Introduce SpurAudio benchmark leveraging natural separability of foreground events and background environments to create controlled context shifts across support/query sets; evaluate multiple FSC methods and audio foundation models; compare performance under standard vs context-disrupted conditions.
AI.result: Performance of state-of-the-art FSC methods degrades when background correlations are disrupted; large pretrained audio models are not immune; different algorithms show different sensitivity; context dependence is not captured by standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Benchmarks should explicitly probe context dependence; design FSC methods robust to contextual shifts; consider classifier-head interactions; SpurAudio serves as a diagnostic tool for audio FSC.

title: EMO: Frustratingly Easy Progressive Training of Extendable MoE
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models offer a powerful way to scale model size without increasing compute, as per-token FLOPs depend only on k active experts rather than the total pool of E experts. Yet, this asymmetry creates an MoE efficiency paradox in practice: adding more experts balloons memory and communication costs, making actual training inefficient. We argue that this bottleneck arises in part because current MoE training allocates too many experts from the beginning, even though early-stage data may not fully utilize such capacity. Motivated by this, we propose EMO, a simple progressive training framework that treats MoE capacity as expandable memory and grows the expert pool over the course of training. EMO explicitly models sparsity in scaling law to derive stage-wise compute-optimal token budgets for progressive expansion. Empirical results show that EMO matches the performance of a fixed-expert setup in large-scale experiments while improving wall-clock efficiency. It offers a surprisingly simple yet effective path to scalable MoE training, preserving the benefits of large expert pools while reducing both training time and GPU cost.

AI.tldr: Progressive expansion of MoE capacity (EMO) to improve training efficiency while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: MoE scales in parameters but not compute; large expert pools incur memory/communication overhead; early data may underutilize capacity, causing inefficiency; need a principled growth strategy.
AI.method: Treat MoE capacity as expandable memory; progressively grow the expert pool during training; derive stage-wise compute budgets by modeling sparsity with a scaling-law; implement simple framework EMO.
AI.result: Matches fixed-expert performance in large-scale experiments; reduces wall-clock time and GPU cost; maintains benefits of large expert pools.
AI.conclusion: EMO provides a simple, effective path to scalable MoE training; balances capacity and efficiency without sacrificing performance.

title: Weakly Supervised Segmentation as Semantic-Based Regularization
summary: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) trains dense pixel-level segmentation models from partial or coarse annotations such as bounding boxes, scribbles, or image-level tags. While recent work leverages foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) to generate pseudo-labels, these approaches typically depend on heuristic prompt choices and offer limited ways to incorporate prior knowledge or heterogeneous labels. We address this gap by taking a neurosymbolic perspective: integrating differentiable fuzzy logic with deep segmentation models. Weak annotations and domain-specific priors are unified as continuous logical constraints that fine-tune SAM under weak supervision. The refined foundation model then produces improved pseudo-labels, from which we train a second-stage prompt-free segmentation model. Experiments on Pascal VOC 2012 and the REFUGE2 optic disc/cup segmentation dataset show that our logic-guided fine-tuning yields higher-quality pseudo-labels, leading to state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy that often exceeds densely supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: Neurosymbolic WSSS: differentiable fuzzy logic constraints guide SAM fine-tuning with weak annotations; yields better pseudo-labels and a second-stage prompt-free model, achieving SOTA on VOC2012 and REFUGE2, often beating fully supervised baselines.
AI.motivation: Current WSSS with foundation models relies on heuristic prompts and lacks a principled way to inject domain priors or heterogeneous labels, limiting performance and generalization.
AI.method: Represent weak annotations and domain priors as continuous logical constraints via differentiable fuzzy logic to fine-tune a foundation model (SAM). Use the refined model to generate improved pseudo-labels, then train a second-stage prompt-free segmentation network.
AI.result: Experiments on Pascal VOC 2012 and REFUGE2 show higher-quality pseudo-labels and state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy, frequently surpassing densely supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: A neurosymbolic, logic-guided fine-tuning framework effectively leverages weak supervision and priors to enhance foundation-model-based WSSS, suggesting broad applicability and potential gains in segmentation tasks.

title: Unified generalization analysis for physics informed neural networks
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and their variational counterparts (VPINNs) are neural networks that incorporate physical laws, making them useful for scientific problems. Existing generalization analyses for PINNs and VPINNs remain limited, often requiring restrictive assumptions such as stability conditions or linear ellipticity. In this paper, we derive generalization bounds for neural networks that involve differentiation with respect to input variables, covering PINNs and VPINNs under a unified framework. We apply Taylor expansion to represent nonlinear differential operators as linear operators on a high-dimensional space, enabling the use of Koopman-based analysis and showing that high-rank networks can generalize well even in settings involving differential operators. We also show that the nonlinearity of the differential operator exponentially enlarges the bound, highlighting its significant impact on generalization.

AI.tldr: A unified generalization framework for PINNs/VPINNs that treats differentiation with respect to inputs; shows high-rank networks can generalize under linearized nonlinear operators via Taylor expansion and Koopman analysis, while nonlinearity of operators exponentially worsens the bounds.
AI.motivation: Generalization analyses for PINNs/VPINNs are limited and often rely on restrictive stability/ellipticity assumptions; a unified framework that handles input derivatives and nonlinear differential operators is needed.
AI.method: Represent nonlinear differential operators through Taylor expansion to linear operators on a high-dimensional space, enabling Koopman-based analysis; analyze networks that involve differentiation w.r.t. input variables; study the role of network rank in generalization; derive bounds.
AI.result: Derived generalization bounds for differentiation-informed networks under a unified framework; show that high-rank networks can generalize well in settings with differential operators; quantify how operator nonlinearity exponentially enlarges the bound.
AI.conclusion: The work broadens theoretical understanding of PINN/VPINN generalization under a unified approach, emphasizes the strong impact of operator nonlinearity on bounds, and provides guidance for network design and analysis.

title: Characterizing Universal Object Representations Across Vision Models
summary: Deep neural networks trained with different architectures, objectives, and datasets have been reported to converge on similar visual representations. However, what remains unknown is which visual properties models actually converge on and which factors may underlie this convergence. To address this, we decompose the object similarity structure of 162 diverse vision models into a small set of non-negative dimensions. To determine universal versus model-specific dimensions, we then estimate how often each dimension reappears across models.
