In contrast to model-specific dimensions, universal dimensions are more interpretable and more strongly driven by conceptual image properties, indicating the relevance of interpretability and semantic content as implicit factors driving universality across models. Differences in architecture, objective function, training data, model size, and model performance do not explain the emergence of universal dimensions. However, models with more universal dimensions also better predict macaque IT activity and human similarity judgments, suggesting that universality reflects representations relevant to biological vision. These findings have important implications for understanding the emergent representations underlying deep neural network models and their alignment with biological vision.

AI.tldr: Universal, interpretable dimensions emerge across diverse vision models, driven by semantic content, and correlate with biology/resemblance judgments; model architecture, data, size, and objectives do not explain universality.
AI.motivation: To uncover which visual properties are universally captured by deep networks and what underlying factors drive this universality, beyond architectural differences.
AI.method: Decompose object similarity structures of 162 diverse vision models into a small set of non-negative dimensions, then assess the repeatability of these dimensions across models to distinguish universal from model-specific factors; evaluate interpretability and semantic content of universal dimensions and their relation to biological vision.
AI.result: A small set of non-negative, universal dimensions reappear across models. Universal dimensions are more interpretable and more dominated by conceptual image properties. Architecture, objective, data, size, and performance do not explain universality. Models with more universal dimensions better predict macaque IT activity and human similarity judgments, suggesting universality aligns with representations in biological vision.
AI.conclusion: Universality captures core representations relevant to biological vision; interpretability and semantic content appear as implicit drivers of shared representations, offering insight into emergent properties of deep nets and their alignment with biology.

title: Cross Modality Image Translation In Medical Imaging Using Generative Frameworks
summary: Medical image-to-image (I2I) translation enables virtual scanning, i.e. the synthesis of a target imaging modality from a source one without additional acquisitions. Despite growing interest, most proposed methods operate on 2D slices, are evaluated on isolated tasks with different experimental set-ups and lack clinical validation. The primary contribution of this work is a reproducible, standardized comparative evaluation of 3D I2I translation methods in oncological imaging, designed to standardize preprocessing, splitting, inference, and multi-level evaluation across heterogeneous clinical tasks. Within this framework, we compare seven generative models, three Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs: Pix2Pix, CycleGAN, SRGAN) and four latent generative models (Latent Diffusion Model, Latent Diffusion Model+ControlNet, Brownian Bridge, Flow Matching), across eleven datasets spanning three anatomical regions (head/neck, lung, pelvis) and four translation directions (cone-beam CT to CT, MRI to CT, CT to PET, MRI T2-weighted to T2-FLAIR), for a total of 77 experiments under uniform training, inference, and evaluation conditions. The results show that GANs outperform latent generative models across all tasks, with SRGAN achieving statistically significant superiority. Our lesion-level analysis reveals that all models struggle with small lesions and that, in CT to PET synthesis, models reproduce lesion shape more reliably than absolute uptake-related intensity. We also performed a Visual Turing test administered to 17 physicians, including 15 radiologists, which shows near-chance classification accuracy (56.7%), confirming that synthetic volumes are largely indistinguishable from real acquisitions, while exposing a dissociation between quantitative metrics and clinical preference.

AI.tldr: A reproducible, standardized framework for 3D medical image-to-image translation in oncology, benchmarking seven generative models (3 GANs: Pix2Pix, CycleGAN, SRGAN; 4 latent methods: Latent Diffusion Model, Latent Diffusion Model+ControlNet, Brownian Bridge, Flow Matching) across 11 datasets, 3 regions (head/neck, lung, pelvis), and 4 translation directions (CBCT→CT, MRI→CT, CT→PET, MRI T2→T2-FLAIR), totaling 77 experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of standardized, clinically validated benchmarks for 3D I2I in medical imaging, as prior work is fragmented across tasks, modalities, and evaluation regimes.
AI.method: Uniform preprocessing, data splitting, inference, and multi-level evaluation framework. Comparative study of seven generative models across 11 datasets and 4 translation directions (77 experiments). Lesion-level analysis and a Visual Turing test with 17 physicians (15 radiologists).
AI.result: GANs outperform latent models across tasks; SRGAN shows statistically significant superiority. Lesion-level analysis: small lesions remain challenging; in CT→PET, shape is reproduced more reliably than absolute uptake. Visual Turing test yields near-chance differentiation (56.7%); synthetic volumes largely indistinguishable from real, yet quantitative metrics do not always align with clinical preference.
AI.conclusion: A reproducible benchmarking framework can standardize 3D oncologic I2I evaluation. GAN-based methods, particularly SRGAN, are more effective than latent models under uniform conditions. Clinical utility is promising but limited by small-lesion performance and metric-clinician preference discordance.

title: LightSplit: Practical Privacy-Preserving Split Learning via Orthogonal Projections
summary: Split learning (SL) enables collaborative training by partitioning a neural network across clients and a central server, but the cut-layer interface introduces a key challenge: high-dimensional activations incur substantial communication overhead while exposing representations vulnerable to reconstruction attacks. Existing approaches typically address efficiency or privacy in isolation, relying on additional mechanisms such as sparsification, quantization, or noise injection. We propose LightSplit, which limits information exposure and reduces communication overhead by applying a lightweight fixed orthogonal random projection at the cut layer. Based on Shannon's information theory, this projection acts as an information bottleneck that restricts instance-specific information and suppresses exploitable per-sample signals. By transmitting low-dimensional projections instead of raw activations, the server operates on lifted representations without requiring architectural modifications, ensuring compatibility with existing SL architectures. By avoiding additional trainable components on the client, the method remains lightweight and suitable for edge devices while preserving end-to-end differentiability via exact gradient propagation. As the projection is non-invertible, part of the original representation is irreversibly discarded at the client, LightSplit reduces the information available for reconstruction and limits information exposure. We extensively evaluate LightSplit on state-of-the-art benchmarks in both IID and non-IID settings across varying projection dimensions and client scales. Our results show that the method retains more than 95% of the baseline accuracy at up to 32x reduction in transmitted dimensionality while maintaining stable training dynamics.

AI.tldr: LightSplit uses a fixed orthogonal random projection at the split layer to reduce activations to a low-dimensional representation, limiting information exposure and cutting communication by up to 32x while preserving >95% baseline accuracy and maintaining end-to-end differentiability.
AI.motivation: Tackle the dual challenge in split learning of high communication overhead from high-dimensional activations and vulnerability to reconstruction attacks, without adding training overhead or changing SL architectures.
AI.method: Insert a lightweight fixed orthogonal random projection at the cut layer to produce low-dimensional, non-invertible representations; no trainable client-side components; gradient can propagate through the projection; compatible with existing SL setups; evaluate across IID/non-IID settings and multiple projection dims and client counts.
AI.result: Empirically preserves >95% of baseline accuracy with up to 32x reduction in transmitted dimensionality; demonstrates stable training dynamics across IID and non-IID settings and varying client scales.
AI.conclusion: LightSplit provides a lightweight, privacy-aware, communication-efficient improvement to split learning by introducing a non-invertible fixed projection that limits information exposure without sacrificing end-to-end differentiability or requiring architectural changes.

title: MedCore: Boundary-Preserving Medical Core Pruning for MedSAM
summary: Medical segmentation foundation models such as SAM and MedSAM provide strong prompt-driven segmentation, but their image encoders are still too large for many clinical settings. Compression is also risky in medicine because a model can keep high Dice while losing boundary fidelity. We propose MedCore, a structured pruning framework for MedSAM. The main idea is to preserve two kinds of structures: structures that became important during SAM-to-MedSAM adaptation, and structures that have high boundary leverage. We identify the first type by a dual-intervention score that compares zeroing a group with resetting it to its original SAM weight. We identify the second type by boundary-aware Fisher estimation. We also introduce a boundary leverage principle, which shows that compression-induced boundary displacement is controlled by logit perturbation on the boundary divided by the logit spatial gradient. This principle explains why boundary metrics can degrade even when Dice remains high. On polyp segmentation benchmarks, MedCore reduces parameters by 60.0% and FLOPs by 58.4% while achieving Dice 0.9549, Boundary F1 0.6388, and HD95 5.14 after recovery fine-tuning. It also reaches 86.6% parameter reduction and 90.4G FLOPs with strong boundary quality. Our analysis further shows that MedSAM lies in a head-fragile boundary regime: head-pruning steps have 2.887 times larger 95th-percentile boundary leverage than MLP-pruning steps, and this logit-level effect is consistent with BF1 and HD95 degradation. Our code is available at https://github.com/cenweizhang/MedCore.

AI.tldr: MedCore is a structured pruning framework for MedSAM that preserves boundary-relevant and adaptation-learned structures to compress medical segmentation models while maintaining boundary fidelity.
AI.motivation: Medical segmentation models must be deployable on resource-constrained devices. Naive compression often preserves Dice but harms boundary fidelity, which is critical in clinical practice.
AI.method: Identify important structures via dual-intervention score (group zeroing vs resetting to original SAM weights) and boundary-leverage structures via boundary-aware Fisher estimation. Introduce boundary leverage principle linking boundary displacement to logit perturbation over logit spatial gradient. Apply recovery fine-tuning and evaluate on polyp segmentation benchmarks.
AI.result: Parameters reduced by 60.0% and FLOPs by 58.4% with Dice 0.9549, Boundary F1 0.6388, HD95 5.14 after recovery. In a more aggressive setting, 86.6% parameter reduction and 90.4G FLOPs with strong boundary quality. Head-pruning shows 2.887× higher boundary leverage than MLP-pruning, aligning BF1 and HD95 degradation.
AI.conclusion: MedCore demonstrates effective, boundary-aware structured pruning for MedSAM, achieving substantial compression while maintaining boundary integrity. It reveals a head-fragile boundary regime and provides code for reproducibility.

title: Byzantine-Robust Distributed Sparse Learning Revisited
summary: We revisit Byzantine robust distributed estimation for high-dimensional sparse linear models. By combining local $\ell_1$-regularized robust estimation with robust aggregation at the server, the framework applies to pseudo-Huber regression, quantile regression, and sparse SVM. We show that the resulting estimators yield non-asymptotic guarantees and attain near-optimal statistical rates under mild conditions, while remaining communication-efficient. Simulations confirm strong robustness in estimation, support recovery and classification accuracy under various Byzantine attacks.

AI.tldr: A robust, Byzantine-robust distributed framework for high-dimensional sparse linear models using local L1-regularized robust estimators with robust aggregation; unifies pseudo-Huber, quantile regression, and sparse SVM; achieves non-asymptotic, near-optimal statistical rates with communication efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address Byzantine faults in distributed high-dimensional estimation, achieving statistical guarantees while keeping communication costs low.
AI.method: Combine local L1-regularized robust estimation with a robust aggregation step at the server; applies to multiple loss functions (pseudo-Huber, quantile regression, sparse SVM) under sparsity, with a unified framework.
AI.result: Non-asymptotic guarantees and near-optimal statistical rates under mild conditions; communication-efficient; simulations demonstrate robustness in estimation, support recovery, and classification under various Byzantine attacks.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, scalable approach to robust distributed estimation in the presence of adversarial workers for high-dimensional sparse models, with broad applicability and empirical validation.

title: Learning to Optimize Radiotherapy Plans via Fluence Maps Diffusion Model Generation and LSTM-based Optimization
summary: Volumetric Modulated Arc Therapy (VMAT) is a cornerstone of modern radiation therapy, enabling highly conformal tumor irradiation and healthy-tissue sparing. Yet, its planning solves inverse and nested optimization for multi-leaf collimators, monitor units and dose parameters, while enforcing their consistency to ensure mechanical deliverability. Nevertheless, this process often requires repeated re-optimization when treatment configurations change, resulting in substantial planning time per patient. To address these problems, we present a diffusion-driven Learning-to-Optimize (L2O) method for end-to-end VMAT planning. A distribution-matching distilled diffusion model learns a clinically feasible manifold of fluence maps, enabling their one-shot generation. On top of this, an LSTM-based L2O module learns gradient update dynamics to swiftly refine fluence maps toward prescribed dose objectives during inference. Experimental results on clinical and public prostate cancer cohorts demonstrate improved planning efficiency, flexibility, and machine deliverability over currently available end-to-end VMAT planners.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based Learning-to-Optimize (L2O) framework for VMAT planning: learns a fluence-map manifold via a distilled diffusion model and uses an LSTM-based L2O module to rapidly refine maps toward dose objectives in one shot.
AI.motivation: VMAT planning is computationally intensive due to inverse and nested optimizations and the need to ensure mechanical deliverability; repeated re-optimizations for configuration changes cause long planning times. A fast, end-to-end, deliverable planning solution is highly desirable.
AI.method: Train a distilled diffusion model to learn a clinically feasible manifold of fluence maps for one-shot generation. Use an LSTM-based L2O module to learn gradient-update dynamics that refine fluence maps toward prescribed dose objectives during inference, enabling end-to-end VMAT planning.
AI.result: Experiments on clinical and public prostate cancer cohorts show improved planning efficiency, flexibility, and machine deliverability compared with existing end-to-end VMAT planners.
AI.conclusion: The diffusion-driven L2O approach enables faster, more flexible, and deliverable VMAT planning, reducing the need for repeated re-optimizations and enabling end-to-end planning.

title: Delightful Exploration
summary: Most exploration algorithms search broadly until uncertainty is resolved. When the action space is too large to resolve within budget, practitioners default to $\varepsilon$-greedy, which bounds disruption but spends its override blindly. We introduce \textit{Delight-gated exploration} (DE), a host--override rule that spends exploratory actions only when their prospective delight (expected improvement times surprisal) exceeds a gate price. This practical heuristic recovers a classical result: Pandora's reservation-value rule for costly search, with surprisal setting the effective inspection cost. Resolved arms exit the gate, fresh arms shut off above a prior-determined threshold, and selected linear-bandit overrides consume finite information budget. Across Bernoulli bandits, linear bandits, and tabular MDPs, the same hyperparameters transfer without retuning, and DE shows much weaker regret growth than Thompson Sampling and $\varepsilon$-greedy in the tested unresolved regimes. Delight improves acting for the same reason it improves learning: it prices scarce resources by the product of upside and surprisal.

AI.tldr: Delight-gated exploration (DE) is a gating rule that only performs exploration when the expected improvement times surprisal (delight) exceeds a gate price, recovering a reservation-value-like solution for costly search and budgeting over multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Improve exploration efficiency under budget constraints in large action spaces where ε-greedy blindly overrides; enable principled resource allocation between exploration and exploitation.
AI.method: Introduce DE: a host–override rule using a gate price. Exploration occurs only if delight (upside × surprisal) surpasses the gate; resolved arms exit the gate, new arms are trimmed by a prior threshold, and linear-bandit overrides consume a finite information budget. Demonstrate via Bernoulli bandits, linear bandits, and tabular MDPs; hyperparameters transfer across settings.
AI.result: DE achieves much weaker regret growth than Thompson Sampling and ε-greedy in unresolved regimes, with the same hyperparameters working across domains without retuning.
AI.conclusion: Delight prices scarce resources via the product of upside and surprisal, enabling more effective action in exploration and improved learning efficiency.

title: AnyFlow: Any-Step Video Diffusion Model with On-Policy Flow Map Distillation
summary: Few-step video generation has been significantly advanced by consistency distillation. However, the performance of consistency-distilled models often degrades as more sampling steps are allocated at test time, limiting their effectiveness for any-step video diffusion. This limitation arises because consistency distillation replaces the original probability-flow ODE trajectory with a consistency-sampling trajectory, weakening the desirable test-time scaling behavior of ODE sampling. To address this limitation, we introduce AnyFlow, the first any-step video diffusion distillation framework based on flow maps. Instead of distilling a model for only a few fixed sampling steps, AnyFlow optimizes the full ODE sampling trajectory. To this end, we shift the distillation target from endpoint consistency mapping $(z_{t}\rightarrow z_{0})$ to flow-map transition learning $(z_{t}\rightarrow z_{r})$ over arbitrary time intervals. We further propose Flow Map Backward Simulation, which decomposes a full Euler rollout into shortcut flow-map transitions, enabling efficient on-policy distillation that reduces test-time errors (i.e., discretization error in few-step sampling and exposure bias in causal generation). Extensive experiments across both bidirectional and causal architectures, at scales ranging from 1.3B to 14B parameters, demonstrate that AnyFlow achieves performance matches or surpasses consistency-based counterparts in the few-step regime, while scaling with sampling step budgets.

AI.tldr: AnyFlow introduces any-step video diffusion distillation based on flow maps, replacing endpoint distillation with flow-map transitions to optimize the full ODE sampling trajectory, improving test-time scalability across all-step budgets.
AI.motivation: Consistency distillation struggles as more test-time steps are allocated; the endpoint-only trajectory distillation weakens the test-time scaling of ODE sampling, limiting effectiveness for any-step generation.
AI.method: Replace distillation target from endpoint mapping z_t -> z_0 to flow-map transition learning z_t -> z_r over arbitrary intervals; introduce Flow Map Backward Simulation to decompose a full Euler rollout into shortcut flow-map transitions, enabling on-policy distillation; evaluate on bidirectional and causal architectures from 1.3B to 14B parameters.
AI.result: AnyFlow matches or surpasses consistency-based methods in the few-step regime and scales with the sampling step budget, demonstrated across diverse architectures and large parameter scales (1.3B–14B).
AI.conclusion: Flow-map-based distillation enables efficient, any-step video diffusion by optimizing the full sampling trajectory, reducing discretization and exposure biases, and maintaining performance as sampling step budgets vary.

title: PaMM: Periodic Motif Memory for Atomistic Models with an Explicit Local-Structure Interface
summary: Periodic crystals repeatedly instantiate similar local coordination motifs across translated cells and chemically related structures, but current equivariant atomistic models usually encode these patterns only implicitly in dense edge features. We introduce PaMM, a periodic motif memory that augments the UMA eSCN-MD edge encoder with explicit pair and triplet lookup features. Pair motifs are keyed by $(Z_j, Z_i, b_r)$ and triplet motifs by $(Z_j, Z_i, Z_k, b_θ)$, hashed into fixed-size tables and fused with the baseline edge representation through lightweight gate-only and affine-equipped variants. We evaluate PaMM in a matched UMA-S OMAT setting and focus on a narrow question: whether explicit motif memory helps at a fixed intermediate training budget. At the 10k-step checkpoint, both PaMM variants improve over the plain baseline; gate-only gives the best energy MAE, while the affine-equipped variant gives the best force MAE. A matched 20k follow-up keeps the same operating-point picture. Aligned controls show that the gain weakens for pair-only, triplet-only, random-bucket, and parameter-matched MLP alternatives, suggesting that the benefit is tied to structured pair/triplet organization rather than generic added capacity. A within-OMAT24 source-family evaluation also shows small but consistent gains across held-out generation families. We therefore make a focused claim: in the studied UMA-S + OMAT regime, explicit pair/ triplet motif memory is a useful inductive bias for periodic atomistic modeling. We do not claim broad cross-dataset transfer, a uniquely preferred fusion variant, or strong scientific interpretability beyond a more inspectable local-structure interface.

AI.tldr: PaMM: explicit pair/triplet motif memory augments UMA-S eSCN-MD with fixed-size, hashed motif tables that capture periodic local structure; yields consistent improvements in energy and force MAE under fixed training budgets, driven by structured motifs rather than naive capacity.
AI.motivation: Periodic crystals exhibit recurring local coordination motifs across translated cells; current equivariant models encode these patterns implicitly in edge features. Explicit motif memory could bias learning toward repeatable local structures and improve data efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce PaMM: pair motifs keyed by (Z_j, Z_i, b_r) and triplet motifs keyed by (Z_j, Z_i, Z_k, b_θ), hashed into fixed-size tables. Fuse with the baseline UMA-S eSCN-MD edge representation via gate-only and affine-equipped variants. Evaluate in a matched UMA-S OMAT setting, at 10k- and 20k-step checkpoints. Compare to controls (pair-only, triplet-only, random-bucket, parameter-matched MLP). Also assess across OMAT24 source-family held-out generations.
AI.result: Both PaMM variants improve energy MAE and force MAE relative to the plain baseline at 10k steps; gate-only variant yields best energy MAE, while the affine-equipped variant yields best force MAE. At 20k steps, the same pattern holds. Gains weaken for pair-only, triplet-only, random-bucket, and MLP controls, suggesting the benefit arises from structured pair/triplet organization rather than generic capacity. Small but consistent gains also observed across held-out OMAT24 generation families.
AI.conclusion: Explicit pair/triplet motif memory provides a useful inductive bias for periodic atomistic modeling in the studied UMA-S + OMAT regime. The authors refrain from broad cross-dataset transfer claims, from asserting a single preferred fusion variant, and from claiming strong interpretability beyond a more inspectable local-structure interface.

title: Coordinating Multiple Conditions for Trajectory-Controlled Human Motion Generation
summary: Trajectory-controlled human motion generation aims to synthesize realistic human motions conditioned on both textual descriptions and spatial trajectories. However, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: first, the conflict between text and trajectory conditions disrupts the denoising process, resulting in compromised motion quality or inaccurate trajectory following; second, the use of redundant motion representations introduces inconsistencies between motion components, leading to instability during trajectory control. To address these challenges, we propose CMC, a decoupled framework that effectively coordinates text and trajectory conditions through a divide-and-conquer strategy. CMC follows a divide-and-conquer paradigm, comprising two cascaded stages: Trajectory Control and Motion Completion. In the first stage, a diffusion model generates a simplified representation of the controlled joints under trajectory guidance, based on the given trajectories, ensuring accurate and stable trajectory following. In the second stage, a text-conditioned diffusion inpainting model generates full-body motions using the simplified representation from the first stage as partial observations. To mitigate overfitting caused by limited inpainting training data, we further introduce the Selective Inpainting Mechanism (SIM), which alternates between text-to-motion generation and motion inpainting tasks during training. Experiments on HumanML3D and KIT datasets demonstrate that CMC achieves state-of-the-art performance in control accuracy and motion quality, demonstrating its effectiveness in coordinating multimodal conditions and representations.

AI.tldr: Proposes CMC, a two-stage diffusion framework that decouples trajectory control from text-conditioned motion completion to improve trajectory accuracy and motion quality, aided by a Selective Inpainting Mechanism (SIM).
AI.motivation: To address (i) conflict between text and trajectory conditions during denoising that degrades motion quality or trajectory following, and (ii) instability caused by redundant motion representations used for trajectory control.
AI.method: A divide-and-conquer approach with two cascaded stages: (1) Trajectory Control diffusion that generates a simplified representation of controlled joints guided by trajectories for accurate, stable trajectory following; (2) Motion Completion diffusion inpainting conditioned on text that completes full-body motion using the simplified representation as partial observations. Training uses SIM, which alternates between text-to-motion generation and motion inpainting to mitigate overfitting.
AI.result: Experiments on HumanML3D and KIT show state-of-the-art performance in control accuracy and motion quality, validating the effectiveness of coordinating multimodal conditions and representations.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling text and trajectory constraints with a two-stage diffusion framework and SIM yields robust trajectory following and coherent full-body motion, effectively mitigating prior limitations.

title: Safe Bayesian Optimization for Uncertain Correlations Matrices in Linear Models of Co-Regionalization
summary: This paper extends safety guarantees for multi-task Bayesian optimization with uncertain correlation matrices from intrinsic co-reginalization models to linear models of co-reginalization. The latter allows for more flexible modeling of the inter-task correlations by composing multiple features. We derive uniform error bounds for vector-valued functions sampled from a Gaussian process with a linear model of co-reginalization kernel. Furthermore, we show the potential improvement of performance using linear models of co-reginalization in a numerical comparison on a safe multi-task Bayesian optimization benchmark.

AI.tldr: Extends safety guarantees in safe multi-task Bayesian optimization by using a linear model of co-regularization (LMC) instead of intrinsic co-regularization; derives uniform error bounds for GP with LMC and shows potential empirical gains on a safe MTBO benchmark.
AI.motivation: To better capture inter-task correlations in MTBO and improve safety and sample efficiency by enabling more flexible correlation structures via LMC.
AI.method: Introduce LMC kernel for multi-task GP, derive uniform (high-probability) error bounds for vector-valued outputs, and perform numerical experiments on a safe MTBO benchmark comparing LMC to ICC.
AI.result: Uniform error bounds established for GP with LMC kernel; numerical experiments indicate potential performance improvements using LMC on safe MTBO benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Linear models of co-regularization offer a more flexible inter-task correlation modeling framework, preserving safety guarantees and potentially enhancing performance in safe MTBO.

title: Aligning Network Equivariance with Data Symmetry: A Theoretical Framework and Adaptive Approach for Image Restoration
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, Supplementary Material can be found at https://github.com/tanfy929/SA-Conv
summary: Image restoration is an inherently ill posed inverse problem. Equivariant networks that embed geometric symmetry priors can mitigate this ill posedness and improve performance. However, current understanding of the relationship between network equivariance and data symmetry remains largely heuristic. Particularly for real world data with imperfect symmetry, existing research lacks a systematic theoretical framework to quantify symmetry, select transformation groups, or evaluate model data alignment. To bridge this gap, we conduct an analysis from an optimization perspective and formalize the intrinsic relationship among data symmetry priors, model equivariance, and generalization capability. Specifically, we propose for the first time a quantifiable definition of non strict symmetry at the dataset level (rather than sample level) and use it as a constraint to formulate the restoration inverse problem. We then show that the equivariance for restoration models can be naturally derived from this inverse problems incorporated the proposed symmetry constraints, and that the equivariance error of the optimal restoration operator is strictly bounded by the data symmetry error and the discretization mesh size. Furthermore, by analyzing the network's empirical risk, we demonstrate that aligning equivariance with data symmetry optimizes the bias variance trade off, minimizing the total expected risk. Guided by these insights, we propose a Sample Adaptive Equivariant Network that uses a hypernetwork and transformation learnable equivariant convolutions to dynamically align with each sample's inherent symmetry. Extensive experiments on super resolution, denoising, and deraining validate our theoretical findings and show significant superiority over standard baselines and traditional equivariant models. Our code and supplementary material are available at https://github.com/tanfy929/SA-Conv.

AI.tldr: Presents a theoretical optimization framework linking dataset symmetry, model equivariance, and generalization in image restoration; introduces dataset-level non-strict symmetry, and a Sample Adaptive Equivariant Network (SA-Conv) with a hypernetwork to adapt to per-sample symmetry, supported by bounds and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Real-world data exhibit imperfect or non-strict symmetry making heuristic symmetry priors unreliable. A formal framework is needed to quantify symmetry, choose transformation groups, and align models to data symmetry to improve restoration tasks.
AI.method: 1) Define a dataset-level non-strict symmetry concept and incorporate it as a constraint in the restoration inverse problem. 2) Show that model equivariance can be derived from these symmetry-constrained inverse problems. 3) Prove that the optimal restoration operator's equivariance error is bounded by data symmetry error and discretization mesh size. 4) Analyze empirical risk to show that aligning equivariance with data symmetry optimizes bias-variance trade-off. 5) Propose Sample Adaptive Equivariant Network (SA-Conv) using a hypernetwork and transformation-learnable equivariant convolutions to align with each sample's symmetry. 6) Validate with experiments on super-resolution, denoising, and deraining; compare against baselines and traditional equivariant models; release code.
AI.result: The framework yields theoretical bounds on equivariance error tied to data symmetry and discretization; demonstrates that symmetry-aligned equivariance reduces expected risk via bias-variance trade-off; empirically outperforms standard baselines and conventional equivariant models across SR, denoising, and deraining tasks.
AI.conclusion: A principled link between data symmetry priors and network equivariance is established, with a practical SA-Conv that adapts to per-sample symmetry, improving restoration performance and generalization. The work suggests further exploration of symmetry quantification and group selection in real-data scenarios.

title: MPINeuralODE: Multiple-Initial-Condition Physics-Informed Neural ODEs for Globally Consistent Dynamical System Learning
summary: Neural ordinary differential equations (Neural ODEs) often fit training trajectories while generalizing poorly to unseen initial conditions and long horizons. We propose MPINeuralODE, which combines a soft physics-informed residual with a Multiple-Initial-Condition (MIC) multiple-shooting curriculum whose ingredients are structurally complementary: the physics term anchors the vector-field magnitude on the support that MIC enlarges. We evaluate along three axes: out-of-sample error, long-horizon stability, and Hamiltonian drift, which together expose whether the learned dynamics recover the underlying vector field. On Lotka-Volterra, MPINeuralODE achieves the lowest out-of-sample and long-horizon MSE among data-driven methods, with a 26% reduction over the baseline Neural ODE, while essentially matching the PINN ablation on Hamiltonian drift.

AI.tldr: MPINeuralODE fuses a soft physics-informed residual with a MIC multiple-shooting curriculum to improve generalization and long-horizon stability in Neural ODEs, outperforming baseline Neural ODEs and matching PINN on Hamiltonian drift in Lotka-Volterra.
AI.motivation: Standard Neural ODEs generalize poorly to unseen initial conditions and long horizons; need mechanisms to anchor dynamics and diversify initial-condition coverage.
AI.method: Introduce a soft physics-informed residual that stabilizes vector-field magnitude on the support, combined with a MIC multiple-shooting curriculum that expands initial-condition coverage; evaluate on out-of-sample error, long-horizon stability, and Hamiltonian drift.
AI.result: On Lotka-Volterra, MPINeuralODE yields the lowest out-of-sample and long-horizon MSE among data-driven methods, ~26% lower than the baseline Neural ODE; Hamiltonian drift comparable to PINN ablation.
AI.conclusion: The combination of physics-informed residual and MIC curriculum effectively recovers the underlying vector field, yielding improved generalization and stability without sacrificing physics-consistency.

title: Weakly-Supervised Spatiotemporal Anomaly Detection
summary: In this paper, we explore a weakly supervised method for anomaly detection. Since annotating videos is time-consuming, we only look at weak video-level labels during training. This means that given a video, we know that it is either normal or contains an anomaly, but no further annotations are used to train the network. Features are extracted from video clips that are either normal or anomalous. These features are used to determine anomaly scores for spatiotemporal regions of the clips based on a classifier and the implementation of a multiple instance ranking loss (MIL). We represent both anomalous and normal video clips as positive and negative bags, respectively, to apply MIL. Furthermore, since anomalies are usually localized to a part of a frame rather than the whole frame, we chose to explore temporal as well as spatial anomaly detection. We show our results on the UCF Crime2Local Dataset, which contains spatiotemporal annotations for a portion of the UCF Crime Dataset.

AI.tldr: Weakly supervised anomaly detection in videos using MIL with video-level labels; localizes anomalies in space-time on UCF Crime2Local.
AI.motivation: Annotating anomalies is expensive; need methods that learn from weak labels while localizing anomalies temporally and spatially.
AI.method: Extract features from clips; use MIL with positive bags for anomalous clips and negative bags for normal clips; train a classifier with a multiple instance ranking loss to produce spatiotemporal anomaly scores; localization across temporal and spatial dimensions.
AI.result: Evaluated on UCF Crime2Local dataset; demonstrates effectiveness in detecting and localizing anomalies under weak supervision; uses spatiotemporal annotations for a subset of the data.
AI.conclusion: MIL-based weakly supervised framework effectively performs spatiotemporal anomaly detection; leveraging both temporal and spatial cues improves localization under weak labeling.

title: Supervised Deep Multimodal Matrix Factorization for Interpretable Brain Network Analysis
summary: We present Supervised Deep Multimodal Matrix Factorization (SD3MF), an interpretable framework for integrative brain network analysis that generalizes Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Tri-Factorization (SNMTF) from unsupervised single-graph clustering to supervised prediction over populations of multimodal graphs. SD3MF learns deep hierarchical factorizations for each modality together with a shared latent representation that aligns subjects across views. An encoder-decoder formulation jointly optimizes graph reconstruction and supervised prediction, while adaptive weights enable data-driven multimodal fusion. By representing each subject through community-level interaction matrices, the model yields interpretable and discriminative features. Experiments on multimodal connectome datasets show that SD3MF consistently outperforms strong deep learning baselines such as CNNs and GNNs, while enabling biologically interpretable insights. Code for reproducibility is available at: https://github.com/amjadseyedi/SD3MF.

AI.tldr: A supervised deep multimodal matrix factorization framework for integrative brain network analysis with interpretable community-level features.
AI.motivation: To extend symmetric nonnegative matrix tri-factorization to a supervised, multimodal setting for brain networks, enabling interpretable, shared representations across subjects and modalities.
AI.method: SD3MF learns deep hierarchical factorizations per modality, a shared latent representation to align subjects across views, an encoder–decoder that jointly optimizes graph reconstruction and supervised prediction, and adaptive weights for data-driven multimodal fusion, yielding interpretable community-level interaction matrices.
AI.result: Empirically, SD3MF outperforms strong deep baselines (e.g., CNNs and GNNs) on multimodal connectome datasets and provides interpretable features; code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: SD3MF delivers interpretable, discriminative representations for population-level multimodal brain networks with supervised predictive capability, facilitating biological insights.

title: Generative Texture Diversification of 3D Pedestrians for Robust Autonomous Driving Perception
comment: Published at SAIAD 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: In recent years, autonomous driving has significantly in creased the demand for high-quality data to train 2D and 3D perception models for safety-critical scenarios. Real world datasets struggle to meet this demand as require ments continuously evolve and large-scale annotated data collection remains costly and time-consuming making syn thetic data a scalable, practical and controllable alterna tive. Pedestrian detection is among the most safety-critical tasks in autonomous driving. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective method for scaling variability in 3D pedestrian assets for synthetic scene generation. Starting from a single 3D base asset, we generate multiple distinct pedestrian instances by synthesizing diverse facial textures and identity-level appearance variations using StyleGAN2 and automatically mapping them onto 3D meshes. This ap proach enables scalable appearance-level asset diversifica tion without requiring the design of new geometries for each instance. Using the assets, we construct synthetic datasets and study the impact of mixing real and synthetic data for RGB-based object detection. Through complementary ex periments, we analyze geometry-driven distribution shifts in point cloud perception for 3D object detection. Our findings demonstrate that controlled synthetic diversifica tion improves robustness in 2D detection while revealing the sensitivity of 3D perception models to geometric domain gaps. Overall, this work highlights how generative AI en ables scalable, simulation-ready pedestrian diversification through controlled facial texture synthesis, along with the benefits and limitations of cross-domain training strategies in autonomous driving pipelines.

AI.tldr: Proposes scalable 3D pedestrian diversification by texture-synthesizing diverse facial identities with StyleGAN2 mapped to 3D meshes, enabling synthetic data generation for RGB detectors and 3D point-cloud detectors; finds improved 2D robustness from synthetic data but reveals geometry-driven domain gaps in 3D perception; discusses cross-domain training implications.
AI.motivation: Address the growing data demands of autonomous driving perception and the cost of real data. Synthetic data offers scalable, controllable diversity, but its effect on 2D vs 3D perception and domain gaps remains unclear.
AI.method: From a single 3D base pedestrian asset, generate multiple identity-level appearances by synthesizing facial textures with StyleGAN2 and mapping them onto the 3D mesh. Create synthetic datasets, mix with real data, and evaluate RGB-based 2D detection and 3D point-cloud detection to analyze effects of appearance diversification and geometric domain shifts.
AI.result: Controlled appearance diversification via synthetic textures improves robustness in 2D (RGB) detection. In 3D perception, results reveal sensitivity to geometric domain gaps, indicating that while texture diversification helps 2D generalization, geometry differences pose challenges for 3D detectors.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI enables scalable pedestrian diversification for synthetic data pipelines and affords insights into the benefits and limits of cross-domain training for autonomous driving perception. Geometry-aware evaluation is essential for 3D perception improvements when using synthetic data.

title: Shortcut Mitigation via Spurious-Positive Samples
summary: Shortcut mitigation strategies commonly rely on training data annotations, group-balanced held-out data or the presence of all groups, i.e., all combinations of (spurious) attributes and classes, in the training data. However, these requirements are rarely met in practice. We instead propose a method for targeted model analysis to identify a small set of instances in which the model relies on spurious attributes. Using that set and following ``this feature should not be used for prediction'' reasoning, we identify highly relevant neurons in an intermediate layer and regularize their impact. This ensures that models learn to depend on informative features rather than being right for the wrong reasons, thereby improving robustness without requiring additional balanced held-out data or annotations.

AI.tldr: Targeted analysis to detect spurious reliance and neuron-level regularization to curb it, improving robustness without requiring balanced data.
AI.motivation: Shortcuts often require annotated or exhaustively balanced data. In practice such data is unavailable; a data-efficient approach is needed to mitigate reliance on spurious attributes.
AI.method: Identify a small set of instances where the model relies on spurious attributes. Use a 'this feature should not be used for prediction' reasoning to locate highly relevant neurons in an intermediate layer, then regularize their impact to prevent reliance on spurious cues.
AI.result: Enhanced robustness and reduced dependence on spurious features, achieved without additional balanced held-out data or annotations.
AI.conclusion: Targeted, neuron-level regularization guided by instance-level analyses is a practical, data-efficient strategy to suppress reliance on spurious attributes and improve model robustness.

title: VoxCor: Training-Free Volumetric Features for Multimodal Voxel Correspondence
summary: Cross-modal 3D medical image analysis requires voxelwise representations that remain anatomically consistent across imaging contrasts, scanners, and acquisition protocols. Recent work has shown that frozen 2D Vision Transformer (ViT) foundation models can support such representations, but typical pipelines extract features along a single anatomical axis and adapt those features inside a registration solver for one image pair at a time, leaving complementary viewing directions unused and producing representations that do not transfer to new volumes. We introduce VoxCor, a training-free fit--transform method for reusable volumetric feature representations from frozen 2D ViT foundation models. During an offline fitting phase, VoxCor combines triplanar ViT inference with a compact closed-form weighted partial least squares (WPLS) projection that uses fitting-time voxel correspondences to select modality-stable anatomical directions in the triplanar feature space. At transform time, new volumes are mapped by triplanar ViT inference and linear projection alone, without fine-tuning or registration. Voxel correspondences can then be queried directly by nearest-neighbor search. We evaluate VoxCor on intra-subject Abdomen MR--CT and inter-subject HCP T2w--T1w tasks using deformable registration, voxelwise k-nearest-neighbor segmentation, and segmentation-center landmark localization. VoxCor improves the hardest cross-subject, cross-modality transfer settings, reduces encoder sensitivity for dense correspondence transfer, and yields registration performance competitive with handcrafted descriptors and learned 3D features. This positions VoxCor as a reusable feature layer for downstream multimodal analysis beyond pairwise registration. Code, configuration files, and implementation details are publicly available on GitHub at \href{https://github.com/guneytombak/VoxCor}{guneytombak/VoxCor}.

AI.tldr: VoxCor is a training-free fit-transform approach that harvests reusable volumetric features from frozen 2D ViT models via triplanar inference and a closed-form WPLS projection, enabling cross-modality, cross-subject voxelwise analysis without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: There is a need for anatomically consistent voxelwise representations that transfer across imaging contrasts, scanners, and protocols. Existing methods rely on axis-aligned, per-pair features and require adaptation within a registration solver, limiting transferability.
AI.method: Offline fitting combines triplanar ViT inference with a compact closed-form weighted partial least squares (WPLS) projection using voxel correspondences to identify modality-stable anatomical directions in 3D feature space. At transform time, new volumes are mapped by triplanar ViT inference and the learned linear projection without fine-tuning or registration; voxel correspondences can be queried by nearest-neighbor search.
AI.result: Evaluated on intra-subject Abdomen MR–CT and inter-subject HCP T2w–T1w with deformable registration, voxelwise k-nearest-neighbor segmentation, and landmark localization. VoxCor improves the hardest cross-subject, cross-modality transfer settings, reduces encoder sensitivity for dense correspondence transfer, and achieves registration performance competitive with handcrafted descriptors and learned 3D features.
AI.conclusion: VoxCor serves as a reusable feature layer for downstream multimodal analysis beyond pairwise registration; code and details are publicly available on GitHub (guneytombak/VoxCor).

title: Contextual Bandits for Resource-Constrained Devices using Probabilistic Learning
summary: Contextual bandits (CB) are online sequential decision-making problems under partial feedback that underpin many adaptive services. There is a growing demand to deploy CB agents directly on-device, under strict constraints on memory, compute, and energy. However, standard linear CB algorithms are often impractical for resource-constrained devices with their unfavorable scaling in computational and memory costs. Recently, HD-CB, a CB approach based on hyperdimensional computing principles, has been proposed to model and solve CB problems by moving into high-dimensional spaces. HD-CB offers faster convergence, favorable scalability, and improves memory efficiency compared to linear CB algorithms. However, its learning rule is accumulation-based: the values of action vectors grow over time, requiring high precision. While periodic binarization can prevent overflow in low-precision components, it may discard important information about magnitudes and degrade decision quality. This paper introduces probabilistic HD-CB, a low-precision variant that replaces deterministic accumulation with a probabilistic update rule. At each step, only a random subset of vector components is updated, with a time-decaying update probability, and component values are constrained to a predefined range [-k,+k]. This approach enables low-precision components, prevents overflow without periodic binarization, and reduces the expected update cost in proportion to the fraction of updated components. Off-policy evaluation on standardized synthetic CB benchmarks using the Open Bandit Pipeline shows that probabilistic HD-CB consistently outperforms binarized HD-CB at equal precision, while approaching the performance of HD-CB with as few as 3 bits per component.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic HD-CB: a low-precision, update-sparse variant of hyperdimensional-based contextual bandits; outperforms binarized HD-CB at equal precision and approaches full HD-CB performance with as few as 3 bits per component.
AI.motivation: Need on-device contextual bandits under strict memory, compute, and energy constraints. Linear CB scales poorly; HD-CB offers scalability but uses accumulation that requires high precision. Binarization reduces loss of magnitude information.
AI.method: Replace deterministic accumulation with a probabilistic update rule. At each step, update a random subset of vector components, with a time-decaying update probability, and constrain components to [-k, k]. This enables low-precision components, prevents overflow without periodic binarization, and reduces update cost proportional to the fraction updated.
AI.result: Off-policy evaluation on standardized synthetic CB benchmarks using the Open Bandit Pipeline shows probabilistic HD-CB consistently outperforms binarized HD-CB at equal precision, while approaching the performance of HD-CB with as few as 3 bits per component.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic HD-CB provides a viable low-precision, update-sparse variant of HD-CB that preserves performance while reducing memory and computation, enabling practical on-device contextual bandits; combines overflow control with partial updates and low-precision encoding.

title: EvoGround: Self-Evolving Video Agents for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: Video temporal grounding (VTG) takes an untrimmed video and a natural-language query as input and localizes the temporal moment that best matches the query. Existing methods rely on large, task-specific datasets requiring costly manual annotation. We introduce EvoGround, a framework of two coupled self-evolving agents, a proposer and a solver, that learn temporal grounding from raw videos without any human-labeled data. The proposer generates query--moment pairs from raw videos, while the solver learns to ground them and feeds back signals that improve the proposer in return. Through this self-reinforcing reinforcement-learning loop, the two agents are initialized from the same backbone and mutually improve across iterations. Trained on 2.5K unlabeled videos, EvoGround matches or surpasses fully supervised models across multiple VTG benchmarks, while emerging as a state-of-the-art fine-grained video captioner without manual labels.

AI.tldr: EvoGround learns VTG without labels via two self-evolving agents (proposer and solver) that iteratively generate and ground query-moment pairs from unlabeled videos, achieving competitive VTG performance and strong unsupervised captioning.
AI.motivation: Avoid costly annotation for video temporal grounding and push toward fully self-supervised video-language grounding by leveraging a feedback loop between generation and grounding.
AI.method: Two coupled agents initialized from the same backbone. Proposer creates query-moment pairs from raw videos; solver grounds them. The solver's grounding signals are fed back to refine the proposer. An iterative self-reinforcing RL loop is trained on 2.5k unlabeled videos; evaluation across VTG benchmarks.
AI.result: Outperformed or matched fully supervised models on multiple VTG benchmarks. Also achieves state-of-the-art performance as a fine-grained video captioner without manual labels.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that unsupervised VTG with mutually improving agents is feasible, reducing annotation needs while maintaining competitive performance and enabling strong zero-shot-like captioning capabilities.

title: GeoFlowVLM: Geometry-Aware Joint Uncertainty for Frozen Vision-Language Embedding
summary: Standard dual-encoder vision-language models that map images and text to deterministic points on a shared unit hypersphere through $\ell_2$ normalization typically expose neither \emph{aleatoric} uncertainty (cross-modal ambiguity) nor \emph{epistemic} uncertainty (lack of training-distribution support). Existing post-hoc methods either recover at most one of the two uncertainty components, or ignore the hyperspherical geometry of these models' embeddings. We propose \textbf{GeoFlowVLM} as a post-hoc adapter that learns the joint distribution of paired $\ell_2$-normalised dual-encoder VLM embeddings on the product hypersphere $\mathbb{S}^{d-1} \times \mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ via Riemannian flow matching with a single masked velocity field. A consistency result shows that, in the population limit, the trained network exposes the joint flow and both cross-modal conditional flows as valid Riemannian flow-matching velocity fields on their respective domains. We derive two quantities from this single model: a conditional retrieval entropy that quantifies aleatoric ambiguity with a decision-theoretic interpretation via a Fano-type bound, and a marginal-typicality epistemic score justified by an exact chain-rule decomposition of the joint NLL. This decomposition isolates a cross-modal pointwise-mutual-information term that is structurally discriminative rather than epistemic, and is empirically the only consistently uninformative standalone component. Empirically, the entropy tracks Recall@1 with near-ideal monotonic calibration across three retrieval benchmarks in both directions, and the marginal-typicality sum yields consistently calibrated selective accuracy across four zero-shot classification benchmarks.

AI.tldr: GeoFlowVLM introduces a post-hoc adapter that models the joint distribution of L2-normalised dual-encoder VLM embeddings on the product hypersphere using Riemannian flow matching, enabling explicit estimation of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. It yields calibrated retrieval entropy and selective accuracy through a single model, with strong empirical results across retrieval and zero-shot classification benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Deterministic, unit-h hypersphere embeddings in standard dual-encoder VLMs do not expose cross-modal aleatoric uncertainty or epistemic uncertainty from distribution shift. Existing post-hoc methods fail to recover both and often ignore the hyperspherical geometry, motivating a geometry-aware post-hoc uncertainty estimator.
AI.method: GeoFlowVLM is a post-hoc adapter that learns the joint distribution of paired L2-normalised embeddings on S^{d-1} × S^{d-1} via Riemannian flow matching using a single masked velocity field. The authors prove a population-consistency result: trained network recovers the joint flow and both cross-modal conditional flows as valid Riemannian flow-matching velocity fields. From this model they derive: (i) a conditional retrieval entropy capturing aleatoric ambiguity with a decision-theoretic interpretation via a Fano-type bound; (ii) a marginal-typicality epistemic score via an exact chain-rule decomposition of the joint negative log-likelihood, isolating a cross-modal pointwise-mutual-information term that is structurally discriminative and empirically the only consistently uninformative standalone component.
AI.result: Empirically, the conditional entropy tracks Recall@1 with near-ideal monotonic calibration across three retrieval benchmarks in both directions. The marginal-typicality sum yields consistently calibrated selective accuracy across four zero-shot classification benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware post-hoc uncertainty estimator for VLMs on hyperspheres that disentangles aleatoric and epistemic components via a unified Riemannian flow-matching framework. The derived entropy and marginal-typicality scores are calibrated and correlate with retrieval and zero-shot classification performance, respectively.

title: JANUS: Anatomy-Conditioned Gating for Robust CT Triage Under Distribution Shift
summary: Automated CT triage requires models that are simultaneously accurate across diverse pathologies and reliable under institutional shift. While Vision Transformers provide strong visual representations, many clinically significant findings are defined by quantitative imaging biomarkers rather than appearance alone. We introduce JANUS, a physiology-guided dual-stream architecture that conditions visual embeddings on macro-radiomic priors via Anatomically Guided Gating. On the MERLIN test set (N=5082), JANUS attains macro-AUROC 0.88 and AUPRC 0.74, outperforming all reproduced baselines. It generalizes to an external dataset N=2000; AUROC 0.87), with the largest gains on findings defined by size and attenuation as well as improved calibration on both datasets. We further quantify prediction suppression using the Physiological Veto Rate (PVR), showing that under domain shift JANUS reduces high-confidence false positives substantially more often than true positives. Together, these results are consistent with physically grounded conditioning that improves both discrimination and reliability in CT triage. Code is made publicly available at github repository https://github.com/lavsendahal/janus and model weights are at https://huggingface.co/lavsendahal/janus.

AI.tldr: Physiology-guided dual-stream JANUS for CT triage; macro-radiomic priors via Anatomically Guided Gating improve discrimination and reliability, achieving macro-AUROC 0.88, AUPRC 0.74 on MERLIN (N=5082) and AUROC 0.87 on external (N=2000); reduces high-confidence FP under domain shift; code and weights released.
AI.motivation: Address the need for accurate CT triage across diverse pathologies and under distribution shifts, where appearance-based models may underperform on biomarker-defined findings.
AI.method: JANUS: dual-stream architecture conditioning visual embeddings on macro-radiomic priors through Anatomically Guided Gating. Trained on MERLIN (N=5082); evaluated on external dataset (N=2000). Metrics include macro-AUROC, AUPRC, calibration measures, and Physiological Veto Rate (PVR) to quantify suppression of predictions under shift.
AI.result: Macro-AUROC 0.88 and AUPRC 0.74 on MERLIN; AUROC 0.87 on external; largest gains on findings defined by size and attenuation; improved calibration on both datasets; PVR indicates reduced high-confidence false positives under domain shift; public code and weights.
AI.conclusion: Physiology-guided conditioning improves discrimination and reliability in CT triage; architectural integration of anatomical priors enhances generalization to new institutions.

title: Building Interactive Real-Time Agents with Asynchronous I/O and Speculative Tool Calling
summary: There is a growing demand for agentic AI technologies for a range of downstream applications like customer service and personal assistants. For applications where the agent needs to interact with a person, real-time low-latency responsiveness is required; for example, with voice-controlled applications, under 1 second of latency is typically required for the interaction to feel seamless. However, if we want the LLM to reason and execute an agentic workflow with tool calling, this can add can add several seconds or more of latency, which is prohibitive for real-time latency-sensitive applications. In our work, we aim to enable real-time interaction even for agents with complex multi-turn tool calling. We propose Asynchronous I/O, which decouples the core agent reason-and-act thread from waiting for additional information from either the user or environment, thereby allowing for overlapping agentic processing while waiting on external delays. We also propose Speculative Tool Calling as a method to manage task execution when the agent is still unsure if it has received the full information or if additional user information may later be provided. For strong cloud models, our method can be applied out-of-the-box to existing real-time cloud APIs, providing 1.3-1.7$\times$ speedups with minor accuracy loss. To enable real-time interaction with small edge-scale models, we also present a clock-based training methodology that adapts the model to handle streaming inputs and asynchronous responses, and demonstrate a synthetic data generation strategy for SFT. Altogether, this approach provides 1.6-2.2$\times$ speedups with the Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct models across multiple tool calling benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Asynchronous I/O and Speculative Tool Calling enable real-time agentic AI with tool usage by overlapping processing with external delays and speculative execution; yield significant speedups with minimal accuracy loss on cloud and edge models.
AI.motivation: To reduce real-time latency in agentic workflows that involve multi-turn tool calls, enabling seamless interaction for latency-sensitive applications.
AI.method: Introduce Asynchronous I/O to decouple reasoning from waiting; propose Speculative Tool Calling to execute actions before full information is received; clock-based training for streaming inputs; synthetic data generation for SFT; evaluate on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct with multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: 1.3-1.7x speedups on strong cloud models with minor accuracy loss; 1.6-2.2x speedups on edge models; demonstrated across multiple tool calling benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Real-time interaction is achievable for agentic systems with complex tool usage by combining asynchronous processing, speculative execution, and specialized training; promising for real-time AI assistants and customer-service applications.

title: Phasor Memory Networks: Stable Backpropagation Through Time for Scalable Explicit Memory
summary: For over a decade, explicit memory architectures like the Neural Turing Machine have remained theoretically appealing yet practically intractable for language modeling due to catastrophic gradient instability during Backpropagation Through Time. In this work, we break this stalemate with \textit{Phasor Memory Network} (PMNet), a novel architecture that structurally resolves memory volatility through \textit{Unitary Phasor Dynamics} and \textit{Hierarchical Learnable Anchors}. Rather than relying on brute-force scaling, we present a mechanistic proof-of-concept in a controlled byte-level setting. By constraining recurrent state updates to phase rotations on a complex unit circle, PMNet preserves gradient norms and inherently prevents divergence without the need for specialized initialization. We empirically demonstrate the active actuation of the memory module through a synthetic Copy-Paste task, where PMNet utilizes an expansive \textit{85-slot hierarchical memory tree} ($=\sum^{4}_{h=1}4^{h-1}$) to achieve near 100\% exact retrieval across temporal distances that completely exceed the local sliding window attention's receptive field. Furthermore, despite being a compact 119M parameter model trained on 18.8B tokens, PMNet matches the zero-shot long-context robustness of a Mamba model that is three times larger. Our ablation studies and gradient analyses confirm that the historical failure of explicit memory was a structural alignment problem, which PMNet effectively overcomes, providing a theoretically grounded foundation for scalable sequence modeling.

AI.tldr: PMNet introduces Unitary Phasor Dynamics with hierarchical anchors to stabilize gradient flow in explicit memory networks, enabling near-perfect long-range retrieval in a compact 119M-parameter model and matching much larger models on long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: Explicit memory architectures (e.g., Neural Turing Machine) struggle with catastrophic gradient instability when trained with backpropagation through time. A principled, scalable mechanism is needed to preserve gradients and enable long-range memory without brute-force scaling or special initializations.
AI.method: Update dynamics are constrained to phase rotations on the complex unit circle (unitary dynamics) to preserve gradient norms. Memory uses hierarchical learnable anchors forming an 85-slot memory tree (sum_{h=1}^4 4^{h-1}). Evaluation conducted on a synthetic Copy-Paste task in a byte-level setting. Ablation studies and gradient analyses support the approach. 119M parameters trained on 18.8B tokens.
AI.result: PMNet achieves near-100% exact retrieval across temporal distances beyond the local attention window. The 85-slot hierarchical memory enables long-range actuation. Despite smaller size, PMNet matches the zero-shot long-context robustness of a model roughly three times larger (Mamba). Abalations indicate gradient stability stems from structural alignment achieved by the proposed dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The historical failures of explicit memory models are attributed to a structural alignment problem rather than scale alone. PMNet provides a theoretically grounded mechanism (unitary phasor dynamics with hierarchical anchors) to stabilize memory and enable scalable sequence modeling.

title: Training Long-Context Vision-Language Models Effectively with Generalization Beyond 128K Context
summary: Long-context modeling is becoming a core capability of modern large vision-language models (LVLMs), enabling sustained context management across long-document understanding, video analysis, and multi-turn tool use in agentic workflows. Yet practical training recipes remain insufficiently explored, particularly for designing and balancing long-context data mixtures. In this work, we present a systematic study of long-context continued pre-training for LVLMs, extending a 7B model from 32K to 128K context with extensive ablations on long-document data. We first show that long-document VQA is substantially more effective than OCR transcription. Building on this observation, our ablations further yield three key findings: i) for sequence-length distribution, balanced data outperforms target-length-focused data (e.g., 128K), suggesting that long-context ability requires generalizable key-information retrieval across various lengths and positions; ii) retrieval remains the primary bottleneck, favoring retrieval-heavy mixtures with modest reasoning data for task diversity; and iii) pure long-document VQA largely preserves short-context capabilities, suggesting that instruction-formatted long data reduces the need for short-data mixing. Based on these findings, we introduce MMProLong, obtained by long-context continued pre-training from Qwen2.5-VL-7B with only a 5B-token budget. MMProLong improves long-document VQA scores by 7.1% and maintains strong performance at 256K and 512K contexts beyond its 128K training window, without additional training. It further generalizes to webpage-based multimodal needle retrieval, long-context vision-text compression, and long-video understanding without task-specific supervision. Overall, our study establishes a practical LongPT recipe and an empirical foundation for advancing long-context vision-language models.

AI.tldr: Long-context continued pre-training (LongPT) is studied for LVLMs, revealing that balanced long-context data improves generalization, retrieval remains the bottleneck, and long-document VQA does not erode short-context abilities. MMProLong (5B-token budget) shows strong gains and broad generalization across tasks and contexts (up to 512K).
AI.motivation: Address the gap in practical training recipes for long-context vision-language models, specifically how to design and balance long-context data mixtures to enable robust performance across very long contexts (tens to hundreds of thousands of tokens).
AI.method: Systematic long-context continued pre-training (LongPT) on a 7B LVLM, scaling context from 32K to 128K, with extensive ablations on long-document data; experiments comparing long-document VQA versus OCR transcription; analysis of sequence-length distributions; evaluation of retrieval-heavy mixtures; analysis of instruction-formatted long data; formation of MMProLong by continuing pre-training with limited budget (5B tokens) from Qwen2.5-VL-7B; assessment of performance on long-context VQA, generalization tasks, and very long contexts (256K, 512K).
AI.result: Three key findings: (i) balanced long-context data outperforms target-length-specific data for generalization across lengths; (ii) retrieval remains the primary bottleneck, favoring retrieval-heavy data mixtures with modest reasoning data; (iii) longer, instruction-formatted data preserves short-context abilities and reduces need for short-data mixing. MMProLong yields a 7.1% improvement on long-document VQA and maintains competency at 256K and 512K contexts without extra training; shows transfer to webpage-based retrieval, vision-text compression, and long-video understanding without task-specific supervision.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a practical LongPT recipe and provides empirical evidence that carefully balanced long-context data plus efficient retrieval-focused training can extend LVLMs to very long contexts without sacrificing short-context abilities, enabling broad generalization to long-context multimodal tasks.

title: Beyond Oversquashing: Understanding Signal Propagation in GNNs Via Observables
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) perform computations on graphs by routing the signal between graph regions using a graph shift operator or a message passing scheme. Often, the propagation of the signal leads to a loss of information, where the signal tends to diffuse across the graph instead of being deliberately routed between regions of interest. Two notions that depict this phenomenon are oversmoothing and oversquashing. In this paper, we propose an alternative approach for modeling signal propagation, inspired by quantum mechanics, using the notion of observables. Specifically, we model the place in the graph where the signal lies, how much the signal is concentrated there, and how much of the signal is propagated towards a location of interest when applying a GNN. Using these new concepts, we prove that standard spectral GNNs have poor signal propagation capabilities. We then propose a new type of spectral GNN, termed Schrödinger GNN, which we show has a superior capacity to route the signal across the graph.

AI.tldr: Introduces Schrödinger GNNs inspired by quantum observables to control signal propagation and routing in graphs, addressing oversmoothing/oversquashing; shows standard spectral GNNs poorly propagate signals and proposes a spectral Schrödinger GNN with superior routing.
AI.motivation: To overcome information loss in GNNs due to diffusion, by modeling signal location, concentration, and propagation using quantum-inspired observables.
AI.method: Define observables for signal localization, concentration, and transfer; theoretically analyze standard spectral GNNs; introduce Schrödinger GNN architecture that leverages quantum-inspired propagation; provide theoretical justification for improved routing.
AI.result: The analysis shows standard spectral GNNs have limited signal propagation. The Schrödinger GNN demonstrates a superior capacity to route signals across the graph, according to the authors' theoretical arguments (and possibly empirical validation).
AI.conclusion: Quantum-inspired observables offer a new lens to design GNNs with improved signal routing. The Schrödinger GNN is proposed as a promising spectral GNN variant for more effective information propagation.

title: Unlocking Patch-Level Features for CLIP-Based Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) enables models to continuously integrate new knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Driven by the remarkable generalization of CLIP, leveraging pre-trained vision-language models has become a dominant paradigm in CIL. However, current work primarily focuses on aligning global image embeddings (i.e., [CLS] token) with their corresponding text prompts (i.e., [EOS] token). Despite their good performance, we find that they discard the rich patch-level semantic information inherent in CLIP's encoders. For instance, when recognizing a rabbit, local patches may encode its distinctive cues, such as long ears and a fluffy tail, which can provide complementary evidence for recognition. Based on the above observation, we propose SPA (Semantic-guided Patch-level Alignment) for CLIP-based CIL, which aims to awaken long-neglected local representations within CLIP. Specifically, for each class, we first construct representative and diverse visual samples and feed them to GPT-5 as visual guidance to generate class-wise semantic descriptions. These descriptions are used to guide the selection of discriminative patch-level visual features. Building upon these selected patches, we further employ optimal transport to align selected patch tokens with semantic tokens from class-wise descriptions, yielding a structured cross-modal alignment that improves recognition. Furthermore, we introduce task-specific projectors for effective adaptation to downstream incremental tasks, and sample pseudo-features from stored class-wise Gaussian statistics to calibrate old-class representations, thereby mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SPA achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: SPA (Semantic-guided Patch-level Alignment) enhances CLIP-based class-incremental learning by exploiting patch-level semantics. It generates class-wise semantic descriptions via GPT-5 from representative samples, selects discriminative patch features guided by these descriptions, and uses optimal transport to align patch tokens with semantic tokens. It includes task-specific adapters and Gaussian-based old-class calibration to mitigate forgetting, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Global CLIP embeddings neglect rich patch-level information; exploiting local patch semantics can provide complementary cues for recognition and improve retention in incremental learning.
AI.method: 1) For each class, collect representative and diverse visual samples. 2) Use GPT-5 to generate class-wise semantic descriptions. 3) Use these descriptions to guide the selection of discriminative patch-level visual features. 4) Apply optimal transport to align the selected patch tokens with semantic tokens from the class descriptions. 5) Introduce task-specific projectors for downstream task adaptation. 6) Sample pseudo-features from stored class-wise Gaussian statistics to calibrate old-class representations and reduce forgetting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on CLIP-based class-incremental learning benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of patch-level semantic guidance and cross-modal alignment.
AI.conclusion: Patch-level semantic guidance and cross-modal patch alignment effectively awaken rich local representations in CLIP, yielding substantial gains in CIL while mitigating forgetting through task-specific adapters and Gaussian-based calibration.

title: Teaching and Learning under Deductive Errors
summary: Most models of machine teaching and learning assume the learner makes no errors in its internal deductive inference. However, humans and large language models in few-shot learning regimes are two important examples of learners where this does not hold. They fail on some consistency checks, and they can fail stochastically. In this paper we introduce a teaching and learning framework that takes these deductive errors into account. We specifically study the case of machine teaching, as different characterizations of the teacher can account for both machine teaching and learning. In an overhauled Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) setting, we study theoretically that, for some estimated error level, the teacher must find a PAC teaching set that with high probability will lead the learner to guess a hypothesis that is approximately correct. We study the computational complexity of six different problems related to computing optimal PAC teaching sets. We give XP algorithms parametrized by size of teaching set, with tight runtime bounds under standard complexity assumptions like ETH. These results are complemented with a small experimental study of which teaching and learning protocols can best represent the observed behavior in some LLM teaching sessions.
