AI.tldr: Introduces a framework for teaching and learning that accounts for deductive errors in learners (humans and LLMs) within an overhauled PAC setting; analyzes six problems for computing optimal PAC teaching sets; provides XP algorithms parameterized by teaching-set size with tight ETH-based bounds; includes a small experimental study of teaching/learning protocols in LLM sessions.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where learners (humans/LLMs) make deductive errors, violating standard error-free assumption in machine teaching. Aim to unify machine teaching and learning under realistic noisy inference, and to provide theoretical and computational insights for designing robust teaching sets.
AI.method: Formulates an overhauled PAC framework incorporating an estimated error level and a PAC guarantee. Analyzes six problems related to computing optimal PAC teaching sets. Develops XP algorithms parameterized by the teaching-set size with tight runtime bounds under ETH. Complements theory with a small empirical study of teaching/learning protocols in LLM sessions.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that for a given error level, a PAC teaching set can lead the learner to an approximately correct hypothesis with high probability. Establishes computational complexity for six related problems and provides XP algorithms with tight bounds. Empirical study suggests which teaching/learning protocols best reflect observed LLM behavior.
AI.conclusion: The framework integrates deductive errors into machine teaching, offering tractable (XP) algorithms under parameterization and empirical validation. It lays a foundation for robust teaching strategies in noisy learners and indicates directions for future work, including deeper empirical validation and refinement of the error model.

title: R-DMesh: Video-Guided 3D Animation via Rectified Dynamic Mesh Flow
comment: Accepted by SIGGRAPH 2026, Project Page: https://r-dmesh.github.io/ Code URL: https://github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/R-DMesh
summary: Video-guided 3D animation holds immense potential for content creation, offering intuitive and precise control over dynamic assets. However, practical deployment faces a critical yet frequently overlooked hurdle: the pose misalignment dilemma. In real-world scenarios, the initial pose of a user-provided static mesh rarely aligns with the starting frame of a reference video. Naively forcing a mesh to follow a mismatched trajectory inevitably leads to severe geometric distortion or animation failure. To address this, we present Rectified Dynamic Mesh (R-DMesh), a unified framework designed to generate high-fidelity 4D meshes that are ``rectified'' to align with video context. Unlike standard motion transfer approaches, our method introduces a novel VAE that explicitly disentangles the input into a conditional base mesh, relative motion trajectories, and a crucial rectification jump offset. This offset is learned to automatically transform the arbitrary pose of the input mesh to match the video's initial state before animation begins. We process these components via a Triflow Attention mechanism, which leverages vertex-wise geometric features to modulate the three orthogonal flows, ensuring physical consistency and local rigidity during the rectification and animation process. For generation, we employ a Rectified Flow-based Diffusion Transformer conditioned on pre-trained video latents, effectively transferring rich spatio-temporal priors to the 3D domain. To support this task, we construct Video-RDMesh, a large-scale dataset of over 500k dynamic mesh sequences specifically curated to simulate pose misalignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that R-DMesh not only solves the alignment problem but also enables robust downstream applications, including pose retargeting and holistic 4D generation.

AI.tldr: Rectified Dynamic Mesh (R-DMesh) tackles pose misalignment in video-guided 3D animation by learning a rectification offset that aligns an arbitrary input mesh with the video’s initial frame. It introduces a VAE to disentangle base pose, relative motion, and a rectification jump, a Triflow Attention for geometry-aware three-flow modulation, and a Rectified Flow-based Diffusion Transformer guided by video latents. A large Video-RDMesh dataset supports evaluation.
AI.motivation: In practical video-driven 3D animation, the initial pose of a user-provided static mesh often misaligns with the reference video’s starting frame, causing distortion or failure if naively animated. The work aims to remove this pose misalignment by learning a rectification offset that aligns the input with the video context before animation.
AI.method: A unified framework comprising: (1) a variational autoencoder that disentangles input into a conditional base mesh, relative motion trajectories, and a rectification jump offset; (2) Triflow Attention that modulates three orthogonal geometric flows with vertex-wise features to preserve physical consistency; (3) a Rectified Flow-based Diffusion Transformer conditioned on pre-trained video latents to transfer spatio-temporal priors to 3D; and (4) a Video-RDMesh dataset (~500k dynamic mesh sequences) for pose misalignment scenarios.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that R-DMesh resolves pose misalignment, enabling robust downstream tasks such as pose retargeting and holistic 4D generation.
AI.conclusion: R-DMesh provides a unified, data-driven solution to the pose misalignment problem in video-guided 3D animation by learning a rectification offset and leveraging geometry-aware flows and video priors to produce high-fidelity 4D meshes.

title: Support-Conditioned Flow Matching Is Kernel Smoothing
comment: Submitted to NeurIPS 2026. 18 pages, 10 figures, 1 table. Code at https://github.com/BaroqueObama/kernel-flow-matching-code
summary: Generative models are often conditioned on a small set of examples via cross-attention. Under the Gaussian optimal-transport path, we show that the exact velocity field induced by a finite support set is a Nadaraya--Watson kernel smoother whose bandwidth decreases with flow time, from broad averaging at early steps to nearest-neighbor at late steps. A single Gaussian-kernel attention head exactly computes this field, connecting cross-attention conditioning to classical kernel theory. The theory predicts three failure regimes: nearest-neighbor collapse of the kernel at high dimension, mismatch between the isotropic kernel and the data geometry, and insufficient support for nonparametric estimation. Experiments on Gaussian mixtures, spherical shells, and DINOv2 ImageNet features confirm that learned conditioning improves in precisely these regimes, and that IP-Adapter's cross-attention implements approximate NW smoothing in practice.

AI.tldr: Under Gaussian OT paths, the exact conditioning field from finite support is a Nadaraya--Watson kernel smoother with bandwidth shrinking over time; a single Gaussian-attention head can implement it; three failure regimes arise (high-dim NN collapse, kernel-data geometry mismatch, insufficient support); experiments validate theory and show IP-Adapter approximates NW smoothing.
AI.motivation: Link cross-attention conditioning in diffusion-like generative models to classical kernel smoothing theory; understand when finite-support conditioning behaves well or fails and how attention kernels perform.
AI.method: Derive the exact velocity field under Gaussian optimal-transport with finite support as a Nadaraya--Watson kernel smoother; show a single Gaussian-attention head computes it; analyze bandwidth evolution with flow time; identify three failure regimes; empirical validation on Gaussian mixtures, spherical shells, and DINOv2 ImageNet features; assess IP-Adapter.
AI.result: Predicted regimes: early broad averaging transitioning to late nearest-neighbor behavior; bandwidth decreases with flow time; a Gaussian head exacts the NW field; conditioning improves when regimes are avoided; IP-Adapter implements approximate NW smoothing in practice.
AI.conclusion: Cross-attention conditioning can be understood via classical kernel theory; attention heads can implement NW smoothing; practical conditioning performance hinges on dimension, geometry, and support; findings guide design of conditioning mechanisms in generative models.

title: Taming the Long Tail: Rebalancing Adversarial Training via Adaptive Perturbation
summary: Deep neural networks are highly vulnerable to adversarial examples, i.e.,small perturbations that can significantly degrade model performance. While adversarial training has become the primary defense strategy, most studies focus on balanced datasets, overlooking the challenges posed by real-world long-tail data. Motivated by the fact that perturbations in adversarial examples inherently alter the training distribution, we theoretically investigate their impact. We first revisit adversarial training for long-tail data and identify two key limitations: (i) a skewed training objective caused by class imbalance, and (ii) unstable evolution of adversarial distributions. Furthermore, we show that perturbations can simultaneously address both adversarial vulnerability and class imbalance. Based on these insights, we propose RobustLT, a plug-and-play framework that adaptively adjusts perturbations during adversarial training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RobustLT consistently enhances adversarial robustness and class-balance on long-tailed datasets. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/zhang-lilin/RobustLT}{https://github.com/zhang-lilin/RobustLT}.

AI.tldr: RobustLT adapts perturbations during adversarial training to improve robustness and class balance on long-tailed data.
AI.motivation: Adversarial training on real-world long-tail data is challenged by severe class imbalance, which skews the training objective, and by unstable evolution of adversarial distributions. Perturbations inherently modify the training distribution, suggesting a joint path to address both robustness and imbalance.
AI.method: RobustLT is a plug-and-play framework that adaptively adjusts perturbations during adversarial training to align the perturbed training distribution with long-tail characteristics, enabling simultaneous improvement in robustness and class balance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show RobustLT consistently improves adversarial robustness and class balance on long-tailed datasets, with code available for replication.
AI.conclusion: RobustLT provides a practical, adaptable enhancement to AT in long-tail settings, bridging robustness and data imbalance and compatible with existing pipelines.

title: The Diffusion Encoder
summary: We construct a new kind of encoder, leveraging the expressive power of diffusion models. In a traditional variational autoencoder, the encoder and decoder jointly negotiate a latent representation of the input. This is made possible by the reparameterization trick, which simplifies training at the cost of restricting the encoder to a simple family of distributions. Replacing this encoder with a diffusion model requires rethinking how the decoder pressure can be transmitted back to the encoder, given that they tend to update their internal estimates of the latent in opposing directions. We solve this problem with an alternating training scheme, inspired by the expectation-maximization algorithm. Our method enables more reliable synchronization between encoder and decoder, while preserving the simple and efficient training objective of standard diffusion models.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based encoder replaces VAE encoder; EM-like alternating training aligns encoder/decoder; preserves diffusion-model training simplicity.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of reparameterization-based VAEs restricting encoder distribution; diffusion models offer richer latent expressivity; need a training scheme to transmit decoder pressure to encoder without destabilizing training.
AI.method: Propose alternating training scheme inspired by EM, jointly updating encoder and decoder to synchronize latent estimates; retains standard diffusion objective.
AI.result: Claims more reliable encoder-decoder synchronization; training objective remains simple and efficient.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of diffusion-model-based encoders to enhance latent representation without sacrificing diffusion-model training simplicity.

title: When is Warmstarting Effective for Scaling Language Models?
summary:
Model growth from a given checkpoint aims to accelerate training of a larger model, offering potential resource savings. Despite recent interest, warmstarting has seen limited practical adoption in large-scale training. We attribute this to two underexplored factors: (1) an overemphasis on preserving the smaller model's performance at initialization, which constrains operator design for new architectures, and (2) insufficient analysis of how growth interacts with hyperparameters and scaling behavior, compounded by inconsistent growth factors across the literature.
  We show that preserving the base model's initial post-growth performance is not necessary for strong final performance, and that simple, architecture-agnostic growth strategies can outperform more complex warmstarting operators. Crucially, we empirically identify an upper bound on the growth factor $g$ beyond which training from scratch is more efficient. We observe this across multiple ablation setups. Notably, this limit is also present, but unreported, in prior published results. Across our experiments on dense MLPs and dense language models, we find that a $2\times$ growth factor is the most reliable in yielding convergence speedups, with gains most pronounced under 20 tokens/parameter budgets and diminishing as budget increases. We fit scaling laws over these observations to provide predictive guidance for practitioners deciding when and how much to grow. Together, our analysis provides practical guidelines and empirical limits for model growth.

AI.tldr: Growth from a checkpoint can accelerate training but has a practical upper bound; 2x growth is typically the most reliable for speedups, with gains strongest at small budgets and diminishing as budgets grow; preserving the base model's initial post-growth performance is not essential; simple, architecture-agnostic growth strategies can surpass complex warmstarting operators; scaling laws are provided to guide practitioners.
AI.motivation: Address why warmstarting is underutilized in large-scale training by identifying overlooked factors and providing practical growth guidelines and limits.
AI.method: Empirical study across dense MLPs and dense language models with ablations on growth factor g, examining interaction with hyperparameters and scaling behavior; compare warmstarting strategies to growth-from-scratch; fit scaling laws to predict when growth is beneficial.
AI.result: Found that initial post-growth performance preservation is unnecessary for strong final performance; an upper bound on g exists beyond which training from scratch is more efficient; 2x growth yields the most reliable convergence speedups, especially at budget levels up to ~20 tokens/parameter; gains diminish at larger budgets; scaling laws are fit to provide predictive guidance.
AI.conclusion: Provide practical guidelines and empirical limits for model growth: use up to ~2x growth for reliable speedups; avoid overemphasizing preserving base model performance; use simple, architecture-agnostic growth strategies; consult the scaling laws to decide when to grow and by how much.

title: DP-KFC: Data-Free Preconditioning for Privacy-Preserving Deep Learning
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 9 pages main text + appendix, 5 figures, 2 tables. Code: https://github.com/molinamarcvdb/DP-KFC
summary: Differentially private optimization suffers from a fundamental geometric mismatch: deep networks have highly anisotropic loss landscapes, yet DP-SGD injects isotropic noise. Second-order preconditioning can resolve this, but estimating curvature typically requires private data (consuming privacy budget) or public data (introducing distribution shift). We show that the Fisher Information Matrix decouples into architectural sensitivity, recoverable via synthetic noise, and input correlations, approximable from modality-specific frequency statistics. We propose DP-KFC, which constructs KFAC preconditioners by probing networks with structured synthetic noise, requiring neither private nor public data. Empirically, DP-KFC consistently outperforms DP-SGD and adaptive baselines across diverse modalities in strong privacy regimes ($\varepsilon \leq 3$). DP-KFC matches private-data preconditioners while public-data variants degrade by up to $4.8\%$, showing that curvature can be estimated without consuming privacy budget or introducing distribution shift. This enables privacy-preserving learning in specialized domains (e.g., medical applications) where regulatory constraints make data scarce.

AI.tldr: DP-KFC enables privacy-preserving curvature-aware optimization by constructing Fisher-based preconditioners from synthetic noise, avoiding private/public data usage and improving DP-SGD in anisotropic loss landscapes.
AI.motivation: DP-SGD suffers from isotropic noise in highly anisotropic loss landscapes. Curvature estimation typically requires private data (privacy budget) or public data (distribution shift). A data-free curvature estimator for DP optimization is needed.
AI.method: Decompose the Fisher Information into architectural sensitivity and input correlations; build DP-KFC by probing networks with structured synthetic noise to estimate a Fisher-based Kronecker-factored (KFAC) preconditioner, without private or public data.
AI.result: DP-KFC consistently outperforms DP-SGD and adaptive baselines across modalities under strong privacy (ε ≤ 3); matches performance of private-data preconditioners; public-data variants degrade by up to 4.8%; demonstrates curvature estimation without privacy budget or distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: DP-KFC enables curvature-aware DP learning in privacy-constrained domains, enabling privacy-preserving learning in data-scarce fields (e.g., medical applications) where regulatory constraints limit data availability.

title: LIFT: Last-Mile Fine-Tuning for Table Explicitation
summary: We propose last-mile fine-tuning, or Lift, a pipeline in which a pre-trained large language model extracts an initial table from unstructured clipboard text, and a fine-tuned small language model (1B-24B parameters SLM) repairs errors in the extracted table. On a benchmark of 2,596 tables from three datasets, Lift matches or exceeds end-to-end SLM fine-tuning on tree-edit-distance-based similarity (TEDS) metric while requiring as little as 1,000 training examples - where it outperforms end-to-end fine-tuning by up to 0.144 TEDS points. We term this approach last-mile fine-tuning and show it also more robust to input format variability. Comparisons with self-debug and end-to-end fine-tuning approaches show that last-mile fine-tuning provides an attractive option when training data is limited or when robustness to input variation is sought without compromising on accuracy.

AI.tldr: Last-mile fine-tuning (Lift): a two-stage pipeline where a pre-trained LLM extracts an initial table from unstructured clipboard text and a fine-tuned small LM repairs errors in the table. It matches or exceeds end-to-end SLM fine-tuning on tree-edit-distance-based similarity (TEDS) with as few as 1k training examples, and shows robustness to input variability.
AI.motivation: Improve data efficiency and robustness in table extraction from unstructured text by delegating extraction to a strong LLM and error correction to a smaller, fine-tuned model, reducing data needs and improving robustness versus end-to-end fine-tuning.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) a pre-trained LLM extracts an initial table from unstructured clipboard text; (2) a fine-tuned small language model (1B–24B) repairs errors in the extracted table. Evaluation on 2,596 tables from three datasets using the tree-edit-distance-based similarity (TEDS) metric, comparing against end-to-end SLM fine-tuning and self-debug methods; analyzes data efficiency (as few as 1,000 training examples) and robustness to input format variability.
AI.result: Lift achieves parity or superiority to end-to-end fine-tuning on TEDS across the benchmark, requiring only ~1,000 training examples and improving by up to 0.144 TEDS points over end-to-end fine-tuning; demonstrates greater robustness to input variation and favorable performance when training data is limited.
AI.conclusion: Last-mile fine-tuning offers a practical, data-efficient alternative to end-to-end fine-tuning for table repair tasks, delivering comparable or better accuracy with less training data and improved robustness to input variability.

title: Strategic PAC Learnability via Geometric Definability
summary: Strategic classification studies learning settings in which individuals can modify their features, at a cost, in order to influence the classifier's decision. A central question is how the sample complexity of the induced (strategic) hypothesis class depends on the complexities of the underlying hypothesis class and the cost structure governing feasible manipulations. Prior work has shown that in several natural settings, such as linear classifiers with norm costs, the induced complexity can be controlled. We begin by showing that such guarantees fail in general - even in simple cases: there exist hypothesis classes of VC dimension $1$ on the real line such that, even under the simplest interval neighborhoods, the induced class has infinite VC dimension. Thus, strategic behavior can turn an easy learning problem into a non-learnable one. To overcome this, we introduce structure via a geometric definability assumption: both the hypothesis class and the cost-induced neighborhood relation can be defined by first-order formulas over $\mathbb{R}_{\mathtt{exp}}$. Intuitively, this means that hypotheses and costs can be described using arithmetic operations, exponentiation, logarithms, and comparisons. This captures a broad range of natural classes and cost functions, including $\ell_p$ distances, Wasserstein distance, and information-theoretic divergences. Under this assumption, we prove that learnability is preserved, with sample complexity controlled by the complexity of the defining formulas.

AI.tldr: Strategic manipulation can blow up a simple learning problem by making the induced hypothesis class non-learnable (unbounded VC dimension). However, imposing a broad geometric definability constraint (first-order definability over R_exp) preserves learnability and ties sample complexity to the complexity of the defining formulas.
AI.motivation: To understand when strategic behavior in classification preserves or destroys learnability, and to identify structural conditions that curb increases in sample complexity caused by strategic manipulation.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze the induced strategic hypothesis class: (i) construct counterexamples showing infinite VC dimension in simple settings, (ii) introduce a geometric definability framework where hypotheses and cost-induced neighborhoods are definable by first-order formulas over R_exp, (iii) derive learnability results with sample complexity bounded by formula complexity; show broad applicability to l_p distances, Wasserstein distance, and information divergences.
AI.result: Negative result: even VC dimension 1 on R can yield an induced class with infinite VC dimension under simple interval neighborhoods; Positive result: under first-order definability over R_exp, learnability is preserved and sample complexity is governed by the defining formulas' complexity.
AI.conclusion: A natural and broad defintion criterion—first-order definability over R_exp—controls the impact of strategic manipulation on learnability, offering a unified framework to bound sample complexity in strategic classification across many cost structures.

title: Rescaled Asynchronous SGD: Optimal Distributed Optimization under Data and System Heterogeneity
summary: Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (ASGD) is a standard way to exploit heterogeneous compute resources in distributed learning: instead of forcing fast workers to wait for slow ones, the server updates the model whenever a gradient arrives. Vanilla ASGD applies each arriving gradient with the same weight. When local data distributions are heterogeneous, this becomes problematic: faster workers contribute more updates, and we show theoretically that the method is biased toward a frequency-weighted average of the local objectives rather than the desired global objective. Existing remedies typically move away from the simple ASGD template by introducing gathering phases, buffering, or extra memory. We show that this is unnecessary. Keeping the standard ASGD mechanism, we recover the correct objective by rescaling worker-specific stepsizes in proportion to their computation times, so that each worker contributes the same aggregate learning rate over a cycle. In the non-convex setting, under smoothness and bounded heterogeneity assumptions, we prove that the resulting method, Rescaled ASGD, converges to stationary points of the correct global objective in the fixed-computation model. Its time complexity matches the known lower bound in the leading term, while the effects of staleness and data heterogeneity appear only in lower-order terms. Experiments confirm that the method converges to the correct objective and is competitive with state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Rescaled ASGD fixes bias from heterogeneity by adjusting stepsizes according to worker compute times, enabling convergence to the correct global objective without buffering or extra memory.
AI.motivation: Standard ASGD with heterogeneous data and compute speeds biases updates toward a frequency-weighted local objective. Existing fixes add buffering or memory; a simple, memory-free remedy is desired that preserves the ASGD template.
AI.method: Introduce per-worker step-size rescaling proportional to computation time so that each worker contributes the same aggregate learning rate per cycle. Analysis in the non-convex setting under smoothness and bounded heterogeneity shows convergence to stationary points of the global objective. Time complexity matches the leading lower bound; staleness and heterogeneity affect only lower-order terms. Experiments validate convergence to the correct objective and competitive performance.
AI.result: Under the proposed rescaling, ASGD converges to stationary points of the global objective in the fixed-computation model; the leading-term time complexity meets the known lower bound; bias from heterogeneity is eliminated.
AI.conclusion: Rescaled ASGD provides a simple, memory-free correction to ASGD that yields the correct global objective in non-convex optimization, with convergence guarantees and competitive empirical performance.

title: Q-Flow: Stable and Expressive Reinforcement Learning with Flow-Based Policy
summary: There is growing interest in utilizing flow-based models as decision-making policies in reinforcement learning due to their high expressive capacity. However, effectively leveraging this expressivity for value maximization remains challenging, as naive gradient-based optimization requires backpropagating through numerical solvers and often leads to instability. Existing approaches typically address this issue by restricting the expressive capacity of flow-based policies, resulting in a trade-off between optimization stability and representational flexibility. To resolve this, we introduce Q-Flow, a framework that leverages the deterministic nature of flow dynamics to explicitly propagate terminal trajectory value to intermediate latent states along the policy-induced flow. This formulation enables stable policy optimization using intermediate value gradients without unrolling the numerical solver, effectively bridging the gap between stability and expressivity. We evaluate Q-Flow in the offline learning setting on the challenging OGBench suite, where it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by an average of 10.6 percentage points, while also enabling stable online adaptation within the same framework.

AI.tldr: Q-Flow proposes a gradient-stable training method for flow-based policies in reinforcement learning by propagating terminal trajectory values through the flow, avoiding backpropagation through solvers; it achieves improved offline performance and supports online adaptation.
AI.motivation: Flow-based policies are highly expressive for RL but hard to optimize for value maximization because backpropagating through numerical solvers is unstable and inefficient; existing work trades expressivity for stability by constraining policy capacity.
AI.method: Exploit the deterministic flow dynamics to explicitly propagate terminal trajectory value to intermediate latent states along the policy-induced flow. This enables stable policy optimization using intermediate value gradients without unrolling the solver, thereby decoupling optimization stability from solver complexity.
AI.result: In offline learning on the OGBench suite, Q-Flow consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by an average of 10.6 percentage points and also shows stable online adaptation within the same framework.
AI.conclusion: Q-Flow advances the use of flow-based policies in RL by preserving expressivity while ensuring stable, solver-free gradient-based optimization, particularly appealing for offline RL with potential online deployment.

title: Robust and Explainable Bicuspid Aortic Valve Diagnosis Using Stacked Ensembles on Echocardiography
summary: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is the first-line imaging modality for diagnosing bicuspid aortic valve (BAV), yet diagnostic performance varies with operator expertise and image quality. We developed an explainable AI model that distinguishes BAV from tricuspid aortic valves (TAV) using routinely acquired parasternal long-axis (PLAX) cine loops. A multi-backbone video ensemble was trained and evaluated using a leakage-aware, stratified outer cross-validation protocol on $N{=}90$ patient studies (48 BAV, 42 TAV). Across fixed outer splits and 10 random seeds, the calibrated stacked ensemble achieved an outer-CV F1-score of $0.907$ and recall of $0.877$. Frame-level Grad-CAM localized salient evidence to the aortic root and leaflet plane, while globally aggregated SHAP values quantified each video backbone's contribution to the stacked prediction, enabling transparent, case-level auditability. These findings indicate that PLAX-based video ensembles can support reliable BAV/TAV classification from routine echocardiographic cine loops and may facilitate earlier detection in non-specialist or resource-limited clinical settings.

AI.tldr: Explainable, multi-backbone video ensemble using PLAX cine-loops can distinguish bicuspid vs tricuspid aortic valves with high F1 (0.907) in leakage-aware cross-validation, with Grad-CAM and SHAP aiding interpretability; suitable for non-specialist, resource-limited settings but requires broader validation.
AI.motivation: Accurate BAV detection is critical for risk stratification and surveillance; TTE performance varies with operator skill and image quality. Using routinely acquired PLAX cine-loops with explainable AI could broaden access to reliable BAV screening.
AI.method: Developed a multi-backbone video ensemble trained on PLAX cine loops. Used leakage-aware, stratified outer cross-validation on N=90 studies (48 BAV, 42 TAV). Per-video backbones contributed via a calibrated stacked ensemble. Matched Grad-CAM for frame-level localization and SHAP for global backbone attribution, enabling case-level auditability.
AI.result: Outer-cross-validated F1=0.907 and recall=0.877. Grad-CAM localized salient evidence to the aortic root/leaflet plane. SHAP quantified each backbone's contribution to stacked prediction, enabling transparent audit of decisions.
AI.conclusion: PLAX-based video ensembles can reliably classify BAV vs TAV from routine cine loops and may enable earlier detection in non-specialist or resource-limited settings. The explainability via Grad-CAM and SHAP supports auditability, but external validation and prospective studies are needed to assess generalization and clinical utility.

title: Efficient Sensor Fusion for Gesture Recognition on Resource-Constrained Devices
comment: The article is already accepted for IEEE Sensors Applications Symposium (IEEE SAS) 2026
summary: Gesture recognition is a cornerstone of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) for smart eyewear, enabling natural and device-free control in augmented reality environments. Traditional vision-based approaches face significant challenges regarding power consumption, computational latency, and user privacy. This paper proposes a lightweight, privacy-preserving gesture recognition system based on the fusion of low-resolution Time-of-Flight (ToF) and Infrared (IR) thermal sensors. We used an 8 times 8 multizone ToF sensor (VL53L8CH) and an 8 times 8 IR array (AMG8833) to capture complementary depth and thermal cues. A compact Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a specialized grouped-convolution architecture is designed to fuse these modalities efficiently on a microcontroller (MCU). Experimental results on a custom dataset of 7 static gestures, validated via k-fold cross-validation, demonstrate that the proposed fusion strategy significantly outperforms single-sensor baselines with an accuracy of 92.3% and a macro F1-score of 0.93. Finally, on-device benchmarks on STM32F4 and STM32H7 MCUs confirm the system's suitability for resource-constrained wearables, requiring only 6,343 parameters and achieving millisecond-level inference latency with a total system power of 50 mW.

AI.tldr: A compact, privacy-preserving multimodal gesture recognition system for smart eyewear using fused low-resolution ToF depth and IR thermal data on MCUs, achieving ~92% accuracy with macro F1 of 0.93, 6.3k parameters, 50 mW, outperforming single-sensor baselines.
AI.motivation: Address power, latency, and privacy limitations of vision-based HCI for wearables by enabling on-device fusion of depth and thermal cues with a lightweight model.
AI.method: Multimodal sensing with an 8×8 ToF VL53L8CH and an 8×8 IR AMG8833 array; a compact CNN with a specialized grouped-convolution architecture fuses modalities on an MCU; 7 static gestures; evaluated with k-fold cross-validation; on-device benchmarks on STM32F4 and STM32H7 with 6,343 parameters.
AI.result: Achieved 92.3% accuracy and macro F1 of 0.93; fusion outperforms single-sensor baselines; on-device latency is millisecond-level; total system power ~50 mW.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of efficient, privacy-preserving multimodal gesture recognition for resource-constrained wearables, suitable for AR HCI and similar applications; the approach yields strong performance with minimal computational and energy footprint.

title: A Unified Three-Stage Machine Learning Framework for Diabetes Detection, Subtype Discrimination, and Cognitive-Metabolic Hypothesis Testing
summary: Diabetes mellitus affects over 537 million adults worldwide and remains a major challenge in preventive healthcare. Existing machine-learning studies primarily formulate diabetes prediction as a binary classification problem, while subtype-oriented analysis and glycaemic-cognitive associations remain comparatively underexplored. We present a reproducible three-stage machine learning framework for diabetes detection, subtype-oriented clustering, and metabolic-cognitive association analysis. In Stage 1, five supervised classifiers together with a stacking ensemble are benchmarked on the NCSU Diabetes Dataset using stratified five-fold cross-validation and evaluation metrics including ROC-AUC, balanced accuracy, recall, and F1-score. SVM-RBF and Logistic Regression achieve the highest ROC-AUC ($0.825 \pm 0.026$), while Random Forest achieves the highest accuracy ($0.762 \pm 0.030$). SHAP explainability identifies Glucose, BMI, and Age as the dominant predictive biomarkers. In Stage 2, silhouette-validated K-Means clustering ($k=2$, silhouette $\approx 0.116$) is applied to confirmed diabetic cases using Glucose, Insulin, and Age, recovering clinically plausible subtype-oriented partitions without requiring ground-truth subtype labels. In Stage 3, statistical analysis of the Ohio Longitudinal Cognitive Dataset ($n=373$) reveals a significant positive association between glycaemic control and cognitive function ($ρ_s = 0.208$, $p = 5.29 \times 10^{-5}$), which survives Holm correction. The findings support the utility of statistically grounded and interpretable ML pipelines for reproducible diabetes analytics and subtype-aware exploratory analysis.

AI.tldr: A reproducible 3-stage ML framework for diabetes detection, subtype discovery, and metabolic–cognitive associations, validated on the NCSU Diabetes Dataset and the Ohio Longitudinal Cognitive Dataset.
AI.motivation: To move beyond binary diabetes prediction to subtype-aware analysis and to link glycemic control with cognitive function, addressing reproducibility via a staged ML pipeline.
AI.method: Stage 1: benchmark five supervised classifiers and a stacking ensemble using stratified five-fold CV; SHAP for feature importance. Stage 2: silhouette-validated K-Means clustering (k=2) on diabetic cases using Glucose, Insulin, Age. Stage 3: statistical analysis (Spearman correlation) on Ohio Longitudinal Cognitive Dataset (n=373) to assess glycemic–cognitive associations, with Holm correction.
AI.result: Stage 1: SVM-RBF and Logistic Regression yield highest ROC-AUC 0.825±0.026; Random Forest highest accuracy 0.762±0.030; SHAP highlights Glucose, BMI, Age. Stage 2: clustering into two plausible subtypes (silhouette ≈ 0.116). Stage 3: significant positive glycemic–cognitive association (ρs = 0.208, p = 5.29×10^-5) surviving Holm correction.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a statistically grounded, interpretable ML pipeline enables reproducible diabetes analytics and feasible subtype-aware exploratory analyses.

title: Discovery of Hidden Miscalibration Regimes
summary: Calibration is commonly evaluated by comparing model confidence with its empirical correctness, implicitly treating reliability as a function of the confidence score alone. However, this view can hide substantial structure: models may be systematically overconfident on some kinds of inputs and underconfident on others, causing global reliability diagnostics to obscure localised calibration failures. To address this, we formulate the problem of discovering hidden miscalibration regimes without assuming access to predefined data slices. We define the corresponding miscalibration field and propose a diagnostic framework for estimating it. Our approach learns a calibration-aware representation of the input space and estimates signed local miscalibration by kernel smoothing in the learned geometry. Across four real-world LLM benchmarks and twelve LLMs, we find that input-dependent calibration heterogeneity is prevalent. We further show that the discovered fields are actionable: they support local confidence correction and reduce calibration error in systematically miscalibrated regions where confidence-based methods such as isotonic regression and temperature scaling are less effective.

AI.tldr: Proposes a miscalibration field to reveal input-dependent calibration errors in large language models, learning a geometry-aware input representation and kernel-smoothing local miscalibration; demonstrates pervasive heterogeneity and enables local confidence correction beyond traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Global calibration metrics (confidence vs. accuracy) mask structured, input-specific miscalibration regimes. A data-slice-free approach is needed to discover and quantify these local miscalibrations to improve reliability and enable targeted corrections.
AI.method: Define a miscalibration field as the signed local deviation between model confidence and empirical correctness. Learn a calibration-aware representation of inputs and estimate local miscalibration via kernel smoothing in the learned geometry. Evaluate across four real-world LLM benchmarks and twelve LLMs, comparing with baseline calibration techniques.
AI.result: Input-dependent calibration heterogeneity is prevalent. The learned miscalibration fields identify regions of systematic miscalibration and enable local confidence correction, reducing calibration error in areas where confidence-based methods like isotonic regression and temperature scaling underperform.
AI.conclusion: The framework reveals structured, local calibration failures in LLMs and provides actionable diagnostics for targeted calibration; miscalibration fields support local interventions and improve reliability beyond global calibration summaries.

title: Min Generalized Sliced Gromov Wasserstein: A Scalable Path to Gromov Wasserstein
summary: We propose min Generalized Sliced Gromov--Wasserstein (min-GSGW), a sliced formulation for the Gromov--Wasserstein (GW) problem using expressive generalized slicers. The key idea is to learn coupled nonlinear slicers that assign compatible push-forward values to both input measures, so that monotone coupling in the projected domain lifts to a transport plan evaluated against the GW objective in the original spaces. The resulting plan induces a GW objective value, and min-GSGW minimizes this cost directly in the original spaces. We further show that min-GSGW is rigid-motion invariant, a crucial property for geometric matching and shape analysis tasks. Our contributions are threefold: 1) we introduce generalized slicers into the sliced GW framework, 2) we construct a slicing-based efficient GW transport plan; and 3) we develop an amortized variant that replaces per-instance optimization with a learned slicer for unseen input pairs. We perform experiments on animal mesh matching, horse mesh interpolation, and ShapeNet part transfer. Results show that min-GSGW produces meaningful geometric correspondences and GW objective values at substantially lower computational cost than existing GW solvers.

AI.tldr: min-GSGW introduces generalized slicers into the sliced GW framework to learn coupled nonlinear projections, enabling a rigid-motion invariant, efficient GW transport plan with an amortized variant, validated on 3D shape tasks with lower computational cost than standard GW solvers.
AI.motivation: Gromov–Wasserstein (GW) is powerful for comparing structured distributions but is computationally intensive. Sliced GW reduces cost but relies on simple, often linear, projections. There is a need for expressive, learnable slicers that preserve geometry and invariances (rigid motion) and for amortization to handle unseen data.
AI.method: Define generalized slicers that produce compatible push-forward values for both input measures. Enforce monotone coupling in the projected domain which lifts to a GW transport plan in the original spaces; evaluate the GW objective with this plan and minimize it directly in the original spaces. Prove rigid-motion invariance. Propose an amortized variant that replaces per-instance optimization with a learned slicer. Apply to 3D geometry tasks and compare against existing GW solvers.
AI.result: min-GSGW yields meaningful geometric correspondences and GW objective values with substantially lower computational cost than existing GW solvers across tasks such as animal mesh matching, horse mesh interpolation, and ShapeNet part transfer.
AI.conclusion: Generalized slicers extend sliced GW to richer projections, enabling efficient, invariant GW transport with an amortized option. The approach improves scalability and quality of geometric matching, suggesting broad applicability in 3D shape analysis and related domains.

title: Twincher: Bijective Representation Learning for Robust Inversion of Continuous Systems
summary: Recent advances in AI have been primarily driven by large-scale neural architectures that excel at function approximation, rather than by tailored inductive biases and inference or learning strategies that could be important for resource-efficient real-world perception and planning through the solution of inverse problems. In this work, we consider the possibility of enabling robust inversion of continuous forward processes $p \mapsto y$ by learning representations of $y$ that are bijectively aligned with $p$ while remaining insensitive to perturbations in $y$ caused by noise or model mismatch. We propose Twincher, a class of architectures based on stacks of structured diffeomorphic transformations and tailored adversarial training strategies that enable learning such bijective representations. We provide a public API for training and inference and empirically demonstrate the ability of the proposed architecture to efficiently learn bijective representations of synthetic systems, thereby enabling robust and efficient iterative inverse inference. Compared to a baseline inverse-modeling approach, the method exhibits improved data efficiency and robustness, providing initial evidence for the potential of bijective representation learning in robotics, vision, and physical AI.

AI.tldr: Twincher learns bijective representations of forward processes p -> y using stacked structured diffeomorphic transforms and adversarial training to enable robust, data-efficient inverse inference; demonstrated on synthetic systems with improved robustness vs baseline.
AI.motivation: Address data/compute inefficiency and brittleness of large neural models in solving inverse problems; enable resource-efficient, robust perception and planning via bijective y representations aligned with p, insensitive to noise and model mismatch.
AI.method: Twincher architecture: stacks of structured diffeomorphic transformations with adversarial training to enforce bijective y representations aligned with input p; provides a public API for training and inference; evaluated on synthetic systems showing data efficiency and robustness improvements over a baseline inverse-modeling approach.
AI.result: Empirical demonstration that the method can efficiently learn bijective representations of synthetic systems; improved data efficiency and robustness compared to baseline inverse-modeling; initial evidence for potential applications in robotics, vision, and physical AI.
AI.conclusion: Bijective representation learning with diffeomorphic transforms and adversarial training offers a promising, resource-efficient route for robust inverse inference in real-world perception and planning, warranting further exploration in diverse domains.

title: OSDN: Improving Delta Rule with Provable Online Preconditioning in Linear Attention
summary: Linear attention and state-space models offer constant-memory alternatives to softmax attention, but often struggle with in-context associative recall. The Delta Rule mitigates this by writing each token via one step of online gradient descent. However, its step size relies on a single scalar gate that ignores the feature-wise curvature of the inner objective. We propose Online Scaled DeltaNet (OSDN), which augments the scalar gate with a diagonal preconditioner updated online via hypergradient feedback. Crucially, this right-preconditioning is algebraically equivalent to a per-feature scaling of the write-side key. This equivalence allows OSDN to strictly preserve the hardware-friendly chunkwise parallel pipeline of DeltaNet without incurring high-dimensional state overhead. Theoretically, by exploiting the exact-quadratic structure of the inner regression loss, we establish super-geometric convergence against a right-Newton comparator and prove an algorithm-aligned token-local residual contraction bound. To handle non-stationary contexts, we further introduce Adaptive Preconditioner Forgetting (APF) to dynamically refresh stale calibration. Empirically, OSDN demonstrates strong performance across scales. At the 340M-parameter scale, OSDN improves JRT-style in-context recall by 32% over DeltaNet. Scaling to 1.3B parameters, it achieves a 39% reduction in the recall residual ratio while maintaining parity on general downstream tasks (e.g., perplexity and LongBench) -- demonstrating that our online-preconditioning mechanism effectively transfers and amplifies at the billion-parameter scale.

AI.tldr: OSDN introduces an online diagonal preconditioner for DeltaNet, achieving feature-wise, online scale-updates that preserve hardware-friendly pipelines while improving in-context recall; supported by exact-quadratic convergence analysis and APF, with strong empirical gains at 340M and 1.3B scales.
AI.motivation: Address the poor in-context associative recall of linear attention/state-space models and the limitation of a single scalar step-size in DeltaRule; require feature-wise curvature adaptation without high-state overhead.
AI.method: Add a diagonal preconditioner to DeltaNet's update rule, updated online via hypergradient feedback (right-preconditioning). Prove algebraic equivalence to per-feature write-key scaling; maintain chunkwise parallelism. Provide Adaptive Preconditioner Forgetting for non-stationarity and derive convergence bounds under exact-quadratic losses.
AI.result: Theoretical: super-geometric convergence against a right-Newton comparator; token-local residual contraction bound. Empirical: 340M params — +32% JRT-style in-context recall vs DeltaNet; 1.3B params — 39% reduction in recall residual ratio; no loss on perplexity or LongBench.
AI.conclusion: Online preconditioning with APF yields strong, scalable improvements in in-context recall while preserving efficiency, effectively transferring benefits to billion-parameter models and aligning with hardware-friendly architectures.

title: HLS-Seek: QoR-Aware Code Generation for High-Level Synthesis via Proxy Comparative Reward Reinforcement Learning
summary: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) compiles algorithmic C/C++ descriptions into hardware, with Quality of Results (QoR) -- latency and resource utilization -- critically governed by pragma configurations and code structure. Existing LLM-based HLS approaches train for functional correctness but ignore QoR entirely. We observe that reinforcement learning (RL) for HLS does not require absolute synthesis results -- only relative comparisons between candidates. Based on this insight, we propose \textbf{HLS-Seek}, a QoR-aware NL-to-HLS framework that replaces expensive synthesis-in-the-loop RL with a comparative proxy reward model achieving 99.53\% Pareto-dominance accuracy. To prevent reward hacking, we introduce \textit{uncertainty-aware Monte Carlo (MC) dropout switching} that selectively invokes real Vitis HLS synthesis for low-confidence candidates and online updates the proxy, creating a self-improving reward system. HLS-Seek achieves 81.5\% syntax correctness pass@1 and 81.4\% Func@5 on HLS-eval with only 7B parameters, surpassing GPT-5.1 and other frontier models while achieving 8.5$\times$ faster training than real-reward RL. On QoR evaluation, HLS-Seek achieves the lowest latency on 16/30 kernels and Pareto-dominates HLS-specific baselines on 9 kernels.

AI.tldr: HLS-Seek is a QoR-aware NL-to-HLS framework that uses a comparative proxy reward and uncertainty-aware Monte Carlo dropout switching to selectively invoke real synthesis, achieving high QoR with 7B-parameter models and faster training than real-reward RL, while dominating QoR baselines on several kernels.
AI.motivation: Quality of Results (QoR) in High-Level Synthesis is heavily influenced by code structure and pragma settings, but prior LLM-based HLS work focused on functional correctness and ignored QoR, while RL-based approaches incur expensive synthesis.
AI.method: Replace expensive synthesis-in-the-loop RL with a comparative proxy reward model for QoR, and use uncertainty-aware Monte Carlo dropout switching to trigger real Vitis HLS synthesis for low-confidence candidates, updating the proxy online to create a self-improving reward system.
AI.result: Proxy achieves 99.53% Pareto-dominance accuracy. HLS-Seek with 7B parameters attains 81.5% syntax correctness pass@1 and 81.4% Func@5 on HLS-eval, outperforming GPT-5.1 and frontier models, with 8.5x faster training than real-reward RL. On QoR, it records the lowest latency on 16 of 30 kernels and Pareto-dominates HLS baselines on 9 kernels.
AI.conclusion: QoR-aware NL-to-HLS with a comparative proxy reward and uncertainty-driven real-synthesis checks enables efficient search that achieves strong QoR while reducing training cost and maintaining competitive model size.

title: Effective Context in Transformers: An Analysis of Fragmentation and Tokenization
summary:
Transformers predict over a representation of a sequence. The same data can be written as bytes, characters, or subword tokens, and these representations may be lossless. Yet, under a fixed context window, they need not expose the same information to the model. This raises a basic question: how does the choice of representation change what a finite-context predictor can achieve?
  We study this question on Markov sources and uncover two complementary phenomena. First, we observe that moving to smaller representation units can hurt prediction even when the context window is enlarged to cover the relevant source history. To explain this, we introduce fragmentation: a lossless recoding that replaces each source symbol by several smaller units. We prove that fragmentation can strictly increase the optimal finite-context log-loss, showing that the gap is not merely an optimization or capacity issue, but can be intrinsic to the representation. This gives a theoretical account of the finite-context gap observed in byte- and character-level models such as ByT5 and CANINE relative to subword-tokenized models. Second, we study the opposite direction: greedy tokenization -- BPE, WordPiece, and related methods -- which groups source symbols into larger units. We show that tokenization can make a short token window behave like a longer source-context window, and we give a loss guarantee describing when this is achievable. The guarantee depends on how reliably token windows span the needed source history, together with the compression rate of the tokenizer. This also yields a simple diagnostic for real tokenizers: measuring how much source context a fixed token window reliably contains. Together, the two directions establish a finite-context information-theoretic framework for reasoning about representation choices in Transformers.

AI.tldr: This work analyzes how representation units (bytes, characters, subwords) affect finite-context prediction in Transformers on Markov sources; reveals fragmentation can worsen predictive loss while tokenization can emulate longer context; offers an information-theoretic framework and a practical diagnostic for tokenizers.
AI.motivation: To understand how different input representations influence a model's predictive power under a fixed context window, and to provide a formal explanation for observed gaps between byte/character models and subword-tokenized models.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis on Markov sources. Introduces fragmentation as a lossless recoding replacing source symbols with multiple smaller units and proves it can strictly increase the optimal finite-context log-loss. Studies greedy tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) to show how larger units can make a short token window behave like a longer source-context window, deriving loss guarantees that depend on token window span reliability and tokenizer compression rate. Proposes a diagnostic for real tokenizers by measuring how much source context a fixed token window reliably contains.
AI.result: Two complementary phenomena identified: (1) fragmentation can increase the optimal finite-context log-loss, indicating the representation choice can impose intrinsic limits beyond optimization; (2) tokenization can effectively extend the usable context window, with a formal loss guarantee. The work provides a finite-context information-theoretic framework for reasoning about representations in Transformers and a practical diagnostic for tokenizers.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes an information-theoretic basis for selecting representations in Transformer architectures, explaining finite-context gaps and offering criteria and diagnostics to assess tokenizer designs and their impact on context utilization.

title: Temper and Tilt Lead to SLOP: Reward Hacking Mitigation with Inference-Time Alignment
summary: Inference-time alignment techniques offer a lightweight alternative or complement to costly reinforcement learning, while enabling continual adaptation as alignment objectives and reward targets evolve. Existing theoretical analyses justify these methods as approximations to sampling from distributions optimally tilted toward a given reward model. We extend these techniques by introducing reference-model temperature adjustment, which leads to further generalization of inference-time alignment to ensembles of generative reward models combined as a sharpened logarithmic opinion pool (SLOP). To mitigate reward hacking, we propose an algorithm for calibrating SLOP weight parameters and experimentally demonstrate that it improves robustness while preserving alignment performance.

AI.tldr: Inference-time alignment enhanced by reference-model temperature and SLOP ensembles of reward models; calibrating SLOP weights improves robustness without sacrificing alignment.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on costly reinforcement learning, enable continual adaptation as reward objectives evolve, and mitigate reward hacking while preserving alignment performance.
AI.method: Introduce temperature adjustment for a reference model; form ensembles of generative reward models combined via a sharpened logarithmic opinion pool (SLOP); provide an algorithm to calibrate the SLOP weights.
AI.result: The approach generalizes inference-time alignment and, empirically, calibration of SLOP weights improves robustness while preserving alignment performance.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time alignment with temperature-adjusted reference models and SLOP ensembles offers robust, adaptable alignment, with a practical calibration step to guard against reward hacking.

title: Path-independent Flow Matching for Multi-parameter Generative Dynamics
comment: 12 pages including references for main part of the document, 26 pages in total when including the appendix. 15 figures in total
summary: Flow Matching is a powerful framework for learning transport maps between probability distributions. Yet its standard single-parameter formulation is not designed to capture multi-parameter variations where the resulting transport should be path-independent. Path independence is crucial because it ensures that transformations depend only on the initial and target distributions, not on the specific path. In this work, we introduce Path-independent Flow Matching (PiFM), a method for learning vector fields whose induced flows yield path-independent transport between distributions. We show that PiFM generalizes Flow Matching to higher-dimensional parameter domains while enforcing structural conditions that ensure consistency of composed transformations. In addition, we show that, under suitable assumptions, PiFM approximates the Wasserstein barycenter, linking the framework to a notion of distributional interpolation. To enable practical training, we propose a tractable, simulation-free objective that regresses onto multi-parameter conditional probability paths. We showcase empirically that PiFM outperforms other approaches on both synthetic and real world data in interpolating path-independent trajectories and generating desired out of distribution samples.

AI.tldr: Path-independent Flow Matching (PiFM) extends Flow Matching to multi-parameter settings to learn path-independent transport maps, enforcing compositional consistency and linking to Wasserstein barycenters; it uses a tractable simulation-free objective and demonstrates empirical gains in interpolation and OOD sample generation.
AI.motivation: Need for multi-parameter, path-independent transport mappings where the outcome depends only on endpoints; align transformations with distributional interpolation concepts like barycenters; enable scalable training.
AI.method: Introduce vector fields over multi-parameter domains, enforce structural conditions for path independence; generalize Flow Matching to higher-dimensional parameter spaces; derive conditions ensuring consistent composition; propose a simulation-free objective regressing onto multi-parameter conditional probability paths.
AI.result: PiFM generalizes Flow Matching, approximates Wasserstein barycenters under assumptions, and empirically outperforms baselines on synthetic and real data for interpolating path-independent trajectories and generating desired OOD samples.
AI.conclusion: PiFM provides a principled, scalable framework for multi-parameter, path-independent transport maps with a connection to barycenters and practical training methodology; suitable for distributional interpolation and controlled OOD sampling.

title: Decoupled and Divergence-Conditioned Prompt for Multi-domain Dynamic Graph Foundation Models
summary: Dynamic graphs are ubiquitous in real-world systems, and building generalizable dynamic Graph Foundation Models has become a frontier in graph learning. However, dynamic graphs from different domains pose fundamental challenges to unified modeling, as their semantic and temporal patterns are inherently inconsistent, making the multi-domain pre-training difficult. Consequently, the widely used "pretrain-then-finetune" paradigm often suffers from severe negative knowledge transfer. To the best of our knowledge, there exists no multi-domain dynamic GFM. In this work, we propose DyGFM, a Dynamic Graph Foundation Model over multiple domains based on decoupled and divergence-conditioned prompting. To disentangle transferable semantics from the domain-specific dynamics, we introduce a dual-branch pre-training strategy with semantic-temporal decoupling. To alleviate negative transfer during domain adaptation, we further develop a cross-domain routing mechanism with divergence-aware expert selection. To enable efficient downstream fine-tuning, we design a divergence-conditioned prompt generator that injects lightweight, learnable graph prompts tailored to semantic and temporal traits. Extensive experiments on continuous dynamic graph benchmarks demonstrate that DyGFM consistently outperforms 12 state-of-the-art baselines on both node classification and link prediction tasks, achieving superior effectiveness and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes DyGFM, a multi-domain dynamic Graph Foundation Model with semantic-temporal decoupling, divergence-aware cross-domain routing, and divergence-conditioned prompts; achieves superior results over 12 baselines on node classification and link prediction.
AI.motivation: Unified modeling of multi-domain dynamic graphs is challenging due to inconsistent semantic and temporal patterns across domains, causing negative transfer in pretrain-finetune pipelines.
AI.method: Dual-branch pre-training for semantic-temporal decoupling; cross-domain routing with divergence-aware expert selection; divergence-conditioned prompt generator for lightweight graph prompts; evaluation on continuous dynamic graph benchmarks for node classification and link prediction.
AI.result: DyGFM consistently outperforms 12 state-of-the-art baselines on both node classification and link prediction tasks, showing improved effectiveness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: DyGFM demonstrates effective generalization across domains by decoupling semantics and dynamics, enabling efficient downstream fine-tuning and reduced negative transfer in multi-domain dynamic graph modeling.

title: Self-Supervised On-Policy Reinforcement Learning via Contrastive Proximal Policy Optimisation
summary: Contrastive reinforcement learning (CRL) learns goal-conditioned Q-values through a contrastive objective over state-action and goal representations, removing the need for hand-crafted reward functions. Despite impressive success in achieving viable self-supervised learning in RL, all existing CRL algorithms rely on off-policy optimisation and are mostly constrained to continuous action spaces, with little research invested in discrete environments. This leaves CRL disconnected from widely used and effective, modern on-policy training pipelines adopted across both single-agent and multi-agent RL in continuous and discrete environments. To establish a first connection, we introduce Contrastive Proximal Policy Optimisation (CPPO). CPPO is an on-policy contrastive RL algorithm that derives policy advantages directly from contrastive Q-values and optimises them via the standard PPO objective, without requiring a reward function or a replay buffer. We evaluate CPPO across continuous and discrete, single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks. Whilst the existence of an on-policy approach is inherently useful, we observe that \textbf{CPPO not only significantly outperforms the previous CRL baselines in 14 out of 18 tasks, but also matches or exceeds PPO's performance, which uses hand-crafted dense rewards, in 12 out of the 18 tasks tested.}

AI.tldr: CPPO is an on-policy contrastive RL method that uses contrastive Q-values to derive policy advantages and optimizes with PPO; it requires no reward function and no replay buffer, and achieves strong performance across continuous/discrete and single/multi-agent tasks, outperforming prior CRL baselines in most tasks and matching/exceeding PPO in several tasks.
AI.motivation: Bridge CRL with on-policy, widely-used RL pipelines; extend CRL from off-policy, continuous-action settings to on-policy, discrete and mixed environments; reduce dependence on handcrafted reward signals by using a contrastive objective.
AI.method: Propose Contrastive Proximal Policy Optimisation (CPPO), an on-policy algorithm that computes policy advantages from contrastive Q-values and optimizes via the standard PPO objective, without requiring a reward function or a replay buffer.
AI.result: CPPO outperforms previous CRL baselines in 14 of 18 tasks and matches or exceeds PPO with dense rewards in 12 of 18 tasks, across continuous/discrete and single/multi-agent settings.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and competitiveness of on-policy CRL, suggesting a path to unify contrastive RL with mainstream on-policy methods in both single- and multi-agent settings and across action spaces.

title: Dynamical Predictive Modelling of Cardiovascular Disease Progression Post-Myocardial Infarction via ECG-Trained Artificial Intelligence Model
comment: submitted to the 9th International Conference on Computational and Mathematical Biomedical Engineering, 4 pages, 1 figure, 1 table
summary: Myocardial infarction (MI) is a leading cause of death, and its adverse outcomes are urgent to predict. Yet ECG-based prognostic models underperform because deep learning requires large, labelled datasets, which are scarce in medicine. Foundation models can learn from unlabelled ECGs via selfsupervision, but medically relevant training strategies remain underexplored. We propose a pretrained artificial intelligence model that combines patient-specific temporal information using contrastive learning with supervised multitask heads, then fine-tunes on post-MI outcome prediction. The proposed model outperformed a model trained from scratch (0.794 vs 0.608 AUC) showing that clinically structured ECG modelling improves classification in limited data regimes.

AI.tldr: Pretrained ECG model using self-supervised contrastive learning with patient-specific temporal information and multitask heads improves post-MI prognostic accuracy, outperforming a model trained from scratch.
AI.motivation: ECG-based prognostic models underperform due to limited labelled data; foundation models can leverage unlabelled ECGs but medically relevant pretraining strategies are needed.
AI.method: Pretrain with contrastive learning incorporating patient-specific temporal information and supervised multitask heads on unlabelled ECGs, then fine-tune on post-MI outcome prediction.
AI.result: Model achieves higher AUC (0.794) than a scratch-trained model (0.608) on post-MI outcome prediction.
AI.conclusion: Clinically structured ECG modelling with self-supervised pretraining and multitask heads improves prognostic performance in limited data regimes; supports use of foundation-model-like approaches for ECG-based risk stratification.

title: Mixed neural posterior estimation for simulators with discrete and continuous parameters
summary: Neural Posterior Estimation (NPE) enables rapid parameter inference for complex simulators with intractable likelihoods. NPE trains an inference network to estimate a probability density over parameters given data, typically assumed to be \emph{continuous}. However, many scientific models involve parameter spaces that are \emph{mixed}, that is, they contain both discrete and continuous dimensions. We address this limitation by extending NPE to mixed parameter spaces through an inference network that jointly handles discrete and continuous parameters. The inference network factorizes the joint posterior into discrete and continuous components, combining an autoregressive classifier for the discrete parameters with a generative model for the continuous parameters, trained jointly under a single simulation-based objective. In addition, we propose a diagnostic tool to assess the calibration of the mixed posterior approximation. Across tractable toy examples and real-world scientific simulators, our joint inference approach yields accurate and calibrated posteriors. The inference framework is available in the \texttt{sbi} Python package.

AI.tldr: Extends Neural Posterior Estimation (NPE) to mixed discrete-continuous parameter spaces by factorizing the posterior into a discrete component and a continuous component; uses an autoregressive classifier for discrete parameters and a conditional generative model for continuous parameters, trained with a single simulation-based objective; includes a calibration diagnostic; implemented in the sbi package.
AI.motivation: NPE traditionally assumes continuous parameter spaces, but many scientific models include both discrete and continuous dimensions. A unified, calibrated inference framework for mixed spaces is needed to enable rapid, likelihood-free inference for complex simulators.
AI.method: Factorize the joint posterior p(θ_d, θ_c | x) into p(θ_d | x) and p(θ_c | θ_d, x). Train an autoregressive classifier for the discrete parameters θ_d and a conditional generative model for the continuous parameters θ_c jointly under a single simulation-based objective. Introduce a diagnostic tool to assess calibration of the mixed posterior.
AI.result: The approach yields accurate and calibrated posteriors across tractable toy problems and real-world scientific simulators. The method is practical and integrates with the sbi package.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and utility of mixed-parameter NPE, broadening the applicability of likelihood-free inference to mixed spaces and providing an accessible tool for scientists.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Prediction of Lung Tumor Growth from Sparse Longitudinal CT Data via Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: This work studies lung tumor growth prediction from sparse and irregular longitudinal computed tomography (CT) observations with measurement variability. A Bayesian physics-informed neural network is developed by combining Gompertz growth dynamics with low-dimensional Bayesian inference in the log-volume domain. The framework employs a two-stage inference strategy combining maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) sampling to estimate posterior predictive distributions and uncertainty intervals. The method was evaluated on longitudinal data from the National Lung Screening Trial (30 patients). Results show that the model captures heterogeneous tumor growth patterns while maintaining reasonable prediction accuracy under limited observations. Compared with deterministic modeling approaches, the proposed approach additionally provides calibrated uncertainty estimates. The inferred posterior parameter correlations were consistent with expected biological growth behavior. The proposed framework achieved a cohort-level log-space RMSE of approximately 0.20 together with well-calibrated 95% credible interval coverage across 30 patients. These findings suggest that Bayesian physics-informed modeling may be useful for uncertainty-aware tumor growth assessment when only limited longitudinal follow-up scans are available.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian physics-informed neural network combines Gompertz tumor growth with low-dimensional Bayesian inference to predict lung tumor growth from sparse, irregular CT data, using MAP estimation and HMC to obtain uncertainty estimates; evaluated on 30 NLST patients with ~0.20 log-space RMSE and calibrated 95% CIs.
AI.motivation: To enable uncertainty-aware predictions of tumor growth from sparse, irregular longitudinal imaging data, addressing measurement variability and limitations of deterministic models.
AI.method: Two-stage Bayesian approach: physics-informed Gompertz growth model in log-volume domain, MAP estimation for a good starting point, followed by Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling to generate posterior predictive distributions and credible intervals; evaluation on longitudinal NLST data (30 patients).
AI.result: Cohort-level log-space RMSE ≈ 0.20 with well-calibrated 95% credible interval coverage across all patients; the model captures heterogeneous growth patterns and yields posterior parameter correlations consistent with known biology; uncertainty-aware predictions outperform purely deterministic models.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian physics-informed modeling is promising for uncertainty-aware tumor growth assessment when follow-up data are limited, enabling calibrated predictive uncertainty in longitudinal tumor growth.

title: Beyond Perplexity: A Geometric and Spectral Study of Low-Rank Pre-Training
summary: Pre-training large language models is dominated by the memory cost of storing full-rank weights, gradients, and optimizer states. Low-rank pre-training has emerged to address this, and the space of methods has grown rapidly. A central question remains open: do low-rank methods produce models that generalize comparably to full-rank training, or does the rank constraint fundamentally alter the solutions reached? Existing comparisons rely almost entirely on validation perplexity from single-seed runs, often carried forward from prior literature. Yet perplexity is a poor proxy for solution quality; two methods can match on perplexity while converging to different loss landscape regions and internal representations. We close this gap by characterizing the solutions found by five low-rank pre-training methods, GaLore and Fira (memory-efficient optimizers), CoLA and SLTrain (architecture reparameterizations), and ReLoRA (adapter-style updates with periodic resets), against full-rank training at three model scales (60M, 130M, 350M). We evaluate each along 16 metrics across four dimensions: 1-D loss landscape along random/top-K PCA directions, 1-D interpolation between checkpoints, spectral structure of the weights and learned updates, and activation similarity to full-rank training. We show that low-rank methods are not equivalent to full-rank training, nor to one another, even when validation perplexity is close. Full-rank training settles into a sharper basin than low-rank methods along random directions, while the reverse holds for the top-1 PCA direction. Each method converges to a geometrically distinct basin. Low-rank activations diverge from full-rank in later layers as training progresses, with GaLore tracking full-rank most closely. Further, validation perplexity does not translate to downstream performance at every scale. Adding geometric and spectral metrics improves the prediction.

AI.tldr: Low-rank pre-training methods are not equivalent to full-rank training or to each other, even when perplexity is similar. The study benchmarked five low-rank approaches across three model scales using 16 metrics along four axes, revealing distinct optimization basins, divergent activation trajectories in later layers, and limited predictive power of perplexity alone. Geometric and spectral diagnostics improve cross-method and scale comparisons.
AI.motivation: Assess whether memory-efficient, low-rank pre-training methods can match full-rank models in generalization, or whether the rank constraint imposes fundamental differences. Prior comparisons relied on perplexity from single runs, which may misrepresent solution quality.
AI.method: Benchmark five low-rank pre-training methods (GaLore, Fira, CoLA, SLTrain, ReLoRA) against full-rank training across three model scales (60M, 130M, 350M). Evaluate 16 metrics along four axes: (1) 1-D loss landscape along random/top-K PCA directions, (2) 1-D interpolation between checkpoints, (3) spectral structure of weights and updates, (4) activation similarity to full-rank. Analyze solutions in terms of basins, geometry, and activation trajectories.
AI.result: Low-rank methods converge to geometrically distinct basins and are not equivalent to full-rank training or to each other. Full-rank models occupy sharper basins along random directions, while low-rank models are sharper along the top-1 PCA direction. Activations from low-rank methods diverge from full-rank in later layers, with GaLore most closely aligning with full-rank. Validation perplexity is an imperfect predictor of downstream performance across scales; geometric and spectral metrics better predict cross-method/downstream behavior.
AI.conclusion: Perplexity-centered evaluations are insufficient for comparing low-rank and full-rank training. A richer suite of geometric and spectral diagnostics reveals baseline differences and improves cross-method and cross-scale predictions. Low-rank methods do not trivially substitute full-rank training and should be evaluated with diverse diagnostics.

title: Spatiotemporal downscaling and nowcasting of urban land surface temperatures with deep neural networks
summary: Land Surface Temperature (LST) is a key variable for various applications, such as urban climate and ecology studies. Yet, existing satellite-derived LST products provide either high spatial or high temporal resolution, resulting in a fundamental trade-off between the two. To address this trade-off, we combine observations from a geostationary and a polar orbiting satellite and provide LST fields at high spatial and high temporal resolution (1 km at 15-min intervals). We demonstrate their application for intraday forecasting of LSTs. To estimate LST fields at high spatiotemporal resolution, a U-Net model is trained to map LST fields from SEVIRI/MSG (3 km and 15 min resolution) to LST fields from Terra/Aqua MODIS (1 km, 4 overpasses per day) that are collocated in space and time.
