The presented model has been trained on LSTs across large European cities with a population exceeding 1 million inhabitants, and achieves an RMSE = $1.92$°C and near-zero bias MBE = $0.01$°C on the hold-out test set. As a second step, we present an LST nowcasting model based on ConvLSTM architecture, trained across downscaled LST fields with forecast lead times of 15 to 75 minutes. The nowcasting model outperforms a persistence and a Climatological Rolling Median benchmarks, with RMSEs of $0.57$ to $1.15$°C for the considered lead times and biases ranging from $-0.1$ to $0.14$°C. An additional validation conducted against independent MODIS overpasses confirms robust performance. Our LST forecast model at high spatiotemporal resolution is directly applicable to operational satellite-based LST monitoring.

AI.tldr: A method to synthesize 1 km, 15‑min LST fields by fusing geostationary and polar-orbit data using a U‑Net, plus a ConvLSTM nowcasting model for 15–75 min forecasts; validated over European cities with favorable RMSE and near-zero bias, supporting operational LST monitoring.
AI.motivation: Overcome the persistent trade-off between spatial and temporal resolution in satellite LST products to enable high-resolution, timely urban climate insights and intraday forecasting.
AI.method: Step 1: use SEVIRI/MSG (3 km, 15 min) to predict Terra/Aqua MODIS (1 km, ~4 overpasses/day) LST fields via a trained U‑Net; Step 2: train a ConvLSTM nowcasting model on downscaled LST fields to forecast 15–75 min ahead. Training and validation span large European cities (>1M population) with independent validation via MODIS overpasses.
AI.result: Mapping model achieves RMSE ≈ 1.92°C with near-zero bias (MBE ≈ 0.01°C) on hold-out; nowcasting yields RMSE ≈ 0.57–1.15°C across lead times with biases ≈ −0.1 to 0.14°C; robust validation against independent overpasses confirms reliability.
AI.conclusion: High spatiotemporal-resolution LST forecasts are feasible and suitable for operational monitoring, enabling accurate intraday LST fields by combining geostationary and polar-orbit data and applying deep learning-based spatiotemporal forecasting.

title: Rethinking Generalization in Graph Neural Networks: A Structural Complexity Perspective
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a fundamental tool for learning from graph-structured data, achieving strong performance across a wide range of applications. However, understanding their generalization capabilities remains challenging due to the complex structural dependencies inherent in such data. Existing generalization analyses largely follow the classical machine learning paradigm, focusing primarily on model complexity while overlooking the fundamental role of graph structure. Therefore, in this work, we systematically investigate this role by asking: does the graph structure actually influence generalization, and if so, by how much? To answer the first question and validate our intuition, we theoretically prove that incorporating more edges into the prediction process transforms the input representations to be overly accommodating to the output model, thereby inducing overfitting. To address the second question, we formulate a structural complexity measure based on the number of effective edges and derive a Rademacher complexity-based generalization bound. In doing so, we demonstrate that GNN generalization depends explicitly on structural complexity, alongside traditional parameter-dependent factors. Motivated by these theoretical findings, we propose a structural entropy regularization method. This approach controls structural complexity by regulating effective edges to balance underfitting and overfitting, ultimately improving the generalization performance of GNNs.

AI.tldr: GNN generalization hinges on structural complexity: more edges can cause overfitting; the paper defines a structural complexity via effective edges, derives a Rademacher-based bound, and proposes structural entropy regularization to regulate effective edges and improve generalization.
AI.motivation: Traditional generalization analyses focus on model complexity, neglecting the graph's structural contribution. This work aims to quantify how graph structure influences generalization and to regulate it.
AI.method: Theoretically show that adding more edges makes input representations too flexible, leading to overfitting; define a structural complexity measure based on effective edges; derive a Rademacher complexity-based generalization bound that depends on structural complexity; propose a structural entropy regularization to control effective edges.
AI.result: Demonstrates that GNN generalization depends on structural complexity in addition to parameter-based factors; provides a generalization bound incorporating structural complexity; introduces a regularization method (structural entropy) that improves generalization by balancing underfitting/overfitting.
AI.conclusion: Structural complexity is a fundamental factor in GNN generalization. Controlling effective edges via regularization is a viable strategy to improve performance, highlighting the need to incorporate graph structure into generalization analyses.

title: A Hierarchical Language Model with Predictable Scaling Laws and Provable Benefits of Reasoning
summary: We introduce a family of synthetic languages with hierarchical structure -- generated by a broadcast process on trees -- for which the role of context length and reasoning in autoregressive generation can be analyzed precisely. At the heart of our analytic approach is an \emph{exact $k$-gram ansatz} in place of transformers with context length $k$, a substitution we then validate empirically. Using this ansatz we derive explicit asymptotic predictions for distributional statistics of the sequences produced by a trained model, instantiated in two settings. For the \emph{Ising broadcast process} (a soft-constrained language), we prove that the variance of the generated sum scales log-linearly in the context depth and its kurtosis converges to that of a Gaussian -- both deviating from the true language for any sublinear context. For the \emph{coloring broadcast process} (a hard-constrained language) in the freezing regime, bounded-context autoregression produces sequences that, with high probability, are inconsistent with \emph{any} valid coloring of the underlying tree. Together these results imply an $Ω(n)$ lower bound on the context length required to faithfully sample length-$n$ sequences. In contrast, we prove that an autoregressive \emph{reasoning} model with only $Θ(\log n)$ working memory can sample exactly from the true language -- an exponential improvement. We confirm both the lower-bound predictions and the reasoning-based upper bound empirically with transformers trained on the synthetic language; the trained models track our asymptotic predictions quantitatively across a wide range of context sizes.

AI.tldr: Presents a formal  analysis of language-like sequences generated on tree broadcasts, deriving a precise k-gram (context-length) ansatz and showing fundamental limits: Ω(n) context needed for faithful sampling in bounded-context autoregression, but Θ(log n) memory suffices for exact sampling via a reasoning model; validated both analytically and empirically on two constrained-language settings.
AI.motivation: To understand how autoregressive models' context length and explicit reasoning influence the ability to accurately sample from hierarchical, tree-structured languages, and to identify fundamental memory/compute trade-offs.
AI.method: Introduce an exact k-gram (context-length) ansatz as a surrogate for transformers with fixed context k; develop two synthetic hierarchical languages (Ising broadcast and coloring broadcast) generated by broadcast processes on trees; derive asymptotic distributional statistics under the ansatz; prove lower bounds for bounded-context autoregression and upper bounds for a reasoning-based model; perform empirical validation with transformers.
AI.result: Ising case: generated sums have variance that grows log-linearly with context depth and kurtosis tends to Gaussian, but both deviate from the true language for any sublinear context. Coloring case (freezing regime): bounded-context autoregression produces sequences that are with high probability not valid colorings. Consequently, any faithful sampling of length-n sequences requires context Ω(n). However, a reasoning model with Θ(log n) memory can sample exactly from the true language. Empirical experiments with transformers align with the theoretical predictions across a range of context sizes.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental context-length trade-off in sampling hierarchical languages: bounded-context autoregression incurs unavoidable misalignment unless the context scales at least linearly with sequence length, whereas explicit reasoning-enabled models with logarithmic memory enable exact sampling efficiently. The work both lowers bounds and offers constructive upper bounds, reinforced by empirical validation.

title: Deep Learning as Neural Low-Degree Filtering: A Spectral Theory of Hierarchical Feature Learning
comment: 62 pages, many figures, companion codes in https://github.com/IdePHICS/Neural-LoFi-Theory
summary: Understanding how deep neural networks learn useful internal representations from data remains a central open problem in the theory of deep learning. We introduce Neural Low-Degree Filtering (Neural LoFi), a stylized limit of gradient-based training in which hierarchical feature learning becomes an explicit iterative spectral procedure. In this limit, the dynamics at each layer decouple: given the current representation, the next layer selects directions with maximal accessible low-degree correlation to the label. This yields a tractable surrogate mechanism for deep learning, together with a natural kernel-space interpretation. Neural LoFi provides a mathematically explicit framework for studying multi-layer feature learning beyond the lazy regime. It predicts how representations are selected layer by layer, explains how emergence of concepts arises with given sample complexity,and gives a concrete mechanism by which depth progressively constructs new features from old ones through low-degree compositionality. We complement the theory with mechanistic experiments on fully connected and convolutional architectures, showing that Neural LoFi improves over lazy random-feature baselines, recovers meaningful structured filters, and predicts representations aligned with early gradient-descent feature discovery with real datasets.

AI.tldr: Neural LoFi is a theoretical framework for deep learning in a gradient-training limit where layerwise learning becomes an explicit iterative spectral process. Each layer greedily selects directions with maximal low-degree correlation to the label, decoupling across layers and admitting a kernel-space interpretation. The result is a tractable surrogate for multi-layer feature learning beyond the lazy regime, with predictions about representation formation, sample complexity, and depth-driven compositional feature construction. Empirical results in fully connected and convolutional nets show improvement over lazy baselines, recovery of structured filters, and alignment with early gradient-descent discoveries on real data.
AI.motivation: To answer how deep neural networks acquire useful internal representations, a major open question in theory, by moving beyond the lazy (kernel) regime and providing an explicit, tractable framework for multi-layer feature learning.
AI.method: Introduce Neural Low-Degree Filtering (Neural LoFi), a stylized limit of gradient-based training in which layerwise dynamics decouple and each layer selects directions with maximal accessible low-degree correlation to the label. This yields an explicit spectral procedure per layer and a kernel-space interpretation, enabling theoretical analysis of representation learning across layers.
AI.result: The framework yields concrete predictions on how representations are built layer by layer, how concepts emerge with finite sample complexity, and how depth constructs new features from old via low-degree compositionality. Mechanistic experiments on fully connected and convolutional networks show Neural LoFi improves over lazy random-feature baselines, recovers meaningful structured filters, and yields representations aligned with early gradient-descent feature discovery on real datasets.
AI.conclusion: Neural LoFi provides a mathematically explicit framework for studying multi-layer feature learning beyond the lazy regime, clarifying how depth facilitates progressive feature construction and guiding analyses of sample complexity and representation formation.

title: The WidthWall: A Strict Expressivity Hierarchy for Hypergraph Neural Networks
summary: Hypergraphs provide a natural framework to model higher-order interactions in scientific, social, and biological systems. Hypergraph neural networks (HGNNs) aim to learn from such data, yet it remains unclear which higher-order structures these models can represent. We show that hypergraph expressivity is governed by which small patterns an architecture can detect and count. We formalize this via homomorphism densities, which measure how often a structural motif appears in a hypergraph. Combining classical homomorphism-count completeness with invariant approximation, we show that homomorphism densities generate all continuous hypergraph invariants and organize them into a strict hierarchy indexed by hypertree width. This yields a Width Wall: a fundamental architectural limit beyond which no hidden dimension, training procedure or fixed-depth HGNN can represent invariants requiring wider patterns. Our framework provides a unified characterization of 15 HGNN architectures, precisely identifies information lost by clique expansion, and motivates density-aware models that extend expressivity beyond bounded-width message passing. We experimentally validate this finding on an APPLICATION NODE CLASSIFICATION SUITE of real-world hypergraphs, where the Width Wall predicts when graph-reduction baselines fail and when density features help.

AI.tldr: Hypergraph neural networks' expressivity is governed by small motif detection; a Width Wall bounds representational power by hypertree width; homomorphism densities form a complete, hierarchical basis for continuous hypergraph invariants, revealing limits of bounded-width architectures and the value of density-aware models.
AI.motivation: To clarify what higher-order structures HGNNs can represent, identify fundamental architectural limits, compare against clique expansion, and guide the design of more expressive models.
AI.method: Formalize homomorphism densities as motif counts; establish completeness with invariant approximation; build a hierarchy of hypergraph invariants indexed by hypertree width; analyze 15 HGNN architectures; define the Width Wall; compare with clique expansion; validate on an application node-classification suite of real-world hypergraphs.
AI.result: Homomorphism densities generate all continuous hypergraph invariants and organize them by hypertree width, revealing a Width Wall beyond which bounded-width HGNNs cannot capture wider patterns; clique expansion loses information; density-aware models extend expressivity and predict when reductions fail; empirical validation on real-world data supports the theory.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a unified theory tying HGNN expressivity to motif densities and hypertree width, identifying a fundamental architectural limit (Width Wall) and advocating density-aware designs to surpass it; results offer practical guidance for when reductions fail and when density features help.

title: Multimodal Graph-based Classification of Esophageal Motility Disorders
summary: Diagnosing esophageal motility disorders pose significant challenges due to the complexity of high-resolution impedance manometry (HRIM) data and variability in clinical interpretation. This work explores the feasibility of a multimodal Machine Learning (ML)-based classification approach that combines HRIM recordings with patient-specific information and incorporates a graph-based modeling of esophageal physiology. We analyze HRIM recordings with corresponding patient information from 104 patients with esophageal motility disorders. Patient data includes demographic, clinical, and symptom information extracted from structured questionnaires and free-text notes using keyword detection and large language model-based processing. HRIM data is represented as spatio-temporal graphs, where nodes correspond to pressure values along the esophagus and edges encode spatial adjacency and impedance dynamics. A graph neural network (GNN) is applied to learn physiologically meaningful representations, which are fused with patient embeddings for multi-category, multi-class classification of swallow events. The impact of patient features and graph-based modeling is evaluated by ablation studies and comparison to vision-based classifier baselines. The proposed multimodal approach indicates improvements over models that rely solely on HRIM-derived features across all classification categories. Additionally, the graph-based modeling provides gains compared to vision-based baselines. Our experiments systematically assess the complementary contribution of multiple modalities, as well as demonstrate the feasibility of our proposed graph-based approach. Our initial findings demonstrate that integrating patient-level data with graph-based representations of HRIM signals appears to be a promising direction for more accurate classification of esophageal motility disorders.

AI.tldr: Multimodal ML for HRIM-based esophageal motility classification using graph representations and patient data; improves over HRIM-only and vision baselines on 104 patients.
AI.motivation: HRIM data are complex and subject to interpretation variability; integrating patient-level information and physiologic graph modeling may enhance classification accuracy.
AI.method: Represent HRIM as spatio-temporal graphs with nodes as esophageal pressure points and edges encoding spatial-adjacent and impedance-dynamic relations; apply graph neural networks to learn representations; fuse with patient embeddings derived from structured and free-text data via keyword detection and LLM-based processing; perform multi-class swallow-event classification; compare via ablation studies to HRIM-only and vision-based baselines.
AI.result: The multimodal and graph-based approach yields improvements over HRIM-only features across all swallow-event categories; graph-based modeling also outperforms vision-based baselines; ablation studies confirm complementary contributions of modalities; feasibility demonstrated in 104 patients.
AI.conclusion: Integrating patient-level data with graph-based HRIM representations is a promising direction for more accurate classification of esophageal motility disorders.

title: Achieving $ε^{-2}$ Sample Complexity for Single-Loop Actor-Critic under Minimal Assumptions
summary: In this paper, we establish last-iterate convergence rates for off-policy actor--critic methods in reinforcement learning. In particular, under a single-loop, single-timescale implementation and a broad class of policy updates, including approximate policy iteration and natural policy gradient methods, we prove the first $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-2})$ sample complexity guarantee for finding an $ε$-optimal policy under minimal assumptions, namely, the existence of a policy that induces an irreducible Markov chain. This stands in stark contrast to the existing literature, where an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-2})$ sample complexity is achieved only through nested-loop updates and/or under strong, algorithm-dependent assumptions on the policies, such as uniform mixing and uniform exploration. Technically, to address the challenges posed by the coupled update equations arising from the single-loop implementation, as well as the potentially unbounded iterates induced by off-policy learning, our analysis is based on a coupled Lyapunov drift framework. Specifically, we establish a geometric convergence rate for the actor and an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/T)$ convergence rate for the critic, and combine the two Lyapunov drift inequalities through a cross-domination property. We believe this analytical framework is of independent interest and may be applicable to other coupled iterative algorithms with unbounded

AI.tldr: First single-loop, single-timescale off-policy actor-critic with broad policy updates achieves near-optimal sample complexity tilde O(ε^-2) under minimal irreducibility assumptions, by a coupled Lyapunov drift framework. Actor converges geometrically; critic at tilde O(1/T).
AI.motivation: Relax the need for nested-loop updates and strong policy/mixing assumptions in off-policy RL; provide practical, scalable guarantees for last-iterate convergence.
AI.method: Coupled Lyapunov drift analysis for jointly updating actor and critic under a single-loop, single-timescale scheme; handles unbounded iterates due to off-policy learning; cross-domination of drift inequalities to relate actor and critic errors; applies to broad policy-update family including approximate policy iteration and natural policy gradient.
AI.result: Proves tilde O(ε^-2) sample complexity to obtain an ε-optimal policy under minimal assumption (existence of irreducible Markov chain policy); establishes geometric convergence for the actor and tilde O(1/T) convergence for the critic; introduces a framework potentially applicable to other coupled iterative algorithms with unbounded iterates.
AI.conclusion: The coupled Lyapunov drift framework advances understanding of last-iterate convergence in off-policy actor-critic methods and may generalize to other coupled, unbounded-iterate iterative algorithms, reducing reliance on nested loops and strong mixing/exploration assumptions.

title: Multi-Objective and Mixed-Reward Reinforcement Learning via Reward-Decorrelated Policy Optimization
summary: Complex reinforcement learning environments frequently employ multi-task and mixed-reward formulations. In these settings, heterogeneous reward distributions and correlated reward dimensions often destabilize the construction of scalar advantages. To address these challenges, we propose Reward-Decorrelated Policy Optimization (RDPO), a reward-processing method designed to explicitly target both failure modes. RDPO first utilizes Magnitude-Aware Quantile normalization to stabilize prompt-level advantage allocation across binary, fractional, and continuous rewards. It then applies Mahalanobis whitening within each active reward subspace to mitigate correlation redundancy prior to aggregation. When applied during the post-training of LongCat-Flash, RDPO enhances instruction following, writing quality, and robustness to hard prompts while remaining broadly competitive on reasoning and coding evaluations.

AI.tldr: RDPO uses reward normalization and decorrelation to stabilize scalar advantages in multi-task RL with mixed rewards, improving post-training performance on LongCat-Flash for instruction following, writing quality, and robustness to hard prompts, with competitive reasoning and coding.
AI.motivation: In complex reinforcement learning settings with multi-task and mixed reward signals, heterogeneous distributions and correlated reward dimensions hinder stable scalar advantage estimation; a method that both normalizes and decorrelates rewards can improve stability and generalization.
AI.method: Magnitude-Aware Quantile normalization to stabilize prompt-level advantage across binary, fractional, and continuous rewards, followed by Mahalanobis whitening within each active reward subspace to reduce correlation before aggregation; applied during post-training of LongCat-Flash.
AI.result: RDPO enhances instruction following, writing quality, and robustness to hard prompts, while remaining broadly competitive on reasoning and coding evaluations.
AI.conclusion: RDPO is a viable reward-processing approach to address failure modes in multi-task RL with mixed rewards, improving stability of scalar advantages and downstream performance, particularly in post-training scenarios like LongCat-Flash.

title: Graph Neural Networks with Triangle-Based Messages for the Multicut Problem
summary: The multicut problem is an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem with diverse applications in fields such as bioinformatics, data mining and computer vision. Graph neural networks have been defined for the multicut problem but can be adapted further to its specific objective function and constraints. In this article, we introduce such an adapted graph neural network architecture in which features are assigned only to edges, and the computation of messages is based on triangles in the underlying graph. Experiments with synthetic and real-world instances with up to 200 nodes show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art heuristic solvers in terms of solution quality while maintaining feasible runtimes. For some instances, our method finds optimal solutions in seconds whereas exact solvers need hours to find and certify optimal solutions.

AI.tldr: Edge-centric GNN with triangle-based message passing for multicut; outperforms heuristics in solution quality on graphs up to 200 nodes, solving some instances near-optimally in seconds while exact solvers take hours.
AI.motivation: Multicut is NP-hard; requires scalable methods that deliver high-quality feasible solutions and exploit problem structure. Existing heuristics underperform in solution quality; learning-based models can be tailored to the objective and constraints.
AI.method: Proposed GNN architecture assigns features only to edges; messages propagate along triangle substructures in the underlying graph; the model is adapted to the multicut objective and its constraints, evaluated on synthetic and real-world graphs up to 200 nodes.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art heuristic solvers in solution quality with feasible runtimes; some instances yield optimal solutions in seconds, whereas exact solvers require hours to certify optimality.
AI.conclusion: Edge-centric, triangle-based GNN design effectively addresses multicut on moderate-sized graphs, offering practical advantages over heuristics and approaching exact-solver performance in some cases; scalability and generalization to larger graphs merit further study.

title: High-Rate Quantized Matrix Multiplication II
summary:
This is the second part of the work investigating quantized matrix multiplication (MatMul). In part I we considered the case of calibration-free quantization, whereas here we discuss the setting where covariance matrix $Σ_X$ of the columns of the second factor is available. This setting arises in the ubiquitous task of weight-only post-training quantization of LLMs.
  Weight-only quantization is related to the problem of weighted mean squared error (WMSE) source coding, whose classical (reverse) waterfilling solution dictates how one should distribute rate between coordinates of the vector. We show how waterfilling can be used to improve practical LLM quantization algorithms (GPTQ), which at present allocate rate equally. A recent scheme (known as ``WaterSIC'') that only uses scalar INT quantizers is analyzed and its high-rate performance is shown to be (a) basis free (i.e., characterized by the determinant of $Σ_X$ and, thus, unlike existing schemes, is immune to applying random rotations); and (b) within a multiplicative factor of $\frac{2πe}{12}$ (or 0.25 bit/entry) of the information-theoretic distortion limit. GPTQ's performance, in turn, is affected by the choice of basis, but for a random rotation and actual $Σ_X$ from Llama-3-8B we find it to be within 0.1 bit (depending on the layer type) of WaterSIC, suggesting that GPTQ with random rotation is also near optimal, at least in the high-rate regime.

AI.tldr: Covariance-aware quantized MatMul for weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ); waterfilling-based rate allocation improves WMSE-based quantization; WaterSIC is basis-free and within a constant gap of the distortion limit; GPTQ with random rotations approaches WaterSIC in the high-rate regime.
AI.motivation: Leverage known covariance of the columns of the second factor to improve weight-only PTQ for LLMs. Link WMSE source coding with practical GPTQ pipelines, address basis dependence, and establish near-optimality in high-rate regimes.
AI.method: Apply the reverse-waterfilling solution from WMSE source coding to distribute rate across coordinates of the quantized matrix. Analyze WaterSIC with scalar INT quantizers, derive basis-free performance dependent on det(Σ_X), and compare to GPTQ under random rotations using Σ_X from LLama-3-8B. Evaluate high-rate behavior and layer-dependent performance.
AI.result: WaterSIC achieves a distortion within a multiplicative factor of (2πe/12) ≈ 0.25 bit per entry of the information-theoretic limit and is basis-free. GPTQ with random rotation and actual Σ_X from LLama-3-8B performs within ~0.1 bit (layer-dependent) of WaterSIC in the high-rate regime.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating Σ_X into weight quantization and applying waterfilling yields near-optimal or near-waterfilling performance for LLM weight quantization. WaterSIC provides a principled, basis-invariant benchmark; GPTQ can approach WaterSIC with random rotations, especially at high rates, guiding practical PTQ design.

title: Three-Stage Learning Unlocks Strong Performance in Simple Models for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
summary: Recent studies on long-term time series forecasting have shown that simple linear models and MLP-based predictors can achieve strong performance without increasingly complex architectures. However, many competitive baselines still rely on structural priors such as frequency-domain modeling, explicit decomposition, multi-scale mixing, or sophisticated cross-variable interaction modules, while paying less attention to how simple temporal mappings should be trained and organized. In this paper, we propose STAIR, short for Stagewise Temporal Adaptation via Individualization and Residual Learning, a training paradigm for long-term time series forecasting that aims to unlock the capacity of simple temporal mapping models without introducing complex architectural modules. STAIR decomposes forecasting ability into three progressive stages: it first learns common temporal dynamics across variables through a shared temporal mapping, then adapts the shared model to each variable via channel-wise fine-tuning to capture variable-specific patterns, and finally complements the backbone with cross-variable information through residual learning. We further introduce Shared-to-Individual Fine-tuning and alpha-RevIN to mitigate the limitations of strict channel independence and the overly strong normalization prior induced by standard RevIN. This design gradually increases modeling flexibility while keeping the core temporal predictor as a shallow MLP in the main experiments, with linear variants analyzed separately. Experiments on nine long-term forecasting benchmarks show that STAIR matches or outperforms recent strong baselines while preserving a simple temporal backbone, providing a concise and effective modeling perspective for long-term time series forecasting.

AI.tldr: STAIR: a stagewise training paradigm for long-term time-series forecasting that leverages a shallow MLP backbone with shared-to-individual fine-tuning and residual learning, plus RevIN-based normalization refinements, achieving strong results on nine benchmarks without complex architectures.
AI.motivation: Many state-of-the-art studies rely on architectural priors and complex modules, while simple temporal mappings are underexplored in training organization. STAIR aims to unlock the capacity of simple models by staged adaptation and minimal architectural changes.
AI.method: Train in three stages: (1) learn a shared temporal mapping across variables; (2) adapt to each variable via channel-wise fine-tuning; (3) add cross-variable information through residual learning. Introduce Shared-to-Individual Fine-tuning and alpha-RevIN to address channel independence and normalization biases. Backbone remains a shallow MLP (linear variants analyzed).
AI.result: On nine long-term forecasting benchmarks, STAIR matches or surpasses strong baselines while keeping a simple temporal backbone.
AI.conclusion: STAIR offers a concise, flexible training paradigm that increases modeling capacity progressively without heavy architectural changes, providing a practical perspective for long-term time-series forecasting.

title: MinT: Managed Infrastructure for Training and Serving Millions of LLMs
summary: We present MindLab Toolkit (MinT), a managed infrastructure system for Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) post-training and online serving. MinT targets a setting where many trained policies are produced over a small number of expensive base-model deployments. Instead of materializing each policy as a merged full checkpoint, MinT keeps the base model resident and moves exported LoRA adapter revisions through rollout, update, export, evaluation, serving, and rollback, hiding distributed training, serving, scheduling, and data movement behind a service interface. MinT scales this path along three axes. Scale Up extends LoRA RL to frontier-scale dense and MoE architectures, including MLA and DSA attention paths, with training and serving validated beyond 1T total parameters. Scale Down moves only the exported LoRA adapter, which can be under 1% of base-model size in rank-1 settings; adapter-only handoff reduces the measured step by 18.3x on a 4B dense model and 2.85x on a 30B MoE, while concurrent multi-policy GRPO shortens wall time by 1.77x and 1.45x without raising peak memory. Scale Out separates durable policy addressability from CPU/GPU working sets: a tensor-parallel deployment supports 10^6-scale addressable catalogs (measured single-engine sweeps through 100K) and thousand-adapter active waves at cluster scale, with cold loading treated as scheduled service work and packed MoE LoRA tensors improving live engine loading by 8.5-8.7x. MinT thus manages million-scale LoRA policy catalogs while training and serving selected adapter revisions over shared 1T-class base models.

AI.tldr: MinT provides a scalable infrastructure for managing LoRA-based policies over large base models, enabling million-scale policy catalogs and efficient training, evaluation, and serving via a service interface while avoiding full-checkpoint materialization.
AI.motivation: In multi-policy RL/production, many policies are derived from a few expensive base models. Materializing each as a full checkpoint is wasteful; there is a need to hide distributed training, data movement, and serving behind a robust service interface and to scale across model families (dense and MoE).
AI.method: MinT keeps the base model resident and streams exported LoRA adapter revisions through rollout, update, export, evaluation, serving, and rollback. It scales along three axes: (1) Scale Up — supports frontier-scale dense and MoE architectures (including MLA and DSA attention paths), validated beyond 1T parameters; (2) Scale Down — uses adapter-only handoffs, often under 1% of base-model size (rank-1), with step-time reductions (18.3x on 4B dense; 2.85x on 30B MoE) and multi-policy GRPO speedups (1.77x and 1.45x) without increasing peak memory; (3) Scale Out — separates durable policy addressability from CPU/GPU working sets, enabling tensor-parallel deployments that manage ~10^6 catalogs and thousands of active adapter waves, with cold loading treated as scheduled work and packed MoE LoRA tensors improving live loading by 8.5–8.7x.
AI.result: The approach is validated with base models at or beyond 1T total parameters, showing large-scale catalog management (10^6 addressable catalogs, 100K single-engine sweeps) and efficient policy revision training/serving over shared 1T-class bases. Adapter-only handoff and packing strategies yield substantial speedups and loading improvements across dense and MoE regimes.
AI.conclusion: MinT demonstrates a practical, scalable solution for managing the lifecycle of LoRA-based policies on massive base models, enabling million-scale policy catalogs with efficient training, evaluation, and serving while hiding infrastructure complexity behind a service interface.

title: Sampling from Flow Language Models via Marginal-Conditioned Bridges
summary: Flow Language Models (FLMs) are a recently introduced class of language models which adapt continuous flow matching for one-hot encoded token sequences. Their denoisers have a special structure absent from generic continuous diffusion models: each block of the denoising mean is a posterior marginal distribution over the clean token at that position. Standard DDPM-style samplers collapse these marginals to a single conditional-mean endpoint and bridge toward this simplex-valued point, which is generally not a valid one-hot sequence. We argue that the natural sampler for an FLM is instead posterior-predictive. At each reverse step, we sample a clean one-hot endpoint from the factorized posterior defined by the FLM token marginals, and then sample the next continuous state from the analytic Ornstein--Uhlenbeck bridge conditioned on that endpoint. The method is training-free, uses the same model evaluations as standard sampling, and gives a principled interface for token-level decoding controls such as temperature scaling and nucleus truncation. We show that, under exact posterior marginals, the endpoint approximation error is exactly the conditional multi-information among token positions. The induced one-step bridge kernel preserves all token-wise posterior-predictive marginals and loses only the residual cross-position dependence. Finally, we prove a Girsanov path-space comparison showing that the marginal-conditioned bridge has a no-larger denoising-error term than the frozen conditional-mean bridge, with strict improvement whenever intermediate coordinate-wise bridge observations reveal additional information about the clean token. Experiments with FLMs show that the sampler improves the quality--diversity tradeoff. Code is available at: github.com/imbirik/mcb.

AI.tldr: Posterior-predictive sampling for Flow Language Models (FLMs) preserves token-wise posteriors and yields improved quality-diversity without training, by sampling clean one-hot endpoints from the marginal posteriors and then sampling the next state via an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck bridge; theoretical guarantees compare to standard DDPM-style samplers and experiments show performance gains; code available.
AI.motivation: Standard DDPM-style samplers collapse token marginals to a single endpoint, producing invalid one-hot sequences for FLMs. Need a sampling method that respects the FLM's unique denoising structure and enables token-level controls.
AI.method: Reverse-step procedure: sample a clean one-hot endpoint from the factorized posterior given token marginals; then sample the next continuous state from the analytic OU bridge conditioned on that endpoint. This yields a training-free sampler with the same model evaluations as standard sampling.
AI.result: Endpoint approximation error equals the conditional multi-information among token positions; the bridge preserves token-wise marginals and reduces cross-position dependence; a Girsanov path-space comparison shows no larger denoising error than the frozen conditional-mean bridge, with strict improvement when bridge observations provide more info. Empirical gains in quality-diversity; supports temperature scaling and nucleus truncation; code at github.com/imbirik/mcb.
AI.conclusion: The proposed posterior-predictive sampling is principled, training-free, and improves the quality-diversity tradeoff for FLMs, with theoretical guarantees and practical controls.

title: Scale-Sensitive Shattering: Learnability and Evaluability at Optimal Scale
summary:
We study the optimal scale at which real-valued function classes exhibit uniform convergence and learnability. Our main result establishes a scale-sensitive generalization of the fundamental theorem of PAC learning: for every bounded real-valued class and every $γ>0$, uniform convergence at scale $γ$, agnostic learnability at scale $γ/2$, and finiteness of the fat-shattering dimension at every scale $γ'>γ$ are equivalent. This resolves a question by Anthony and Bartlett (Cambridge Univ. Press 1999) on the precise scales governing learnability, refuting a conjecture attributed there to Phil Long that a multiplicative 2-factor gap is unavoidable, and improves the upper bounds of Bartlett and Long (JCSS 1998), which incur such a loss.
  The key technical ingredient is a direct bound on empirical $\ell_\infty$ covering numbers, avoiding the standard detour through packing numbers. As a consequence, we obtain sharp asymptotic metric-entropy bounds in terms of the fat-shattering scale $γ$: an $O(\log^2 n)$ bound holds already at scale $γ/2$, while an $O(\log n)$ bound holds at scale $2γ$. We further show that the $O(\log^2 n)$ bound is sometimes tight. These results resolve open questions by Alon et al. (JACM 1997) and Rudelson and Vershynin (Ann. of Math. 2006).
  As an application, we establish a sharp dichotomy for bounded integral probability metrics: every such IPM is either estimable or cannot be weakly evaluated within any multiplicative factor $c<3$, while $3$-weak evaluability always holds, resolving an open question from Aiyer et al. (ICML 2026). We also highlight several open questions on quantitative sample complexity and evaluability.

AI.tldr: Presents a scale-sensitive generalization of PAC learnability: for any bounded real-valued class and γ>0, uniform convergence at scale γ, agnostic learnability at scale γ/2, and finiteness of fat-shattering at all scales above γ are equivalent. Provides sharp metric-entropy bounds and resolves long-standing scale-gap questions for real-valued learning; yields a sharp IPM evaluability dichotomy.
AI.motivation: To identify the precise scale dependence of learnability and uniform convergence for real-valued function classes, resolving conjectures about scale gaps, tightening entropy bounds, and understanding evaluability of integral probability metrics.
AI.method: Introduce a direct bound on empirical L∞ covering numbers to avoid detours via packing numbers. Derive scale-sensitive equivalences between uniform convergence, agnostic learnability, and fat-shattering finiteness. Obtain sharp asymptotic metric-entropy bounds (O(log^2 n) at γ/2 and O(log n) at 2γ) and prove tightness in some cases. Apply these tools to establish a dichotomy for bounded IPMs and investigate evaluability thresholds.
AI.result: Equivalence of three scale-dependent notions (uniform convergence at γ, agnostic learnability at γ/2, fat-shattering finiteness above γ). Sharp entropy bounds: O(log^2 n) at γ/2, O(log n) at 2γ, with tightness in some regimes. A sharp IPM evaluability dichotomy: estimable or not within any c<3; 3-weak evaluability always holds. Resolutions of open questions from multiple prior works (Anthony & Bartlett; Alon et al.; Rudelson & Vershynin; Aiyer et al.).
AI.conclusion: This work settles the precise scale-dependent learnability picture for real-valued classes, refutes the conjectured universal 2-factor gap, and provides tight entropy bounds and evaluability results with several open questions remaining in quantitative sample complexity and evaluability.

title: ENSEMBITS: an alphabet of protein conformational ensembles
summary: Protein structure tokenizers (PSTs) are workhorses in protein language modeling, function prediction, and evolutionary analysis. However, existing PSTs only capture local geometry of static structures, and miss the correlated motions and alternative conformational states revealed by protein ensembles. Here we introduce Ensembits, the first tokenizer of protein conformational ensembles. Ensembits address challenges inherent to tokenizing dynamics: deriving informative geometric descriptors across conformations, permutation-invariance encoding of variable-size ensembles, and conquering sparsity in dynamics data. Trained with a Residual VQ-VAE using a frame distillation objective on a large molecular dynamics corpus, Ensembits outperforms all related methods on RMSF prediction, and is the strongest standalone structural tokenizer on an token-conditioned ANOVA test on per-residue motion amplitude. Ensembits further matches or exceeds static tokenizers on EC, GO, binding site/affinity prediction, and zero-shot mutation-effect prediction despite using far less pretraining data. Notably, the distillation objective enables Ensembits to predict dynamics token from one single predicted structure, which alleviates dynamics data sparsity. As the field moves from static structure prediction toward ensemble generation, Ensembits offer the discrete vocabulary needed to bring dynamics into protein language modeling and design.

AI.tldr: Introduces Ensembits, a protein conformational ensemble tokenizer using a residual VQ-VAE and frame distillation, enabling dynamics-aware protein language modeling with improved performance across RMSF, structure-based tasks, and zero-shot mutation prediction, with reduced pretraining data requirements.
AI.motivation: Existing protein structure tokenizers (PSTs) capture only local geometry of static structures, neglecting correlated motions and alternative conformations revealed by ensembles; a discrete, permutation-invariant, and sparsity-robust tokenization of dynamics is needed to advance protein language modeling and design.
AI.method: Train a Residual Vector-Quantized VAE (R-VQ-VAE) with a frame distillation objective on a large molecular dynamics corpus; design permutation-invariant encoding for variable-size ensembles; address sparsity in dynamics data; produce dynamics tokens; enable distillation of a dynamics token from a single predicted structure.
AI.result: Outperforms related methods on RMSF prediction; strongest standalone structural tokenizer on token-conditioned ANOVA for per-residue motion amplitude; matches or exceeds static tokenizers on EC/GO/binding affinity prediction and zero-shot mutation-effect prediction with substantially less pretraining data; distillation allows predicting dynamics tokens from one structure, mitigating data sparsity.
AI.conclusion: Ensembits provide a discrete vocabulary to incorporate protein dynamics into language modeling and design, aligning with the shift from static structure prediction to ensemble generation and enabling dynamics-aware protein analysis.

title: Di-BiLPS: Denoising induced Bidirectional Latent-PDE-Solver under Sparse Observations
summary: Partial differential equations (PDEs) are fundamental for modeling complex natural and physical phenomena. In many real-world applications, however, observational data are extremely sparse, which severely limits the applicability of both classical numerical solvers and existing neural approaches. While neural methods have shown promising results under moderately sparse observations, their inference efficiency at high resolutions is limited, and their accuracy degrades substantially in the extremely sparse regime. In this work, we propose the Di-BiLPS, a unified neural framework that effectively handle both forward and inverse PDE problems under extremely sparse observations. Di-BiLPS combines a variational autoencoder to compress high-dimensional inputs into a compact latent space, a latent diffusion module to model uncertainty, and contrastive learning to align representations. Operating entirely in this latent space, the framework achieves efficient inference while retaining flexible input-output mapping. In addition, we introduce a PDE-informed denoising algorithm based on a variance-preserving diffusion process, which further improves inference efficiency. Extensive experiments on multiple PDE benchmarks demonstrate that Di-BiLPS consistently achieves SOTA performance under extremely sparse inputs (as low as 3%), while substantially reducing computational cost. Moreover, Di-BiLPS enables zero-shot super-resolution, as it allows predictions over continuous spatial-temporal domains.

AI.tldr: Introduces Di-BiLPS, a unified neural framework for forward and inverse PDE problems under extremely sparse observations, combining VAE, latent diffusion, and contrastive learning, plus a PDE-informed denoising diffusion; achieves SOTA with very sparse data and enables zero-shot super-resolution.
AI.motivation: Tackle extremely sparse observational data that hinder classical solvers and neural methods, and address inefficiency and accuracy drop at high resolutions.
AI.method: VAE to compress inputs to latent space; latent diffusion to model uncertainty; contrastive learning to align representations; operate entirely in latent space for efficient, flexible input–output mapping; PDE-informed denoising using a variance-preserving diffusion.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple PDE benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance at sparsities as low as 3%, with substantially lower computational cost; enables zero-shot super-resolution over continuous spatial–temporal domains.
AI.conclusion: Di-BiLPS provides a unified, efficient framework for forward/inverse PDEs under extreme sparsity and enables continuous-domain predictions, with strong empirical validation.

title: Improving Reproducibility in Evaluation through Multi-Level Annotator Modeling
summary: As generative AI models such as large language models (LLMs) become more pervasive, ensuring the safety, robustness, and overall trustworthiness of these systems is paramount. However, AI is currently facing a reproducibility crisis driven by unreliable evaluations and unrepeatable experimental results. While human raters are often used to assess models for utility and safety, they introduce divergent biases and subjective opinions into their annotations. Overcoming this variance is exceptionally challenging because very little data exists to study how experimental repeatability actually improves as the annotator pool grows. Standard evaluation practices typically rely on a small number of annotations per item (often 3 to 5) and lack the persistent rater identifiers necessary to model individual variance across items. In this work, we introduce a multi-level bootstrapping approach to realistically model annotator behavior. Leveraging datasets with a large number of ratings and persistent rater identifiers, we analyze the tradeoffs between the number of items ($N$) and the number of responses per item ($K$) required to achieve statistical significance.

AI.tldr: A multi-level bootstrapping framework is proposed to model annotator variance in human evaluations of AI safety. It analyzes how the number of evaluation items (N) and the number of responses per item (K) affect statistical significance, addressing reproducibility challenges in LLM assessments.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility in AI evaluations is hampered by unreliable human judgments and small annotation counts. The lack of persistent rater identifiers makes it hard to model individual biases and variance across items.
AI.method: Develop a multi-level bootstrapping approach that uses datasets with many ratings and persistent rater IDs to simulate and quantify annotator behavior. Use this framework to explore the N-K tradeoffs (items vs responses) required for statistically significant conclusions.
AI.result: A framework to quantify how N and K influence evaluation significance is introduced, enabling empirical guidance on data collection strategies and annotator pool sizing for robust safety and utility assessments.
AI.conclusion: Modeling annotator heterogeneity with multi-level bootstrapping helps optimize data collection for reproducible evaluations, informing when to increase item coverage versus response repetition to achieve reliable results.

title: Polyhedral Instability Governs Regret in Online Learning
summary: Many online decision problems over combinatorial actions are addressed via convex relaxations, leading to online convex optimization with piecewise linear objectives and induced polyhedral structure. We show that regret in such problems is governed by \emph{polyhedral instability}: the number of changes of the active region. Under full information feedback and fixed partition assumptions, if $\mathrm{RS}_T$ denotes the number of region switches and $V_{\max}$ the maximum number of vertices per region, we prove $\Regret_T= Θ(\sqrt{(1+\mathrm{RS}_T)\,T\,\log V_{\max}})$ interpolating between experts-like and dimension-dependent OCO rates. For online submodular--concave games under Lovász convexification, this reduces to the permutation-switch count $\mathrm{SC}_T$, yielding the matching rate $\Regret_T= Θ(\sqrt{(1+\mathrm{SC}_T)\,T\,\log n})$. Experiments on synthetic and real combinatorial problems (shortest path, influence maximization) validate the predicted scaling and indicate that low-instability regimes can arise in practice without explicit enumeration of actions.

AI.tldr: Regret in online convex optimization with polyhedral piecewise-linear objectives scales with polyhedral instability (region switches) and, under Lovász convexification, permutation switches; the work yields interpolating rates between experts-like and dimension-dependent OCO, supported by experiments.
AI.motivation: Address how the polyhedral partition induced by convex relaxations governs regret in online decision problems, and provide a unified rate that interpolates established OCO regimes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis under full information with a fixed partition, deriving regret bounds in terms of the number of region switches RS_T and the maximum number of vertices V_max per region. For online submodular--concave games with Lovász convexification, the bound reduces to permutation-switch count SC_T. Matching rates are established and validated experimentally.
AI.result: Regret_T = Θ( sqrt( (1+RS_T) * T * log V_max ) ). Under Lovász convexification, Regret_T = Θ( sqrt( (1+SC_T) * T * log n ) ). Experiments on synthetic and real combinatorial problems (shortest path, influence maximization) support the predicted scaling; low-instability regimes can arise without explicit action enumeration.
AI.conclusion: Polyhedral instability is the governing factor for regret in these settings, yielding a continuum of rates between classical experts-like and dimension-dependent OCO; practical implications include identifying regimes with few region switches where performance is favorable; empirical results corroborate the theory.

title: MILM: Large Language Models for Multimodal Irregular Time Series with Informative Sampling
summary: Multimodal irregular time series (MITS) consist of asynchronous and irregularly sampled observations from heterogeneous numerical and textual channels. In healthcare, for example, patients' electronic health records (EHR) include irregular lab measurements and clinical notes. The irregular timing and channel patterns of observations carry predictive signal alongside the numerical values and textual content. LLMs are natural candidates for processing such heterogeneous data, given their extensive pretrained knowledge spanning textual and numerical domains. We introduce MILM (Multimodal Irregular time series Language Model), which represents MITS as time-ordered triplets in Extensible Markup Language (XML) format and fine-tunes an LLM through a two-stage strategy for MITS classification. The first stage trains on value-redacted MITS to predict from sampling patterns alone, and the second stage trains on full MITS to jointly model sampling patterns and observed values. Our two-stage model (MILM-2S) and its single-stage counterpart (MILM-Direct) achieve the best and second-best average performance on multiple EHR datasets. Further value redaction evaluations confirm that sampling patterns carry predictive signal and that MILM-2S learns to exploit them. In the value pending evaluation we introduce, where some values are unavailable at prediction time, MILM-2S outperforms MILM-Direct by a larger margin compared to standard evaluation. For MILM-2S, preserving the time and channel of value-pending observations as additional sampling information further improves in-hospital mortality prediction.

AI.tldr: MILM introduces a two-stage, LLM-based framework (MILM-2S) for classifying multimodal irregular time series in EHRs by representing data as XML triplets; it shows sampling patterns carry predictive signal, with MILM-2S outperforming MILM-Direct, especially in value-pending scenarios, and time/channel info further boosting performance.
AI.motivation: Irregular and asynchronous MITS in healthcare contain predictive signals in sampling patterns and values. LLMs' multilingual capabilities can leverage both numerical and textual information. The work aims to reveal the predictive value of sampling patterns and improve MITS classification under missing data.
AI.method: Represent MITS as time-ordered XML triplets. Fine-tune LLM in two stages: (1) on value-redacted MITS to learn from sampling patterns alone; (2) on full MITS to jointly model sampling patterns and observed values. Compare MILM-2S with a single-stage MILM-Direct. Conduct value-redaction ablations, value-pending evaluations, and tests preserving time/channel of pending observations.
AI.result: MILM-2S achieves the best average performance across multiple EHR datasets; MILM-Direct is second-best. Value-redaction confirms sampling patterns carry predictive signal and that MILM-2S exploits them. In value-pending settings, MILM-2S outperforms MILM-Direct by a larger margin; preserving time and channel information for value-pending observations further improves in-hospital mortality prediction.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage MILM framework effectively leverages sampling patterns in multimodal irregular time series. XML triplet representation and staged fine-tuning enhance predictive performance, especially under missing-value scenarios, indicating sampling dynamics are a crucial predictive source in MITS.

title: Tight Sample Complexity Bounds for Entropic Best Policy Identification
summary: We study best-policy identification for finite-horizon risk-sensitive reinforcement learning under the entropic risk measure. Recent work established a constant gap in the exponential horizon dependence between lower and upper bounds on the number of samples required to identify an approximately optimal policy. Precisely, known lower bounds scale in $Ω(e^{|β| H})$ where $H$ is the horizon of the MDP, while the state-of-the-art upper bound achieves at best $O(e^{2|β| H})$ (arXiv:2506.00286v2) using a generative model. We show that this extra exponential factor can be traced to overly loose concentration control for exponential utilities. To close this open gap, we revisit the analysis of this problem through a forward-model based algorithm building on KL-based exploration bonuses that we adapt to the entropic criterion. The improvement we get is due to two main novel technical innovations. We leverage the smoothness properties of the exponential utility to derive sharper concentration bounds, and we propose a new stopping rule that exploits further this tightness to obtain a sample complexity that matches the lower bound.

AI.tldr: Closes the exponential horizon gap in sample complexity for best-policy identification under entropic risk by sharpening concentration bounds and a new stopping rule within a forward-model/KL-exploration framework, achieving matching upper and lower bounds.
AI.motivation: There is a known Ω(e^{|β|H}) lower bound and an O(e^{2|β|H}) upper bound on sample complexity for identifying near-optimal policies in finite-horizon risk-sensitive RL with entropic risk, leaving a gap. The goal is to remove this gap and attain tight characterization under a generative model.
AI.method: Forward-model based algorithm with KL-based exploration bonuses adapted to the entropic risk criterion. The authors exploit the smoothness of the exponential utility to derive sharper concentration bounds and introduce a new stopping rule that leverages this tightness to improve sample efficiency.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves a sample complexity that matches the Ω(e^{|β|H}) lower bound, thereby closing the previous exponential gap in horizon dependence.
AI.conclusion: Sharper concentration analysis for exponential utilities and a refined stopping rule enable tight sample complexity characterization for risk-sensitive RL under the entropic criterion; the techniques may extend to related risk measures and forward-model settings.

title: Distinguishing performance gains from learning when using generative AI
summary: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly being integrated into education, where it can boost learners' performance. However, these uses do not promote the deep cognitive and metacognitive processing that are required for high-quality learning.

AI.tldr: Generative AI in education can improve performance but may not foster the deep cognitive/metacognitive processing required for high-quality learning; design interventions are needed to address this gap.
AI.motivation: To understand how AI-assisted learning affects cognitive engagement and learning quality, and to identify gaps between performance gains and depth of learning.
AI.method: Not specified in the abstract; likely conceptual framing or literature synthesis; future empirical work needed to test cognitive engagement with AI tools.
AI.result: AI usage is associated with performance gains but may not promote deep processing or metacognition.
AI.conclusion: Instructional design and AI-tool scaffolds are needed to promote deep learning and metacognitive skills when leveraging generative AI in education.

title: Learning POMDP World Models from Observations with Language-Model Priors
summary: Whether navigating a building, operating a robot, or playing a game, an agent that acts effectively in an environment must first learn an internal model of how that environment works. Partially-observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) provide a flexible modeling class for such internal world models, but learning them from observation-action trajectories alone is challenging and typically requires extensive environment interaction. We ask whether language-model priors can reduce costly interaction by leveraging prior knowledge, and introduce \emph{Pinductor} (POMDP-inductor): an LLM proposes candidate POMDP models from a few observation-action trajectories and iteratively refines them to optimize a belief-based likelihood score. Despite using strictly less information, \emph{Pinductor} matches the performance and sample efficiency of LLM-based POMDP learning methods that assume privileged access to the hidden state, while significantly surpassing the sample efficiency of tabular POMDP baselines. Further results show that performance scales with LLM capability and degrades gracefully as semantic information about the environment is withheld. Together, these results position language-model priors as a practical tool for sample-efficient world-model learning under partial observability, and a step toward generalist agents in real-world environments. Code is available at https://github.com/atomresearch/pinductor.

AI.tldr: Language-model priors enable sample-efficient learning of POMDP world models from few trajectories, rivaling privileged-access LLM methods and outperforming tabular baselines; performance scales with LLM capability and degrades gracefully when semantic env info is withheld; code available.
AI.motivation: Partial observability makes world-model learning data-hungry; leveraging LLM priors may reduce environment interaction by injecting prior knowledge.
AI.method: Proposes Pinductor: an LLM-driven loop that generates candidate POMDP models from small observation-action trajectories and iteratively refines them to maximize a belief-based likelihood; uses POMDP structure with partial observability; does not rely on access to hidden state.
AI.result: Achieves comparable performance and sample efficiency to LLM-based POMDP learners with privileged access; significantly better than tabular baselines; performance scales with LLM capability; degrades gracefully when semantic environment information is withheld.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates practicality of language-model priors for sample-efficient world-model learning under partial observability and advances toward generalist agents; provides code for reproducibility.

title: GHGbench: A Unified Multi-Entity, Multi-Task Benchmark for Carbon Emission Prediction
summary: Open datasets and benchmarks for entity-level carbon-emission prediction remain fragmented across access, scale, granularity, and evaluation. We introduce GHGbench, an open dataset and benchmark for company- and building-level greenhouse-gas prediction. The company track contains 32,000+ company-year records from 12,000+ firms with Scope 1+2 and Scope 3 disclosures and financial/sectoral signals; the building track harmonises 491,591 building-year records from 13 open sources into a single schema across 26 metropolitan areas (10 U.S., 15 Australian, 1 Singaporean), with climate covariates and multimodal remote-sensing embeddings. GHGbench defines canonical splits with in-distribution and cross-region/city transfer as primary tasks and temporal hold-out plus short-horizon forecasting as supplementary appendix evidence; headline baselines span gradient-boosted trees, a tabular foundation model, MLP, FT-Transformer, and multimodal fusion, with an LLM panel as auxiliary, all evaluated under multi-seed paired-bootstrap tests. Three benchmark-level findings emerge: (i) building emissions are structurally harder than company emissions; (ii) the in-distribution to out-of-distribution gap dwarfs any within-model gap across both the company track and the building track, and a tabular foundation model is, to our knowledge, the first baseline to open a paired-bootstrap-significant gap over tuned trees on a multi-city building-emissions task; (iii) multimodal remote-sensing embeddings help precisely where tabular generalisation breaks. GHGbench also exposes catastrophic city transfer and the sector-factor lookup ceiling as systematic failure modes. Code and reconstruction recipes are available at GHGbench.

AI.tldr: Open dataset and benchmark GHGbench for company- and building-level emissions; reveals building emissions are structurally harder, large cross-city transfer gaps, and multimodal embeddings help where tabular models generalize poorly; provides baselines and code.
AI.motivation: To address fragmentation across access, scale, granularity, and evaluation in entity-level carbon-emission prediction by offering a unified open dataset and benchmark with canonical evaluation splits for both company- and building-level emissions.
AI.method: Construct two tracks: (1) company with 32k+ company-year records from 12k+ firms (Scope 1–3; financial/sector signals); (2) building with 491,591 building-year records from 13 open sources across 26 metros. Harmonize into a single schema; incorporate climate covariates and multimodal remote-sensing embeddings. Define canonical in-distribution and cross-region/city transfer splits, plus temporal hold-out and short-horizon forecasting. Evaluate baselines including gradient-boosted trees, a tabular foundation model, MLP, FT-Transformer, multimodal fusion, and an auxiliary LLM panel, using multi-seed paired-bootstrap tests.
AI.result: Three benchmark findings: (i) building emissions are structurally harder than company emissions; (ii) ID→OOD gap dominates any within-model gap for both tracks; a tabular foundation model is the first baseline to show a paired-bootstrap-significant gap over tuned trees on multi-city building emission tasks; (iii) multimodal remote-sensing embeddings improve performance where tabular generalization fails. Identified failure modes include catastrophic city transfer and a ceiling in sector-factor lookup. Code and reconstruction recipes are released.
AI.conclusion: GHGbench is a comprehensive open dataset and benchmark that reveals systematic generalization barriers in cross-city transfer and demonstrates the value of multimodal data and modern baselines. It provides a resource to study and improve entity-level carbon-emission prediction, with ready-to-use baselines and reproducible evaluation.

title: Fast and effective algorithms for fair clustering at scale
summary: Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning task that consists of identifying groups of similar objects. It has numerous applications and is increasingly used in fairness-sensitive domains where objects represent individuals, such as customers, employees, or students. We address a fair clustering problem in which objects belong to protected groups. The problem consists of partitioning the objects into a predefined number of clusters while attaining a user-defined target level of fairness, meaning that each protected group is sufficiently represented in each cluster. The objective is to minimize the clustering cost, defined as the sum of squared Euclidean distances between the objects and the centers of their clusters. Since clustering cost and fairness are generally in conflict, managing the trade-off between them is essential in practical applications. Existing methods provide limited control over this trade-off and either fail to scale to large datasets or, when they scale, produce low-quality solutions. We propose a general framework for fair clustering that provides precise control over the cost-fairness trade-off and introduce three heuristics based on it. The first heuristic focuses on solution quality and the flexibility to incorporate additional constraints, the second improves scalability while retaining high solution quality, and the third is designed for maximum scalability, producing solutions for instances with millions of objects in seconds. The proposed heuristics outperform existing approaches in comprehensive numerical experiments on benchmark datasets. The source code of our heuristics and instructions for reproducing the experiments are publicly available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: General fair clustering framework with tunable cost–fairness trade-off; introduces three heuristics for quality, scalability, and ultra-scale; outperforms baselines; code released on GitHub.
AI.motivation: In fairness-sensitive domains, clustering must represent protected groups within clusters while minimizing clustering cost; existing methods offer limited trade-off control, scalability, or solution quality.
AI.method: A general framework for fair clustering with three heuristics built on it: (1) a quality-focused method enabling additional constraints; (2) a scalable method maintaining high solution quality; (3) an ultra-fast, scalable method for large-scale data (millions of objects).
AI.result: Empirical results show the heuristics outperform existing approaches on benchmark datasets; framework enables precise cost–fairness control and scalability; source code and reproduction instructions are publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides flexible, precise control over the cost–fairness trade-off and scalable, high-quality heuristics, with practical impact demonstrated via experiments and open-source release.

title: Toward AI-Driven Digital Twins for Metropolitan Floods: A Conditional Latent Dynamics Network Surrogate of the Shallow Water Equations
summary: AI-driven flood digital twins demand fast hydrodynamic surrogates for ensemble forecasting and observation assimilation. Yet even GPU-accelerated two-dimensional shallow water equation (SWE) solvers still require $\sim 55$ minutes per $96$-hour run on a $\sim 4.2$-million-active-cell metropolitan basin (the Des~Plaines River basin at $30\,\mathrm{m}$ resolution), making such workloads prohibitive at native resolution. We present the Conditional Latent Dynamics Network (CLDNet): a low-dimensional latent neural ODE driven by rainfall, paired with a coordinate-based decoder conditioned on static terrain (elevation, slope, Manning roughness) that reconstructs depth and discharge at arbitrary query points. Pointwise decoding decouples memory from grid size and handles irregular watersheds natively, enabling metropolitan-scale training on a single compute node and direct queries at exact gauge coordinates without raster snapping. We evaluate CLDNet on a synthetic $250{,}000$-cell Texas benchmark and on a new Des~Plaines case study of $114$ real-rainfall Stage~IV storms whose reference simulator we validate against United States Geological Survey (USGS) gauges at the April~2013 flood-of-record (Nash--Sutcliffe efficiency $0.57$--$0.94$ on mean-recentered water-surface elevation). CLDNet roughly halves the relative root-mean-squared error of an unconditional baseline, outperforms regular-grid VAE--ConvLSTM and FNO baselines on the Texas benchmark (both presuppose a Cartesian grid and do not apply to the irregular Des~Plaines watershed), reaches a critical success index of $\approx 86\%$ at the $0.5\,\mathrm{m}$ inundation threshold, and produces a full $96$-hour basin-wide forecast in $\sim 29$ seconds -- a $\sim 115\times$ speedup.

AI.tldr: A fast, irregular-watershed hydrodynamic surrogate (CLDNet) using a latent neural ODE and coordinate-based decoder achieves accurate, rapid 96-hour flood forecasts on metropolitan basins, outperforming grid-based baselines and enabling queries at exact gauge locations.
AI.motivation: Enable ensemble forecasting and data assimilation for AI-driven flood digital twins by providing a fast, accurate surrogate that can handle irregular watersheds and arbitrary query points without costly raster operations.
AI.method: A low-dimensional latent neural ODE driven by rainfall; a coordinate-based decoder conditioned on static terrain (elevation, slope, Manning roughness) reconstructs depth and discharge at arbitrary query points. This pointwise decoding decouples memory from grid size and supports irregular watershed geometries. Training can be done on a single compute node; queries are issued at exact gauge coordinates without raster snapping.
AI.result: On a synthetic 250k-cell Texas benchmark and a Des Plaines case study with 114 real-rainfall storms, CLDNet reduces relative RMSE versus an unconditional baseline by about 50%. It outperforms regular-grid VAE–ConvLSTM and FNO baselines (which require Cartesian grids) on the Texas benchmark. It achieves a critical success index of ~86% at 0.5 m inundation threshold and delivers a full 96-hour forecast in ~29 seconds, about 115× faster than the native solver. The Des Plaines results align with USGS gauges during the 2013 flood (Nash–Sutcliffe efficiency 0.57–0.94).
AI.conclusion: CLDNet provides a scalable, accurate, and fast surrogate for metro-scale flood forecasting in irregular watersheds, enabling real-time or near-real-time ensemble forecasting and data assimilation. Its coordinate-based decoding enables exact gauge querying and bypasses grid snapping, outperforming grid-based baselines and enabling large-scale training on modest hardware.

title: Attention Once Is All You Need: Efficient Streaming Inference with Stateful Transformers
summary: Conventional transformer inference engines are request-driven, paying an O(n) prefill cost on every query. In streaming workloads, where data arrives continuously and queries probe an ever-growing context, this cost is prohibitive. We introduce a data-driven computational model centred on stateful sessions: a persistent KV cache advanced incrementally as new data arrives, so prefill is moved off the critical path and query latency becomes O(|q|), independent of accumulated context size. Building on this, Flash Queries reclaim idle GPU cycles between data arrivals to pre-evaluate registered questions and return cached answers before the user asks, a pattern that is structurally impossible in stateless engines because they discard intermediate state between requests. A multi-tenant continuous-batching scheduler with cell-budget admission and prefix-aware grouped prefill lets dozens of stateful sessions coexist on a single GPU while preserving full quadratic self-attention. On streaming market-data benchmarks the reference implementation achieves up to 5.9x speedup over conventional inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp), holding query latency constant as accumulated context grows.

AI.tldr: A data-driven, stateful-session transformer inference approach with a persistent KV cache and Flash Queries yields significant latency and throughput gains for streaming workloads by decoupling query latency from growing context, enabling multi-tenant, GPU-efficient execution with preserved quadratic self-attention; demonstrated up to 5.9x speedup over baselines on streaming market data.
AI.motivation: Streaming workloads produce continuously arriving data and queries that increasingly enlarge context. Conventional request-driven inference incurs O(n) prefill per query, causing latency to grow with history. A stateful, cache-driven model can move prefill off the critical path and stabilize latency.
AI.method: Introduce stateful sessions with a persistent key-value cache incrementally updated by new data. Employ Flash Queries to pre-evaluate registered questions during idle GPU cycles. Implement a multi-tenant continuous-batching scheduler with cell-budget admission and prefix-aware grouped prefill to host dozens of sessions on a single GPU while retaining full quadratic self-attention.
AI.result: Reference implementation achieves up to 5.9× speedup over conventional inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp) on streaming market-data benchmarks, keeping query latency constant as accumulated context grows.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that data-driven, stateful inference with persistent caching and proactive query evaluation can deliver substantial performance and latency stability in streaming settings, paving the way for scalable, real-time AI serving. Practical adoption will require integration overhead and management of persistent state across sessions.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning for Antepartum Prediction of Pregnancy-Associated Thrombotic Microangiopathy Using Routine Longitudinal Laboratory Data
summary: Background: Pregnancy-associated thrombotic microangiopathy (P-TMA) is rare but life-threatening. Early risk prediction before overt clinical presentation remains challenging, as the associated laboratory abnormalities are subtle, multidimensional, and frequently masked by common physiological changes such as gestational thrombocytopenia and pregnancy-related proteinuria, thus overlapping heavily with benign obstetric and renal conditions. This complexity is poorly captured by univariate or rule-based approaches; however, it is addressable by machine learning, which can extract latent, time-dependent risk signatures from longitudinal clinical tests. Methods: This retrospective study included 300 pregnancies comprising 142 P-TMA cases and 158 controls. After exclusion of identifiers and non-informative variables, 146 longitudinal laboratory predictors were retained.
