Participants were divided into a training cohort (80%) and a held-out test cohort (20%) using stratified sampling. Five algorithms were evaluated: logistic regression, support vector machine with radial basis function kernel, random forest, extra trees, and gradient boosting. The final model was selected by mean cross-validated AUROC, refitted on the full training cohort, and evaluated once in the held-out test cohort. Interpretability analyses examined global feature importance and distributional patterns of leading predictors. Results: Gradient boosting was prespecified by cross-validation in the training cohort. The model achieved an AUROC of 0.872 (95% CI: 0.769-0.952) and an AUPRC of 0.883 (95% CI: 0.780-0.959) in a held-out test cohort, with sensitivity of 0.750 and specificity of 0.812. Conclusions: Longitudinal clinical laboratory tests obtained during routine care contained informative and clinically plausible signals for P-TMA risk. Notably, cystatin C at week 6 showed promise as an early monitoring indicator.

AI.tldr: Gradient boosting on 146 longitudinal laboratory predictors differentiates P‑TMA risk from controls with AUROC ~0.87; cystatin C at week 6 identified as potential early monitoring indicator; supports near-term risk stratification using routinely collected lab data.
AI.motivation: To enable early, data-driven risk stratification for pregnancy-associated thrombotic microangiopathy (P-TMA) by leveraging longitudinal laboratory data and overcoming limitations of univariate and rule-based approaches.
AI.method: Retrospective study of 300 pregnancies (142 P-TMA cases, 158 controls). After excluding non-informative variables, 146 longitudinal laboratory predictors were retained. Data split into training (80%) and held-out test (20%) cohorts. Five algorithms evaluated: logistic regression, SVM with RBF kernel, random forest, extra trees, and gradient boosting. Final model chosen by mean cross-validated AUROC, refitted on full training cohort, then evaluated on the test cohort. Interpretability analyses assessed global feature importance and predictor patterns.
AI.result: Gradient boosting was prespecified by cross-validation. In the held-out test cohort, the model achieved AUROC = 0.872 (95% CI: 0.769–0.952) and AUPRC = 0.883 (95% CI: 0.780–0.959), with sensitivity 0.750 and specificity 0.812.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal routine laboratory tests carry informative, clinically plausible signals for P-TMA risk. Cystatin C measured at week 6 showed promise as an early monitoring indicator.

title: Force-Aware Neural Tangent Kernels for Scalable and Robust Active Learning of MLIPs
summary: Active learning for machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) must address several challenges to be practical: scaling to large candidate pools, leveraging energy-force supervision, and maintaining robustness when candidate pools are biased relative to the target distribution. In this work, we jointly address these challenges. We first introduce a linearly scaling acquisition framework based on chunked feature-space posterior-variance shortlisting. By avoiding materialisation of the candidate and train set kernels, this approach enables screening of ~200k structures within hours and applies broadly to acquisition strategies that score candidates based on molecular similarity metrics. We then extend the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) to a force-aware setting via mixed parameter-coordinate derivatives, yielding a force NTK and a joint energy-force NTK that provide natural similarity metrics for vector-field prediction. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the joint energy-force NTK on the OC20 dataset, where force-aware acquisition is crucial: it achieves the lowest energy and force MAE and RMSE across all metrics and distribution splits. Across T1x, PMechDB, and RGD benchmarks, our force NTK methods remain competitive with established baselines while being significantly more efficient than committee-based approaches. Under a controlled candidate-pool shift case study on T1x, acquisition based on pretrained MLIP embeddings and NTKs remains robust, whereas committee-based methods exhibit higher variance. Overall, these results show that a single pretrained MLIP can enable scalable, force-aware, and distribution-robust active learning for foundation-model fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Scalable, force-aware active learning for MLIPs using chunked feature-space posterior-variance shortlisting and force-aware NTKs, enabling robust, efficient acquisition over large candidate pools and under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Address practical active-learning challenges for MLIPs: scaling to large candidate pools, leveraging energy–force supervision, and maintaining robustness when the candidate pool is biased with respect to the target distribution.
AI.method: 1) Develop a linearly scaling acquisition framework via chunked feature-space posterior-variance shortlisting that avoids materialising train/candidate kernels, enabling screening of ~200k structures; 2) extend Neural Tangent Kernel to force-aware setting using mixed parameter-coordinate derivatives, yielding a force NTK and a joint energy–force NTK for vector-field similarity; 3) empirical evaluation on OC20 (force-aware acquisition crucial) and benchmarks T1x, PMechDB, RGD; 4) assess robustness under controlled pool-shift and compare to committee-based baselines.
AI.result: On OC20, force-aware acquisition achieves the lowest energy and force MAE/RMSE across metrics and splits. Across T1x, PMechDB, RGD, force-NTK methods are competitive with strong baselines and far more efficient than committees. In a controlled pool-shift study on T1x, pretrained MLIP embeddings and NTKs show robustness, while committee methods exhibit higher variance.
AI.conclusion: A single pretrained MLIP enables scalable, force-aware, and distribution-robust active learning for foundation-model fine-tuning, combining efficient acquisition with improved vector-field prediction performance.

title: Provable Quantization with Randomized Hadamard Transform
summary:
Vector quantization via random projection followed by scalar quantization is a fundamental primitive in machine learning, with applications ranging from similarity search to federated learning and KV cache compression. While dense random rotations yield clean theoretical guarantees, they require $Θ(d^2)$ time. The randomized Hadamard transform $HD$ reduces this cost to $O(d \log d)$, but its discrete structure complicates analysis and leads to weaker or purely empirical compression guarantees.
  In this work, we study a variant of this approach: dithered quantization with a single randomized Hadamard transform. Specifically, the quantizer applies $HD$ to the input vector and subtracts a random scalar offset before quantizing, injecting additional randomness at negligible cost. We prove that this approach is unbiased and provides mean squared error bounds that asymptotically match those achievable with truly random rotation matrices. In particular, we prove that a dithered version of TurboQuant achieves mean squared error $\bigl(π\sqrt{3}/2 + o(1)\bigr) \cdot 4^{-b}$ at $b$ bits per coordinate, where the $o(1)$ term vanishes uniformly over all unit vectors and all dimensions as the number of quantization levels grows.

AI.tldr: Dithered scalar quantization with a single randomized Hadamard transform achieves unbiased quantization with MSE scaling equivalent to random rotations, providing efficient O(d log d) computation and explicit asymptotic guarantees for TurboQuant.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of vector quantization while preserving strong theoretical guarantees, addressing analytic challenges posed by the discrete Hadamard transform and enabling reliable compression for ML workloads (similarity search, federated learning, KV caches).
AI.method: Apply the Hadamard transform HD to the input, subtract a random scalar offset (dither), then perform scalar quantization. Analyze unbiasedness and derive mean squared error bounds that asymptotically match those of truly random rotations; relate to a dithered TurboQuant variant.
AI.result: The scheme is unbiased; the MSE matches the ideal random-rotation case asymptotically. For a b-bit per-coordinate quantizer, the MSE is (π√3/2 + o(1)) · 4^{-b}, with the o(1) term vanishing uniformly over all unit vectors and dimensions as the number of quantization levels grows.
AI.conclusion: A single randomized Hadamard transform with dithering yields near-optimal quantization guarantees at a fraction of the computational cost, supporting efficient, provably reliable vector quantization in practical ML systems.

title: Uncertainty-Driven Anomaly Detection for Psychotic Relapse Using Smartwatches: Forecasting and Multi-Task Learning Fusion
summary: Digital phenotyping enables continuous passive monitoring of behavior and physiology, offering a promising paradigm for early detection of psychotic relapse. In this work, we develop and systematically study two smartwatch-based frameworks for daily relapse detection. The first forecasts cardiac dynamics and flags deviations between predicted and observed features as indicators of abnormality. The second adopts a multi-task formulation that fuses sleep with motion and cardiac-derived signals, learning time-aware embeddings and predicting measurement timing. Both pipelines use Transformer encoders and output a daily anomaly score, derived from predictive uncertainty estimated via an ensemble of multilayer perceptrons to improve robustness to real-world wearable variability. While each framework independently demonstrates strong predictive power, we show that they capture complementary physiological signatures. Consequently, we propose a late-fusion strategy that synergistically combines the anomaly signals from both architectures into a unified decision score. We benchmark our methodology on the 2nd e-Prevention Grand Challenge dataset, where our fused model achieves a 8% relative improvement over the competition-winning baseline. Our results, supported by extensive ablation studies, suggest that the integration of diverse digital phenotypes, cardiac, motion, and sleep, is essential for the high-fidelity detection of psychotic relapse in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: Two transformer-based smartwatch pipelines for daily psychotic relapse detection: (1) forecast cardiac dynamics and flag deviations as abnormalities; (2) a multi-task model fusing sleep, motion, and cardiac signals with time-aware embeddings. Both output daily anomaly scores via ensemble-derived predictive uncertainty; a late-fusion combines signals. On the 2nd e-Prevention Grand Challenge, fused model achieves 8% relative improvement over the baseline. Ablations show complementary signatures and the necessity of integrating diverse phenotypes for high-fidelity relapse detection.
AI.motivation: Enable continuous, passive monitoring to enable early relapse detection in psychotic disorders. Leverage diverse digital phenotypes (cardiac, sleep, motion) and model complementarity to improve robustness to real-world wearable variability.
AI.method: Two architectures using Transformer encoders: (A) a cardiac-dynamics forecasting model where deviations between predictions and observations indicate abnormality, with predictive uncertainty estimated via an ensemble of multilayer perceptrons; (B) a multi-task model fusing sleep, motion, and cardiac signals with time-aware embeddings to predict measurement timing, also yielding an anomaly score from an ensemble. Both produce daily anomaly scores; a late fusion combines the two anomaly signals into a unified score.
AI.result: The fused model achieves an 8% relative improvement over the competition-winning baseline on the 2nd e-Prevention Grand Challenge dataset. Ablation studies indicate that the two architectures capture complementary physiological signatures, and the integration of cardiac, motion, and sleep phenotypes is key for high-fidelity relapse detection in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Complementary digital phenotypes derived from cardiac, motion, and sleep data, processed via two Transformer-based frameworks with ensemble uncertainty and a late-fusion strategy, yield improved daily relapse detection performance and robustness in real-world wearable conditions.

title: Reducing cross-sample prediction churn in scientific machine learning
summary: Scientific machine learning reports predictive performance. It does not report whether the same prediction would survive a different draw of training data. Across $9$ chemistry benchmarks, two classifiers trained on independent bootstraps of the same training set agree on aggregate accuracy to within $1.3\text{--}4.2$ percentage points but disagree on the class label of $8.0\text{--}21.8\%$ of test molecules. We call this gap \emph{cross-sample prediction churn}. The standard parameter-side techniques (deep ensembles, MC dropout, stochastic weight averaging) do not reduce this gap; two data-side methods do. The first is $K$-bootstrap bagging, which cuts the rate $40\text{--}54\%$ on every dataset at no accuracy cost ($K{\times}$-ERM compute). The second is \emph{twin-bootstrap}, our proposal: two networks trained jointly on independent bootstraps with a sym-KL consistency loss between their predictions, which at matched $2{\times}$-ERM compute reduces churn a further median $45\%$ beyond bagging-$K{=}2$. Cross-sample prediction churn deserves a column alongside predictive performance in scientific-ML benchmark reports, because without it the parameter-side and data-side methods are indistinguishable on the metric they actually differ on.

AI.tldr: Cross-sample prediction churn: even when aggregate accuracy is similar, different training data draws yield conflicting test-label predictions (8–22% churn). Data-side bootstrap methods (K-bootstrap bagging and twin-bootstrap) reduce churn significantly without hurting accuracy, unlike common parameter-side methods.
AI.motivation: Assess robustness of predictive labels to fluctuations in training data; demonstrate that standard accuracy metrics hide instability and propose a new churn metric to benchmark scientific-ML models.
AI.method: Empirical study on 9 chemistry benchmarks. Compare two classifiers trained on independent training-set bootstraps to measure cross-sample churn. Evaluate standard parameter-side techniques (deep ensembles, MC dropout, stochastic weight averaging) and two data-side methods: (1) K-bootstrap bagging (K×-ERM) and (2) twin-bootstrap with a sym-KL consistency loss between two jointly trained networks on independent bootstraps.
AI.result: Cross-sample churn ranges 8.0–21.8% of test molecules; aggregated accuracy differs by only 1.3–4.2 percentage points. Deep ensembles, MC dropout, and SWA do not reduce churn. K-bootstrap bagging cuts churn 40–54% with no loss in accuracy. Twin-bootstrap reduces churn further by a median 45% beyond bagging with K=2 at matched 2×-ERM compute.
AI.conclusion: Churn is a critical metric alongside predictive accuracy in scientific-ML benchmarks. Data-side bootstrap methods offer substantial reductions in churn, whereas parameter-side methods fail to address this issue; reporting churn will distinguish methods that affect robustness to training-data variability.
